EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moderately unstable conditions developed over eastern Nebraska during the afternoon and evening of May 18th as temperatures warmed into the 80s and dew point temperatures reached the mid to upper 60s.  As an upper level trough approached from the Rockies, thunderstorms developed in western Nebraska and western Kansas.  These storms moved east across eastern Nebraska and western Iowa and produced isolated areas of wind damage.

Drought conditions continued improving across eastern Nebraska in May eventually ending or upgrading into the Moderate or Abnormally Dry.  Widespread rainfall of 5 to 8 inches fell over a large part of eastern Nebraska during the month, in some cases at least several inches above normal.

The drought that began in June and expanded to become an Exceptional (D4) Drought in 2012 continues into January 2013. While the weather in January was characterized as progressive the storm track provided little relief or recharge to the depleted soil moisture. Short term impacts are expected to hit agriculture and grasslands, while the long-term impacts to hydrological and ecological interests are expected to linger. In January some rainfall did occur although departures remain over four inches that keep fire dangers and potential impacts at the forefront and in the news.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The drought that began in June 2012 continued into January 2013. The exceptional (D4) drought lingered as below normal precipitation fell during the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced three tornadoes which tracked over Dixon and Dakota Counties in extreme northeast Nebraska on the late afternoon of October 4th. One damaging tornado crossed into Dixon County from Wayne County Nebraska, a brief tornado formed and dissipated in Dixon County, and another tornado developed in northern Dakota County before crossing the Missouri River into Union County South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado developed in northern Dakota County and caused no reported damage before crossing the Missouri River into Union County of South Dakota.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As low pressure aloft deepened from western Nebraska into South Dakota, thunderstorms developed across central and eastern Nebraska during the early to mid afternoon hours.  Temperatures climbed into the 70s and 80s and dew point  temperatures in the 60s created moderately unstable conditions across the region.  Several lines of severe thunderstorms moved across the area producing some wind damage along with several reports of large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The hail was mostly nickel-size but a few reached quarter-size.

As an upper level trough approached from the Rockies, thunderstorms developed in western Nebraska and western Kansas.  These storms moved east across eastern Nebraska and western Iowa and produced isolated areas of wind damage.
Late afternoon thunderstorms produced some large hail across portions of the northern Nebraska Panhandle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Marble to quarter size hail was reported five miles south of Chadron Campground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As low pressure aloft deepened from western Nebraska into South Dakota, thunderstorms developed across central and eastern Nebraska during the early to mid afternoon hours.  Temperatures climbed into the 70s and 80s and dew point  temperatures in the 60s created moderately unstable conditions across the region.  Several lines of severe thunderstorms moved across the area producing some wind damage along with several reports of large hail.

As low pressure aloft deepened from western Nebraska into South Dakota, thunderstorms developed across central and eastern Nebraska during the early to mid afternoon hours.  Temperatures climbed into the 70s and 80s and dew point  temperatures in the 60s created moderately unstable conditions across the region.  Several lines of severe thunderstorms moved across the area producing some wind damage along with several reports of large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts estimated at 65 to 70 mph downed trees in the Plattsmouth area, some of which caused vehicle and house damage.  The winds were accompanied by hail that reached half dollar-size.

The drought that began in June and expanded to become an Exceptional (D4) Drought in 2012 continues into January 2013. While the weather in January was characterized as progressive the storm track provided little relief or recharge to the depleted soil moisture. Short term impacts are expected to hit agriculture and grasslands, while the long-term impacts to hydrological and ecological interests are expected to linger. In January some rainfall did occur although departures remain over four inches that keep fire dangers and potential impacts at the forefront and in the news.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The drought that began in June 2012 continued into January 2013. The exceptional (D4) drought lingered as below normal precipitation fell during the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The drought that began in June of 2012 lingered in May, although thunderstorms produced some significant rainfall to further ease the ongoing drought. All areas received a minimum of two inches of rainfall and some over eight inches. The areal coverage of the Exceptional Drought (D4) conditions in western Nebraska was reduced to focus over the south central sandhills, while a larger portion of northern and north central Nebraska were lowered into the category of Severe Drought.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The drought that began in June 2012 lingered into May. Convective rainfall produced a range of rainfall amounts from 3 to 10 inches with the highest amounts across the north. The rain helped to ease drought conditions to a Moderate (D1) Drought in the far north, while most of the central and southeast remained in Extreme (D3) Drought conditions by the end of the month. The drought lingered into June.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful storm system moved into the central Plains on the 3rd of October and allowed for low pressure developed over the western high Plains. Ahead of this low, unseasonably warm and humid air streamed north from the Gulf of Mexico. The combination of the warm humid air and the strong upper level storm system created an atmosphere of high instability and strong shear across parts of eastern Nebraska. As a warm front slowly lifted north into southeast Nebraska during the evening of the 3rd, isolated supercell thunderstorms developed. These storms were responsible for large hail, some significant, damaging winds, and a couple of tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado began 4.5 miles south of Elmwood where minor property damage was observed. The tornado tracked northeast for 4 miles ending 3.5 mile southeast of Elmwood. The tornado generally moved across open country and caused minor tree damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The same powerful storm system that produced severe weather across eastern Nebraska on October 3rd remain in the area on the 4th. A strong area of low pressure moved from eastern Colorado into southern South Dakota during the day. As this occurred, a dry line was sweeping across central and into eastern Nebraska. A warm front continued to lift north into northeast Nebraska and western Iowa by late afternoon. With unseasonably warm and moist air in the region the atmosphere became strongly unstable. This instability coupled with a strong wind field associated with the powerful storm system and created and environment favorable for significant severe weather. Supercells developed near the intersection of the dry line and warm front over northeast Nebraska during the afternoon and tracked northeast. The supercells were responsible for large hail, damaging winds, and significant tornadoes. Other isolated thunderstorms developed south along the dry line into western Iowa producing very large hail and damaging winds during the evening of the 4th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado, captured by storm chasers, moved through rural Stanton County with no reported damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The same powerful storm system that produced severe weather across eastern Nebraska on October 3rd remain in the area on the 4th. A strong area of low pressure moved from eastern Colorado into southern South Dakota during the day. As this occurred, a dry line was sweeping across central and into eastern Nebraska. A warm front continued to lift north into northeast Nebraska and western Iowa by late afternoon. With unseasonably warm and moist air in the region the atmosphere became strongly unstable. This instability coupled with a strong wind field associated with the powerful storm system and created and environment favorable for significant severe weather. Supercells developed near the intersection of the dry line and warm front over northeast Nebraska during the afternoon and tracked northeast. The supercells were responsible for large hail, damaging winds, and significant tornadoes. Other isolated thunderstorms developed south along the dry line into western Iowa producing very large hail and damaging winds during the evening of the 4th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were also small limbs blown down at the spotter location, but winds were below severe limits.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The drought that began in June of 2012 lingered in May, although thunderstorms produced some significant rainfall to further ease the ongoing drought. All areas received a minimum of two inches of rainfall and some over eight inches. The areal coverage of the Exceptional Drought (D4) conditions in western Nebraska was reduced to focus over the south central sandhills, while a larger portion of northern and north central Nebraska were lowered into the category of Severe Drought.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The drought that began in June 2012 lingered into May. Convective rainfall produced  rainfall amounts from 3 to 4 inches with locally higher amounts to 6 inches in the northwest and extreme south. The rain while beneficial helped lower the far northwest into the Extreme (D3) Drought category. The remainder of the county remained in the Exceptional (D4) Drought conditions by the end of the month. The drought lingered into June.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The cool temperatures of July spilled over into early August across most of eastern Nebraska and western Iowa. There was appreciable rainfall in the first half of the month and this allowed for the top and subsoil moisture to remain fairly stable, but still only 55 percent of the topsoil was rated as adequate and 36 percent of the subsoil. The main agriculture impacts were seen in pastureland where browning was noted in many areas, especially in dryland farm fields where low yields were seen in hay cutting. In addition the last 13 days of August were the 7th warmest on record for Norfolk and included 9 days with highs in the 90s that contributed to the ongoing drought.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Extreme to exceptional drought abated slowly to the severe to extreme category in extreme northeast Nebraska during April. Near to a little above normal precipitation was not enough to end the long standing drought entirely, given the long term dry soil conditions that carried over into the month. Accordingly, the area was still deemed very sensitive to the potential of below normal rainfall during the planting and growing seasons. The decrease in the drought magnitude was tempered because the area was along the Missouri River in which these were continued low flows and low runoff expectations, with some of the area still having extreme drought. No new effects of the drought were noted during the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Long term dry soil conditions at the start of April abated a little during the month, with extreme to exceptional drought becoming severe to extreme. The improvement was due to normal to above normal precipitation, although low Missouri River flows tempered the improvement. No new effects were noted, but the area remained sensitive to possible worsening again of conditions, depending on precipitation during the planting and growing seasons.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed near a stationary front in northern Nebraska early in the afternoon of August 1.  The storms intensified and moved southeast through the evening and high levels of wind shear combined with moderate instability present to aid in a few of the storms becoming super-cellular in nature.  The stronger storms produced damaging winds and very heavy rainfall, but some hail reports were also received and at least one apparent tornado occurred.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter estimated wind gusts of 60-70 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The drought that began in June of 2012 lingered in May, although thunderstorms produced some significant rainfall to further ease the ongoing drought. All areas received a minimum of two inches of rainfall and some over eight inches. The areal coverage of the Exceptional Drought (D4) conditions in western Nebraska was reduced to focus over the south central sandhills, while a larger portion of northern and north central Nebraska were lowered into the category of Severe Drought.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The drought that began in June 2012 lingered into May. Convective rainfall produced rainfall amounts from 4 to 8 inches with the higher amounts across the southern portion of the county. The rain helped to ease drought conditions to Severe (D2) Drought in the southeast half of the county, while Extreme (D3) conditions lingered in the northwest by the end of the month. The drought lingered into June.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered strong to severe thunderstorms developed mostly behind a cold front that pushed across the area late on April 8th.  By the early morning hours of the 9th a cluster of these storms formed west of Columbus began surging east in a bow echo that produced a swath of wind damage from near Schuyler to just west of Fremont.  Winds were estimated to have reached around 100 mph at the peak near and just west of North Bend where an empty coal train was derailed and an unsecured mobile home was destroyed injuring 2 people.  Numerous center pivots were overturned along the path and some trees were snapped.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts were estimated to have reached 65 mph just east of Columbus.  The winds were accompanied by hail up to quarter-size.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms moved across eastern Nebraska and southwest Iowa during the morning of April 17th.  The storms developed well north of a stationary front as low pressure aloft moved from the southern Rockies toward the plains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pea to dime-size hail covered the ground in the Nebraska City area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed into southeast Nebraska during the early morning hours of June 25th.  The storms developed north of a weak boundary near the Kansas border aided by modestly high instability and a low level jet.  A few of the storms dropped large hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy precipitation with rapid cooling produced a combination of freezing rain, sleet, and snow in Dixon County in extreme northeast Nebraska from the evening of April 9th, to the morning of April 11th. Moderate to heavy ice accumulations were reported, followed moderate to heavy snow and sleet accumulations. The ice accumulations, a quarter to a half inch, caused tree and power line damage. Fallen branches and limbs were numerous, roads were blocked, and some vehicles suffered damages from the falling limbs and branches. There were also power outages because of the power line and power pole damage. Strong winds with and following the storm, not strong enough to cause significant damage under dry conditions, contributed to the tree and power line damage. The snow and sleet accumulations made travel difficult, especially in areas where downed trees and power lines were already causing road blockages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Periods of light rain and drizzle changed to freezing rain and freezing drizzle, producing light icing. The precipitation then changed to snow and sleet, with accumulations of 2 to 4 inches. The winter made travel hazardous and closed or delayed some schools.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed along an advancing cold front during the late afternoon on the 27th, and moved into northeast Nebraska during the early morning on the 28th. Widespread severe weather was not observed, but an isolated area of damaging winds did occur near Battle Creek.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported that 12 healthy trees were blown down on their property. There was also a boat flipped over, as well as shingles removed from the house.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms developed along a cold front that moved through eastern Nebraska and southwest Iowa during the afternoon of September 19.  A few of these thunderstorms produced damaging wind gusts, and at least one storm brought large hail.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts estimated at over 60 mph blew over a semi-trailer near Highways 67 and 75 with other wind strewn debris noted in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed near a stationary front in northern Nebraska early in the afternoon of August 1.  The storms intensified and moved southeast through the evening and high levels of wind shear combined with moderate instability present to aid in a few of the storms becoming super-cellular in nature.  The stronger storms produced damaging winds and very heavy rainfall, but some hail reports were also received and at least one apparent tornado occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts estimated at around 70 mph caused considerable tree damage in the Leigh area.  The fallen trees caused scattered vehicle and house damage and also knocked power out to the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance tracking across the plains caused low pressure to deepen as it tracked from northern Oklahoma into northeast Missouri.  Snow, occasionally moderate and even briefly heavy, fell from late on the 29th into the morning hours of the 30th.  Snow amounts in southeast Nebraska were generally 3 to 6 inches, with around 6 inches reported across much of Otoe county.  The snow was accompanied by north winds of 20 to 35 which caused considerable blowing and drifting snow and several hours of poor visibilities.  Due to poor visibilities, Interstate 80 was briefly closed west of Lincoln.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 3 to 5 inches with isolated amounts possibly a little higher were reported across much of Lancaster county.  Due to north winds of 20 to 35 mph, the snow and blowing snow caused poor visibilities which closed Interstate 80 west of Lincoln for several hours during the early morning of Jan. 30.  The snow and slick roads caused at least 70 accidents in the Lincoln area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the pre-dawn hours on this Wednesday morning, a fast moving line of elevated thunderstorms raced east-northeast at 50-60 MPH across several counties in South Central Nebraska mainly along and north of Interstate 80. While the majority of this activity was sub-severe, producing only small hail, the southeast periphery of the line intensified between 530-630 am CDT, dropping quarter to golf ball size hail at various points in Merrick and Polk counties before exiting the local area.  The largest reported golf ball size hail occurred in Shelby at 620 am CDT. A few miles northeast of town, an NWS cooperative observer reported half dollar size hail still on the ground two hours after it fell, thanks to cool surface temperatures in the upper 30s F. This ended up being the final severe thunderstorm event of 2013 within South Central Nebraska.||The first thunderstorms of the morning flared up over Dawson County shortly after 4 am CDT. During the ensuing few hours, storms quickly expanded into a northwest-southeast oriented line as they tracked east-northeast. On the synoptic scale aloft, this event occurred in the presence of subtle shortwave disturbances ejecting northeastward into the Central Plains out ahead of an expansive, large scale trough centered over the Intermountain West. On the smaller scale, storm initiation and intensification was tied to increasing warm air/moisture advection evident in the 850-700 millibar layer, accompanied by steepening mid level lapse rates. Mesoscale severe weather parameters around the time of the large hail featured around 1000 J/kg most-unstable CAPE in the presence of strong mid level flow around 50 knots at 500 millibars. This elevated convection occurred well north-northwest of a quasi-stationary surface front looped from far northeast Kansas into the Oklahoma Panhandle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Half dollar size hail was still on the ground over two hours after it fell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent Pacific storm system over the southern Rockies moved into the central Great Plains and produced light to moderate snow over the southwest Nebraska Panhandle.  Total storm snowfall ranged from three to six inches.  Blowing and drifting snow from gusty northerly winds caused hazardous driving conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An observer ten miles south-southeast of Broadwater measured 4.4 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance tracking across the plains caused low pressure to deepen as it tracked from northern Oklahoma into northeast Missouri.  Snow, occasionally moderate and even briefly heavy, fell from late on the 29th into the morning hours of the 30th.  Snow amounts in southeast Nebraska were generally 3 to 6 inches, with around 6 inches reported across much of Otoe county.  The snow was accompanied by north winds of 20 to 35 which caused considerable blowing and drifting snow and several hours of poor visibilities.  Due to poor visibilities, Interstate 80 was briefly closed west of Lincoln.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 2 to 5 inches likely fell across much of Seward county based on surrounding observations, with the heaviest across the southern part of the county.  Due to north winds of 20 to 35 mph, the snow and blowing snow caused poor visibilities which closed Interstate 80 west of Lincoln for several hours during the early morning of Jan. 30.  Highway 6 near Milford was also closed for a while due to semi-trucks unable to navigate hills in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Arctic high pressure building southeast across the northern plains brought nearly steady temperatures in the single digits to northeast Nebraska and west central Iowa much of Jan. 31st.  When combined with north winds of 20 to 30 mph, wind chill values fell to around 20 below to 25 below zero.

A thunderstorm produced damaging winds in Maskell in Dixon County on the evening of July 7th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds caused tree damage, including several trees blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Although this Wednesday afternoon started out with a fairly low-key development of isolated strong to severe thunderstorms in northern portions of South Central Nebraska, this convective event ended up being one of the most significant heavy rain episodes of 2013 for much of the northeast two-thirds of the 24-county area, highlighted by a small zone of 8+ inch rainfall in southeastern Greeley County. In terms of actual severe weather, this event was small in scale, featuring only a handful of quarter to golf ball size hail reports in southern Greeley and northwestern Howard counties during the late afternoon and early evening, with the largest hail stones reported four miles south of Greeley at 535 pm CDT. However, as the large hail threat diminished by sunset, heavy rainfall took over as the main story for the next several hours lasting well into Thursday morning the 3rd, as the initially isolated development in northern counties morphed into a slow-moving, southward-drifting linear mesoscale convective system (MCS). By the time all convection finally exited South Central Nebraska shortly before sunrise on the 3rd, most places generally northeast of a line from Loup City-Gibbon-Blue Hill-Superior had received at least 2-3 of rain, but with a corridor of higher amounts generally 4-6 concentrated across much of Adams, northwestern Nuckolls and southwestern Clay counties. Within these counties, just a few of the highest reported rainfall totals included 6.35 a few miles south of Juniata and 4.75 a few miles south of Trumbull. At Hastings Municipal Airport, 3.49 of the storm total of 4.13 fell on the calendar day of Oct. 3rd, shattering the previous record for highest rainfall on any given day in October (2.95 on Oct. 16, 1968). Of even greater significance, however, was the incredible amount of rain that fell mainly between 4-9pm CDT within a small portion of southeastern Greeley County. Despite limited ground-truth from the epicenter of heavy rainfall in that area, an observer five miles northwest of Wolbach measured 8.46, and radar estimates suggested that even higher amounts might have fallen roughly 4-8 miles northeast of Wolbach. Although several counties in South Central Nebraska undoubtedly experienced short-term flooding issues from this event including lowland flooding and damage to rural roads, specific flooding reports were fairly limited. However, a few known reports included water running across several county roads in southeastern Greeley County, and also water up to the hood of a vehicle on Highway 92 east of Ashton near the Sherman-Howard County line. Fortunately, a stretch of predominantly dry weather during the preceding few weeks likely mitigated more significant flooding on larger creeks and rivers. As for thunderstorm winds during this event, there were no known-instances of severe-criteria gusts, although a RAWS sensor in western Clay County clocked a near-severe gust to 54 MPH. ||Taking a closer look at event timing, the initial development of localized strong to severe storms got underway within primarily Sherman, Howard, Valley and especially southern Greeley counties between 3-7 pm CDT. During these early stages, several very slow-moving to nearly stationary multicell storms dumped heavy rainfall and isolated large hail over many of the same areas. Then between 7-10 pm CDT, the initial storm clusters finally started sinking slowly southward toward the Interstate 80 corridor, while gradually expanding in coverage and elongating into a west-southwest to east-northeast linear system. From about 10 pm to 1 am CDT, the MCS propagated slowly southward across Interstate 80 and into counties along Highway 6 from near Holdrege to Geneva. During the pre-dawn hours of the 3rd, widespread convection continued drenching primarily those counties south of Interstate 80 and near/east of Highway 281, with backbuilding along the western edge of the complex resulting in the enhanced rainfall totals over Adams County and vicinity. Finally, between 6-7 am CDT, the back edge of weakening convection departed the eastern fringes of the local area, ending nearly 16 consecutive hours of thunderstorm activity within South Central Nebraska. On the synoptic scale aloft this was a fairly weakly-forced event, as South Central Nebraska resided under relatively weak west-southwest mid-upper level flow, downstream from a larger scale trough organizing over the Pacific Northwest and Northern Rockies. However, a low-level focus for convective development existed in the form of a southwest-northeast oriented cold front that roughly bisected Nebraska during the late afternoon and evening hours. The initial mid-late afternoon storms flared up near this frontal boundary, aided by forcing from a subtle shortwave trough passing through the area. During these early stages, mesoscale severe weather parameters within the local area featured fairly modest mixed-layer CAPE values of 1000-1500 J/kg and generally no more than 30 knots of 0-6 kilometer vertical wind shear. However, the combination of the weak shear environment featuring mid-upper level flow nearly parallel to the surface front, along with seasonably-high low level moisture reflected by surface dewpoints in the low-mid 60s F, fostered the slow storm movement and resultant heavy rainfall. As the night wore on, the southward expansion of the MCS was aided by the development of a south-southwesterly low level jet that ramped up to around 40 knots at 850 millibars, enhancing moisture transport into the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter to ping pong size hail occurred. Moderate to heavy rain also occurred.

The event-total was also the second-highest two-day October rainfall on record, falling just short of 4.25 that fell on Oct. 16-17, 1968.

In terms of actual severe weather, this event was small in scale, featuring only a handful of quarter to golf ball size hail reports in southern Greeley and northwestern Howard counties during the late afternoon and early evening, with the largest hail stones reported four miles south of Greeley at 535 pm CDT. By the time all convection finally exited South Central Nebraska shortly before sunrise on the 3rd, most places generally northeast of a line from Loup City-Gibbon-Blue Hill-Superior had received at least 2-3 of rain, but with a corridor of higher amounts generally 4-6 concentrated across much of Adams, northwestern Nuckolls and southwestern Clay counties. Within these counties, just a few of the highest reported rainfall totals included 6.35 a few miles south of Juniata and 4.75 a few miles south of Trumbull. At Hastings Municipal Airport, 3.49 of the storm total of 4.13 fell on the calendar day of Oct. 3rd, shattering the previous record for highest rainfall on any given day in October (2.95 on Oct. 16, 1968). Of even greater significance, however, was the incredible amount of rain that fell mainly between 4-9pm CDT within a small portion of southeastern Greeley County. Despite limited ground-truth from the epicenter of heavy rainfall in that area, an observer five miles northwest of Wolbach measured 8.46, and radar estimates suggested that even higher amounts might have fallen roughly 4-8 miles northeast of Wolbach. Although several counties in South Central Nebraska undoubtedly experienced short-term flooding issues from this event including lowland flooding and damage to rural roads, specific flooding reports were fairly limited. However, a few known reports included water running across several county roads in southeastern Greeley County, and also water up to the hood of a vehicle on Highway 92 east of Ashton near the Sherman-Howard County line. Fortunately, a stretch of predominantly dry weather during the preceding few weeks likely mitigated more significant flooding on larger creeks and rivers. As for thunderstorm winds during this event, there were no known-instances of severe-criteria gusts, although a RAWS sensor in western Clay County clocked a near-severe gust to 54 MPH. ||Taking a closer look at event timing, the initial development of localized strong to severe storms got underway within primarily Sherman, Howard, Valley and especially southern Greeley counties between 3-7 pm CDT. During these early stages, several very slow-moving to nearly stationary multicell storms dumped heavy rainfall and isolated large hail over many of the same areas. Then between 7-10 pm CDT, the initial storm clusters finally started sinking slowly southward toward the Interstate 80 corridor, while gradually expanding in coverage and elongating into a west-southwest to east-northeast linear system. From about 10 pm to 1 am CDT, the MCS propagated slowly southward across Interstate 80 and into counties along Highway 6 from near Holdrege to Geneva. During the pre-dawn hours of the 3rd, widespread convection continued drenching primarily those counties south of Interstate 80 and near/east of Highway 281, with backbuilding along the western edge of the complex resulting in the enhanced rainfall totals over Adams County and vicinity. Finally, between 6-7 am CDT, the back edge of weakening convection departed the eastern fringes of the local area, ending nearly 16 consecutive hours of thunderstorm activity within South Central Nebraska. On the synoptic scale aloft this was a fairly weakly-forced event, as South Central Nebraska resided under relatively weak west-southwest mid-upper level flow, downstream from a larger scale trough organizing over the Pacific Northwest and Northern Rockies. However, a low-level focus for convective development existed in the form of a southwest-northeast oriented cold front that roughly bisected Nebraska during the late afternoon and evening hours. The initial mid-late afternoon storms flared up near this frontal boundary, aided by forcing from a subtle shortwave trough passing through the area. During these early stages, mesoscale severe weather parameters within the local area featured fairly modest mixed-layer CAPE values of 1000-1500 J/kg and generally no more than 30 knots of 0-6 kilometer vertical wind shear. However, the combination of the weak shear environment featuring mid-upper level flow nearly parallel to the surface front, along with seasonably-high low level moisture reflected by surface dewpoints in the low-mid 60s F, fostered the slow storm movement and resultant heavy rainfall. As the night wore on, the southward expansion of the MCS was aided by the development of a south-southwesterly low level jet that ramped up to around 40 knots at 850 millibars, enhancing moisture transport into the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A rainfall total of 6.35 inches occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: According to the U.S. Drought Monitor, November 2013 marked the 18th consecutive month during which drought of at least category D2 Severe intensity plagued at least some portion of South Central Nebraska, prolonging one of the most notable droughts in decades. In contrast to the improvements that took place during September and October, the U.S. Drought Monitor reflected no change whatsoever in drought classification during November across the 24-county area. This maintenance of the status quo largely resulted from the area having neither an extremely dry nor extremely wet month, as the driest places still managed around 0.50 of precipitation, while the wettest places only tallied slightly under 2.00. As a result, the total number of counties within the area entirely or partially assigned D2 Drought remained at six. All six of these worst-off counties were clustered in the far southwest portion of the area: Dawson, Gosper, Phelps, Furnas, Harlan and Franklin. Meanwhile, a fairly narrow zone of Category D1 Moderate Drought continued to border up against the D2 areas, and covered the majority of several counties mainly west of Highway 281 such as Valley, Sherman, Kearney and Webster. Although these poorer drought conditions persisted in western/southwestern counties, the majority of South Central Nebraska, including nearly all areas along/east of the Highway 281 corridor, finished November in Category D0 Abnormally Dry conditions (considered to be one category better than true drought). In addition, a small corridor running from eastern Adams County through southwest Clay County into northern Nuckolls County remained void of all drought classification (including D0). ||Reports from around 50 NWS Cooperative Observers, and also radar-derived estimation, revealed that at least 60% of South Central Nebraska observed below normal November precipitation (based on long-term November normals generally between 0.95-1.40). A dividing line between wetter-in-the-southeast and drier-in-the-northwest set up roughly along an axis from Red Cloud-Hastings-Gresham. For most of the area, a significant percentage of monthly precipitation fell during an early-month rain event on the 5th, while very little, if hardly any fell thereafter. As just one example of this: Hastings airport measured 0.97 on the 5th, but went on to receive a meager 0.02 through the rest of the month. Per a combination of NWS cooperative observers and NeRAIN observers, a few of the highest November precipitation totals in southeast areas featured 1.78 four miles east of Superior, 1.76 one mile north of Red Cloud, 1.65 at Clay Center and Davenport, and 1.64 at Lawrence. Within the drier northwestern portions of the area, some of the lowest monthly amounts included 0.33 at Spalding, 0.50 at Ord airport, 0.56 eight miles south-southwest of Bertrand and 0.59 at Arcadia. In between these wettest and driest extremes, the majority of the 24-county area totaled somewhere between 0.75-1.50, including stations in or near Grand Island, Hastings, York, Osceola, Minden, Holdrege and Gothenburg. ||Taking a look at longer-term precipitation trends, without question the driest conditions so far in 2013 continued to focus within the southwest one-third of South Central Nebraska, mainly southwest of a line from Lexington-Red Cloud. According to the USDA Nebraska Crop Progress and Condition Report for the week ending November 24th, state-averaged topsoil moisture supplies had changed relatively little since late-October, now rating 25% short or very short and 75% adequate. Subsoil moisture supplies exhibited slight improvement during that time frame, as the short or very short category decreased from 55% to 43%.

A potent Pacific storm system over the southern Rockies moved into the central Great Plains and produced light to moderate snow over the southwest Nebraska Panhandle.  Total storm snowfall ranged from three to six inches.  Blowing and drifting snow from gusty northerly winds caused hazardous driving conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An observer eight miles north of Lodgepole measured six inches of snow.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An estimated wind gust of 60 mph was reported north of Crawford.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms developed during the afternoon of July 2 on the backside of an upper level low pressure system.  Modest instability and shear allowed one or two of these slowly moving storms to become severe with large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail as large as ping pong ball-size fell north of Western and west of Wilber for about 5 minutes.  The hail partially shredded crops in the area and some accumulation was noted on roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic airmass brought bitter cold temperatures and dangerous wind chills to portions of North Central Nebraska during the overnight hours December 4th into the morning hours December 5th, 2013.  Dangerous wind chills of 30 below to 40 below affected Sheridan and Cherry County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An arctic airmass brought bitter cold temperatures and dangerous wind chills of 30 below to 35 below to Eastern Cherry County during the overnight hours December 4th into the morning hours December 5th, 2013.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An arctic airmass brought bitter cold temperatures of 15 to 25 below and dangerous wind chills of 30 below to 45 below to Western Cherry County during the overnight hours December 5th into the morning hours December 6th, 2013.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts estimated at over 60 mph downed some very large trees in the Tailwaters campground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms develop along a weak cold front in northeast Nebraska during the late morning and afternoon of July 25th. Isolated reports of severe hail occurred with one of the storms.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Appreciable rainfall across the area was sparse during the month. The city of Omaha had it second driest July on record only behind the July of 2012, Norfolk and Lincoln had their 19th and 13th driest July's respectfully. With soil moisture limited from previous dry periods, this allowed for drought conditions to increase across most of eastern Nebraska and western Iowa, but especially across parts of northeast Nebraska where the combination of warmer temperatures and drier ground to start created worse drought conditions. Cooler than normal temperatures across most of western Iowa and eastern Nebraska tended to mitigate more extreme drought conditions. Nevertheless in northeast Nebraska nearly 50 percent of the ground soil moisture was rated at short and 24 percent was very short by the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions improved little across eastern Nebraska during January.  Precipitation during the month generally ranged from 1/4 inch or less in northeast Nebraska to nearly an inch across the southeast.  These amounts were near or a little above normal across the southeast but a little below average elsewhere.  Thus, Exceptional Drought conditions continued in northeast Nebraska near and northwest of a line from Columbus to Pender with the Drought intensity remaining in the Extreme category southeast of there from around Lincoln and Beatrice northeast through Omaha and Tekamah.  Conditions were slightly better southeast of Lincoln and Omaha, but they were still in the Severe category.  The drought persisted into February.

Numerous thunderstorms moved east across Hitchcock and Red Willow counties during the evening.  An industrial building was half destroyed in McCook when an intense downburst blew off the roof and one wall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large metal industrial building oriented east-west lost its roof and the south wall by an intense thunderstorm downburst.  The roof debris was blown to the north, with pieces thrown onto Hwy 34.  Time of damage is estimated from a nearby wind gust at the McCook airport and by Doppler radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous thunderstorms moved east across Hitchcock and Red Willow counties during the evening.  An industrial building was half destroyed in McCook when an intense downburst blew off the roof and one wall.

A small but intense thunderstorm developed and moved northeast across Dundy county during the early evening.  The storm produced quarter size hail and strong outflow broke six power poles along highway 34 east of Benkelman.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Newspaper reported a semi-trailer was overturned near Haigler sometime during the evening.  Time of this event is approximate based on reviewing Doppler radar archives.

According to the U.S. Drought Monitor, November 2013 marked the 18th consecutive month during which drought of at least category D2 Severe intensity plagued at least some portion of South Central Nebraska, prolonging one of the most notable droughts in decades. In contrast to the improvements that took place during September and October, the U.S. Drought Monitor reflected no change whatsoever in drought classification during November across the 24-county area. This maintenance of the status quo largely resulted from the area having neither an extremely dry nor extremely wet month, as the driest places still managed around 0.50 of precipitation, while the wettest places only tallied slightly under 2.00. As a result, the total number of counties within the area entirely or partially assigned D2 Drought remained at six. All six of these worst-off counties were clustered in the far southwest portion of the area: Dawson, Gosper, Phelps, Furnas, Harlan and Franklin. Meanwhile, a fairly narrow zone of Category D1 Moderate Drought continued to border up against the D2 areas, and covered the majority of several counties mainly west of Highway 281 such as Valley, Sherman, Kearney and Webster. Although these poorer drought conditions persisted in western/southwestern counties, the majority of South Central Nebraska, including nearly all areas along/east of the Highway 281 corridor, finished November in Category D0 Abnormally Dry conditions (considered to be one category better than true drought). In addition, a small corridor running from eastern Adams County through southwest Clay County into northern Nuckolls County remained void of all drought classification (including D0). ||Reports from around 50 NWS Cooperative Observers, and also radar-derived estimation, revealed that at least 60% of South Central Nebraska observed below normal November precipitation (based on long-term November normals generally between 0.95-1.40). A dividing line between wetter-in-the-southeast and drier-in-the-northwest set up roughly along an axis from Red Cloud-Hastings-Gresham. For most of the area, a significant percentage of monthly precipitation fell during an early-month rain event on the 5th, while very little, if hardly any fell thereafter. As just one example of this: Hastings airport measured 0.97 on the 5th, but went on to receive a meager 0.02 through the rest of the month. Per a combination of NWS cooperative observers and NeRAIN observers, a few of the highest November precipitation totals in southeast areas featured 1.78 four miles east of Superior, 1.76 one mile north of Red Cloud, 1.65 at Clay Center and Davenport, and 1.64 at Lawrence. Within the drier northwestern portions of the area, some of the lowest monthly amounts included 0.33 at Spalding, 0.50 at Ord airport, 0.56 eight miles south-southwest of Bertrand and 0.59 at Arcadia. In between these wettest and driest extremes, the majority of the 24-county area totaled somewhere between 0.75-1.50, including stations in or near Grand Island, Hastings, York, Osceola, Minden, Holdrege and Gothenburg. ||Taking a look at longer-term precipitation trends, without question the driest conditions so far in 2013 continued to focus within the southwest one-third of South Central Nebraska, mainly southwest of a line from Lexington-Red Cloud. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions improved little across eastern Nebraska during January.  Precipitation during the month generally ranged from 1/4 inch or less in northeast Nebraska to nearly an inch across the southeast.  These amounts were near or a little above normal across the southeast but a little below average elsewhere.  Thus, Exceptional Drought conditions continued in northeast Nebraska near and northwest of a line from Columbus to Pender with the Drought intensity remaining in the Extreme category southeast of there from around Lincoln and Beatrice northeast through Omaha and Tekamah.  Conditions were slightly better southeast of Lincoln and Omaha, but they were still in the Severe category.  The drought persisted into February.
No change was seen in drought conditions across Dundy, Hitchcock and Red Willow counties during August.  The area continued to be rated as being in D3 (Extreme) Drought.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A highly unusual, albeit minor and fairly low-impact episode of Platte River flooding that began in South Central Nebraska in late September continued into the first few days of October before river levels commenced a steadier drop. More specifically, minor flooding during the first few days of October primarily concentrated along the river through Buffalo and Hall counties (and the adjacent extreme northern borders of Phelps and Kearney counties), with the gauge near Kearney slipping below minor flood stage of 6.0 feet on Wednesday morning the 2nd. This ended roughly 10 days of minor flooding along the Platte within South Central Nebraska that mainly impacted lowlands immediately adjacent to the river as minor flooding first reached the Gothenburg area around Sep. 22 and gradually spread as far downstream as the Merrick/Hamilton County border northeast of Grand Island. However, by the time flood waters got very far past Grand Island, the multiple channels of the river helped disperse enough water to diminish any flooding threat farther downstream. ||The unusual aspect of this river flooding event was that it had very little, if anything to do with rain that fell directly over South Central Nebraska or even nearby upstream areas over western Nebraska. Instead, this event resulted from historic rainfall that inundated parts of the Colorado Front Range and northeast Colorado between Sep. 9-15. During this second week of September, an upper-level low pressure system formed and became cut off from the mean flow over the Intermountain West. This low slowly migrated from near Las Vegas north to Idaho. Subtropical high pressure remained stationary over the Southern Plains. This synoptic configuration channeled a stream of anomalously high moisture into the Rockies. With surface high pressure essentially stationary over the Northern Plains, several days of easterly upslope flow into the Colorado Rockies resulted in tremendous rainfall and catastrophic flash flooding in and around Boulder, Colorado. The heaviest rains of all occurred on Sep. 11-13. Widespread multiple-day rainfall ended up totaling from around 7 to as high as 17 inches in some areas. In the city of Boulder, all rainfall records were broken for the month of September, for any calendar month and for any year. Given that northeastern Colorado is the headwaters of the South Platte River, this tremendous volume of water began a slow but steady journey down the South Platte, reaching the confluence with the North Platte River near North Platte, Nebraska, on Friday, September 20th. ||By Sunday Sep. 22nd, the leading edge of flooding reached western Dawson County within the local South Central Nebraska area. River levels rose rapidly and dramatically at Gothenburg and points downstream. Prior to the arrival of the flood waters, Platte River levels were quite low due to prevailing below normal long term precipitation within and near the local Platte basin. In fact, river flow in some areas prior to the flooding was down to a narrow trickle up to a couple feet wide. As a result, the ensuing flood water filled the breadth of the riverbed and its multiple channels, with standing water occurring in some low-lying areas adjacent to the riverbanks. As the water reached the gauge near Cozad, the river level increased from one foot to five feet. The peak crest at Cozad was around 7 ft. on Sep. 24. It was a similar story as the water continued to move downstream, with river levels peaking in the Overton and Kearney areas between Sep. 25-29 and peaking near Grand Island in the Sep. 29-Oct. 1 time frame. At all locations along the river, once water levels reached the crest they were slow to recede and took around three days to drop one foot from the crest height.  This stood as a testament to the significant volume of water generated from the flooding rains far upstream, and it was especially impressive given that it took little more than a single day for river levels to initially rise several feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm brought heavy snow to portions of North Central Nebraska during the evening hours of December 3rd into the early morning hours of December 4th.  Northwest winds of 15 to 25 mph combined with snowfall amounts of 4 to 15 inches to produce snow drifts up to 3 feet across Sheridan, Western Cherry, Eastern Cherry Keya Paha, and Brown County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A winter storm brought heavy snowfall of 4 to 10 inches to Sheridan County during the evening hours of December 3rd into the early morning hours of December 4th. The heaviest snowfall near 10 inches fell across central Sheridan county. Northwest winds of 15 to 25 mph produced low visibilities less than 1 mile and snow drifts of 1 to 2 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The drought that began in June 2012 continued across western Nebraska through December 2013. The greatest impacts were across the southwest where Extreme Drought (D3) conditions were observed. Generally less than normal precipitation occurred during the month for little change in the drought. The drought continued into January.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The drought that began in June 2012 continued into December 2013. Below normal precipitation fell with one to two tenths inch observed that resulted in the Severe Drought (D2) to Extreme Drought (D3) conditions lingering. The drought continued into January 2014.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the pre-dawn hours on this Wednesday morning, a fast moving line of elevated thunderstorms raced east-northeast at 50-60 MPH across several counties in South Central Nebraska mainly along and north of Interstate 80. While the majority of this activity was sub-severe, producing only small hail, the southeast periphery of the line intensified between 530-630 am CDT, dropping quarter to golf ball size hail at various points in Merrick and Polk counties before exiting the local area.  The largest reported golf ball size hail occurred in Shelby at 620 am CDT. A few miles northeast of town, an NWS cooperative observer reported half dollar size hail still on the ground two hours after it fell, thanks to cool surface temperatures in the upper 30s F. This ended up being the final severe thunderstorm event of 2013 within South Central Nebraska.||The first thunderstorms of the morning flared up over Dawson County shortly after 4 am CDT. During the ensuing few hours, storms quickly expanded into a northwest-southeast oriented line as they tracked east-northeast. On the synoptic scale aloft, this event occurred in the presence of subtle shortwave disturbances ejecting northeastward into the Central Plains out ahead of an expansive, large scale trough centered over the Intermountain West. On the smaller scale, storm initiation and intensification was tied to increasing warm air/moisture advection evident in the 850-700 millibar layer, accompanied by steepening mid level lapse rates. Mesoscale severe weather parameters around the time of the large hail featured around 1000 J/kg most-unstable CAPE in the presence of strong mid level flow around 50 knots at 500 millibars. This elevated convection occurred well north-northwest of a quasi-stationary surface front looped from far northeast Kansas into the Oklahoma Panhandle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ground was about 50 percent covered by half dollar size hail. The time was estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated strong to severe thunderstorms flared up in eastern portions of South Central Nebraska on this Thursday evening, primarily affecting Nance, Clay and especially Fillmore counties. Although at least small hail affected various parts of these counties, including nickel size hail reported north of Genoa, all severe-criteria reports originated in Fillmore County from a supercell storm, including golf ball size stones in the Geneva area around 9 pm CDT. While hail was the main issue, strong thunderstorm winds also blew over a semi trailer just south of Geneva. In east central Fillmore County, enhanced low-level rotation was detected by radar for a short time around 930 pm CDT, but no tornadoes were reported with this storm as all tornadic activity on this night occurred east of the local area in southeast Nebraska. These storms also produced localized heavy rainfall up to around 2 inches, with the highest reported amount of 2.36 from the cooperative observer in Geneva. ||Thunderstorms initiated over Clay and Nuckolls counties between 7-8 pm CDT, along a rather sharp quasi-stationary surface front aligned from southeast Nebraska into north central/west central Kansas. During this early evening time frame, winds north of the front were from the northeast while breezes south of the boundary prevailed from the south-southeast in the presence of surface dewpoints well into the 60s F, providing ample low level convergence. Between 8-11 pm CDT, a small cluster of strong to severe storms became established within Clay and Fillmore counties, with the eastern-most storm in Fillmore County organizing into a high-precipitation supercell before exiting east into Saline County. This upswing in storm coverage/severity shortly after nightfall focused along the nose of a developing 40 knot low level jet evident at 850 millibars. As the initial round of storms along the Highway 6 corridor started to weaken and depart, a separate cluster of strong thunderstorms blossomed farther north, primarily affecting Nance, northeastern Merrick and northern Polk counties between 10 pm-12 am CDT. Non-severe thunderstorms festered across these far northeast counties into the early morning hours of the 4th, but by 3 am CDT all convection had departed South Central Nebraska for the night.||The environment was ripe for supercell development by early evening, with mesoscale parameters featuring around 1500 J/kg most-unstable CAPE in the presence of around 40 knots of 0-6 kilometer deep layer wind shear. On the synoptic scale aloft, this event occurred in the presence of subtle, low amplitude shortwave troughs embedded within strengthening southwesterly flow in the mid-upper levels, downstream from a powerful closed low centered over the Central Rockies. On the following afternoon and evening of Oct. 4th, this stout low pressure system entered the Central Plains, but South Central Nebraska was narrowly spared a significant weather event thanks to a strong surface cold front and dryline pushing east of the area by late afternoon. It was a close call, however, as several damaging tornadoes raked northeast Nebraska and northwest Iowa during the late afternoon and evening of the 4th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This report of golf ball size hail was received via Facebook. The observer explicitly stated that the hail fell at 9 pm CDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Although this Wednesday afternoon started out with a fairly low-key development of isolated strong to severe thunderstorms in northern portions of South Central Nebraska, this convective event ended up being one of the most significant heavy rain episodes of 2013 for much of the northeast two-thirds of the 24-county area, highlighted by a small zone of 8+ inch rainfall in southeastern Greeley County. In terms of actual severe weather, this event was small in scale, featuring only a handful of quarter to golf ball size hail reports in southern Greeley and northwestern Howard counties during the late afternoon and early evening, with the largest hail stones reported four miles south of Greeley at 535 pm CDT. However, as the large hail threat diminished by sunset, heavy rainfall took over as the main story for the next several hours lasting well into Thursday morning the 3rd, as the initially isolated development in northern counties morphed into a slow-moving, southward-drifting linear mesoscale convective system (MCS). By the time all convection finally exited South Central Nebraska shortly before sunrise on the 3rd, most places generally northeast of a line from Loup City-Gibbon-Blue Hill-Superior had received at least 2-3 of rain, but with a corridor of higher amounts generally 4-6 concentrated across much of Adams, northwestern Nuckolls and southwestern Clay counties. Within these counties, just a few of the highest reported rainfall totals included 6.35 a few miles south of Juniata and 4.75 a few miles south of Trumbull. At Hastings Municipal Airport, 3.49 of the storm total of 4.13 fell on the calendar day of Oct. 3rd, shattering the previous record for highest rainfall on any given day in October (2.95 on Oct. 16, 1968). Of even greater significance, however, was the incredible amount of rain that fell mainly between 4-9pm CDT within a small portion of southeastern Greeley County. Despite limited ground-truth from the epicenter of heavy rainfall in that area, an observer five miles northwest of Wolbach measured 8.46, and radar estimates suggested that even higher amounts might have fallen roughly 4-8 miles northeast of Wolbach. Although several counties in South Central Nebraska undoubtedly experienced short-term flooding issues from this event including lowland flooding and damage to rural roads, specific flooding reports were fairly limited. However, a few known reports included water running across several county roads in southeastern Greeley County, and also water up to the hood of a vehicle on Highway 92 east of Ashton near the Sherman-Howard County line. Fortunately, a stretch of predominantly dry weather during the preceding few weeks likely mitigated more significant flooding on larger creeks and rivers. As for thunderstorm winds during this event, there were no known-instances of severe-criteria gusts, although a RAWS sensor in western Clay County clocked a near-severe gust to 54 MPH. ||Taking a closer look at event timing, the initial development of localized strong to severe storms got underway within primarily Sherman, Howard, Valley and especially southern Greeley counties between 3-7 pm CDT. During these early stages, several very slow-moving to nearly stationary multicell storms dumped heavy rainfall and isolated large hail over many of the same areas. Then between 7-10 pm CDT, the initial storm clusters finally started sinking slowly southward toward the Interstate 80 corridor, while gradually expanding in coverage and elongating into a west-southwest to east-northeast linear system. From about 10 pm to 1 am CDT, the MCS propagated slowly southward across Interstate 80 and into counties along Highway 6 from near Holdrege to Geneva. During the pre-dawn hours of the 3rd, widespread convection continued drenching primarily those counties south of Interstate 80 and near/east of Highway 281, with backbuilding along the western edge of the complex resulting in the enhanced rainfall totals over Adams County and vicinity. Finally, between 6-7 am CDT, the back edge of weakening convection departed the eastern fringes of the local area, ending nearly 16 consecutive hours of thunderstorm activity within South Central Nebraska. On the synoptic scale aloft this was a fairly weakly-forced event, as South Central Nebraska resided under relatively weak west-southwest mid-upper level flow, downstream from a larger scale trough organizing over the Pacific Northwest and Northern Rockies. However, a low-level focus for convective development existed in the form of a southwest-northeast oriented cold front that roughly bisected Nebraska during the late afternoon and evening hours. The initial mid-late afternoon storms flared up near this frontal boundary, aided by forcing from a subtle shortwave trough passing through the area. During these early stages, mesoscale severe weather parameters within the local area featured fairly modest mixed-layer CAPE values of 1000-1500 J/kg and generally no more than 30 knots of 0-6 kilometer vertical wind shear. However, the combination of the weak shear environment featuring mid-upper level flow nearly parallel to the surface front, along with seasonably-high low level moisture reflected by surface dewpoints in the low-mid 60s F, fostered the slow storm movement and resultant heavy rainfall. As the night wore on, the southward expansion of the MCS was aided by the development of a south-southwesterly low level jet that ramped up to around 40 knots at 850 millibars, enhancing moisture transport into the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A rainfall total of 8.46 inches occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions improved little across eastern Nebraska during January.  Precipitation during the month generally ranged from 1/4 inch or less in northeast Nebraska to nearly an inch across the southeast.  These amounts were near or a little above normal across the southeast but a little below average elsewhere.  Thus, Exceptional Drought conditions continued in northeast Nebraska near and northwest of a line from Columbus to Pender with the Drought intensity remaining in the Extreme category southeast of there from around Lincoln and Beatrice northeast through Omaha and Tekamah.  Conditions were slightly better southeast of Lincoln and Omaha, but they were still in the Severe category.  The drought persisted into February.

The initial mid-late afternoon storms flared up near this frontal boundary, aided by forcing from a subtle shortwave trough passing through the area. During these early stages, mesoscale severe weather parameters within the local area featured fairly modest mixed-layer CAPE values of 1000-1500 J/kg and generally no more than 30 knots of 0-6 kilometer vertical wind shear. However, the combination of the weak shear environment featuring mid-upper level flow nearly parallel to the surface front, along with seasonably-high low level moisture reflected by surface dewpoints in the low-mid 60s F, fostered the slow storm movement and resultant heavy rainfall. As the night wore on, the southward expansion of the MCS was aided by the development of a south-southwesterly low level jet that ramped up to around 40 knots at 850 millibars, enhancing moisture transport into the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter to half dollar size hail occurred with rain mixed in.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the pre-dawn hours on this Wednesday morning, a fast moving line of elevated thunderstorms raced east-northeast at 50-60 MPH across several counties in South Central Nebraska mainly along and north of Interstate 80. While the majority of this activity was sub-severe, producing only small hail, the southeast periphery of the line intensified between 530-630 am CDT, dropping quarter to golf ball size hail at various points in Merrick and Polk counties before exiting the local area.  The largest reported golf ball size hail occurred in Shelby at 620 am CDT. A few miles northeast of town, an NWS cooperative observer reported half dollar size hail still on the ground two hours after it fell, thanks to cool surface temperatures in the upper 30s F. This ended up being the final severe thunderstorm event of 2013 within South Central Nebraska.||The first thunderstorms of the morning flared up over Dawson County shortly after 4 am CDT. During the ensuing few hours, storms quickly expanded into a northwest-southeast oriented line as they tracked east-northeast. On the synoptic scale aloft, this event occurred in the presence of subtle shortwave disturbances ejecting northeastward into the Central Plains out ahead of an expansive, large scale trough centered over the Intermountain West. On the smaller scale, storm initiation and intensification was tied to increasing warm air/moisture advection evident in the 850-700 millibar layer, accompanied by steepening mid level lapse rates. Mesoscale severe weather parameters around the time of the large hail featured around 1000 J/kg most-unstable CAPE in the presence of strong mid level flow around 50 knots at 500 millibars. This elevated convection occurred well north-northwest of a quasi-stationary surface front looped from far northeast Kansas into the Oklahoma Panhandle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Golf ball size hail fell in the community of Shelby.

Severe thunderstorms developed on Thursday afternoon along a warm front across  northern Nebraska. Numerous reports of large hail were noted, with the largest up to the size of baseballs.  Damaging winds, funnel clouds and heavy rainfall were also reported during this severe weather event. The preliminary local storm damage survey information, estimated the highest winds speeds in North Platte were near 100 miles an hour - where numerous large trees and large limbs were downed.  This also led to some power outages and damage to vehicles and homes. Large hail caused broken windows, from north of Whitman to southeast of the Bartlett area.  No tornadoes were confirmed, although funnel clouds were reported with supercell thunderstorms near Mullen, Arnold, and in southwestern Custer County. Extensive crop damage was found in southwestern Custer County where acres of corn and bean crops were damaged or destroyed from hail estimated up to the size of golfballs.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree limbs up to 14 inches in diameter down.

Extensive crop damage was found in southwestern Custer County where acres of corn and bean crops were damaged or destroyed from hail estimated up to the size of golfballs.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous funnel clouds were observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions improved little across eastern Nebraska during February, although precipitation amounts of 1/2 to 3/4 of an inch were near normal for the month.  Thus, Exceptional Drought conditions continued in northeast Nebraska near and northwest of a line from Columbus to Pender with the Drought intensity remaining in the Extreme category southeast of there from around Lincoln and Beatrice northeast through Omaha and Tekamah.  Conditions were slightly better southeast of Lincoln and Omaha, but they were still in the Severe category.  The drought persisted into March.

Conditions were slightly better southeast of Lincoln and Omaha, but they were still in the Severe category.  The drought persisted into March.

Drought conditions continued in extreme northeast Nebraska in February, despite precipitation which was a little above normal. The excess of a few tenths of an inch in the driest month of the year did little to relieve the long term dry soil conditions. There was little noted in the way of new effects of the drought, with the dry conditions giving a poor outlook for the Spring and Summer, including the poor germination of the winter wheat crop during the dry fall. Water restrictions continued to be few during the winter because of the low water usage, but the area was deemed vulnerable to even marginally dry weather if it developed in the spring and summer. Drought was generally listed as continued extreme to exceptional for the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Long term dry soil conditions continued, despite above normal precipitation, due to the excess being only a few tenths of an inch during the normally driest month. Few new effects were noted, with the seasonally low water usage. The main problem, aside from winter livestock feed, was the poor outlook for the spring and summer, which were expected to be extremely sensitive to dry weather.

Drought conditions continued in extreme northeast Nebraska in March. Though precipitation was near normal, drought was still rated extreme to exceptional over the area. There continued to be a lack of soil moisture as winter approached its end, since even normal winter precipitation is low compared to the warmer seasons. Also, the frozen ground forced runoff of what precipitation and snowmelt there was. There was little noted in the way of new effects of the drought, with the dry conditions giving a poor outlook for the Spring and Summer, including the winter wheat crop, which was also affected by poor germination conditions in the dry fall. Water restrictions continued to be few because of the low water usage, but the area was deemed vulnerable to even marginally dry weather if it developed in the spring and summer. Some effect was noted on livestock, although the majority of the livestock was in good condition despite persistent feed shortages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Long term dry soil conditions continued in March, with runoff over frozen ground leaving soil moisture conditions very dry, despite near normal precipitation. Few new effects were noted, though the dry conditions coming out of winter were expected to immediately affect the winter wheat crop, as they had affected germination in the fall. Seasonally low water usage resulted in few other new effects. The main problem, aside from winter livestock feed, was the poor outlook for the spring and summer, which were expected to be extremely sensitive to dry weather.

An upper level disturbance tracking across the plains caused low pressure to deepen as it tracked from northern Oklahoma into northeast Missouri.  Snow, occasionally moderate and even briefly heavy, fell from late on the 29th into the morning hours of the 30th.  Snow amounts in southeast Nebraska were generally 3 to 6 inches, with around 6 inches reported across much of Otoe county.  The snow was accompanied by north winds of 20 to 35 which caused considerable blowing and drifting snow and several hours of poor visibilities.  Due to poor visibilities, Interstate 80 was briefly closed west of Lincoln.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nebraska City reported around 7 inches of snow from this storm, with amounts of 5 to 7 inches likely over much of Otoe county.

A sluggish spring snowstorm affected the area with significant snowfall over a 36-48 hour period.  The band of heaviest snow, with amounts up to 17 inches, fell across Yuma and Kit Carson counties in Colorado, eastward into Cheyenne, Sherman and Thomas counties in northwest Kansas.  Some notable amounts in Colorado include 14 at Yuma, Joes and Kirk, 13 in Stratton with 17 in Burlington. In Kansas, 12 was reported in St Francis, 15 in Goodland, 12 in Colby and 9 in Atwood. Across southwest Nebraska, snow totals were less, with 8 in Parks, 9 in Benkelman and 8.5 near Stratton.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall across the county included 10 northwest of Haigler, 8 in Parks, 7 north of Benkelman and 9.2 in Benkelman.  Reports were received from cooperative observers, emergency management, law enforcement, spotters and CoCoRaHS.

There was little noted in the way of new effects of the drought, with the dry conditions giving a poor outlook for the Spring and Summer, including poor germination of the winter wheat crop during the dry fall. Water restrictions continued to be few during the winter because of the low water usage, but the area was becoming more vulnerable to even marginally weather if it developed in the spring and summer. Drought was generally listed as continued severe to extreme for the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Long term dry soil conditions continued, with below to much below normal precipitation during the month bringing no relief. Few new effects were noted, with the seasonally low water usage. The main problem, aside from winter livestock feed, was the poor outlook for the spring and summer, which were expected to be extremely sensitive to dry weather.

This was the the final severe weather event in a series of occurrences that were associated with a seasonably deep upper level low pressure over the northern Plains. Instability increased during the afternoon along and ahead of a stationary front across eastern Nebraska from low pressure over the Dakotas. Isolated to scattered thunderstorms developed in the late afternoon and tracked across eastern Nebraska and into western Iowa during the evening hours. Although isolated reports of damaging winds were reported, most of the events were large hail, some of which was significant.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to 1.5 inches in diameter fell across parts of Lincoln from the first supercell thunderstorm that tracked across the city this evening.  Among areas that received the large hail from this first storm included the North Bottoms neighborhood near 9th and Charleston, near Coddington and South Streets and at least one location in southwest Lincoln.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: According to the U.S. Drought Monitor, May marked the eleventh consecutive month of moderate (or worse) drought across South Central Nebraska, continuing one of the most notable droughts since the 1950s. However, thanks to above normal rainfall across roughly the northeast two-thirds of the 24-county area, many places saw improving conditions for the second consecutive month. More specifically, most locations northeast of a line from Grand Island-Hebron improved to D1 Moderate Drought over the course of the month, marking the first time since July 2012 that any portion of the area featured a classification better than D2 Severe Drought. In the drier southwestern areas primarily consisting of Gosper, Phelps, Kearney, Furnas, Harlan and Franklin counties, D3 Extreme Drought continued to prevail, with southwestern Furnas County holding onto D4 Exceptional Drought (the worst-possible category). In between these aforementioned extremes, several counties such as Valley, Greeley, Sherman, Adams, Webster and Nuckolls improved to (or remained in) D2 Severe Drought during the month. Based on reports from around 50 NWS cooperative observers, and also incorporating radar-derived estimation, the northeast-southwest split between predominantly-above normal and predominantly-below normal rainfall aligned along an axis from Gothenburg-Minden-Hebron (based on normals generally between 3.75-4.75 inches). Areas northeast of this line (including the Tri-Cities) ending up considerably wetter, and in fact roughly one-fourth of South Central Nebraska measured at least 150 percent of normal May rainfall. Fortunately, even in the drier southwestern counties, nearly all places managed to see at least 1.50 for the month. Per a combination of NWS cooperative observers and NeRAIN observers, several of the highest-known May precipitation totals included 9.83 seven miles southeast of Stromsburg, 9.20 three miles northeast of Shelby, 8.73 six miles southwest of Columbus, 8.49 four miles west-northwest of Alexandria and 8.33 at Hampton. At Grand Island, 6.77 was the 15th-wettest May on record dating to 1896. More remarkably, the 24-hour total of 3.09 that fell from the 24th-25th was more than all cumulative rain that fell during the four-month period between June-September 2012 (2.84)! In the drier side of the area, some of the lowest reported totals included 1.57 at Cambridge, 1.77 at Edison and 1.91 eight miles south of Elwood. In between these wettest and driest extremes, the majority of South Central Nebraska reported May totals somewhere between 2.50 and 6.50. Remarkably, for some areas a small fraction of May precipitation consisted of snow, as an unusually cold start to the month brought light accumulations up to around 1 primarily on the 1st, with isolated pockets up to 2 at northern sites such as Genoa and Greeley. ||Focusing on the longer-range picture of year-to-date precipitation amounts (January-May), most of the 24-county area received between 75 and 115 percent of normal. Although this near normal to slightly above normal trend certainly was not enough to end drought issues, it at least eased the drought severity since fall 2012 in many places. According to the USDA Nebraska Crop Progress and Condition Report for the week ending June 9th, state-averaged topsoil moisture continued to improve, rating 27% short to very short and 69% adequate, a marked improvement from an 80% short to very short rating as recently as late-March. Although subsoil moisture supplies lagged behind, they also exhibited considerable improvement since early-May, decreasing from 84% short to very short to only 58% short to very short. Crop-wise, the state-averaged young corn and soybean stands both rated 75% good to excellent, while wheat was not as favorable at 50% poor to very poor.
