EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The drought that began in June 2012 lingered through July 2013. Exceptional (D4) drought conditions began the month over the central sandhills then lessened as convective rains moved across the area. At the end of the month the drought impact ranged from Extreme in portions of western Nebraska down to abnormally dry in the far north and north central. Precipitation for the month showed a range from a half inch to over 10 inches, with higher amounts in Hooker and Cherry counties that were beneficial in reducing the drought impacts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The drought that began in June 2012 eased further in July. Extreme (D3) drought conditions were observed over half of the county throughout the month, but eased locally in the far north to Moderate (D1) conditions due to heavy rains. AHPS observed precipitation showed precipitation amounts ranging from 1.5 to local areas up to 15 inches. The drought continued into August.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed over the northwestern Sandhills and moved east during the late afternoon and evening hours of July 30th.  Hail to the size of golfballs and winds estimated to 70 MPH broke windows out of cars and homes near Crookston. Later that evening, a large supercell thunderstorm developed over northern Lincoln County and tracked to the southeast, impacting North Platte and the surrounding areas. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful storm system moved into the central Plains on the 3rd of October and allowed for low pressure developed over the western high Plains. Ahead of this low, unseasonably warm and humid air streamed north from the Gulf of Mexico. The combination of the warm humid air and the strong upper level storm system created an atmosphere of high instability and strong shear across parts of eastern Nebraska. As a warm front slowly lifted north into southeast Nebraska during the evening of the 3rd, isolated supercell thunderstorms developed. These storms were responsible for large hail, some significant, damaging winds, and a couple of tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several reports of large hail, some very large, were received from about 6 miles northeast of Western to in and around Wilber. Emergency management reported hail up to 2 inches in diameter from Wilber to near 6 miles west of Wilber, while a storm chaser reported hail to 2.75 inches in diameter to the northeast of Western to just north of Wilber.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The drought that began in June 2012 persisted into August 2013. Thunderstorms provided rainfall in the amount of 0.5 to 2.00 inches across the county but were not enough to ease Severe Drought (D2) and Extreme Drought (D3) conditions. The drought persisted into September.

Besides large hail and damaging winds a few of the storms produced heavy rain which caused localized flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts which were measured at 63 mph at the Albion Awos caused scattered damage in town which included trees and power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Early this Monday evening, scattered thunderstorms developed just north of the Kansas border, from Hitchcock County east into Furnas County. The only severe thunderstorm over South Central Nebraska was the first to storm to develop. It formed over eastern Red Willow County around 7:45 p.m. CDT and crossed into northern Furnas county by 8:30 p.m. CDT. The storm intensified around 9 p.m. CDT, and produced winds strong enough to damage trees across the Arapahoe Municipal Golf Course. Hail up to the size of nickels also occurred in the town of Edison.||All these storms developed within an area of convergence along a stationary front that extended from west to east across Nebraska. Temperatures were in the 80s with dewpoints in the 60s, resulting in MUCAPE around 1500 J/kg. Deep layer shear was near 40 knots. Aloft, a subtropical high was over the Desert Southwest, with zonal flow over the northern U.S., along with embedded shortwave troughs. One such trough was just west of the region over the High Plains.

Drought conditions remained essentially unchanged in July, despite a few rounds of precipitation.  Dundy, Hitchcock and Red Willow counties continue to be rated in D3 (Extreme) to D4 (Exceptional) Drought.

Warm and humid air returned to eastern Nebraska on October 2nd ahead of a cold front that was moving through eastern South Dakota to central Nebraska. As instability increased during the afternoon along and ahead of the cold front thunderstorms developed. Widespread severe weather was not observed, but isolated large hail occurred in northeast Nebraska. The thunderstorms formed into a line during the evening and slowly moved southeast producing heavy rain and isolated flash flooding. The line of thunderstorms also produced at least one report of damaging wind in southeast Nebraska overnight.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust to 61 mph was recorded at the Beatrice Airport. Local law enforcement indicated only a few small branches to around 1 inch in diameter were downed in town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms which developed over western Nebraska during the evening of June 14th intensified as they moved into northeast Nebraska after 6 am on the 15th. The thunderstorms initially produced large hail, but after continuing east, damaging wind become predominate before the storms exited into southeast South Dakota and northwest Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts estimated at least 60 mph caused extensive damage in south Yankton. Numerous tree limbs and power lines were downed by the storm. There was also structural damage to Riverside Auto Body. Here the roof was removed, and a number of storage units were destroyed. Damage was scattered for a half block downstream from the site.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed into southeast Nebraska during the early morning hours of June 25th.  The storms developed north of a weak boundary near the Kansas border aided by modestly high instability and a low level jet.  A few of the storms dropped large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail, in some cases a little larger than golf ball-size even measured a few hours after the storm, caused scattered crop damage near Friend.  There were still some small drifts of hail left in the fields a few hours after the storm as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of thunderstorms developed overnight across northeast Nebraska along the nose of a strong low level jet. These storms continued to move southeast across southeast Nebraska. Heavy rain, damaging winds nearing 70 miles per hour and hail were reported across southeast Nebraska. Several trees were uprooted and power lines were down near Adams in Gage county and in Saline county center pivots were damaged and trees downed east of Dorchester.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A brief, EF-0 tornado formed and tracked east-southeast for a little less than 2 miles, near the far north-central border of Dawson County on this Tuesday evening. This tornado occurred over a sparsely-populated area and no damage was reported. The parent storm rapidly developed along the Custer-Dawson County line, north of Cozad, just after 5 p.m. CDT. Golf ball size hail also occurred with this storm. By 6 p.m. CDT, this storm weakened as it moved southeast, but two new storms developed in its wake and headed northeast into Sherman and Valley counties by 8 p.m. CDT. The western-most storm was a prolific hail-producer, with multiple reports of hail as large as baseballs over southeastern Custer County. This storm was the left-mover of a supercell that split along the Custer-Dawson County line just before 6:30 p.m. CDT. Shortly before dissipating over southern Valley County, the storm produced severe hail near Arcadia. The largest hail was the size of golf balls. The right-mover dissipated over eastern Dawson County. Other storms formed over Frontier County and advanced southeastward into Gosper and Furnas counties. Several people observed severe hail in the town of Wilsonville, in southwestern Furnas county. The largest hail reported was the size of golf balls.||The mesoscale setup for this event was unique. An old front was sinking southward and dissipating. By late afternoon, its remnant trough was over northern Kansas. A lee-side low had moved along it, and was located over north central Kansas.  Resultant low-level winds were from the northeast over South Central Nebraska, even as a new cold front was sagging southward from northern Nebraska. Temperatures were near 90 degrees with dewpoints near 70 degrees. This resulted in an extremely unstable atmosphere, with MLCAPE between 2000 and 3000 J/kg. Deep layer shear was around 40 knots.

According to the U.S. Drought Monitor, July 2013 marked the 14th consecutive month of D1 Moderate (or worse) drought within South Central Nebraska, continuing one of the most notable droughts since the 1950s. Unfortunately, the majority of the 24-county area endured its second consecutive month of below normal rainfall, with several southwestern counties such as Harlan and Phelps recording their third-consecutive predominantly dry month. On a positive note, the below normal July rainfall in most areas was accompanied by monthly temperatures averaging slightly cooler than normal...in sharp contrast to the notably hot and dry July 2012 one year ago that put considerable stress on crops. Despite the drier-than-normal trend over the past few months, there was essentially no change whatsoever in official drought classification according to the U.S. Drought Monitor over the course of July. As a result, most counties along and east of the Highway 281 corridor remained in D1 Moderate Drought, although pockets of D2 Severe Drought prevailed across much of Greeley, Howard, Webster and Nuckolls counties. The situation remained worse to the west of Highway 281, where nearly all areas were assigned either D2 Severe or D3 Extreme Drought, with the main concentration of D3 within Gosper, Phelps, Furnas, Harlan and Franklin counties. In addition, southwestern Furnas County remained in D4 Exceptional Drought (the worst-possible category). According to reports from around 50 NWS cooperative observers, and also incorporating radar-derived estimation, over 90% of the 30-county area tallied below normal July rainfall (based on normals generally between 3.30-4.10 inches). More significantly, at least 40% of the area received less than half of normal July rainfall. The greatest concentration of totals under 2 focused along a roughly 30-mile wide swath through the heart of the area, generally extending from around Elwood and Holdrege in the west, eastward through the Grand Island/Hastings areas, and then northeast toward Aurora and Osceola. The very limited areas of slightly above normal rainfall were most-concentrated in Valley, Sherman and Nuckolls counties. Per NWS cooperative observers, several of the lowest July precipitation totals featured 0.63 six miles south-southeast of Lexington (Canaday Steam Plant), 0.91 eight miles south of Elwood, 1.21 four miles north of Aurora, 1.25 six miles north-northwest of Oxford and 1.28 at Minden. Grand Island and Hastings tied for their 25th and 29th-driest months of July on record, respectively, out of 118 years. Within the limited above normal areas were sites such as Nelson (5.28) and Ord airport (3.59). In between these wettest and driest extremes, the majority of South Central Nebraska measured July rainfall between 1.50-3.00, including stations in or near York, Hastings, Grand Island, Gothenburg, Franklin, Kearney, Hebron and Fullerton.||Focusing on the longer-range picture of year-to-date precipitation (January 1-July 24), most of the 24-county area stood somewhere between 70-100% of normal. In general, the greatest concentration of 90% of normal or higher focused north of Interstate 80, including 122% of normal at Genoa 2W. In contrast, the greatest coverage of locations with 70% of normal or less focused southwest of a line from Elwood-Red Cloud, including just 59% of normal at Red Cloud. According to the USDA Nebraska Crop Progress and Condition Report for the week ending July 21st, state-averaged topsoil moisture supplies were again on the decline, rating 70% short to very short and only 30% adequate...a marked change from only 27% short to very short in early June. Statewide subsoil moisture also declined, rating 75% short to very short, up from 58% six weeks prior. Crop-wise, irrigated corn across the state rated 82% good to excellent, while dryland corn only rated 45% good to excellent with the poorest conditions focused in the local South Central counties. Soybeans across the state rated 63% good to excellent, while wheat was not as favorable at 50% poor to very poor and only 17% good to excellent. Stock water supplies across Nebraska averaged 25% short to very short, an 11% degradation since early June. Pasture and range conditions exhibited fairly little change over that time span, checking in 39% poor to very poor.

Within the limited above normal areas were sites such as Nelson (5.28) and Ord airport (3.59). In between these wettest and driest extremes, the majority of South Central Nebraska measured July rainfall between 1.50-3.00, including stations in or near York, Hastings, Grand Island, Gothenburg, Franklin, Kearney, Hebron and Fullerton.||Focusing on the longer-range picture of year-to-date precipitation (January 1-July 24), most of the 24-county area stood somewhere between 70-100% of normal. In general, the greatest concentration of 90% of normal or higher focused north of Interstate 80, including 122% of normal at Genoa 2W. In contrast, the greatest coverage of locations with 70% of normal or less focused southwest of a line from Elwood-Red Cloud, including just 59% of normal at Red Cloud. According to the USDA Nebraska Crop Progress and Condition Report for the week ending July 21st, state-averaged topsoil moisture supplies were again on the decline, rating 70% short to very short and only 30% adequate...a marked change from only 27% short to very short in early June. Statewide subsoil moisture also declined, rating 75% short to very short, up from 58% six weeks prior. Crop-wise, irrigated corn across the state rated 82% good to excellent, while dryland corn only rated 45% good to excellent with the poorest conditions focused in the local South Central counties. Soybeans across the state rated 63% good to excellent, while wheat was not as favorable at 50% poor to very poor and only 17% good to excellent. Stock water supplies across Nebraska averaged 25% short to very short, an 11% degradation since early June. Pasture and range conditions exhibited fairly little change over that time span, checking in 39% poor to very poor.

According to the U.S. Drought Monitor, July 2013 marked the 14th consecutive month of D1 Moderate (or worse) drought within South Central Nebraska, continuing one of the most notable droughts since the 1950s. Unfortunately, the majority of the 24-county area endured its second consecutive month of below normal rainfall, with several southwestern counties such as Harlan and Phelps recording their third-consecutive predominantly dry month. On a positive note, the below normal July rainfall in most areas was accompanied by monthly temperatures averaging slightly cooler than normal...in sharp contrast to the notably hot and dry July 2012 one year ago that put considerable stress on crops. Despite the drier-than-normal trend over the past few months, there was essentially no change whatsoever in official drought classification according to the U.S. Drought Monitor over the course of July. As a result, most counties along and east of the Highway 281 corridor remained in D1 Moderate Drought, although pockets of D2 Severe Drought prevailed across much of Greeley, Howard, Webster and Nuckolls counties. The situation remained worse to the west of Highway 281, where nearly all areas were assigned either D2 Severe or D3 Extreme Drought, with the main concentration of D3 within Gosper, Phelps, Furnas, Harlan and Franklin counties. In addition, southwestern Furnas County remained in D4 Exceptional Drought (the worst-possible category). According to reports from around 50 NWS cooperative observers, and also incorporating radar-derived estimation, over 90% of the 30-county area tallied below normal July rainfall (based on normals generally between 3.30-4.10 inches). More significantly, at least 40% of the area received less than half of normal July rainfall. The greatest concentration of totals under 2 focused along a roughly 30-mile wide swath through the heart of the area, generally extending from around Elwood and Holdrege in the west, eastward through the Grand Island/Hastings areas, and then northeast toward Aurora and Osceola. The very limited areas of slightly above normal rainfall were most-concentrated in Valley, Sherman and Nuckolls counties. Thunderstorms developed along a frontal boundary in southern Custer and Frontier counties on the evening of July 9th.  Two landspout tornadoes were reported prior to thunderstorms quickly became severe and producing hail up to the size of baseballs.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ping pong sized hail reported at Mile Marker 291 on Highway 2.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed along a frontal boundary in southern Custer and Frontier counties on the evening of July 9th.  Two landspout tornadoes were reported prior to thunderstorms quickly became severe and producing hail up to the size of baseballs.

The drought that began in June 2012 lingered through July 2013. Exceptional (D4) drought conditions began the month over the central sandhills then lessened as convective rains moved across the area. At the end of the month the drought impact ranged from Extreme in portions of western Nebraska down to abnormally dry in the far north and north central. Precipitation for the month showed a range from a half inch to over 10 inches, with higher amounts in Hooker and Cherry counties that were beneficial in reducing the drought impacts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The drought that began in June 2012 continued in July. Severe (D2) drought conditions were observed throughout most of the county with locally Moderate (D1) drought conditions in the southwest. AHPS observed precipitation showed precipitation amounts ranging from 0.5 inches to locally 4 inches. The drought continued into August.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of intense thunderstorms developed in the Nebraska panhandle during the evening and rolled southeast near Ogallala and Imperial before entering Hitchcock county. Golf ball size hail damaged crops and siding on the north side of a house, and strong storm outflow downed live tree limbs.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed live tree branches five inches in diameter. Time of report is estimated from Doppler radar.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail estimated to be quarter to golf ball size damaged crops and the siding on the north side of a house.  Approximately 2.5 of rain also fell with the storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed over the northwestern Sandhills and moved east during the late afternoon and evening hours of July 30th.  Hail to the size of golfballs and winds estimated to 70 MPH broke windows out of cars and homes near Crookston. Later that evening, a large supercell thunderstorm developed over northern Lincoln County and tracked to the southeast, impacting North Platte and the surrounding areas.  A storm survey north of North Platte estimated the highest thunderstorm wind gusts at 97 MPH.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported 60 MPH wind gusts along with quarter sized hail.

AHPS observed precipitation showed highest precipitation amounts over the northwest with rainfall amounts from 1 to 6 inches for the month. The drought continued into August.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The same powerful storm system that produced severe weather across eastern Nebraska on October 3rd remain in the area on the 4th. A strong area of low pressure moved from eastern Colorado into southern South Dakota during the day. As this occurred, a dry line was sweeping across central and into eastern Nebraska. A warm front continued to lift north into northeast Nebraska and western Iowa by late afternoon. With unseasonably warm and moist air in the region the atmosphere became strongly unstable. This instability coupled with a strong wind field associated with the powerful storm system and created and environment favorable for significant severe weather. Supercells developed near the intersection of the dry line and warm front over northeast Nebraska during the afternoon and tracked northeast. The supercells were responsible for large hail, damaging winds, and significant tornadoes. Other isolated thunderstorms developed south along the dry line into western Iowa producing very large hail and damaging winds during the evening of the 4th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tornado damage started in Thurston County approximately 3 miles southwest of Macy in rural areas. The tornado then tracked northeast through Macy. The tornado weakened in intensity as it tracked northeast from the edge of Macy across highway 75 and moved toward the Missouri River. The damage in Macy varied in width from 100 to 250 yards wide. There were a total of 6 homes that were considered to have recieved heavy damage or destroyed and can not be occupied. There were a total of 12 homes and 1 business that received at least minor damage. Numerous vehicles were damaged and there were two minor injuries. Outside of Macy, the majority of the damage was to trees but some farmsteads received minor damage to buildings.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Late afternoon and early evening thunderstorms in the northern Nebraska Panhandle produced hail, gusty winds and locally heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter to half dollar size hail was reported south of Harrison.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of thunderstorms developed overnight across northeast Nebraska along the nose of a strong low level jet. These storms continued to move southeast across southeast Nebraska. Heavy rain, damaging winds nearing 70 miles per hour and hail were reported across southeast Nebraska. Several trees were uprooted and power lines were down near Adams in Gage county and in Saline county center pivots were damaged and trees downed east of Dorchester.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees and power lines were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The drought that began in June 2012 persisted through August 2013. Exceptional (D4) and Extreme (D3) conditions covered nearly 30 percent of the state. The Exceptional Drought (D4) conditions had improved to Extreme drought (D3) conditions in the Central Sandhills. However, most of the Extreme Drought (D3) conditions existed in western Nebraska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The drought that began in June 2012 persisted into August 2013. Thunderstorms provided rainfall in the amount of 3.25 to 6.50 inches across the county, which was enough to ease drought conditions to Moderate Drought (D1) across the far northern portion of the county. Severe Drought (D2) conditions persisted across the remainder of the county through the end of the month. The drought persisted into September.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The drought that began in June 2012 persisted through August 2013. Exceptional (D4) and Extreme (D3) conditions covered nearly 30 percent of the state. The Exceptional Drought (D4) conditions had improved to Extreme drought (D3) conditions in the Central Sandhills. However, most of the Extreme Drought (D3) conditions existed in western Nebraska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The drought that began in June 2012 persisted into August 2013. Thunderstorms provided rainfall in the amount of 1.00 to 2.00 inches across the county, which was enough to ease Severe Drought (D2) and Extreme Drought (D3) to Severe Drought (D2) conditions across the county. The drought persisted into September.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The drought that began in June 2012 persisted through August 2013. Exceptional (D4) and Extreme (D3) conditions covered nearly 30 percent of the state. The Exceptional Drought (D4) conditions had improved to Extreme drought (D3) conditions in the Central Sandhills. However, most of the Extreme Drought (D3) conditions existed in western Nebraska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The drought that began in June 2012 persisted into August 2013. Thunderstorms provided rainfall in the amount of 2.50 to 4.25 inches across the county, which was enough to ease previous Exceptional Drought (D4) conditions to Severe Drought (D2) and Extreme Drought (D3) conditions across the county. The drought persisted into September.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hundreds of heat-related cattle deaths occurred durring the afternoon hours on July 9th over portions of central Nebraska. This included areas of Dawson and Polk counties, and possibly other neighboring counties. Ranchers and feedlot operators typically do not reveal specifics and some do not report their losses at all, so exact numbers were not available. A spokesman for the Nebraska Cattlemen told the Lincoln Journal Star that the number of deaths across Nebraska was near what occurred in 2009, when casualties were near 4,000 head. Other reports confirmed that this was the worst loss of cattle since 2009. Each head was worth about $1,500, resulting in losses in the millions of dollars. Feedlot cattle are especially prone to heat-related death, due to large, unshaded pens.||Temperatures were in the lower 90s, with dewpoints in the lower 70s. This resulted in heat index values in the lower 100s. The stagnant air mass contributed to the misery of the cattle. Winds were low, averaging around 5 mph. The pressure gradient was weak, with a stationary front dissipating over Nebraska, ahead of an approaching cool front from the Northern Plains. Subtropical high pressure was over New Mexico and Texas, with a fairly potent short-wave trough moving through its ridge position, over the Northern Plains.

Crop-wise, irrigated corn across the state rated 82% good to excellent, while dryland corn only rated 45% good to excellent with the poorest conditions focused in the local South Central counties. Soybeans across the state rated 63% good to excellent, while wheat was not as favorable at 50% poor to very poor and only 17% good to excellent. Stock water supplies across Nebraska averaged 25% short to very short, an 11% degradation since early June. Pasture and range conditions exhibited fairly little change over that time span, checking in 39% poor to very poor.

According to the U.S. Drought Monitor, July 2013 marked the 14th consecutive month of D1 Moderate (or worse) drought within South Central Nebraska, continuing one of the most notable droughts since the 1950s. Unfortunately, the majority of the 24-county area endured its second consecutive month of below normal rainfall, with several southwestern counties such as Harlan and Phelps recording their third-consecutive predominantly dry month. On a positive note, the below normal July rainfall in most areas was accompanied by monthly temperatures averaging slightly cooler than normal...in sharp contrast to the notably hot and dry July 2012 one year ago that put considerable stress on crops. Despite the drier-than-normal trend over the past few months, there was essentially no change whatsoever in official drought classification according to the U.S. Drought Monitor over the course of July. As a result, most counties along and east of the Highway 281 corridor remained in D1 Moderate Drought, although pockets of D2 Severe Drought prevailed across much of Greeley, Howard, Webster and Nuckolls counties. The situation remained worse to the west of Highway 281, where nearly all areas were assigned either D2 Severe or D3 Extreme Drought, with the main concentration of D3 within Gosper, Phelps, Furnas, Harlan and Franklin counties. In addition, southwestern Furnas County remained in D4 Exceptional Drought (the worst-possible category). According to reports from around 50 NWS cooperative observers, and also incorporating radar-derived estimation, over 90% of the 30-county area tallied below normal July rainfall (based on normals generally between 3.30-4.10 inches). More significantly, at least 40% of the area received less than half of normal July rainfall. The greatest concentration of totals under 2 focused along a roughly 30-mile wide swath through the heart of the area, generally extending from around Elwood and Holdrege in the west, eastward through the Grand Island/Hastings areas, and then northeast toward Aurora and Osceola. The very limited areas of slightly above normal rainfall were most-concentrated in Valley, Sherman and Nuckolls counties. Per NWS cooperative observers, several of the lowest July precipitation totals featured 0.63 six miles south-southeast of Lexington (Canaday Steam Plant), 0.91 eight miles south of Elwood, 1.21 four miles north of Aurora, 1.25 six miles north-northwest of Oxford and 1.28 at Minden. Grand Island and Hastings tied for their 25th and 29th-driest months of July on record, respectively, out of 118 years. Within the limited above normal areas were sites such as Nelson (5.28) and Ord airport (3.59). In between these wettest and driest extremes, the majority of South Central Nebraska measured July rainfall between 1.50-3.00, including stations in or near York, Hastings, Grand Island, Gothenburg, Franklin, Kearney, Hebron and Fullerton.||Focusing on the longer-range picture of year-to-date precipitation (January 1-July 24), most of the 24-county area stood somewhere between 70-100% of normal. In general, the greatest concentration of 90% of normal or higher focused north of Interstate 80, including 122% of normal at Genoa 2W. In contrast, the greatest coverage of locations with 70% of normal or less focused southwest of a line from Elwood-Red Cloud, including just 59% of normal at Red Cloud. The drought that began in June 2012 lingered through July 2013. Exceptional (D4) drought conditions began the month over the central sandhills then lessened as convective rains moved across the area. At the end of the month the drought impact ranged from Extreme in portions of western Nebraska down to abnormally dry in the far north and north central. Precipitation for the month showed a range from a half inch to over 10 inches, with higher amounts in Hooker and Cherry counties that were beneficial in reducing the drought impacts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The drought that began in June 2012 continued in July. Extreme (D3) drought conditions were observed throughout. AHPS observed precipitation showed precipitation amounts from 2 to 4 inches. The drought continued into August.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The drought that began in June 2012 eased further in July. The Extreme (D3) drought conditions eased to Severe (D2) conditions across the county. AHPS observed precipitation showed precipitation amounts ranged from 0.50 inch in the east to locally 6 inch amounts in the west. The drought continued into August.

AHPS observed precipitation showed precipitation amounts ranged from 0.5 inch in the west central to local 6 inch amounts in the central. The drought continued into August.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An overnight mesoscale convective system tracked southeastward across South Central Nebraska during the predawn hours of this Tuesday. While this MCS provided beneficial rainfall, no severe weather occurred. However, near-severe wind gusts were reported from Hastings down to Glenvil. Nickel-size hail also occurred in Red Cloud. Between 5 and 6 a.m. CDT, while overall MCS strength was diminishing, its southern end did temporarily strengthen across Adams, Clay, Webster and Nuckolls counties. ||This MCS had its origin over northeastern Wyoming late Monday afternoon. The initial storms formed near weak low pressure that was located along a polar front which extended southeastward into southern Nebraska, and then northeastward to moderately strong low pressure over Ontario. The thunderstorm cluster tracked southeastward along the instability and low-level moisture gradient. MUCAPE values were between 500 and 1000 J/kg. The upper-level flow was zonal, atop a subtropical high over Texas and New Mexico with a trough over the eastern United States. A subtle short-wave trough was just upstream of the MCS throughout its lifetime. Deep layer shear was between 40 and 50 knots.

A line of thunderstorms developed across the northeastern Nebraska Panhandle and portions of Cherry County during the evening of July 20th. The line moved south to produce damaging winds, large hail and a brief tornado.

Severe thunderstorms developed over the northwestern Sandhills and moved east during the late afternoon and evening hours of July 30th.  Hail to the size of golfballs and winds estimated to 70 MPH broke windows out of cars and homes near Crookston. Later that evening, a large supercell thunderstorm developed over northern Lincoln County and tracked to the southeast, impacting North Platte and the surrounding areas.  A storm survey north of North Platte estimated the highest thunderstorm wind gusts at 97 MPH.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts estimated at 60 MPH damaged trees south of Valentine.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The drought that began in June 2012 continued with generally Extreme (D3) drought conditions in the central and north. AHPS precipitation observed showed higher rainfall across the southern portion of the county that was in Severe (D2) drought conditions at the end of July. The drought continued into August.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The drought that began in June 2012 persisted through August 2013. Exceptional (D4) and Extreme (D3) conditions covered nearly 30 percent of the state. The Exceptional Drought (D4) conditions had improved to Extreme drought (D3) conditions in the Central Sandhills. However, most of the Extreme Drought (D3) conditions existed in western Nebraska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The drought that began in June 2012 persisted into August 2013. Thunderstorms provided rainfall in the amount of 1.75 to 4.25 inches across the county, which was enough to ease Moderate Drought (D1) and Severe Drought (D2) to mainly Moderate Drought (D1) and Abnormally Dry (D0), with only a small portion of Severe Drought (D2) remaining in the south portion of the county. The drought persisted into September.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms developed across the northeastern Nebraska Panhandle and portions of Cherry County during the evening of July 20th. The line moved south to produce damaging winds, large hail and a brief tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts estimated at 60 MPH caused minor damage to trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms developed across the northeastern Nebraska Panhandle and portions of Cherry County during the evening of July 20th. The line moved south to produce damaging winds, large hail and a brief tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts estimated at 70 MPH uprooted a large tree.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms developed across the northeastern Nebraska Panhandle and portions of Cherry County during the evening of July 20th. The line moved south to produce damaging winds, large hail and a brief tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter estimated thunderstorm wind gusts of 55 to 60 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms developed across the northeastern Nebraska Panhandle and portions of Cherry County during the evening of July 20th. The line moved south to produce damaging winds, large hail and a brief tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 1626MDT, the Rushville Fire Department reported a tornado on the ground for approximately 10 minutes. The tornado was stationary over open range land with no damage reported. The path width was 60 yards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms formed between O'Neill and North Platte during the early evening hours on this Sunday. Around 8:45 p.m. CDT, a growing, slow-moving line segment of storms began drifting into northern Valley and Greeley counties. A pair of thunderstorms intensified between 9:30 and 10:15 p.m. CDT over northern Valley County, dropping mostly sub-severe hail. There was one report of hail as large as golf balls in the town of Ord. The slow movement of these storms resulted in hefty rainfall totals between 1.32 and 2.35 over north-central and northwestern Valley County.||These evening thunderstorms formed along a stationary front that extended from the Northern Rockies down to Nebraska, and then eastward across the northern United States. The upper-level flow was fairly flat, atop a subtropical high over New Mexico and Texas. Temperatures were in the upper 80s to near 90, with dewpoints around 70, resulting in MLCAPE between 2000 and 2500 J/kg. Deep layer shear was around 30 knots.

Early this Monday evening, scattered thunderstorms developed just north of the Kansas border, from Hitchcock County east into Furnas County. The only severe thunderstorm over South Central Nebraska was the first to storm to develop. It formed over eastern Red Willow County around 7:45 p.m. CDT and crossed into northern Furnas county by 8:30 p.m. CDT. The storm intensified around 9 p.m. CDT, and produced winds strong enough to damage trees across the Arapahoe Municipal Golf Course. Hail up to the size of nickels also occurred in the town of Edison.||All these storms developed within an area of convergence along a stationary front that extended from west to east across Nebraska. Temperatures were in the 80s with dewpoints in the 60s, resulting in MUCAPE around 1500 J/kg. Deep layer shear was near 40 knots. Aloft, a subtropical high was over the Desert Southwest, with zonal flow over the northern U.S., along with embedded shortwave troughs. One such trough was just west of the region over the High Plains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts estimated at 70 mph resulted in tree damage at the Arapahoe Golf Course.  At least two trees were uprooted.

Later that evening, a large supercell thunderstorm developed over northern Lincoln County and tracked to the southeast, impacting North Platte and the surrounding areas.  A storm survey north of North Platte estimated the highest thunderstorm wind gusts at 97 MPH.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts were estimated at 60 MPH north of North Platte.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful storm system moved into the central Plains on the 3rd of October and allowed for low pressure developed over the western high Plains. Ahead of this low, unseasonably warm and humid air streamed north from the Gulf of Mexico. The combination of the warm humid air and the strong upper level storm system created an atmosphere of high instability and strong shear across parts of eastern Nebraska. As a warm front slowly lifted north into southeast Nebraska during the evening of the 3rd, isolated supercell thunderstorms developed. These storms were responsible for large hail, some significant, damaging winds, and a couple of tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado initially touched down in Lancaster County where EF2 damage was produced. The tornado crossed in Otoe County around 2 miles east of Bennet. The tornado continued to around 1 mile northwest of Palmyra before lifting. The tornado produced damage consistent with EF2 as it crossed highway 20 to the west of Palmyra to farmsteads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of thunderstorms developed overnight across northeast Nebraska along the nose of a strong low level jet. These storms continued to move southeast across southeast Nebraska. Heavy rain, damaging winds nearing 70 miles per hour and hail were reported across southeast Nebraska. Several trees were uprooted and power lines were down near Adams in Gage county and in Saline county center pivots were damaged and trees downed east of Dorchester.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts estimated at around 60 mph damaged pivots and downed trees east of Dorchester.  Time was estimated from radar.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees and power lines were downed and a grain bin toppled and pushed into a surrounding field by thunderstorm wind gusts estimated at around 70 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large trees were uprooted and other tree and house damage was noted in the Adams area by thunderstorm wind gusts estimated at around 70 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: According to the U.S. Drought Monitor, July 2013 marked the 14th consecutive month of D1 Moderate (or worse) drought within South Central Nebraska, continuing one of the most notable droughts since the 1950s. Unfortunately, the majority of the 24-county area endured its second consecutive month of below normal rainfall, with several southwestern counties such as Harlan and Phelps recording their third-consecutive predominantly dry month. On a positive note, the below normal July rainfall in most areas was accompanied by monthly temperatures averaging slightly cooler than normal...in sharp contrast to the notably hot and dry July 2012 one year ago that put considerable stress on crops. Despite the drier-than-normal trend over the past few months, there was essentially no change whatsoever in official drought classification according to the U.S. Drought Monitor over the course of July. As a result, most counties along and east of the Highway 281 corridor remained in D1 Moderate Drought, although pockets of D2 Severe Drought prevailed across much of Greeley, Howard, Webster and Nuckolls counties. The situation remained worse to the west of Highway 281, where nearly all areas were assigned either D2 Severe or D3 Extreme Drought, with the main concentration of D3 within Gosper, Phelps, Furnas, Harlan and Franklin counties. In addition, southwestern Furnas County remained in D4 Exceptional Drought (the worst-possible category). According to reports from around 50 NWS cooperative observers, and also incorporating radar-derived estimation, over 90% of the 30-county area tallied below normal July rainfall (based on normals generally between 3.30-4.10 inches). More significantly, at least 40% of the area received less than half of normal July rainfall. The greatest concentration of totals under 2 focused along a roughly 30-mile wide swath through the heart of the area, generally extending from around Elwood and Holdrege in the west, eastward through the Grand Island/Hastings areas, and then northeast toward Aurora and Osceola. The very limited areas of slightly above normal rainfall were most-concentrated in Valley, Sherman and Nuckolls counties. Per NWS cooperative observers, several of the lowest July precipitation totals featured 0.63 six miles south-southeast of Lexington (Canaday Steam Plant), 0.91 eight miles south of Elwood, 1.21 four miles north of Aurora, 1.25 six miles north-northwest of Oxford and 1.28 at Minden. Grand Island and Hastings tied for their 25th and 29th-driest months of July on record, respectively, out of 118 years. Within the limited above normal areas were sites such as Nelson (5.28) and Ord airport (3.59). In between these wettest and driest extremes, the majority of South Central Nebraska measured July rainfall between 1.50-3.00, including stations in or near York, Hastings, Grand Island, Gothenburg, Franklin, Kearney, Hebron and Fullerton.||Focusing on the longer-range picture of year-to-date precipitation (January 1-July 24), most of the 24-county area stood somewhere between 70-100% of normal. In general, the greatest concentration of 90% of normal or higher focused north of Interstate 80, including 122% of normal at Genoa 2W. In contrast, the greatest coverage of locations with 70% of normal or less focused southwest of a line from Elwood-Red Cloud, including just 59% of normal at Red Cloud. According to the USDA Nebraska Crop Progress and Condition Report for the week ending July 21st, state-averaged topsoil moisture supplies were again on the decline, rating 70% short to very short and only 30% adequate...a marked change from only 27% short to very short in early June. Statewide subsoil moisture also declined, rating 75% short to very short, up from 58% six weeks prior. Crop-wise, irrigated corn across the state rated 82% good to excellent, while dryland corn only rated 45% good to excellent with the poorest conditions focused in the local South Central counties. Soybeans across the state rated 63% good to excellent, while wheat was not as favorable at 50% poor to very poor and only 17% good to excellent. Stock water supplies across Nebraska averaged 25% short to very short, an 11% degradation since early June. Pasture and range conditions exhibited fairly little change over that time span, checking in 39% poor to very poor.

The drought that began in June 2012 lingered through July 2013. Exceptional (D4) drought conditions began the month over the central sandhills then lessened as convective rains moved across the area. At the end of the month the drought impact ranged from Extreme in portions of western Nebraska down to abnormally dry in the far north and north central. Precipitation for the month showed a range from a half inch to over 10 inches, with higher amounts in Hooker and Cherry counties that were beneficial in reducing the drought impacts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The drought that began in June 2012 continued into July. Extreme (D3) Drought conditions clipped the far southwest with Severe (D2) to Moderate (D1) conditions for the rest of the county. AHPS observed precipitation showed precipitation amounts ranged from 1.5 inches in the east to locally 10 inch amounts in the west. The drought continued into August.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The drought that began in June 2012 lingered into July. The Exceptional (D4) Drought conditions in the northwest continued with Extreme (D3) Drought conditions with central and east, except for the far southeast where Severe (D2) conditions were observed.  AHPS observed precipitation showed precipitation amounts ranged from 0.5 inch in the west central to local 6 inch amounts in the central. The drought continued into August.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The drought that began in June 2012 lingered into July. Beneficial rains did occur although no change to the existing Severe (D2) Drought conditions over most of the county. AHPS observed precipitation showed precipitation amounts between 3 to 4 inches. The drought continued into August.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms developed across the northeastern Nebraska Panhandle and portions of Cherry County during the evening of July 20th. The line moved south to produce damaging winds, large hail and a brief tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several tree branches were downed by estimated 60 MPH winds as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms developed across the northeastern Nebraska Panhandle and portions of Cherry County during the evening of July 20th. The line moved south to produce damaging winds, large hail and a brief tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gust to 74 MPH, measured at the Broken Bow Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms developed across the northeastern Nebraska Panhandle and portions of Cherry County during the evening of July 20th. The line moved south to produce damaging winds, large hail and a brief tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts were estimated at 60 MPH and downed numerous 4 inch diameter tree limbs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A supercell thunderstorm developed over northwestern Sheridan County during the early evening hours of July 23rd.  The storm tracked to the south southeast across the eastern Nebraska Panhandle to produce a swath of large hail into Deuel County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Leaves stripped off of corn crops northeast of Lewellen.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A supercell thunderstorm developed over northwestern Sheridan County during the early evening hours of July 23rd.  The storm tracked to the south southeast across the eastern Nebraska Panhandle to produce a swath of large hail into Deuel County.

A supercell thunderstorm developed over northwestern Sheridan County during the early evening hours of July 23rd.  The storm tracked to the south southeast across the eastern Nebraska Panhandle to produce a swath of large hail into Deuel County.

A supercell thunderstorm developed over northwestern Sheridan County during the early evening hours of July 23rd.  The storm tracked to the south southeast across the eastern Nebraska Panhandle to produce a swath of large hail into Deuel County.

A warm front lifted north across southwest Nebraska and was the focus for thunderstorms to develop, intensify and produce large hail and damaging winds. Frontier County was hit the hardest by damaging winds estimated up to 80 mph as a thunderstorm bowed out. The high winds produced damage at 12 farmsteads. The most extensive damage occurred just north of County Road 741, where two large buildings and nine silos were destroyed. The wind damage included over 60 center pivot irrigation systems, livestock killed, large tree limbs down and approximately 40 power poles snapped. The McCook Public Power District reported 173 consumers lost power during the storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large storage building destroyed. Building was 60 by 80 feet. Siding of home and combine damaged by wind driven corn stalk remnants.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The drought that began in June 2012 persisted through August 2013. Exceptional (D4) and Extreme (D3) conditions covered nearly 30 percent of the state. The Exceptional Drought (D4) conditions had improved to Extreme drought (D3) conditions in the Central Sandhills. However, most of the Extreme Drought (D3) conditions existed in western Nebraska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The drought that began in June 2012 persisted into August 2013. Thunderstorms provided rainfall in the amount of 1.75 to 3.50 inches across the county, which was enough to ease mainly Extreme Drought (D3) to a mix of Extreme Drought (D3) and Severe Drought across of the county. The drought persisted into September.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The drought that began in June 2012 persisted through August 2013. Exceptional (D4) and Extreme (D3) conditions covered nearly 30 percent of the state. The Exceptional Drought (D4) conditions had improved to Extreme drought (D3) conditions in the Central Sandhills. However, most of the Extreme Drought (D3) conditions existed in western Nebraska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The drought that began in June 2012 persisted into August 2013. Thunderstorms provided rainfall in the amount of 1.50 to 2.50 inches across the county, which was enough to ease mainly Exceptional Drought (D4) and Extreme Drought (D3) to Extreme Drought (D3) conditions across of the county. The drought persisted into September.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The drought that began in June 2012 persisted through August 2013. Exceptional (D4) and Extreme (D3) conditions covered nearly 30 percent of the state. The Exceptional Drought (D4) conditions had improved to Extreme drought (D3) conditions in the Central Sandhills. However, most of the Extreme Drought (D3) conditions existed in western Nebraska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The drought that began in June 2012 persisted into August 2013. Thunderstorms provided rainfall in the amount of 3.25 to 4.25 inches across the county but were not enough to ease Severe Drought (D2) conditions. The drought persisted into September.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The drought that began in June 2012 persisted through August 2013. Exceptional (D4) and Extreme (D3) conditions covered nearly 30 percent of the state. The Exceptional Drought (D4) conditions had improved to Extreme drought (D3) conditions in the Central Sandhills. However, most of the Extreme Drought (D3) conditions existed in western Nebraska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The drought that began in June 2012 persisted into August 2013. Thunderstorms provided rainfall in the amount of 1.50 to 3.00 inches across the county and was enough to improve Exceptional Drought (D4) conditions to Extreme Drought (D3) conditions. The drought persisted into September.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A brief, EF-0 tornado formed and tracked east-southeast for a little less than 2 miles, near the far north-central border of Dawson County on this Tuesday evening. This tornado occurred over a sparsely-populated area and no damage was reported. The parent storm rapidly developed along the Custer-Dawson County line, north of Cozad, just after 5 p.m. CDT. Golf ball size hail also occurred with this storm. By 6 p.m. CDT, this storm weakened as it moved southeast, but two new storms developed in its wake and headed northeast into Sherman and Valley counties by 8 p.m. CDT. The western-most storm was a prolific hail-producer, with multiple reports of hail as large as baseballs over southeastern Custer County. This storm was the left-mover of a supercell that split along the Custer-Dawson County line just before 6:30 p.m. CDT. Shortly before dissipating over southern Valley County, the storm produced severe hail near Arcadia. The largest hail was the size of golf balls. The right-mover dissipated over eastern Dawson County. Other storms formed over Frontier County and advanced southeastward into Gosper and Furnas counties. Several people observed severe hail in the town of Wilsonville, in southwestern Furnas county. The largest hail reported was the size of golf balls.||The mesoscale setup for this event was unique. An old front was sinking southward and dissipating. By late afternoon, its remnant trough was over northern Kansas. A lee-side low had moved along it, and was located over north central Kansas.  Resultant low-level winds were from the northeast over South Central Nebraska, even as a new cold front was sagging southward from northern Nebraska. Temperatures were near 90 degrees with dewpoints near 70 degrees. This resulted in an extremely unstable atmosphere, with MLCAPE between 2000 and 3000 J/kg. Deep layer shear was around 40 knots.

A supercell produced extensive severe weather over Valley and Sherman counties this Wednesday evening. Around 2 p.m. CDT, a lone thunderstorm formed near the South Dakota-Nebraska border and eventually became supercellular. As it moved southeastward toward central Nebraska, other isolated supercells joined. Storm evolution was complex this afternoon, with multiple storm splits and cell mergers. At 4 p.m. CDT, one of the new cells formed in close proximity to the initial storm, along the Holt-Rock county border. This storm took over, increased in size and became the dominant storm. Given its proximity to the initial storm, it is likely that some constructive interference occurred between the two. While all other storms tracked southeast or south-southeastward, this storm tracked nearly due south, entering northern Valley County by 6 p.m. CDT. This storm had a history of producing severe wind and hail, so it is likely that continued as the storm tracked across Valley county. However, due to the sparse population, no severe reports were received until the storm hit the town of Ord. Hail as large as golf balls fell in town and the airport ASOS measured winds of 60 mph. Isolated, but intense, wind damage occurred between Ord and the Valley-Sherman county border. A NWS storm survey estimated winds between 70 and 100 mph. The roof of a home approximately 4 miles east-southeast of Ord was damaged. Roughly 120 square feet of roofing material was removed and blown into the yard, including 60 square feet of roof sheathing. The portion removed was at a gable end where the wind lifted up the outlookers/soffit, taking some of the roof with it. Damage also occurred to crops, a barn, a large storage shed and a center pivot irrigation system. Multiple grain bins were also blown over and/or destroyed. The storm continued south, hugging the Sherman-Howard county line, and still producing hail as large as 1.5 inches in diameter. It then rapidly dissipated over northwestern Hall County.||The synoptic setup was very innocuous, with weak forcing. The surface was dominated by high pressure that extended from the Central Plains into the Great Lakes. The storms initiated along a weak surface trough which was situated in the warm sector a low pressure system near Lake Winnipeg. Temperatures were in the low 80s, with dewpoints in the low 60s. Despite unimpressive mid-level lapse rates, MLCAPE was around 2000 J/kg. Deep layer shear was near 50 knots, and storm-relative helicity was between 100 and 200 m2/s2. Both of these shear values were supportive of supercell storm mode. The upper-level flow was west-northwest, with a ridge over the western U.S. and a trough over the east.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A supercell thunderstorm developed over northwestern Sheridan County during the early evening hours of July 23rd.  The storm tracked to the south southeast across the eastern Nebraska Panhandle to produce a swath of large hail into Deuel County.

The drought that began in June 2012 lingered through July 2013. Exceptional (D4) drought conditions began the month over the central sandhills then lessened as convective rains moved across the area. At the end of the month the drought impact ranged from Extreme in portions of western Nebraska down to abnormally dry in the far north and north central. Precipitation for the month showed a range from a half inch to over 10 inches, with higher amounts in Hooker and Cherry counties that were beneficial in reducing the drought impacts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The drought that began in June 2012 lingered into July. Beneficial rains did occur although not sufficient to decrease the impacts of the Exceptional (D4) Drought conditions observed. AHPS observed precipitation showed precipitation amounts generally around a half inch with local amounts to 4 inches in the northwest. The drought continued into August.

Severe thunderstorms developed over the northwestern Sandhills and moved east during the late afternoon and evening hours of July 30th.  Hail to the size of golfballs and winds estimated to 70 MPH broke windows out of cars and homes near Crookston. Later that evening, a large supercell thunderstorm developed over northern Lincoln County and tracked to the southeast, impacting North Platte and the surrounding areas.  A storm survey north of North Platte estimated the highest thunderstorm wind gusts at 97 MPH.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Half dollar sized hail reported with 50 to 60 MPH winds.

A cluster of weak thunderstorms developed over northeastern Colorado during the previous evening. It tracked eastward across the southern third of Nebraska, arriving in the early morning hours of Tuesday. The thunderstorms were high-based (cloud bases around 11,000 feet) atop a very hot, dry air mass. This created a favorable environment for heat bursts due to substantial sub-cloud evaporation and accelerating, mostly dry downdrafts. Several wind gusts were measured between 54 and 63 mph, across eastern Buffalo, central Hall and along the southern border of Hamilton and York counties. These winds resulted in the collapse of a new restaurant under construction along Highway 281 in Grand Island. The exterior 2 by 6 inch wood walls were framed, standing and temporarily braced, but the bracing was not enough to avoid succumbing to the winds. The Grand Island Independent also reported tree damage and power outages in the area. ||Temperatures during these heat bursts increased well into the 90s and humidity levels dropped into the teens. The hottest surge in temperature occurred at a mesonet station 2 miles south of Henderson in extreme southwestern York County. Its temperature peaked at 102 degrees, with a minimum dewpoint of 46 degrees. This resulted in a relative humidity of 15%.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A building under construction was blown down due to winds from a heat burst. Time was estimated based on local wind observations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Primarily between 3 and 8 pm CDT on this Friday afternoon and evening, strong to severe thunderstorms blossomed along a fairly narrow, southwest to northeast corridor across the heart of South Central Nebraska, resulting in several hail and wind reports. The vast majority of this activity focused near a weakening cold front within 10 to 20 miles either side of a line extending from near Beaver City-Minden-Grand Island-Silver Creek. A few of the most notable severe weather reports included quarter size hail covering the ground in Edison, ping pong ball size hail in Silver Creek and a measured 60 MPH wind gust at the Aurora airport. ||The first, high-based storms of the afternoon flared up in southwestern areas generally between Beaver City and Minden between 3 and 5 pm CDT. These storms were closely tied to the invading cold front, and developed in a deeply mixed environment featuring surface temperatures in the mid-upper 90s F and dewpoints in the mid-50s to around 60. Then between 5-7 pm CDT, storms rapidly expanded into the Grand Island/Aurora vicinity and points north and east, where surface dewpoints averaged a bit higher near a north-south oriented warm front. By 8 pm CDT, the primary round of severe storms had exited east and northeast of the local area. However, even as storms started weakening and moving out of the area between 7-8 pm CDT, strong wind gusts of 54-58 MPH were measured in Adams and Hall counties, likely a result of heat burst activity. During the later evening and overnight hours, additional thunderstorms lingered or moved back into parts of the area, but were non-severe. In addition to convergence along the aforementioned cold front, large-scale forcing for this event was aided by weak shortwave disturbances rippling through modest southwesterly flow, displaced well southeast of a much stronger shortwave trough translating across eastern Montana. Late afternoon mesoscale severe weather parameters across South Central Nebraska featured mixed layer CAPE generally ranging from 1000-2500 J/kg and 0-6 kilometer deep layer wind shear on the order of 30-35 knots.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Although much of South Central Nebraska observed showers and thunderstorms on this late Sunday afternoon and evening, severe weather in the form of hail, damaging winds and locally heavy rainfall mainly concentrated within a fairly narrow northwest-to-southeast corridor across western portions of the area. More specifically, severe storms primarily targeted western Sherman County, along with locations roughly 10-15 miles either side of a line extending from near Eddyville-Elm Creek-Franklin. Some of the most noteworthy storm reports included quarter to golf ball size hail in or near Litchfield, a semi-truck blown over on Interstate 80 between Lexington and Overton by estimated 70 MPH winds, quarter to golf ball size hail in northern Phelps County, and estimated 60 MPH winds accompanied by penny size hail southeast of Upland. Although rainfall amounts across most affected areas averaged 1-2 inches at most, parts of Franklin and far western Webster counties received more widespread 2+ inch totals. This resulted in minor flooding along the Thompson Creek near Riverton through the night, with several roads still blocked well into the following day. ||Initially, severe storms flared up just west and northwest of South Central Nebraska during the mid-afternoon hours. The first severe storm to enter the local 24-county area was a fairly small, slow-moving supercell that affected mainly the southwestern quadrant of Sherman County between 430-600 pm CDT, dropping large hail in the Litchfield area. As this initial storm was ongoing, a conglomeration of strong to severe multicell storms developed over, or moved into portions of eastern Dawson, western Buffalo and Phelps counties. Mainly between 630-830 pm CDT, several of these storms morphed into a small, forward-propagating mesoscale convective system (MCS) that primarily tracked through Harlan, Franklin and Kearney counties. Meanwhile, farther northwest, a trailing storm briefly took on supercell characteristics in eastern Dawson County and was responsible for blowing the semi-truck over on I-80. By 830 pm CDT, the most intense storms had pushed southward into Kansas, leaving behind lingering, non-severe convection over much of South Central Nebraska through around 11 pm CDT. On the synoptic scale aloft, this event occurred in seasonable quasi-zonal flow containing embedded, low-amplitude shortwave troughs, with Nebraska located slightly south of the strongest upper level flow aligned from the Dakotas to the Great Lakes. At the surface, the primary feature at mid-afternoon was a quasi-stationary front extending from southwest Nebraska to south central Kansas, with South Central Nebraska positioned just northeast of this boundary in a low-level easterly flow regime. Mid-late afternoon mesoscale severe weather parameters over western portions of the local area featured mixed layer CAPE of 1500-2000 J/kg in the presence of 0-6 kilometer deep layer wind shear of 35-45 knots.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Golf ball sized hail fell one mile north of Litchfield.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the late night/early morning a line of thunderstorms moved across southwest Nebraska. Strong to severe wind gusts accompanied the thunderstorms as they moved through the area. A peak wind gust of 60 MPH was reported at McCook.  The severe wind gusts also uprooted multiple trees, causing property damage in McCook.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees split/uprooted.  One tree crushed two vehicles and damaged a garage near E 1st and D St.  Damage report relayed via Spotter Network.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stagnant upper level high pressure system brought an extended period of very hot temperatures to eastern Nebraska the last week of August.  Heat index values peaked in the 104 to 109 degree range on August 30th as temperatures approached 100 degrees to go along with dew point temperatures in the upper 60s to mid 70s.  Even though other days during the last week of August had highs well into the 90s, dew point readings were lower which generally kept heat index values at or below 100 degrees.  Lows during this stretch were mostly in the upper 60s to mid 70s, and a few warmest minimum temperatures were tied or broken to go along with a few record highs tied or broken.

A brief, EF-0 tornado formed and tracked east-southeast for a little less than 2 miles, near the far north-central border of Dawson County on this Tuesday evening. This tornado occurred over a sparsely-populated area and no damage was reported. The parent storm rapidly developed along the Custer-Dawson County line, north of Cozad, just after 5 p.m. CDT. Golf ball size hail also occurred with this storm. By 6 p.m. CDT, this storm weakened as it moved southeast, but two new storms developed in its wake and headed northeast into Sherman and Valley counties by 8 p.m. CDT. The western-most storm was a prolific hail-producer, with multiple reports of hail as large as baseballs over southeastern Custer County. This storm was the left-mover of a supercell that split along the Custer-Dawson County line just before 6:30 p.m. CDT. Shortly before dissipating over southern Valley County, the storm produced severe hail near Arcadia. The largest hail was the size of golf balls. The right-mover dissipated over eastern Dawson County. Other storms formed over Frontier County and advanced southeastward into Gosper and Furnas counties. Several people observed severe hail in the town of Wilsonville, in southwestern Furnas county. The largest hail reported was the size of golf balls.||The mesoscale setup for this event was unique. An old front was sinking southward and dissipating. By late afternoon, its remnant trough was over northern Kansas. A lee-side low had moved along it, and was located over north central Kansas.  Resultant low-level winds were from the northeast over South Central Nebraska, even as a new cold front was sagging southward from northern Nebraska. Temperatures were near 90 degrees with dewpoints near 70 degrees. This resulted in an extremely unstable atmosphere, with MLCAPE between 2000 and 3000 J/kg. Deep layer shear was around 40 knots.

This late Friday evening kicked off a stretch of four consecutive nights of strong-severe storms within South Central Nebraska. This opening act featured damaging wind gusts of 60-65 MPH along the leading edge of a line of severe thunderstorms across far west-northwest portions of the area, with all severe weather reports confined to western Dawson and Valley counties between 950-1100 pm CDT. A few of the most notable reports included estimated 60 MPH gusts in Gothenburg, measured 63 MPH gusts at the Ord airport, and a machine shed destroyed approximately 11 miles west of Ord. Although severe weather only impacted a small portion of South Central Nebraska, widespread non-severe storms tracked across many areas north of Interstate 80 all night long and even past sunrise on the 22nd. Rainfall totals in most places measured no more than 1-2 inches, although an isolated much-higher amount of 3.60 inches was reported 7.5 miles west-northwest of Genoa. ||Severe storms initially flared up in western Nebraska during the late afternoon and early evening hours before tracking east toward northwest portions of South Central Nebraska, generally along and near a weak cold front roughly bisecting Nebraska from southwest-to-northeast. Shortly before entering the local area into western Dawson County, storms organized into a generally north-south oriented squall line, sending severe outflow winds surging out ahead. As the line tracked just outside of the local area across Custer County, the leading edge started to bow out, enhancing damaging gusts as the outflow raced into Valley County between 1030-1100 pm CDT. Shortly thereafter, the outflow surged well-ahead of the main area of storms, diminishing the damaging wind threat by midnight CDT. Large-scale forcing for this event was aided by subtle disturbances passing through the Central Plains in modest southwesterly flow aloft, along the southeast periphery of a broad trough anchored by a Pacific Northwest closed low. In addition to convergence along the aforementioned surface front, the nocturnal upscale growth of storms was aided by the development of a 40-50 knot southerly low level jet evident at 850 millibars. Late evening severe weather parameters in South Central Nebraska featured mixed-layer CAPE around 2000 J/kg and 0-6 kilometer deep layer wind shear around 35 knots.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A machine shed located 11 miles west and 1 mile north of Ord was destroyed. A number of power poles were knocked down from this area and extended to  approximately 4 miles west of Ord. Irrigation pivots were overturned approximately 4 miles west of Ord. The start time was an estimate from radar data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On this Saturday evening and early Sunday morning, strong to severe thunderstorms tracked into western portions of South Central Nebraska for the second consecutive night. Also for the second night in a row, the Ord airport measured a severe wind gust, this time topping out at 67 MPH shortly before 1230 am CDT as a powerful outflow boundary surged nearly 15 miles out ahead of a broken line of storms approaching from the west. The most notable storm of the night, however, was an outflow-dominant supercell that developed on the more-organized south end of the line as it pushed in from southwest Nebraska. The brunt of this storm blasted Gosper County as it entered the local area between 1230-130 am CDT, producing a swath of crop damage mainly in southern portions of the county thanks to the combination of quarter to golf ball size hail, and estimated wind gusts of 60-80 MPH. Although the worst part of the storm quickly weakened as it passed east-northeast into Phelps and Dawson counties, it still yielded quarter size hail and a downed power line east-southeast of Smithfield, and a measured 59 MPH gust roughly halfway between Lexington and Eddyville. By 2 am CDT, all storms had weakened below severe limits, but multiple clusters of sometimes-strong storms persisted through the night across several counties mainly along and north of Highway 6, at times producing hail up to nickel size and wind gusts up to around 50 MPH. The back edge of all thunderstorm activity finally exited the far eastern counties of South Central Nebraska along Highway 81 around 630 am CDT on the 23rd. ||Backing up to the very beginning of this event, an isolated strong storm rapidly sprung up as early as 5-6 pm CDT on the 22nd in western Dawson County, but only produced nickel size hail in the Gothenburg area before weakening and fading away. Thereafter, South Central Nebraska experienced a lull of several hours before the aforementioned broken line of severe storms initially raced into the area from the west between 11pm-midnight CDT. In general, the brunt of thunderstorm activity on this night focused near a west-southwest to east-northeast oriented quasi-stationary front/outflow boundary stretched across the heart of Nebraska. In the mid-upper levels, forcing was tied to low-amplitude disturbances passing through modestly strong southwesterly flow, along the periphery of a broad trough anchored by a vigorous Northern Rockies vorticity max. In addition to convergence along the aforementioned surface front, the nocturnal maintenance of storms was augmented warm air advection evident at 850 millibars along a 40-50 knot low level jet. Mesoscale severe weather parameters around midnight CDT in South Central Nebraska featured most-unstable CAPE around 1500 J/kg in the presence of seasonably strong 0-6 kilometer deep layer wind shear of 40+ knots.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail occurred along with estimated wind gusts of 60 to 70 mph on Highway 283 near mile marker 30.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed over the northwestern Sandhills and moved east during the late afternoon and evening hours of July 30th.  Hail to the size of golfballs and winds estimated to 70 MPH broke windows out of cars and homes near Crookston. Later that evening, a large supercell thunderstorm developed over northern Lincoln County and tracked to the southeast, impacting North Platte and the surrounding areas.  A storm survey north of North Platte estimated the highest thunderstorm wind gusts at 97 MPH.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three metal buildings were destroyed northwest of North Platte, the largest of which was 70 by 90 feet. Power lines reported down on Suburban road along with numerous large trees down. A house sustained major damage to siding and shingles on the north side of the structure.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stagnant upper level high pressure system brought an extended period of very hot temperatures to eastern Nebraska the last week of August.  Heat index values peaked in the 104 to 109 degree range on August 30th as temperatures approached 100 degrees to go along with dew point temperatures in the upper 60s to mid 70s.  Even though other days during the last week of August had highs well into the 90s, dew point readings were lower which generally kept heat index values at or below 100 degrees.  Lows during this stretch were mostly in the upper 60s to mid 70s, and a few warmest minimum temperatures were tied or broken to go along with a few record highs tied or broken.

A supercell produced extensive severe weather over Valley and Sherman counties this Wednesday evening. Around 2 p.m. CDT, a lone thunderstorm formed near the South Dakota-Nebraska border and eventually became supercellular. As it moved southeastward toward central Nebraska, other isolated supercells joined. Storm evolution was complex this afternoon, with multiple storm splits and cell mergers. At 4 p.m. CDT, one of the new cells formed in close proximity to the initial storm, along the Holt-Rock county border. This storm took over, increased in size and became the dominant storm. Given its proximity to the initial storm, it is likely that some constructive interference occurred between the two. While all other storms tracked southeast or south-southeastward, this storm tracked nearly due south, entering northern Valley County by 6 p.m. CDT. This storm had a history of producing severe wind and hail, so it is likely that continued as the storm tracked across Valley county. However, due to the sparse population, no severe reports were received until the storm hit the town of Ord. Hail as large as golf balls fell in town and the airport ASOS measured winds of 60 mph. Isolated, but intense, wind damage occurred between Ord and the Valley-Sherman county border. A NWS storm survey estimated winds between 70 and 100 mph. The roof of a home approximately 4 miles east-southeast of Ord was damaged. Roughly 120 square feet of roofing material was removed and blown into the yard, including 60 square feet of roof sheathing. The portion removed was at a gable end where the wind lifted up the outlookers/soffit, taking some of the roof with it. Damage also occurred to crops, a barn, a large storage shed and a center pivot irrigation system. Multiple grain bins were also blown over and/or destroyed. The storm continued south, hugging the Sherman-Howard county line, and still producing hail as large as 1.5 inches in diameter. It then rapidly dissipated over northwestern Hall County.||The synoptic setup was very innocuous, with weak forcing. The surface was dominated by high pressure that extended from the Central Plains into the Great Lakes. The storms initiated along a weak surface trough which was situated in the warm sector a low pressure system near Lake Winnipeg. Temperatures were in the low 80s, with dewpoints in the low 60s. Despite unimpressive mid-level lapse rates, MLCAPE was around 2000 J/kg. Deep layer shear was near 50 knots, and storm-relative helicity was between 100 and 200 m2/s2. Both of these shear values were supportive of supercell storm mode. The upper-level flow was west-northwest, with a ridge over the western U.S. and a trough over the east.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts estimated at 60 MPH were accompanied by walnut size hail.  Some minor tree damage was reported.
