EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Starting during the late afternoon and evening of Saturday the 7th, peaking in intensity during the overnight hours and lingering in lighter form well into the daytime hours of Sunday the 8th, this was the first widespread measurable snow of the 2013-2014 winter season in South Central Nebraska. However, the 24-county area was bisected by two distinct zones of lighter and heavier accumulations. In general, most locations northwest of a line from near Elwood-Kearney-Grand Island-Osceola only observed storm total amounts ranging from around one-half inch up to around 2.5 inches. Meanwhile, locations south of this aforementioned line caught the brunt of the storm with widespread amounts of 3-7 inches. Thanks to cold surface temperatures that only averaged between 5-15 degrees, this was a light, fluffy snow that accumulated efficiently. Fortunately, blowing and drifting was not a significant issue as east-southeast winds only averaged 10-15 MPH. According to NWS cooperative observers, a few of the highest storm total snow amounts featured 8 at Harlan County Lake, 7.5 three miles north of York and 7 at Clay Center, Naponee and two miles northeast of Upland. Several other places reported between 5.0 and 6.5, including Bradshaw, Minden Geneva, Holdrege, Wilsonville, Red Cloud, Nelson and Hebron. The Nebraska Tri-Cities resided within the transition zone between higher amounts to the south and lower totals to the north, as Hastings NWS office led the way with 4.4, followed by Kearney airport with 2.4 and Grand Island airport with 2.1. On the lowest end of the spectrum were far northern and west-central locations such as Ord, Greeley and Cozad, which measured no more than one-half inch. ||Accumulating snow first broke out over the area during the 5-7pm time frame on the 7th, but peaked in intensity between 8pm-8am, during which time modest mesoscale banding took place. Although most accumulations had already occurred by sunrise or shortly thereafter, at least on-and-off episodes of light snow or flurries continued through much of the day on the 8th before ending. On the synoptic scale aloft, this snow event was forced by a broad shortwave trough that gradually ejected east-northeast from the Intermountain West into the Central Plains. Out ahead of the primary trough axis, the main time frame of steadiest snowfall occurred as strong mid level isentropic lift developed over South Central Nebraska within the entrance region of an upper jet streak centered from the southern Minnesota area across the Great Lakes region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A snowfall total of 6.5 inches occurred in the community of Minden.

A potent winter storm system moved across eastern Colorado into western Kansas.  Strong northerly winds gusting as high as 50 mph combined with falling snow to create periods of visibilities less than a quarter mile during the late morning and afternoon across the southern Nebraska Panhandle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter in Sidney reported near zero visibility in falling and blowing snow.  Two to three inches of snow accumulated in Sidney.  Winds were sustained between 35 and 40 mph with gusts to 50 mph.

Strong northerly winds gusting as high as 50 mph combined with falling snow to create periods of visibilities less than a quarter mile during the late morning and afternoon across the southern Nebraska Panhandle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility was a quarter mile in falling and blowing snow at Kimball.  Winds were sustained between 35 and 40 mph with gusts to 50 mph.  Two inches of snow was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced three tornadoes which tracked over Dixon and Dakota Counties in extreme northeast Nebraska on the late afternoon of October 4th. One damaging tornado crossed into Dixon County from Wayne County Nebraska, a brief tornado formed and dissipated in Dixon County, and another tornado developed in northern Dakota County before crossing the Missouri River into Union County South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado, crossing into Dixon County from Wayne County, severely damaged the roofs of two houses, one of which was a manufactured house, and caused damage to the siding and windows of the houses. Windows of at least one other house were blown out. The tornado also overturned a camper, destroyed or severely damaged a grain bin, numerous farm outbuildings, farm storage buildings, several pieces of farm equipment, and flattened corn crops in several areas. The tornado caused tree damage along its path, including trees uprooted and snapped off at the trunks, and blew down power poles and power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Starting during the late afternoon and evening of Saturday the 7th, peaking in intensity during the overnight hours and lingering in lighter form well into the daytime hours of Sunday the 8th, this was the first widespread measurable snow of the 2013-2014 winter season in South Central Nebraska. However, the 24-county area was bisected by two distinct zones of lighter and heavier accumulations. In general, most locations northwest of a line from near Elwood-Kearney-Grand Island-Osceola only observed storm total amounts ranging from around one-half inch up to around 2.5 inches. Meanwhile, locations south of this aforementioned line caught the brunt of the storm with widespread amounts of 3-7 inches. Thanks to cold surface temperatures that only averaged between 5-15 degrees, this was a light, fluffy snow that accumulated efficiently. Fortunately, blowing and drifting was not a significant issue as east-southeast winds only averaged 10-15 MPH. According to NWS cooperative observers, a few of the highest storm total snow amounts featured 8 at Harlan County Lake, 7.5 three miles north of York and 7 at Clay Center, Naponee and two miles northeast of Upland. Several other places reported between 5.0 and 6.5, including Bradshaw, Minden Geneva, Holdrege, Wilsonville, Red Cloud, Nelson and Hebron. The Nebraska Tri-Cities resided within the transition zone between higher amounts to the south and lower totals to the north, as Hastings NWS office led the way with 4.4, followed by Kearney airport with 2.4 and Grand Island airport with 2.1. On the lowest end of the spectrum were far northern and west-central locations such as Ord, Greeley and Cozad, which measured no more than one-half inch. ||Accumulating snow first broke out over the area during the 5-7pm time frame on the 7th, but peaked in intensity between 8pm-8am, during which time modest mesoscale banding took place. Although most accumulations had already occurred by sunrise or shortly thereafter, at least on-and-off episodes of light snow or flurries continued through much of the day on the 8th before ending. On the synoptic scale aloft, this snow event was forced by a broad shortwave trough that gradually ejected east-northeast from the Intermountain West into the Central Plains. Out ahead of the primary trough axis, the main time frame of steadiest snowfall occurred as strong mid level isentropic lift developed over South Central Nebraska within the entrance region of an upper jet streak centered from the southern Minnesota area across the Great Lakes region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A snowfall total of 8 inches occurred at Harlan Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions improved little across eastern Nebraska during March.  Precipitation during the month was generally around one half inch to one inch, although a swath of totals around 2 inches prevailed near the I80 corridor from Lincoln through Omaha.  Normals for March are around 1.75 to 2.0 inches. Thus, Exceptional Drought conditions continued in northeast Nebraska near and north of a line from Columbus to Tekamah with the Drought intensity remaining in the Extreme category southeast of there from around Lincoln and Beatrice northeast through Omaha and Blair.  Conditions were slightly better southeast of Lincoln and Omaha, but they were still in the Severe category.  The drought persisted into April.

The drought that began in June 2012 and expanded to become an Exceptional (D4) Drought in October 2012 continues into February 2013. While the weather in February became more active, with two major snowfall events, only minor relief or recharge to the depleted soil moisture occurred. Long-term impacts to hydrological and ecological interests are expected to linger. In February, areas of significant snowfall did occur with monthly precipitations departures ranging from around a half inch above normal at North Platte and Valentine to near normal at Broken Bow and Imperial.  This helped to keep fire dangers and long term impacts from worsening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The drought that began in June 2012 continued through February 2013. The exceptional (D4) drought lingered despite near normal precipitation during the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper level low pressure system moved from the Rocky Mountains into the central plains spreading snow across a large portion of the nation's mid section. Light snow developed the morning of the 21st across eastern Nebraska and slowly spread east northeast throughout the day and into the early morning hours of the 22nd. A blanket of snow around 3 to 8 inches covered all of eastern Nebraska, with the heaviest amounts around Douglas, Saunders, Washington and Sarpy counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of around 6 inches fell across much of the county with 6 inches reported in Staplehurst and Utica.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system moved out of the southern Rockies bringing a prolonged period of snow to western and north central Nebraska beginning during the early evening on February 20th through the overnight hours on February 21st. Snowfall totals ranged from around eight inches across portions of west central Nebraska into eastern portions of north central Nebraska to near 12 inches in Hayes and Frontier county in southwest Nebraska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snowfall amounts of 8 inches occurred across Custer County beginning during the early evening hours on February 20th through the evening hours on February 22nd.  Northeast winds up to 15 to 25 mph also created areas of blowing snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: January 11, 2013 a powerful upper level low pressure system moved through the Plains and brought winter weather conditions to western and north central Nebraska. Snow and blowing snow was forecasted for the eastern Panhandle and the western Sandhills of Nebraska. Further southeast, to include Lee Bird Field (LBF) in Lincoln County, areas of patchy freezing drizzle or light freezing would give way to snow before the threat for freezing precipitation would end around midnight. A plane that took off from the North Platte Regional Airport at 1553 CST crashed about 11 miles northeast of the airport, in the Maxwell area. The aircraft crashed resulting in four fatalities (indirect).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Nebraska State Patrol notified WFO North Platte of a plane that took off from the North Platte Regional Airport, Lee Bird Field (LBF) at 1546 CST and lost contact seven minutes later at 1553 CST. At the time weather conditions at LBF included freezing drizzle and visibility of three miles, with a cloud ceiling of 900 feet. The plane was a multi-engine BE-58 manufactured by Raytheon according to a FAA spokeswoman. The plane crashed 11 miles northeast of North Platte in the Maxwell area. Four male fatalities (indirect) occurred in the crash.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: According to the U.S. Drought Monitor, December 2013 marked the 19th consecutive month during which drought of at least category D2 Severe intensity plagued at least a portion of South Central Nebraska, prolonging one of the most notable droughts in decades. Despite monthly precipitation checking in well-below normal across the entire 24-county area, there was literally no change whatsoever in drought classification for the second month in a row, according to the U.S. Drought Monitor. As a result, the total number of South Central Nebraska counties entirely or partially assigned D2 Drought remained at six. All six of these worst-off counties were clustered in the far southwest portion of the area: Dawson, Gosper, Phelps, Furnas, Harlan and Franklin. Meanwhile, a fairly narrow zone of Category D1 Moderate Drought continued to border up against the D2 areas, and covered the majority of several counties mainly west of Highway 281 such as Valley, Sherman, Kearney and Webster. Although these poorer drought conditions persisted in western/southwestern counties, the majority of South Central Nebraska, including nearly all areas along/east of the Highway 281 corridor, finished December in Category D0 Abnormally Dry conditions (considered to be one category better than true drought). ||Even though the majority of South Central Nebraska totaled between 2-8��� of snow during December, essentially the entire area suffered a notably dry month as most snowfall contained minimal liquid equivalent. Based on reports from around 50 NWS Cooperative Observers, and also radar-derived estimation, nearly the entire area totaled less than one-third of normal December precipitation and around 70% of the area had less than one-fourth of normal (based on long-term December normals generally around 0.75).  Per NWS cooperative observers, the lowest December precipitation totals were primarily concentrated north of a line from Elwood-Kearney-Grand Island-Osceola, as this area largely missed out on a winter storm from the 7th-8th that dropped several inches of snow on counties farther south and east. Within northern and western counties, some of the paltriest December precipitation totals included  a non-measurable trace at Arcadia and Ravenna, 0.02 at Ord and 0.03 at Miller, six miles south-southeast of Lexington and four miles north of Aurora. Although still well-below normal, a few of the highest monthly precipitation amounts featured 0.47 at Bruning, 0.26 at Harlan County Lake and 0.24 at Edison.  In between these extremes, December precipitation at the official Tri-Cities airport sites included only 0.14��� at Hastings, 0.11��� at Grand Island and 0.10��� at Kearney. At Grand Island, this was the 18th-driest December on record dating back 119 years, and was the driest since 2004. According to the USDA Nebraska Crop Progress and Condition Report at the end of December, state-averaged topsoil moisture supplies had degraded slightly since late-November, now rating 33% short to very short and 67% adequate. A powerful winter storm moved into the central Plains during the weekend of March 9-10th. The system initially brought warm weather and scattered showers and thunderstorms on the evening of the 8th into the morning of the 9th. As the main upper level storm system emerged on the Plains late on the 9th, a strong cold front moved from northwest to southeast across the area. Precipitation redeveloped behind this cold front associated with the upper level storm system and quickly changed from light rain to light snow during the early morning hours of the 10th. Isolated reports of sleet and light freezing rain were reported as the precipitation changed from light rain to snow, but significant ice accumulations were not reported. The snow, occasionally heavy, then continued through the day on the 10th and ended in the early morning hours on Monday March 11. Also associated with the system were strong northwest winds. Wind speeds of 30 to 40 mph were common during the day on Sunday March 10th leading to considerable blowing and drifting of the falling snow and reduced visibilities. This combination led to numerous traffic accidents and for authorities to close Interstate 80 for a time west of Lincoln in Lancaster and Seward Counties. Most schools were closed on Monday the 11th due to the poor travel conditions. Some of the higher snowfall totals ranged from 8.5 inches at the Naitonal Weather Service in Valley and in Seward, 9 inches at Eppley Airfield in Omaha and also in North Bend, 11 inches was reported in David City, and Fremont recorded 11.5 inches. Generally snowfall amounts of 6 to 11 inches were common with the event in the heaviest band.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong gradient of heavy snow fell across the county. Snowfall ranged from a reported 8.3 inches in Raymond to near 2 inches in the far southern part of the county. Lincoln reported 7 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions improved little across eastern Nebraska during March.  Precipitation during the month was generally around one half inch to one inch, although a swath of totals around 2 inches prevailed near the I80 corridor from Lincoln through Omaha.  Normals for March are around 1.75 to 2.0 inches. Thus, Exceptional Drought conditions continued in northeast Nebraska near and north of a line from Columbus to Tekamah with the Drought intensity remaining in the Extreme category southeast of there from around Lincoln and Beatrice northeast through Omaha and Blair.  Conditions were slightly better southeast of Lincoln and Omaha, but they were still in the Severe category.  The drought persisted into April.

The drought that began in June 2012 and expanded to become an Exceptional (D4) Drought in October 2012 continues into February 2013. While the weather in February became more active, with two major snowfall events, only minor relief or recharge to the depleted soil moisture occurred. Long-term impacts to hydrological and ecological interests are expected to linger. In February, areas of significant snowfall did occur with monthly precipitations departures ranging from around a half inch above normal at North Platte and Valentine to near normal at Broken Bow and Imperial.  This helped to keep fire dangers and long term impacts from worsening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The drought that began in June 2012 continued through February 2013. The exceptional (D4) drought lingered despite slightly above normal precipitation during the month.

A strong Pacific storm system moved across the western Nebraska Panhandle. Bitter cold air from the north combined with abundant moisture to produce widespread moderate to heavy snowfall. Storm totals ranged from 6 to 10 inches, heaviest near the Wyoming border. Northwest winds of 30 to 40 mph produced blowing snow, poor visibilities and very cold wind chill temperatures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six to eight inches of snow with two foot drifts was reported 12 miles southwest of Chadron.  Eight inches fell at Chadron.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Seven inches of snow was reported east of Watson Ranch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm system moved over Southwest Nebraska on the 20th from the southeast. With little to no wind, the snow was able to fall straight down. The only reductions in visibility occurred under the heavier bands of snow, which had snowfall rates of an inch or more an hour.  Snowfall reports ranged from 12.5 inches at McCook, to 10 inches at Trenton, and 8 inches at Stratton.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A total snowfall amount of 9 inches was reported at Benkelman.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense low pressure system tracked across Colorado into Nebraska.  Occasional moderate to heavy snow developed over the western Nebraska Panhandle, with gusty northerly winds of 35 to 45 mph.  The combination of falling snow and gusty winds created considerable blowing and drifting snow with near zero visibilities.  Snow drifts varied from one to four feet in some areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibilities were near zero with winds gusting as high as 45 mph in heavy snow.  Snow totals ranged from four to six inches.  Snow drifts of three to four feet were reported in some areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper level low pressure system moved from the Rocky Mountains into the central plains spreading snow across a large portion of the nation's mid section. Light snow developed the morning of the 21st across eastern Nebraska and slowly spread east northeast throughout the day and into the early morning hours of the 22nd. A blanket of snow around 3 to 8 inches covered all of eastern Nebraska, with the heaviest amounts around Douglas, Saunders, Washington and Sarpy counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 6 inches was reported near Oakdale with similar amounts likely across the southern part of the county based on surrounding observations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Starting during the late afternoon and evening of Saturday the 7th, peaking in intensity during the overnight hours and lingering in lighter form well into the daytime hours of Sunday the 8th, this was the first widespread measurable snow of the 2013-2014 winter season in South Central Nebraska. However, the 24-county area was bisected by two distinct zones of lighter and heavier accumulations. In general, most locations northwest of a line from near Elwood-Kearney-Grand Island-Osceola only observed storm total amounts ranging from around one-half inch up to around 2.5 inches. Meanwhile, locations south of this aforementioned line caught the brunt of the storm with widespread amounts of 3-7 inches. Thanks to cold surface temperatures that only averaged between 5-15 degrees, this was a light, fluffy snow that accumulated efficiently. Fortunately, blowing and drifting was not a significant issue as east-southeast winds only averaged 10-15 MPH. According to NWS cooperative observers, a few of the highest storm total snow amounts featured 8 at Harlan County Lake, 7.5 three miles north of York and 7 at Clay Center, Naponee and two miles northeast of Upland. Several other places reported between 5.0 and 6.5, including Bradshaw, Minden Geneva, Holdrege, Wilsonville, Red Cloud, Nelson and Hebron. The Nebraska Tri-Cities resided within the transition zone between higher amounts to the south and lower totals to the north, as Hastings NWS office led the way with 4.4, followed by Kearney airport with 2.4 and Grand Island airport with 2.1. On the lowest end of the spectrum were far northern and west-central locations such as Ord, Greeley and Cozad, which measured no more than one-half inch. ||Accumulating snow first broke out over the area during the 5-7pm time frame on the 7th, but peaked in intensity between 8pm-8am, during which time modest mesoscale banding took place. Although most accumulations had already occurred by sunrise or shortly thereafter, at least on-and-off episodes of light snow or flurries continued through much of the day on the 8th before ending. On the synoptic scale aloft, this snow event was forced by a broad shortwave trough that gradually ejected east-northeast from the Intermountain West into the Central Plains. Out ahead of the primary trough axis, the main time frame of steadiest snowfall occurred as strong mid level isentropic lift developed over South Central Nebraska within the entrance region of an upper jet streak centered from the southern Minnesota area across the Great Lakes region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A snowfall total of 3 inches occurred in the community of Polk.

The drought that began in June and expanded to become an Exceptional (D4) Drought in 2012 continues into January 2013. While the weather in January was characterized as progressive the storm track provided little relief or recharge to the depleted soil moisture. Short term impacts are expected to hit agriculture and grasslands, while the long-term impacts to hydrological and ecological interests are expected to linger. In January some rainfall did occur although departures remain over four inches that keep fire dangers and potential impacts at the forefront and in the news.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The drought that began in June continued into January 2013. The exceptional (D4) drought lingered as below normal precipitation fell during the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous Pacific low pressure system brought widespread heavy snowfall and northerly winds up to 50 mph as it moved across the central Rockies. Storm total snow accumulations up to two feet were observed across the western Nebraska Panhandle.  The combination of falling snow and strong winds produced considerable drifting snow, which resulted in very low visibilities and snow packed roads. Numerous highways, including Interstate 80 from Sidney to the Wyoming border were closed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An observer nine miles northeast of Harrison measured 16 inches of snow with four to six foot drifts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During a roughly 30-hour period starting on Wednesday the 20th, but peaking in intensity on Thursday the 21st, the majority of the 24-county area experienced its heaviest snowfall of the 2012-2013 winter season. For some locations near the Kansas border this was actually one of the largest storm-total snowfalls on record. This included Red Cloud, where 13.5 inches ranked as the sixth-highest two-day total since 1894. According to NWS cooperative observers, essentially the entire area totaled at least 6-10, with the highest amounts of 10-14 concentrated in southwestern counties generally south of a line from Cozad-Holdrege-Nelson. In addition to 13.5 at Red Cloud, a few of the biggest totals included 14 at Harlan County Lake and Beaver City, 13.5 eight miles south of Elwood, 12.5 at Naponee and Nelson, and 12 near Blue Hill and Upland. The overall lowest amounts affected eastern areas near the Highway 81 corridor, including 6.5 at Osceola, 6 near York and 7 at Geneva and Bruning. In between these lowest and highest extremes were places such as Grand Island and Holdrege (10), and Hastings (9.5). Although the heavy snow itself created considerable impact across the region with very poor visibilities and at least minor drifting, the magnitude of blowing snow was held in check by sustained easterly to northerly winds that generally topped out around 20 MPH and mainly gusted under 25 MPH, keeping this storm from reaching blizzard status. Liquid-equivalent moisture between one-half inch and one inch was common, and also welcome across the drought-stricken area, with locally higher totals including 1.25 at Red Cloud. ||Although accumulating snow fell off-and-on for roughly 30 hours across all of South Central Nebraska, the timing of the most intense snowfall varied. For most places, the first light accumulations got underway between the mid-morning and late afternoon hours on the 20th. Then from the evening of the 20th into the pre-dawn hours of the 21st, a heavier snow band affected primarily southwestern counties. By sunrise on the 21st, southern and western locations such as Cozad, Cambridge and Red Cloud had already totaled 5-8, while the majority of South Central Nebraska reported no more than 1-3. That changed in a big way during the daytime hours on the 21st, as the most intense snow bands of the event blossomed across the area, accompanied by steady northeast winds and temperatures generally hovering between 18-22 degrees. These snow bands gradually weakened and dissipated from south-to-north during the evening, with most of South Central Nebraska void of falling snow by 9 PM as a closed mid level low pressure system lifted farther north. On the synoptic scale aloft, this prolonged winter storm was forced by the approach and eventual passage of an expansive, amplified mid-upper level trough. The primary mid-level 700-500 millibar closed low tracked from southern California on the morning of the 20th, to the Texas Panhandle on the morning of the 21st, before deepening and lifting slowly north-northeast across central Kansas into southeast Nebraska during the daytime and evening hours. Periods of isentropic lift associated with smaller-scale shortwave troughs lifting out ahead of the main system were responsible for snowfall from the 20th into the morning of the 21st, with the most intense snow bands during the day on the 21st driven by pronounced deformation on the northwest periphery of the mid level low track. From a forecast perspective, although northern and eastern portions of South Central Nebraska did not receive quite as much snow as originally expected, this winter storm was overall one of the best-anticipated in recent memory thanks to unusually high consistency in computer model solutions. Although the finer details of timing and full snowfall potential did not come into focus until a few days out, signs of a potentially significant winter storm were recognized over one week in advance.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm total of 10.9 inches of snow fell at Franklin, 12.5 inches of snow fell at Naponee and 12 inches of snow fell at Upland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very atypical early spring storm brought a mix of winter weather and severe thunderstorms to eastern Nebraska April 9th and 10th.  An Arctic cold front pushed across the area early on April 9th bringing subfreezing temperatures to northeast Nebraska.  Meanwhile, a large upper level low pressure system tracking northeast out of the southern Rockies fed warmer and unstable air across the area above this shallow cold air.  A mix of freezing rain and snow spread over northeast Nebraska during the morning of the 9th and by afternoon scattered thunderstorms developed and moved across that area.  These storms intensified the freezing rain, mixing at times with heavy sleet and in some cases even large hail.  Farther southeast, even with temperatures in the 30s and 40s, severe thunderstorms developed during the evening that produced even larger hail.  One storm that moved across the western sections of the Omaha metro caused extensive damage as it dropped quarter to golf ball-size and even larger stones that covered the ground.  The freezing rain and sleet caused some power outages in northeast Nebraska along with the cancellation of school on the 10th.

Drought conditions improved or ended over much of eastern Nebraska during April as precipitation of 3.5 to around 5 inches fell over most of the area.  These amounts were around 1 to over 2.5 inches above normal and coincided with below normal temperatures and reduced evaporation.  Thus no counties in eastern Nebraska maintained Exceptional Drought intensity with northeast Nebraska, along and northwest of a line from Columbus to Wayne, improving into the Extreme Category.  The rest of eastern Nebraska had improved to Severe or Moderate Intensities, with counties in the latter category generally south through east of Lincoln.  The drought in northeast Nebraska persisted into May.

Thus no counties in eastern Nebraska maintained Exceptional Drought intensity with northeast Nebraska, along and northwest of a line from Columbus to Wayne, improving into the Extreme Category.  The rest of eastern Nebraska had improved to Severe or Moderate Intensities, with counties in the latter category generally south through east of Lincoln.  The drought in northeast Nebraska persisted into May.

A vigorous Pacific low pressure system brought widespread heavy snowfall and northerly winds up to 50 mph as it moved across the central Rockies. Storm total snow accumulations up to two feet were observed across the western Nebraska Panhandle.  The combination of falling snow and strong winds produced considerable drifting snow, which resulted in very low visibilities and snow packed roads. Numerous highways, including Interstate 80 from Sidney to the Wyoming border were closed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An observer in Harrison estimated two feet of snow with six to eight foot drifts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The second official blizzard of the season in South Central Nebraska pounded much of the southeast half of the area on this Sunday, dropping generally 3-9 inches of snow whipped by strong sustained north-northwest winds of 25-35 MPH that frequently gusted over 40 MPH. The vast majority of snow focused east of a line from near Fullerton-Hastings-Franklin, with the heart of the worst blizzard conditions centered through Hamilton, York, Clay, Fillmore, Nuckolls and Thayer counties. Interstate 80 was closed for several hours from Grand Island to Lincoln. In sharp contrast, and thanks to an extremely sharp divide in snow bands, most locations west of the Fullerton-Hastings-Franklin line hardly observed any snow at all. Snow first started falling in southwest-to-northeast bands around midnight, after transitioning from a rain/sleet mix that fell on the evening of the 9th. By sunrise, 1-3 inches had fallen across much of the affected area, with higher amounts to around 5 inches reported near Superior. Blizzard conditions with visibility of around 1/4 mile or less were present from the start in many areas, as Hastings airport measured one of the highest known wind gusts of the event at 54 MPH around 5 AM CDT. By the mid-morning hours, conditions had further deteriorated as a particularly intense snow band roughly 20 miles wide persisted along an axis from York to Nuckolls counties. By the early-mid afternoon hours, banding was not quite as intense, but a broad area of light to moderate snow continued. During the late afternoon and evening hours, falling snow tapered off to flurries and eventually ended from west to east. However, at least minor blowing snow issues continued well into the night as sustained winds slowly eased below 20 MPH. ||Per NWS cooperative observers, a few of the highest storm-total snow amounts included 9 near Shickley, 8.1 near Superior, 8 at Geneva and 6 in or near Aurora, Bruning and Hubbell. A bit farther west, both Hastings and Grand Island set or tied daily snowfall records for March 10th with 5.3 and 3.5, respectively. As evidence of the sharp west edge of the snowfall area, places such as Kearney airport and Minden only reported 0.2 and a non-measurable trace, respectively. On the synoptic scale aloft, this event was powered by the passage of a highly amplified and slightly positively tilted mid-upper trough that slowly lumbered across the Central United States. At 700 millibars, the heaviest snow bands concentrated under a pronounced deformation zone on the northwest periphery of a closed low/vort max that tracked from southwest Kansas on the evening of the 9th to southern Iowa by late evening on the 10th. At mid-afternoon on the 10th, surface maps confirmed that strong north winds and blizzard conditions were promoted by a tight pressure gradient between a 1004 millibar low over north central Missouri and a 1022 millibar ridge extending southward into northwest Nebraska. Enhancing the March misery across the local area were temperatures averaging in the low-mid 20s, yielding wind chill values generally 4-12 degrees above zero.

Late afternoon and early evening thunderstorms over portions of the western Nebraska Panhandle produced large hail, damaging winds, a few tornadoes, and a funnel cloud.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny to golf ball size hail was reported two miles east of Sidney.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions improved little across eastern Nebraska during March.  Precipitation during the month was generally around one half inch to one inch, although a swath of totals around 2 inches prevailed near the I80 corridor from Lincoln through Omaha.  Normals for March are around 1.75 to 2.0 inches. Thus, Exceptional Drought conditions continued in northeast Nebraska near and north of a line from Columbus to Tekamah with the Drought intensity remaining in the Extreme category southeast of there from around Lincoln and Beatrice northeast through Omaha and Blair.  Conditions were slightly better southeast of Lincoln and Omaha, but they were still in the Severe category.  The drought persisted into April.

A very atypical early spring storm brought a mix of winter weather and severe thunderstorms to eastern Nebraska April 9th and 10th.  An Arctic cold front pushed across the area early on April 9th bringing subfreezing temperatures to northeast Nebraska.  Meanwhile, a large upper level low pressure system tracking northeast out of the southern Rockies fed warmer and unstable air across the area above this shallow cold air.  A mix of freezing rain and snow spread over northeast Nebraska during the morning of the 9th and by afternoon scattered thunderstorms developed and moved across that area.  These storms intensified the freezing rain, mixing at times with heavy sleet and in some cases even large hail.  Farther southeast, even with temperatures in the 30s and 40s, severe thunderstorms developed during the evening that produced even larger hail.  One storm that moved across the western sections of the Omaha metro caused extensive damage as it dropped quarter to golf ball-size and even larger stones that covered the ground.  The freezing rain and sleet caused some power outages in northeast Nebraska along with the cancellation of school on the 10th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to nickel size fell from southwest Madison into Madison for about 10 minutes with a few stones eventually reaching quarter-size at 3 pm CDT.  The hail fell from thunderstorms that were occurring with temperatures below freezing, thus they were accompanied by freezing rain and sleet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early evening thunderstorm produced large hail in Box Butte County of the northern Nebraska Panhandle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter reported ping pong ball size hail.

The drought that began in June 2012 lingered with Exceptional Drought (D4) conditions continuing into March. There was some localized improvements in portions of Sheridan, Cherry and Deuel counties as sufficient moisture resulted in a downgrade to Extreme Drought (D3) conditions by the end of the month. A combination of rain and snowfall during the month provided some relief, however the a large percentage of the area received less than 75 percent of the normal precipitation of 1.5 to 2 inches in March.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The drought that began in June 2012 lingered with Exceptional Drought (D4) conditions. Observed precipitation was near normal between the range from 1 to 1.5 inches which helped the parched ground, yet insufficient to reduce the overall impact of the ongoing drought. The drought continued into April.

During the afternoon and evening a band of heavy snow moved in from Colorado.  Strong wind gusts combined with the heavy snow caused blizzard conditions as the band of snow moved through Dundy county.  As the band progressed east, the wind gusts declined below blizzard criteria.  However radar imagery indicated the band of heavy snow was just as strong as it was over Dundy county where blizzard conditions were reported.  Despite the lighter wind gusts, the heavy snow likely produced near blizzard conditions over Hitchcock county as the band of snow moved through the county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The observer at 19N Parks reported less than 1/4 mile visibility and estimated the wind gusts to be 30-40MPH.

Scattered strong to severe thunderstorms developed mostly behind a cold front that pushed across the area late on April 8th.  By the early morning hours of the 9th a cluster of these storms formed west of Columbus began surging east in a bow echo that produced a swath of wind damage from near Schuyler to just west of Fremont.  Winds were estimated to have reached around 100 mph at the peak near and just west of North Bend where an empty coal train was derailed and an unsecured mobile home was destroyed injuring 2 people.  Numerous center pivots were overturned along the path and some trees were snapped.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts estimated at over 80 mph caused a swath of damage from west of Schuyler through Rogers and into Dodge county.  In Colfax county trees were snapped and center pivots were overturned.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very atypical early spring storm brought a mix of winter weather and severe thunderstorms to eastern Nebraska April 9th and 10th.  An Arctic cold front pushed across the area early on April 9th bringing subfreezing temperatures to northeast Nebraska.  Meanwhile, a large upper level low pressure system tracking northeast out of the southern Rockies fed warmer and unstable air across the area above this shallow cold air.  A mix of freezing rain and snow spread over northeast Nebraska during the morning of the 9th and by afternoon scattered thunderstorms developed and moved across that area.  These storms intensified the freezing rain, mixing at times with heavy sleet and in some cases even large hail.  Farther southeast, even with temperatures in the 30s and 40s, severe thunderstorms developed during the evening that produced even larger hail.  One storm that moved across the western sections of the Omaha metro caused extensive damage as it dropped quarter to golf ball-size and even larger stones that covered the ground.  The freezing rain and sleet caused some power outages in northeast Nebraska along with the cancellation of school on the 10th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain, mixed at times with sleet and snow, fell across the county beginning late in the morning of April 9th.  Later that afternoon, a few strong and even severe thunderstorms moved across the area producing heavier bouts of freezing rain and sleet and even hailstones that reached at least 1 inch in diameter.  The precipitation changed to mostly snow on April 10th before ending early on the 11th.  Some ice and sleet accumulated to an inch or so by the afternoon of the 9th,  with storm total snow/sleet accumulations reaching 3 to 6 inches over much of the county by late on the 10th.  Norfolk picked up over 2 inches of liquid equivalent from the storm.

Since temperatures were below freezing, the storms brought a mix of freezing rain and sleet and even hail greater than 1 inch in diameter.  The precipitation changed to mostly snow or sleet on April 10th before ending early on the 11th.  Some ice and sleet accumulated to an inch or so by the evening of the 9th, with storm total snow/sleet accumulations reaching 2 to 4 inches over much of the county by late on the 10th.  Norfolk picked up over 2 inches of liquid equivalent from the storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon and evening a band of heavy snow moved in from Colorado.  Strong wind gusts combined with the heavy snow caused blizzard conditions as the band of snow moved through Dundy county.  As the band progressed east, the wind gusts declined below blizzard criteria.  However radar imagery indicated the band of heavy snow was just as strong as it was over Dundy county where blizzard conditions were reported.  Despite the lighter wind gusts, the heavy snow likely produced near blizzard conditions over Hitchcock county as the band of snow moved through the county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Based on the AWOS observations at Atwood and Colby as the band of heavy snow moved, the wind gusts did not meet blizzard criteria.  However radar imagery indicated the band of snow was just as strong as it was over Dundy county where blizzard conditions did occur, so near blizzard conditions likely occurred due to the heavy snowfall as the band moved through.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions improved or ended over much of eastern Nebraska during April as precipitation of 3.5 to around 5 inches fell over most of the area.  These amounts were around 1 to over 2.5 inches above normal and coincided with below normal temperatures and reduced evaporation.  Thus no counties in eastern Nebraska maintained Exceptional Drought intensity with northeast Nebraska, along and northwest of a line from Columbus to Wayne, improving into the Extreme Category.  The rest of eastern Nebraska had improved to Severe or Moderate Intensities, with counties in the latter category generally south through east of Lincoln.  The drought in northeast Nebraska persisted into May.

The drought that began in June 2012 lingered with Exceptional Drought (D4) conditions continuing into March. There was some localized improvements in portions of Sheridan, Cherry and Deuel counties as sufficient moisture resulted in a downgrade to Extreme Drought (D3) conditions by the end of the month. A combination of rain and snowfall during the month provided some relief, however the a large percentage of the area received less than 75 percent of the normal precipitation of 1.5 to 2 inches in March.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The drought that began in June 2012 lingered with Exceptional Drought (D4) conditions. Observed precipitation ranged from a half inch to 1.5 inches in the east, which is close to the normal rainfall of 1.5 inches. The precipitation helped the parched ground, yet insufficient to reduce the overall impact of the ongoing drought. The drought continued into April.

The second official blizzard of the season in South Central Nebraska pounded much of the southeast half of the area on this Sunday, dropping generally 3-9 inches of snow whipped by strong sustained north-northwest winds of 25-35 MPH that frequently gusted over 40 MPH. The vast majority of snow focused east of a line from near Fullerton-Hastings-Franklin, with the heart of the worst blizzard conditions centered through Hamilton, York, Clay, Fillmore, Nuckolls and Thayer counties. Interstate 80 was closed for several hours from Grand Island to Lincoln. In sharp contrast, and thanks to an extremely sharp divide in snow bands, most locations west of the Fullerton-Hastings-Franklin line hardly observed any snow at all. Snow first started falling in southwest-to-northeast bands around midnight, after transitioning from a rain/sleet mix that fell on the evening of the 9th. By sunrise, 1-3 inches had fallen across much of the affected area, with higher amounts to around 5 inches reported near Superior. Blizzard conditions with visibility of around 1/4 mile or less were present from the start in many areas, as Hastings airport measured one of the highest known wind gusts of the event at 54 MPH around 5 AM CDT. By the mid-morning hours, conditions had further deteriorated as a particularly intense snow band roughly 20 miles wide persisted along an axis from York to Nuckolls counties. By the early-mid afternoon hours, banding was not quite as intense, but a broad area of light to moderate snow continued. During the late afternoon and evening hours, falling snow tapered off to flurries and eventually ended from west to east. However, at least minor blowing snow issues continued well into the night as sustained winds slowly eased below 20 MPH. ||Per NWS cooperative observers, a few of the highest storm-total snow amounts included 9 near Shickley, 8.1 near Superior, 8 at Geneva and 6 in or near Aurora, Bruning and Hubbell. A bit farther west, both Hastings and Grand Island set or tied daily snowfall records for March 10th with 5.3 and 3.5, respectively. As evidence of the sharp west edge of the snowfall area, places such as Kearney airport and Minden only reported 0.2 and a non-measurable trace, respectively. On the synoptic scale aloft, this event was powered by the passage of a highly amplified and slightly positively tilted mid-upper trough that slowly lumbered across the Central United States. At 700 millibars, the heaviest snow bands concentrated under a pronounced deformation zone on the northwest periphery of a closed low/vort max that tracked from southwest Kansas on the evening of the 9th to southern Iowa by late evening on the 10th. At mid-afternoon on the 10th, surface maps confirmed that strong north winds and blizzard conditions were promoted by a tight pressure gradient between a 1004 millibar low over north central Missouri and a 1022 millibar ridge extending southward into northwest Nebraska. Enhancing the March misery across the local area were temperatures averaging in the low-mid 20s, yielding wind chill values generally 4-12 degrees above zero.

During the late afternoon and evening hours, falling snow tapered off to flurries and eventually ended from west to east. However, at least minor blowing snow issues continued well into the night as sustained winds slowly eased below 20 MPH. ||Per NWS cooperative observers, a few of the highest storm-total snow amounts included 9 near Shickley, 8.1 near Superior, 8 at Geneva and 6 in or near Aurora, Bruning and Hubbell. A bit farther west, both Hastings and Grand Island set or tied daily snowfall records for March 10th with 5.3 and 3.5, respectively. As evidence of the sharp west edge of the snowfall area, places such as Kearney airport and Minden only reported 0.2 and a non-measurable trace, respectively. On the synoptic scale aloft, this event was powered by the passage of a highly amplified and slightly positively tilted mid-upper trough that slowly lumbered across the Central United States. At 700 millibars, the heaviest snow bands concentrated under a pronounced deformation zone on the northwest periphery of a closed low/vort max that tracked from southwest Kansas on the evening of the 9th to southern Iowa by late evening on the 10th. At mid-afternoon on the 10th, surface maps confirmed that strong north winds and blizzard conditions were promoted by a tight pressure gradient between a 1004 millibar low over north central Missouri and a 1022 millibar ridge extending southward into northwest Nebraska. Enhancing the March misery across the local area were temperatures averaging in the low-mid 20s, yielding wind chill values generally 4-12 degrees above zero.

The second official blizzard of the season in South Central Nebraska pounded much of the southeast half of the area on this Sunday, dropping generally 3-9 inches of snow whipped by strong sustained north-northwest winds of 25-35 MPH that frequently gusted over 40 MPH. The vast majority of snow focused east of a line from near Fullerton-Hastings-Franklin, with the heart of the worst blizzard conditions centered through Hamilton, York, Clay, Fillmore, Nuckolls and Thayer counties. Interstate 80 was closed for several hours from Grand Island to Lincoln. In sharp contrast, and thanks to an extremely sharp divide in snow bands, most locations west of the Fullerton-Hastings-Franklin line hardly observed any snow at all. Snow first started falling in southwest-to-northeast bands around midnight, after transitioning from a rain/sleet mix that fell on the evening of the 9th. By sunrise, 1-3 inches had fallen across much of the affected area, with higher amounts to around 5 inches reported near Superior. Blizzard conditions with visibility of around 1/4 mile or less were present from the start in many areas, as Hastings airport measured one of the highest known wind gusts of the event at 54 MPH around 5 AM CDT. By the mid-morning hours, conditions had further deteriorated as a particularly intense snow band roughly 20 miles wide persisted along an axis from York to Nuckolls counties. By the early-mid afternoon hours, banding was not quite as intense, but a broad area of light to moderate snow continued. According to the U.S. Drought Monitor, March marked the ninth consecutive month of severe (or worse) drought across South Central Nebraska. This continued one of the worst droughts since the 1950s, as the majority of the 24-county area measured below normal March precipitation. Based on Drought Monitor weekly issuances, there was overall little change in drought classification over the course of the month. A small part of the area actually experienced a slight degradation from category D3 extreme drought to D4 exceptional drought (the worst-possible category). This degradation was especially noted across much of Buffalo, Hall and Kearney counties. At month's end, the D4 area primarily focused within 13 counties generally northwest of a line from Alma-Grand Island-Fullerton. Most of the other 11 counties farther southeast remained in D3 extreme drought, although the majority of Webster, Nuckolls and Thayer counties improved to the somewhat less significant D2 severe drought during the last week of March. Based on reports from around 50 NWS cooperative observers, and also incorporating radar-derived estimation, roughly 70 percent of the area tallied below normal March precipitation (based on normals generally between 1.70-2.15). This included nearly all locations northwest of a line from Franklin-Grand Island-Fullerton. The limited area that observed slightly above normal precipitation was primarily concentrated 15-20 miles either side of an axis running from near Red Cloud northeastward through Clay Center and Fairmont. Much of the precipitation in this corridor fell between March 9th-10th, thanks to a combination of thunderstorms followed by generally 3-7 of snow and blizzard conditions. A few of the driest cooperative observer precipitation sites during March included Harlan County Lake (0.24), six miles north-northwest of Oxford (0.27), eight miles south of Elwood (0.30) and Gothenburg (0.31). On the opposite spectrum, a few of the wettest locations were Geneva (2.64), Hastings NWS office (2.40), four miles south of Shickley (2.33) and two miles west of Genoa (2.10). As for March snowfall, the majority of South Central Nebraska tallied near-normal to above normal amounts between 3-8, highlighted by 11.0 four miles east of Superior and 10.7 at Red Cloud.||Although severe to exceptional drought conditions remained firmly entrenched across the 24-county area at the end of March, the past four months since December 2012 signaled an overall-encouraging trend, as the majority of South Central Nebraska measured near normal to slightly above normal precipitation during that time, versus the solidly below normal values that defined much of the summer and fall of 2012. However, keeping optimism in check, December-March precipitation normally accounts for less than 15% of total annual precipitation. According to the USDA Crop Progress and Condition Report for the week ending March 31st, state-averaged topsoil moisture rated 80% short to very short and subsoil moisture 96% short to very short. Winter wheat across the state rated 49% poor to very poor, 41% fair and only 10% good. Hay and forage supplies checked in 59% short to very short, and stock water supplies were 36% short to very short and 63% adequate.

Clear skies behind behind a cold front from the previous night allowed the strong winds above the surface to mix down to the ground over Dundy county during the afternoon. Sustained winds of 40 MPH were reported through the afternoon at Haigler.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds of 40 MPH were reported at Haigler through the afternoon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The drought that began in June 2012 lingered with Exceptional Drought (D4) conditions continuing into March. There was some localized improvements in portions of Sheridan, Cherry and Deuel counties as sufficient moisture resulted in a downgrade to Extreme Drought (D3) conditions by the end of the month. A combination of rain and snowfall during the month provided some relief, however the a large percentage of the area received less than 75 percent of the normal precipitation of 1.5 to 2 inches in March.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The drought that began in June 2012 lingered with Exceptional Drought (D4) conditions. Observed precipitation ranged from a half inch to locally higher amounts to 2 inches. The averaged was below the normal 1.5 inches. The precipitation helped the parched ground, yet insufficient to reduce the overall impact of the ongoing drought. The drought continued into April.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very atypical early spring storm brought a mix of winter weather and severe thunderstorms to eastern Nebraska April 9th and 10th.  An Arctic cold front pushed across the area early on April 9th bringing subfreezing temperatures to northeast Nebraska.  Meanwhile, a large upper level low pressure system tracking northeast out of the southern Rockies fed warmer and unstable air across the area above this shallow cold air.  A mix of freezing rain and snow spread over northeast Nebraska during the morning of the 9th and by afternoon scattered thunderstorms developed and moved across that area.  These storms intensified the freezing rain, mixing at times with heavy sleet and in some cases even large hail.  Farther southeast, even with temperatures in the 30s and 40s, severe thunderstorms developed during the evening that produced even larger hail.  One storm that moved across the western sections of the Omaha metro caused extensive damage as it dropped quarter to golf ball-size and even larger stones that covered the ground.  The freezing rain and sleet caused some power outages in northeast Nebraska along with the cancellation of school on the 10th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain, mixed at times with sleet and snow, fell across the county beginning around midday of April 9th.  Later that afternoon, a few strong thunderstorms moved across the area producing heavier bouts of freezing rain and sleet and even possibly some hail.  The precipitation changed to mostly snow on April 10th before ending early on the 11th.  Some ice accumulations reached at least 1/4 inch by the evening of the 9th, and storm total snow/sleet accumulations reached 4 to 6 inches over much of the county by late on the 10th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving Pacific low pressure system produced moderate to heavy snowfall across the western Nebraska Panhandle.  East to northeast winds of 35 to 45 mph produced blowing and drifting snow and very low visibilities.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An observer nine miles northeast of Harrison measured 7.5 inches of snow.

An Arctic cold front pushed across the area early on April 9th bringing subfreezing temperatures to northeast Nebraska.  Meanwhile, a large upper level low pressure system tracking northeast out of the southern Rockies fed warmer and unstable air across the area above this shallow cold air.  A mix of freezing rain and snow spread over northeast Nebraska during the morning of the 9th and by afternoon scattered thunderstorms developed and moved across that area.  These storms intensified the freezing rain, mixing at times with heavy sleet and in some cases even large hail.  Farther southeast, even with temperatures in the 30s and 40s, severe thunderstorms developed during the evening that produced even larger hail.  One storm that moved across the western sections of the Omaha metro caused extensive damage as it dropped quarter to golf ball-size and even larger stones that covered the ground.  The freezing rain and sleet caused some power outages in northeast Nebraska along with the cancellation of school on the 10th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain, mixed at times with sleet and snow, fell across the county beginning in the early afternoon of April 9th.  Later that afternoon, a few strong and even severe thunderstorms moved across the area producing heavier bouts of freezing rain and sleet and even hailstones that reached at least quarter-size. The precipitation changed to mostly snow on April 10th before ending early on the 11th.  Some ice and sleet accumulations reached an inch or so by the late afternoon of the 9th causing some vehicles to slide into ditches on local highways.  Storm total snow/sleet accumulations reached 3 to 6 inches over much of the county by late on the 10th.  Norfolk picked up over 2 inches of liquid equivalent from the storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous Pacific low pressure system brought widespread heavy snowfall and northerly winds up to 50 mph as it moved across the central Rockies. Storm total snow accumulations up to two feet were observed across the western Nebraska Panhandle.  The combination of falling snow and strong winds produced considerable drifting snow, which resulted in very low visibilities and snow packed roads. Numerous highways, including Interstate 80 from Sidney to the Wyoming border were closed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An observer in Gering measured 8.5 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: According to the U.S. Drought Monitor, March marked the ninth consecutive month of severe (or worse) drought across South Central Nebraska. This continued one of the worst droughts since the 1950s, as the majority of the 24-county area measured below normal March precipitation. Based on Drought Monitor weekly issuances, there was overall little change in drought classification over the course of the month. A small part of the area actually experienced a slight degradation from category D3 extreme drought to D4 exceptional drought (the worst-possible category). This degradation was especially noted across much of Buffalo, Hall and Kearney counties. At month's end, the D4 area primarily focused within 13 counties generally northwest of a line from Alma-Grand Island-Fullerton. Most of the other 11 counties farther southeast remained in D3 extreme drought, although the majority of Webster, Nuckolls and Thayer counties improved to the somewhat less significant D2 severe drought during the last week of March. Based on reports from around 50 NWS cooperative observers, and also incorporating radar-derived estimation, roughly 70 percent of the area tallied below normal March precipitation (based on normals generally between 1.70-2.15). This included nearly all locations northwest of a line from Franklin-Grand Island-Fullerton. The limited area that observed slightly above normal precipitation was primarily concentrated 15-20 miles either side of an axis running from near Red Cloud northeastward through Clay Center and Fairmont. Much of the precipitation in this corridor fell between March 9th-10th, thanks to a combination of thunderstorms followed by generally 3-7 of snow and blizzard conditions. A few of the driest cooperative observer precipitation sites during March included Harlan County Lake (0.24), six miles north-northwest of Oxford (0.27), eight miles south of Elwood (0.30) and Gothenburg (0.31). On the opposite spectrum, a few of the wettest locations were Geneva (2.64), Hastings NWS office (2.40), four miles south of Shickley (2.33) and two miles west of Genoa (2.10). As for March snowfall, the majority of South Central Nebraska tallied near-normal to above normal amounts between 3-8, highlighted by 11.0 four miles east of Superior and 10.7 at Red Cloud.||Although severe to exceptional drought conditions remained firmly entrenched across the 24-county area at the end of March, the past four months since December 2012 signaled an overall-encouraging trend, as the majority of South Central Nebraska measured near normal to slightly above normal precipitation during that time, versus the solidly below normal values that defined much of the summer and fall of 2012. However, keeping optimism in check, December-March precipitation normally accounts for less than 15% of total annual precipitation. According to the USDA Crop Progress and Condition Report for the week ending March 31st, state-averaged topsoil moisture rated 80% short to very short and subsoil moisture 96% short to very short. Winter wheat across the state rated 49% poor to very poor, 41% fair and only 10% good. Hay and forage supplies checked in 59% short to very short, and stock water supplies were 36% short to very short and 63% adequate.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail was reported in Alliance.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The drought that began in June 2012 lingered with Exceptional Drought (D4) conditions continuing into March. There was some localized improvements in portions of Sheridan, Cherry and Deuel counties as sufficient moisture resulted in a downgrade to Extreme Drought (D3) conditions by the end of the month. A combination of rain and snowfall during the month provided some relief, however the a large percentage of the area received less than 75 percent of the normal precipitation of 1.5 to 2 inches in March.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The drought that began in June 2012 lingered with Exceptional Drought (D4) conditions. Observed precipitation ranged from a quarter inch to 1 inch and was below the normal 1.5 inches for the month. The precipitation helped the parched ground, yet insufficient to reduce the overall impact of the ongoing drought. Some improvement in the drought was seen in far northeast Sheridan County, where by the months end Extreme Drought (D3) conditions were observed. The drought continued into April.

Farther southeast, even with temperatures in the 30s and 40s, severe thunderstorms developed during the evening that produced even larger hail.  One storm that moved across the western sections of the Omaha metro caused extensive damage as it dropped quarter to golf ball-size and even larger stones that covered the ground.  The freezing rain and sleet caused some power outages in northeast Nebraska along with the cancellation of school on the 10th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The hail was mostly pea-size, but some stones reached quarter-size.  The hail covered the ground and was accompanied by freezing rain and sleet with temperatures below freezing.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very atypical early spring storm brought a mix of winter weather and severe thunderstorms to eastern Nebraska April 9th and 10th.  An Arctic cold front pushed across the area early on April 9th bringing subfreezing temperatures to northeast Nebraska.  Meanwhile, a large upper level low pressure system tracking northeast out of the southern Rockies fed warmer and unstable air across the area above this shallow cold air. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to half-dollar size was reported near Cornlea.  With temperatures below freezing, the hail was accompanied by freezing rain and sleet.

A vigorous Pacific low pressure system brought widespread heavy snowfall and northerly winds up to 50 mph as it moved across the central Rockies. Storm total snow accumulations up to two feet were observed across the western Nebraska Panhandle.  The combination of falling snow and strong winds produced considerable drifting snow, which resulted in very low visibilities and snow packed roads. Numerous highways, including Interstate 80 from Sidney to the Wyoming border were closed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An observer in Scottsbluff measured 10.7 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving Pacific low pressure system produced moderate to heavy snowfall across the western Nebraska Panhandle.  East to northeast winds of 35 to 45 mph produced blowing and drifting snow and very low visibilities.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An observer 39 miles west-southwest of Chadron measured seven inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions improved or ended over much of eastern Nebraska during April as precipitation of 3.5 to around 5 inches fell over most of the area.  These amounts were around 1 to over 2.5 inches above normal and coincided with below normal temperatures and reduced evaporation.  Thus no counties in eastern Nebraska maintained Exceptional Drought intensity with northeast Nebraska, along and northwest of a line from Columbus to Wayne, improving into the Extreme Category.  The rest of eastern Nebraska had improved to Severe or Moderate Intensities, with counties in the latter category generally south through east of Lincoln.  The drought in northeast Nebraska persisted into May.

The second official blizzard of the season in South Central Nebraska pounded much of the southeast half of the area on this Sunday, dropping generally 3-9 inches of snow whipped by strong sustained north-northwest winds of 25-35 MPH that frequently gusted over 40 MPH. The vast majority of snow focused east of a line from near Fullerton-Hastings-Franklin, with the heart of the worst blizzard conditions centered through Hamilton, York, Clay, Fillmore, Nuckolls and Thayer counties. Interstate 80 was closed for several hours from Grand Island to Lincoln. In sharp contrast, and thanks to an extremely sharp divide in snow bands, most locations west of the Fullerton-Hastings-Franklin line hardly observed any snow at all. Snow first started falling in southwest-to-northeast bands around midnight, after transitioning from a rain/sleet mix that fell on the evening of the 9th. By sunrise, 1-3 inches had fallen across much of the affected area, with higher amounts to around 5 inches reported near Superior. Blizzard conditions with visibility of around 1/4 mile or less were present from the start in many areas, as Hastings airport measured one of the highest known wind gusts of the event at 54 MPH around 5 AM CDT. By the mid-morning hours, conditions had further deteriorated as a particularly intense snow band roughly 20 miles wide persisted along an axis from York to Nuckolls counties. By the early-mid afternoon hours, banding was not quite as intense, but a broad area of light to moderate snow continued. During the late afternoon and evening hours, falling snow tapered off to flurries and eventually ended from west to east. However, at least minor blowing snow issues continued well into the night as sustained winds slowly eased below 20 MPH. ||Per NWS cooperative observers, a few of the highest storm-total snow amounts included 9 near Shickley, 8.1 near Superior, 8 at Geneva and 6 in or near Aurora, Bruning and Hubbell. A bit farther west, both Hastings and Grand Island set or tied daily snowfall records for March 10th with 5.3 and 3.5, respectively. As evidence of the sharp west edge of the snowfall area, places such as Kearney airport and Minden only reported 0.2 and a non-measurable trace, respectively. On the synoptic scale aloft, this event was powered by the passage of a highly amplified and slightly positively tilted mid-upper trough that slowly lumbered across the Central United States. At 700 millibars, the heaviest snow bands concentrated under a pronounced deformation zone on the northwest periphery of a closed low/vort max that tracked from southwest Kansas on the evening of the 9th to southern Iowa by late evening on the 10th. At mid-afternoon on the 10th, surface maps confirmed that strong north winds and blizzard conditions were promoted by a tight pressure gradient between a 1004 millibar low over north central Missouri and a 1022 millibar ridge extending southward into northwest Nebraska. Enhancing the March misery across the local area were temperatures averaging in the low-mid 20s, yielding wind chill values generally 4-12 degrees above zero.

The drought that began in June 2012 lingered with Exceptional Drought (D4) conditions continuing into March. There was some localized improvements in portions of Sheridan, Cherry and Deuel counties as sufficient moisture resulted in a downgrade to Extreme Drought (D3) conditions by the end of the month. A combination of rain and snowfall during the month provided some relief, however the a large percentage of the area received less than 75 percent of the normal precipitation of 1.5 to 2 inches in March.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The drought that began in June 2012 lingered with Exceptional Drought (D4) conditions. Observed precipitation ranged from a half to 1 inch and was below the normal 2 inches for the month. The precipitation helped the parched ground, yet insufficient to reduce the overall impact of the ongoing drought. The drought continued into April.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm tracked northeast across western and north central Nebraska to produce areas of heavy snow and blowing snow on March 9th.  The snow began during the morning hours and persisted through through the afternoon hours. Snowfall amounts ranged from 3 to 8 inches with locally higher amounts to around 12 inches extending from western portions of southwest Nebraska, northward into Cherry County in north central Nebraska. North winds of 25 to 35 mph with gusts to around 40 mph occurred creating considerable blowing and drifting and reduced visibilities to a quarter mile or less at times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts of 5 to 9 inches fell across Arthur County. The snow combined with strong north winds of 25 to 35 mph with gusts to 40 mph to create considerable blowing and drifting with reduced visibility to a quarter mile or less at times.

The first severe-criteria hail of 2013 within South Central Nebraska occurred on this Friday evening, with reports of stones up to quarter size in the Beaver City and Edison areas of Furnas County. Scattered strong to severe storms initially developed in western Nebraska and northwest Kansas during the late afternoon. Between 8 PM and 10 PM CDT, a cluster of multicell storms first entered the local area, tracking east-southeast primarily through Dawson, Gosper and Furnas counties. Following the Furnas county hail reports around 930 PM CDT, the intensity of convection steadily declined as the strongest storms consolidated into a small quasi-linear complex that continued eastward mainly along and south of the Highway 6 corridor for the next several hours. Meanwhile, a shield of weaker storms affected most areas north of Highway 6. Eventually, between 2-3 AM CDT on the 30th, the vast majority of lingering non-severe activity exited South Central Nebraska to the east of the Highway 81 corridor. On the synoptic scale aloft, this event was triggered by a low amplitude shortwave trough that tracked from Wyoming into Nebraska on west-northwest flow aloft. At the surface, southerly breezes prevailed across South Central Nebraska during the afternoon and evening, to the east of a north-south oriented lee trough in the Nebraska Panhandle. Although these breezes only ushered in modest surface dewpoints into the 40s, the combination of elevated CAPE values around 500 J/kg and 0-6 kilometer deep layer wind shear of 30-40 knots proved sufficient to generate marginally severe storms. This convection was sustained well after dark along the axis of a 30-40 knot low level jet evident at 850 millibars.

An isolated thunderstorm developed over eastern Keith County and intensified while moving east into central Lincoln County. The storm produced hail up to golf ball size and the first tornadoes of the season.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 1702CST, a weak tornado touched down in harvested corn field west of the Sutherland Reservoir. The tornado moved southeast across the road and railroad tracks before dissipating in a wheat field. No damage was observed. Average path width was 25 yards.

One storm that moved across the western sections of the Omaha metro caused extensive damage as it dropped quarter to golf ball-size and even larger stones that covered the ground.  The freezing rain and sleet caused some power outages in northeast Nebraska along with the cancellation of school on the 10th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to 1.5 inches in diameter fell for around 10 minutes in the Wayne area.  With temperatures below freezing, the hail was accompanied by freezing rain and sleet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions continued from January with southwest Nebraska rated as being in Exceptional (D4) Drought.  February precipitation ranged from 75-125% of normal across Dundy, Hitchcock and Red Willow counties.

Drought conditions continued from January with southwest Nebraska rated as being in Exceptional (D4) Drought.  February precipitation ranged from 75-125% of normal across Dundy, Hitchcock and Red Willow counties.

A vigorous Pacific low pressure system brought widespread heavy snowfall and northerly winds up to 50 mph as it moved across the central Rockies. Storm total snow accumulations up to two feet were observed across the western Nebraska Panhandle.  The combination of falling snow and strong winds produced considerable drifting snow, which resulted in very low visibilities and snow packed roads. Numerous highways, including Interstate 80 from Sidney to the Wyoming border were closed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An observer in Gurley measured 10 inches of snow with three to four foot drifts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A passing upper level disturbance interacted with a moist and unstable environment which produced numerous severe thunderstorms with large hail, funnel clouds and a few tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weak tornado touched down in open country south of State Highway 71 and lasted five minutes before it lifted.  No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A passing upper level disturbance interacted with a moist and unstable environment which produced numerous severe thunderstorms with large hail, funnel clouds and a few tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weak tornado was on the ground for five minutes before it lifted.  The observer was looking west from State Highway 71.  No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: According to the U.S. Drought Monitor, February marked the eighth consecutive month of severe (or worse) drought across South Central Nebraska. This continued one of the worst droughts since the 1950s, despite above normal February precipitation across most of the 24-county area. Based on Drought Monitor weekly issuances, there was hardly any change in drought classification during the month. Category D4 exceptional drought (the worst possible category) remained firmly in place across all or part of 14 counties, primarily those north of Interstate 80 and also west of Highway 183. Meanwhile, category D3 extreme drought persisted across most of the other 10 counties, with only a small sliver of category D2 severe drought depicted at month's end across far southern portions of Webster, Nuckolls and Thayer counties. Based on reports from around 50 NWS cooperative observers, and also incorporating radar-derived estimation, roughly 90 percent of the area observed above normal February precipitation (based on normals generally between 0.50-0.75). Of greater significance, roughly 20 percent of the area measured at least twice-normal February precipitation. The limited areas that observed slightly below normal precipitation during the month were mainly concentrated in far eastern counties near Highway 81. For most places, the majority of precipitation fell during a major winter storm that began during the daytime hours on the 20th and peaked in intensity that night and into the day of the 21st. This storm dumped at least 6-10 of snow across most of the area, and higher amounts of 10-14 roughly southwest of a line from Gothenburg-Nelson. Per NWS cooperative observers, a few of the highest February precipitation totals included 1.59 at Ord, 1.46 at Cambridge and 1.45 at Holdrege and also eight miles south of Elwood. Leading the drier side of things were sites such as three miles west of Gresham (0.30), three miles northeast of Shelby (0.31) and Geneva (0.44). ||Although severe to exceptional drought conditions remained firmly entrenched across the 30-county area, prevailing above normal precipitation during the meteorological winter season of December-February at least put a small dent in the dryness heading into the spring. However, keeping optimism in check, precipitation during the meteorological winter season of December-February normally accounts for less than 10 percent of total annual precipitation. The February USDA Nebraska Weather and Crops report indicated little change since January, with 50% of the state-averaged wheat crop still in poor to very poor condition, and only 12% in good condition. Hay and forage supplies across the state rated 49% short to very short, well-below values from one year ago.
