A very atypical early spring storm brought a mix of winter weather and severe thunderstorms to eastern Nebraska April 9th and 10th.  An Arctic cold front pushed across the area early on April 9th bringing subfreezing temperatures to northeast Nebraska.  Meanwhile, a large upper level low pressure system tracking northeast out of the southern Rockies fed warmer and unstable air across the area above this shallow cold air.  A mix of freezing rain and snow spread over northeast Nebraska during the morning of the 9th and by afternoon scattered thunderstorms developed and moved across that area.  These storms intensified the freezing rain, mixing at times with heavy sleet and in some cases even large hail.  Farther southeast, even with temperatures in the 30s and 40s, severe thunderstorms developed during the evening that produced even larger hail.  One storm that moved across the western sections of the Omaha metro caused extensive damage as it dropped quarter to golf ball-size and even larger stones that covered the ground.  The freezing rain and sleet caused some power outages in northeast Nebraska along with the cancellation of school on the 10th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: With temperatures below freezing, the hail was accompanied by freezing rain and sleet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous Pacific low pressure system brought widespread heavy snowfall and northerly winds up to 50 mph as it moved across the central Rockies. Storm total snow accumulations up to two feet were observed across the western Nebraska Panhandle.  The combination of falling snow and strong winds produced considerable drifting snow, which resulted in very low visibilities and snow packed roads. Numerous highways, including Interstate 80 from Sidney to the Wyoming border were closed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An observer in Hemingford measured 16 inches of snow with four to six foot drifts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Late afternoon and early evening thunderstorms over portions of the western Nebraska Panhandle produced large hail, damaging winds, a few tornadoes, and a funnel cloud.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud was reported eight miles north of Mitchell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Late afternoon and early evening thunderstorms over portions of the western Nebraska Panhandle produced large hail, damaging winds, a few tornadoes, and a funnel cloud.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weak tornado touched down in open country 10 miles north-northeast of Mitchell.  The tornado moved east-southeast.  It lifted briefly before crossing State Highway 71 and lowered east of the highway.  The tornado dissipated about four miles north of Lake Alice.  No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Late afternoon and early evening thunderstorms over portions of the western Nebraska Panhandle produced large hail, damaging winds, a few tornadoes, and a funnel cloud.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The supercell thunderstorm that spawned the EF0 tornado in southern Sioux County moved east into southwest Box Butte County.  Another EF0 tornado developed about 14 miles northeast of Lake Alice and touched down a few times in open country before dissipating.  No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: According to the U.S. Drought Monitor, February marked the eighth consecutive month of severe (or worse) drought across South Central Nebraska. This continued one of the worst droughts since the 1950s, despite above normal February precipitation across most of the 24-county area. Based on Drought Monitor weekly issuances, there was hardly any change in drought classification during the month. Category D4 exceptional drought (the worst possible category) remained firmly in place across all or part of 14 counties, primarily those north of Interstate 80 and also west of Highway 183. Meanwhile, category D3 extreme drought persisted across most of the other 10 counties, with only a small sliver of category D2 severe drought depicted at month's end across far southern portions of Webster, Nuckolls and Thayer counties. Based on reports from around 50 NWS cooperative observers, and also incorporating radar-derived estimation, roughly 90 percent of the area observed above normal February precipitation (based on normals generally between 0.50-0.75). Of greater significance, roughly 20 percent of the area measured at least twice-normal February precipitation. The limited areas that observed slightly below normal precipitation during the month were mainly concentrated in far eastern counties near Highway 81. For most places, the majority of precipitation fell during a major winter storm that began during the daytime hours on the 20th and peaked in intensity that night and into the day of the 21st. This storm dumped at least 6-10 of snow across most of the area, and higher amounts of 10-14 roughly southwest of a line from Gothenburg-Nelson. Per NWS cooperative observers, a few of the highest February precipitation totals included 1.59 at Ord, 1.46 at Cambridge and 1.45 at Holdrege and also eight miles south of Elwood. Leading the drier side of things were sites such as three miles west of Gresham (0.30), three miles northeast of Shelby (0.31) and Geneva (0.44). ||Although severe to exceptional drought conditions remained firmly entrenched across the 30-county area, prevailing above normal precipitation during the meteorological winter season of December-February at least put a small dent in the dryness heading into the spring. However, keeping optimism in check, precipitation during the meteorological winter season of December-February normally accounts for less than 10 percent of total annual precipitation. The February USDA Nebraska Weather and Crops report indicated little change since January, with 50% of the state-averaged wheat crop still in poor to very poor condition, and only 12% in good condition. Hay and forage supplies across the state rated 49% short to very short, well-below values from one year ago.

Hay and forage supplies across the state rated 49% short to very short, well-below values from one year ago.

During a roughly 30-hour period starting on Wednesday the 20th, but peaking in intensity on Thursday the 21st, the majority of the 24-county area experienced its heaviest snowfall of the 2012-2013 winter season. For some locations near the Kansas border this was actually one of the largest storm-total snowfalls on record. This included Red Cloud, where 13.5 inches ranked as the sixth-highest two-day total since 1894. According to NWS cooperative observers, essentially the entire area totaled at least 6-10, with the highest amounts of 10-14 concentrated in southwestern counties generally south of a line from Cozad-Holdrege-Nelson. In addition to 13.5 at Red Cloud, a few of the biggest totals included 14 at Harlan County Lake and Beaver City, 13.5 eight miles south of Elwood, 12.5 at Naponee and Nelson, and 12 near Blue Hill and Upland. The overall lowest amounts affected eastern areas near the Highway 81 corridor, including 6.5 at Osceola, 6 near York and 7 at Geneva and Bruning. In between these lowest and highest extremes were places such as Grand Island and Holdrege (10), and Hastings (9.5). Although the heavy snow itself created considerable impact across the region with very poor visibilities and at least minor drifting, the magnitude of blowing snow was held in check by sustained easterly to northerly winds that generally topped out around 20 MPH and mainly gusted under 25 MPH, keeping this storm from reaching blizzard status. Liquid-equivalent moisture between one-half inch and one inch was common, and also welcome across the drought-stricken area, with locally higher totals including 1.25 at Red Cloud. ||Although accumulating snow fell off-and-on for roughly 30 hours across all of South Central Nebraska, the timing of the most intense snowfall varied. For most places, the first light accumulations got underway between the mid-morning and late afternoon hours on the 20th. Then from the evening of the 20th into the pre-dawn hours of the 21st, a heavier snow band affected primarily southwestern counties. By sunrise on the 21st, southern and western locations such as Cozad, Cambridge and Red Cloud had already totaled 5-8, while the majority of South Central Nebraska reported no more than 1-3. That changed in a big way during the daytime hours on the 21st, as the most intense snow bands of the event blossomed across the area, accompanied by steady northeast winds and temperatures generally hovering between 18-22 degrees. These snow bands gradually weakened and dissipated from south-to-north during the evening, with most of South Central Nebraska void of falling snow by 9 PM as a closed mid level low pressure system lifted farther north. On the synoptic scale aloft, this prolonged winter storm was forced by the approach and eventual passage of an expansive, amplified mid-upper level trough. The primary mid-level 700-500 millibar closed low tracked from southern California on the morning of the 20th, to the Texas Panhandle on the morning of the 21st, before deepening and lifting slowly north-northeast across central Kansas into southeast Nebraska during the daytime and evening hours. Periods of isentropic lift associated with smaller-scale shortwave troughs lifting out ahead of the main system were responsible for snowfall from the 20th into the morning of the 21st, with the most intense snow bands during the day on the 21st driven by pronounced deformation on the northwest periphery of the mid level low track. From a forecast perspective, although northern and eastern portions of South Central Nebraska did not receive quite as much snow as originally expected, this winter storm was overall one of the best-anticipated in recent memory thanks to unusually high consistency in computer model solutions. Although the finer details of timing and full snowfall potential did not come into focus until a few days out, signs of a potentially significant winter storm were recognized over one week in advance.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm total of 14 inches fell at Harlan County Lake and 11.5 inches of snow fell at Alma.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Precipitation did arrive in April in the form of a mixture of rain, snow and thunderstorms. The north central received the higher totals, where up to 5 inches was recorded during the month. The drought that began in June 2012 has lingered for 10 months and Exceptional (D4) conditions since October. By April 30th twelve counties retained D4 conditions with McPherson County receiving the least rainfall and no change to drought severity. The drought continued into May.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Precipitation in April helped ease the Exceptional (D4) drought conditions that began in June of 2012.  The county received 2 to 4 inches of precipitation that helped improve conditions to Extreme (D3) drought conditions by the end of the month. The drought continued into May.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A passing upper level disturbance interacted with a moist and unstable environment and produced scattered strong to severe thunderstorms during the afternoon and early evening hours in the western Nebraska Panhandle.  There were reports of large hail, funnel clouds, and a brief tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Chadron warning point relayed 15 reports of funnel clouds north of Crawford.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed along a stationary boundary near the Kansas and Nebraska border on the afternoon of May 27th. Due to high amounts of instability and shear in the atmosphere these thunderstorms quickly became severe. The thunderstorms moved east over southern Nebraska, moving into southeast Nebraska by early evening. Although other isolated severe weather was reported, one particular storm moving through Jefferson and Gage Counties produced the majority of the severe weather. The storm complex exited southeast Nebraska prior to midnight producing severe weather around the Falls City Nebraska area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported winds gusts to near 60 mph in the city of Beatrice. This resulted in downed tree branches in the city of Beatrice.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions continued improving across eastern Nebraska in May eventually ending or upgrading into the Moderate or Abnormally Dry.  Widespread rainfall of 5 to 8 inches fell over a large part of eastern Nebraska during the month, in some cases at least several inches above normal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifted north across southwest Nebraska and was the focus for thunderstorms to develop, intensify and produce large hail and damaging winds. Frontier County was hit the hardest by damaging winds estimated up to 80 mph as a thunderstorm bowed out. The high winds produced damage at 12 farmsteads. The most extensive damage occurred just north of County Road 741, where two large buildings and nine silos were destroyed. The wind damage included over 60 center pivot irrigation systems, livestock killed, large tree limbs down and approximately 40 power poles snapped. The McCook Public Power District reported 173 consumers lost power during the storm.

Frontier County was hit the hardest by damaging winds estimated up to 80 mph as a thunderstorm bowed out. The high winds produced damage at 12 farmsteads. The most extensive damage occurred just north of County Road 741, where two large buildings and nine silos were destroyed. The wind damage included over 60 center pivot irrigation systems, livestock killed, large tree limbs down and approximately 40 power poles snapped. The McCook Public Power District reported 173 consumers lost power during the storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pasture fences and gates blown down.

Precipitation did arrive in April in the form of a mixture of rain, snow and thunderstorms. The north central received the higher totals, where up to 5 inches was recorded during the month. The drought that began in June 2012 has lingered for 10 months and Exceptional (D4) conditions since October. By April 30th twelve counties retained D4 conditions with McPherson County receiving the least rainfall and no change to drought severity. The drought continued into May.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Precipitation in April helped ease the Exceptional (D4) drought conditions that began in June of 2012.  The county received 3 to 4 inches of precipitation that helped reduce the drought conditions with Extreme (D3) conditions throughout by end of the month. The drought continued into May.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed along a stationary boundary near the Kansas and Nebraska border on the afternoon of May 27th. Due to high amounts of instability and shear in the atmosphere these thunderstorms quickly became severe. The thunderstorms moved east over southern Nebraska, moving into southeast Nebraska by early evening. Although other isolated severe weather was reported, one particular storm moving through Jefferson and Gage Counties produced the majority of the severe weather. The storm complex exited southeast Nebraska prior to midnight producing severe weather around the Falls City Nebraska area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines were reported downed by strong thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Nebraska was on the cool side of the primary polar front on this Tuesday evening. Numerous elevated showers, and a few thunderstorms, formed and expanded over central and eastern Nebraska, during the evening hours. This occurred as the low and mid-level atmosphere adjusted to an approaching short-wave trough over Idaho and Utah. Frontogenesis and warm air advection resulted in destabilization. The strongest storm formed over northern Adams county and grew into a short line segment that tracked east across Hamilton and York counties. While these storms were strongest, they produced several reports of sub-severe hail. Surface and mean-layer parcels were stable, but most-unstable CAPE was around 1000 J/kg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Extreme to exceptional drought abated slowly to the severe to extreme category in extreme northeast Nebraska during April. Near to a little above normal precipitation was not enough to end the long standing drought entirely, given the long term dry soil conditions that carried over into the month. Accordingly, the area was still deemed very sensitive to the potential of below normal rainfall during the planting and growing seasons. The decrease in the drought magnitude was tempered because the area was along the Missouri River in which these were continued low flows and low runoff expectations, with some of the area still having extreme drought. No new effects of the drought were noted during the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Long term dry soil conditions at the start of April abated a little during the month, with extreme to exceptional drought becoming severe to extreme. The improvement was due to normal to above normal precipitation, although low Missouri River flows tempered the improvement. No new effects were noted, but the area remained sensitive to possible worsening again of conditions, depending on precipitation during the planting and growing seasons.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Early evening thunderstorms produced large hail and strong winds over portions of the southwest Nebraska Panhandle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blowing dust reduced visibility to less than a mile at the Wildcat Hills State Recreation Area.  Wind gusts were estimated between 50 and 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The second official blizzard of the season in South Central Nebraska pounded much of the southeast half of the area on this Sunday, dropping generally 3-9 inches of snow whipped by strong sustained north-northwest winds of 25-35 MPH that frequently gusted over 40 MPH. The vast majority of snow focused east of a line from near Fullerton-Hastings-Franklin, with the heart of the worst blizzard conditions centered through Hamilton, York, Clay, Fillmore, Nuckolls and Thayer counties. Interstate 80 was closed for several hours from Grand Island to Lincoln. In sharp contrast, and thanks to an extremely sharp divide in snow bands, most locations west of the Fullerton-Hastings-Franklin line hardly observed any snow at all. Snow first started falling in southwest-to-northeast bands around midnight, after transitioning from a rain/sleet mix that fell on the evening of the 9th. By sunrise, 1-3 inches had fallen across much of the affected area, with higher amounts to around 5 inches reported near Superior. Blizzard conditions with visibility of around 1/4 mile or less were present from the start in many areas, as Hastings airport measured one of the highest known wind gusts of the event at 54 MPH around 5 AM CDT. By the mid-morning hours, conditions had further deteriorated as a particularly intense snow band roughly 20 miles wide persisted along an axis from York to Nuckolls counties. By the early-mid afternoon hours, banding was not quite as intense, but a broad area of light to moderate snow continued. During the late afternoon and evening hours, falling snow tapered off to flurries and eventually ended from west to east. However, at least minor blowing snow issues continued well into the night as sustained winds slowly eased below 20 MPH. ||Per NWS cooperative observers, a few of the highest storm-total snow amounts included 9 near Shickley, 8.1 near Superior, 8 at Geneva and 6 in or near Aurora, Bruning and Hubbell. A bit farther west, both Hastings and Grand Island set or tied daily snowfall records for March 10th with 5.3 and 3.5, respectively. As evidence of the sharp west edge of the snowfall area, places such as Kearney airport and Minden only reported 0.2 and a non-measurable trace, respectively. On the synoptic scale aloft, this event was powered by the passage of a highly amplified and slightly positively tilted mid-upper trough that slowly lumbered across the Central United States. At 700 millibars, the heaviest snow bands concentrated under a pronounced deformation zone on the northwest periphery of a closed low/vort max that tracked from southwest Kansas on the evening of the 9th to southern Iowa by late evening on the 10th. At mid-afternoon on the 10th, surface maps confirmed that strong north winds and blizzard conditions were promoted by a tight pressure gradient between a 1004 millibar low over north central Missouri and a 1022 millibar ridge extending southward into northwest Nebraska. Enhancing the March misery across the local area were temperatures averaging in the low-mid 20s, yielding wind chill values generally 4-12 degrees above zero.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Nebraska was on the cool side of the primary polar front on this Tuesday evening. Numerous elevated showers, and a few thunderstorms, formed and expanded over central and eastern Nebraska, during the evening hours. This was the first event of an unsettled weather pattern that set up over the central Plains for the last several days in May. Warm moist air was returning to eastern Nebraska as a warm front moved north into the area. Temperatures climbed into the 80s and dew points increased into the 60s by late afternoon over central Nebraska. This led to increasing instability, and eventually scattered thunderstorms by early evening. Thunderstorms initially developed over far northeast Nebraska during the evening hours, but as more thunderstorms moved into northeast Nebraska late in the evening, a forward propagating complex developed and moved southeast across eastern Nebraska eventually exiting into northeast Kansas and southwest Iowa. Initially isolated large hail fell, but as the line of thunderstorms developed, sporadic wind damage was produced over parts of northeast and east central Nebraska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A measured gust of 77 mph was recorded at the Fremont Airport. As these winds moved through the city tree and power line damage was scattered throughout the city.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This was the the final severe weather event in a series of occurrences that were associated with a seasonably deep upper level low pressure over the northern Plains. Instability increased during the afternoon along and ahead of a stationary front across eastern Nebraska from low pressure over the Dakotas. Isolated to scattered thunderstorms developed in the late afternoon and tracked across eastern Nebraska and into western Iowa during the evening hours. Although isolated reports of damaging winds were reported, most of the events were large hail, some of which was significant.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The second official blizzard of the season in South Central Nebraska pounded much of the southeast half of the area on this Sunday, dropping generally 3-9 inches of snow whipped by strong sustained north-northwest winds of 25-35 MPH that frequently gusted over 40 MPH. The vast majority of snow focused east of a line from near Fullerton-Hastings-Franklin, with the heart of the worst blizzard conditions centered through Hamilton, York, Clay, Fillmore, Nuckolls and Thayer counties. Interstate 80 was closed for several hours from Grand Island to Lincoln. In sharp contrast, and thanks to an extremely sharp divide in snow bands, most locations west of the Fullerton-Hastings-Franklin line hardly observed any snow at all. Snow first started falling in southwest-to-northeast bands around midnight, after transitioning from a rain/sleet mix that fell on the evening of the 9th. By sunrise, 1-3 inches had fallen across much of the affected area, with higher amounts to around 5 inches reported near Superior. Blizzard conditions with visibility of around 1/4 mile or less were present from the start in many areas, as Hastings airport measured one of the highest known wind gusts of the event at 54 MPH around 5 AM CDT. By the mid-morning hours, conditions had further deteriorated as a particularly intense snow band roughly 20 miles wide persisted along an axis from York to Nuckolls counties. By the early-mid afternoon hours, banding was not quite as intense, but a broad area of light to moderate snow continued. During the late afternoon and evening hours, falling snow tapered off to flurries and eventually ended from west to east. However, at least minor blowing snow issues continued well into the night as sustained winds slowly eased below 20 MPH. ||Per NWS cooperative observers, a few of the highest storm-total snow amounts included 9 near Shickley, 8.1 near Superior, 8 at Geneva and 6 in or near Aurora, Bruning and Hubbell. A bit farther west, both Hastings and Grand Island set or tied daily snowfall records for March 10th with 5.3 and 3.5, respectively. As evidence of the sharp west edge of the snowfall area, places such as Kearney airport and Minden only reported 0.2 and a non-measurable trace, respectively. On the synoptic scale aloft, this event was powered by the passage of a highly amplified and slightly positively tilted mid-upper trough that slowly lumbered across the Central United States. At 700 millibars, the heaviest snow bands concentrated under a pronounced deformation zone on the northwest periphery of a closed low/vort max that tracked from southwest Kansas on the evening of the 9th to southern Iowa by late evening on the 10th. At mid-afternoon on the 10th, surface maps confirmed that strong north winds and blizzard conditions were promoted by a tight pressure gradient between a 1004 millibar low over north central Missouri and a 1022 millibar ridge extending southward into northwest Nebraska. Enhancing the March misery across the local area were temperatures averaging in the low-mid 20s, yielding wind chill values generally 4-12 degrees above zero.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Sub-severe hail fell from one of numerous elevated thunderstorms that formed in a strong pulse of warm air advection and frontogenesis this Tuesday night. A shallow continental-polar air mass was in place over the northern two-thirds of the United States. With a deep upper-level trough, over the western U.S., moisture and warm air advection were occurring aloft and there was enough instability to initiate thunderstorms. One of the more significant reports was from the town of Fairmont, in Fillmore county, where hail accumulated one inch deep. While surface and mean-layer parcels were stable, the most-unstable parcels resulted in 500-1000 J/kg of CAPE.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This was the first event of an unsettled weather pattern that set up over the central Plains for the last several days in May. Warm moist air was returning to eastern Nebraska as a warm front moved north into the area. Temperatures climbed into the 80s and dew points increased into the 60s by late afternoon over central Nebraska. This led to increasing instability, and eventually scattered thunderstorms by early evening. Thunderstorms initially developed over far northeast Nebraska during the evening hours, but as more thunderstorms moved into northeast Nebraska late in the evening, a forward propagating complex developed and moved southeast across eastern Nebraska eventually exiting into northeast Kansas and southwest Iowa. Initially isolated large hail fell, but as the line of thunderstorms developed, sporadic wind damage was produced over parts of northeast and east central Nebraska.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A unseasonably cold upper level system brought snow, or sleet that changed to snow to much of eastern Nebraska.  The cold air rushed into northeast Nebraska during the morning of May 1st, causing rain to change to snow north of Norfolk before the morning commute.  Otherwise the rain changed to sleet or snow late on May 1st and continued into the early morning hours of May 2nd.  Snow and sleet intensities were enhanced by scattered thunderstorms that moved across the area.  Snowfall totals of 1 to 3 inches fell over most of the region with a swath of 3 to 5 inches reported from near Lincoln and Wahoo northeast through Omaha and Blair.  The snow and sleet created slushy and rutted roads and winds gusting over 20 mph combined with poor visibilities in moderate snow making for a period of difficult driving conditions which caused some vehicles to slide into ditches.

An area of thunderstorms consolidated and strengthened over western Omaha during the mid morning hours of June 24th eventually proving to be the beginning of a Derecho that raced through Iowa and Illinois.  In Nebraska the storm produced wind gusts that were measured at 69 mph by Asos at Omaha Eppley Airfield and produced widespread tree and other damage from west central Omaha into Iowa.  The storms initially formed across the western high plains the previous evening and steadily moved east feeding on modest instability and an increased low level jet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A bowing thunderstorm complex brought fairly widespread tree damage to a large part of Omaha.  Many trees were split or uprooted which caused damage to vehicles and homes and brought power outages to a little over 50,000 OPPD customers.  Damage reports started coming in across west Omaha near 144th or 156th Streets from near Center north to around Blondo with the damage become more widespread from central into north Omaha. Several reports of winds around or higher than 60 mph came in across the city, and the Asos at Omaha Eppley Airfield measured a wind gust of 69 mph at 1000 am CDT.   The strong winds and damage continued into western Iowa.

Precipitation did arrive in April in the form of a mixture of rain, snow and thunderstorms. The north central received the higher totals, where up to 5 inches was recorded during the month. The drought that began in June 2012 has lingered for 10 months and Exceptional (D4) conditions since October. By April 30th twelve counties retained D4 conditions with McPherson County receiving the least rainfall and no change to drought severity. The drought continued into May.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Precipitation in April helped ease the Exceptional (D4) drought conditions that began in June of 2012.  The county received 3 to 4 inches of precipitation that helped improve the soil moisture with Extreme (D3) conditions by the end of the month. The drought continued into May.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This Tuesday afternoon yielded the first tornado of 2013 in the state of Nebraska, in the form of a weak, brief EF0 landspout photographed by several people as it tracked through extreme southeast Buffalo County between Shelton and Interstate 80. The landspout was associated with a narrow line of rain and snow showers containing only a few embedded lightning strikes as it pushed southeastward across the area. This event occurred in an unusually cool and dry environment featuring surface temperatures only in the mid-40s and dewpoints in the low-30s. Despite these negative factors, low and mid-level lapse rates were very steep across the area, with 0-3 kilometer values on the order of 9-10 Celsius/kilometer. These steep lapse rates yielded modest surface-based CAPE on the order of 100-250 J/kg, likely just enough to promote weak tornado development. On the synoptic scale aloft, South Central Nebraska was under fairly strong west-northwest flow in the mid-upper levels featuring subtle shortwave troughs, between a deep longwave trough centered over the northern Great Lakes and a ridge building over the western Unites States.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief landspout tornado was reported just south of Shelton and just north of Interstate 80.  No damage was reported, and top winds were estimated to be between 50 and 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Precipitation did arrive in April in the form of a mixture of rain, snow and thunderstorms. The north central received the higher totals, where up to 5 inches was recorded during the month. The drought that began in June 2012 has lingered for 10 months and Exceptional (D4) conditions since October. By April 30th twelve counties retained D4 conditions with McPherson County receiving the least rainfall and no change to drought severity. The drought continued into May.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Precipitation in April helped ease the Exceptional (D4) drought conditions that began in June of 2012.  The county however received the least with a maximum of an inch in the county, resulting in no change to the drought conditions in the county. The drought continued into May.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions continued in extreme northeast Nebraska in March. Though precipitation was near normal, drought was still rated extreme to exceptional over the area. There continued to be a lack of soil moisture as winter approached its end, since even normal winter precipitation is low compared to the warmer seasons. Also, the frozen ground forced runoff of what precipitation and snowmelt there was. There was little noted in the way of new effects of the drought, with the dry conditions giving a poor outlook for the Spring and Summer, including the winter wheat crop, which was also affected by poor germination conditions in the dry fall. Water restrictions continued to be few because of the low water usage, but the area was deemed vulnerable to even marginally dry weather if it developed in the spring and summer. Some effect was noted on livestock, although the majority of the livestock was in good condition despite persistent feed shortages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Long term dry soil conditions continued in March, with runoff over frozen ground leaving soil moisture conditions very dry, despite near normal precipitation. Few new effects were noted, though the dry conditions coming out of winter were expected to immediately affect the winter wheat crop, as they had affected germination in the fall. Seasonally low water usage resulted in few other new effects. The main problem, aside from winter livestock feed, was the poor outlook for the spring and summer, which were expected to be extremely sensitive to dry weather.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For roughly seven hours on this Monday night and early Tuesday morning, the first widespread severe thunderstorm event of 2013 impacted mainly the northwest two-thirds of the 24-county area, largely focused northwest of a line from Alma-Hastings-Osceola along a very strong cold front advancing southeast through the region. Between 9 PM and 4 AM CDT, several rounds of multicell storms along with a few embedded supercell structures tracked north to northeast across the area, resulting in multiple reports of severe-criteria hail ranging from quarter to golf ball size and damaging winds of at least 60-70 MPH. Some of the most extensive damage occurred in Kearney around 1 AM CDT, due to measured wind gusts up to 76 MPH and hail up to golf ball size. In addition to considerable tree damage within the city, a mobile home was destroyed and several buildings on the University of Nebraska-Kearney campus sustained damage. This included the university library where a portion of the roof was ripped off, resulting in water damage to over 6,000 books. There were also several reports of severe winds and damage in and near Holdrege between 12-1 AM CDT, including a measured 74 MPH gust at the airport, a semi truck blown over on Highway 6/34 a few miles southwest of town and a large metal outbuilding destroyed a few miles northeast of town. Other severe weather reports during the night included quarter size hail at the Hastings NWS office and golf ball size hail in Phillips. In addition to hail and wind, widespread rainfall of 1-3 inches resulted from multiple storms training over the same general southwest-to-northeast corridor, with most 2+ inch amounts concentrated within 15 miles either side of a line extending from near Holdrege-Cairo-Fullerton. A few observers reported totals in excess of 3 inches, including 3.61 near Rockville and 3.41 near Scotia. Although flooding issues were held to a minimum, water was reported over a road near St. Libory. ||The first storms of the evening developed near and north of the Interstate 80 corridor between 9 PM and midnight CDT. Some of these initial storms exhibited low-level rotation on radar, and were likely near-surface based in an environment featuring temperatures in the low-60s and dewpoints in the upper-50s, although no tornadoes were reported. After midnight CDT, storms gradually tended to become more elongated and elevated as they were undercut by the surging cold front, although interactions with this front likely enhanced the damaging winds in the Holdrege and Kearney areas. Around 4 AM CDT, the last severe-warned storm exited the local area eastward out of Polk County, and thereafter only a few weak storms lingered through sunrise. As evidence of the strength of the cold front, 4 AM CDT temperatures across South Central Nebraska ranged from 33 degrees at Ord, to 44 at Hastings, to 66 at Hebron. On the mesoscale, the primary trigger for this long-lasting severe weather episode was pronounced low-to-mid level warm air advection and resultant upglide over the top of the surging surface cold front, which effectively bisected Nebraska from southwest-to-northeast at sunset. After dark, an 850 millibar low level jet ramped up to around 40 knots, resulting in a rapid uptick in convection. Around midnight, most unstable CAPE values averaged around 1000 J/kg in the presence of very strong 0-6 kilometer vertical wind shear of 60-70 knots. On the synoptic scale, this event occurred downstream from an expansive, highly-amplified mid level trough centered near the Four Corners, and in the presence of low-amplitude shortwave troughs ejecting northeast from the parent system into the Central Plains on strengthening southwesterly flow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel size hail was mixed in with a few quarter size hail stones.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed in south central Nebraska during the afternoon of June 14th as an upper level trough over the northern Rockies lifted across the northern plains causing a warm front to move over eastern Nebraska.  The storms intensified as they moved across mainly northeast Nebraska and west central Iowa.  Besides large hail and damaging winds a few of the storms produced heavy rain which caused localized flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to the size of quarters fell at the National Weather Service Office in Valley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated severe storm developed along a surface front situated across southwest Nebraska.

Another burst of late winter visited mainly northern portions of South Central Nebraska on Monday the 22nd, as parts of Valley, Greeley and Nance counties received 1-4 inches of wet snow from a rather quick-moving precipitation shield that passed through the area primarily between 11 AM and 2 PM CDT. Although snow did not last all that long in any given location, it accumulated quickly as some of the highest totals per NWS cooperative observers and NeRAIN observers featured 4.5 nine miles northwest of Ord, 4 at Arcadia and eight miles north-northeast of Scotia, and 3.5 in Ord. Accompanying this falling snow was sustained north winds around 20 MPH and gusting over 30 MPH behind a sharp cold front. While accumulating snow was confined to these far northern counties, temperatures remained just warm enough across the majority of the 24-county area to keep precipitation in the form of a steady, chilly rain. Temperature-wise, this passing front ushered in a very cold few days by late April standards, as Ord airport only registered highs of 39 and 37 degrees, respectively, on the 22nd and 23rd. ||In the mid and upper levels, forcing for this event was provided in part by lift associated with a low-amplitude lead shortwave trough ejecting out ahead of a larger-scale, positively tilted trough centered along an axis from south central Canada to Idaho. Central Nebraska was also positioned under a pronounced area of upper level divergence between the exit region of a 110 knot upper jet streak oriented from northern Colorado into western Kansas, and the entrance region of another jet streak centered from northern Minnesota into Ontario. At the surface, mid-day analysis depicted the leading edge of the aforementioned strong cold front aligned from extreme southwest Kansas into southeast Nebraska, with temperatures across the local frontal zone ranging from 29 degrees at Ord, to 38 in Hastings, to 53 in Hebron.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A total of 4.0 inches of snow fell in Arcadia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong shortwave trough and associated upper level low began to influence the weather conditions, with a stationary front across far northern Nebraska and a dry line across far western Nebraska. Scattered severe thunderstorms focused along the aforementioned boundaries.

For roughly seven hours on this Monday night and early Tuesday morning, the first widespread severe thunderstorm event of 2013 impacted mainly the northwest two-thirds of the 24-county area, largely focused northwest of a line from Alma-Hastings-Osceola along a very strong cold front advancing southeast through the region. Between 9 PM and 4 AM CDT, several rounds of multicell storms along with a few embedded supercell structures tracked north to northeast across the area, resulting in multiple reports of severe-criteria hail ranging from quarter to golf ball size and damaging winds of at least 60-70 MPH. Some of the most extensive damage occurred in Kearney around 1 AM CDT, due to measured wind gusts up to 76 MPH and hail up to golf ball size. In addition to considerable tree damage within the city, a mobile home was destroyed and several buildings on the University of Nebraska-Kearney campus sustained damage. This included the university library where a portion of the roof was ripped off, resulting in water damage to over 6,000 books. There were also several reports of severe winds and damage in and near Holdrege between 12-1 AM CDT, including a measured 74 MPH gust at the airport, a semi truck blown over on Highway 6/34 a few miles southwest of town and a large metal outbuilding destroyed a few miles northeast of town. Other severe weather reports during the night included quarter size hail at the Hastings NWS office and golf ball size hail in Phillips. In addition to hail and wind, widespread rainfall of 1-3 inches resulted from multiple storms training over the same general southwest-to-northeast corridor, with most 2+ inch amounts concentrated within 15 miles either side of a line extending from near Holdrege-Cairo-Fullerton. A few observers reported totals in excess of 3 inches, including 3.61 near Rockville and 3.41 near Scotia. Although flooding issues were held to a minimum, water was reported over a road near St. Libory. ||The first storms of the evening developed near and north of the Interstate 80 corridor between 9 PM and midnight CDT. Some of these initial storms exhibited low-level rotation on radar, and were likely near-surface based in an environment featuring temperatures in the low-60s and dewpoints in the upper-50s, although no tornadoes were reported. After midnight CDT, storms gradually tended to become more elongated and elevated as they were undercut by the surging cold front, although interactions with this front likely enhanced the damaging winds in the Holdrege and Kearney areas. Around 4 AM CDT, the last severe-warned storm exited the local area eastward out of Polk County, and thereafter only a few weak storms lingered through sunrise. As evidence of the strength of the cold front, 4 AM CDT temperatures across South Central Nebraska ranged from 33 degrees at Ord, to 44 at Hastings, to 66 at Hebron. On the mesoscale, the primary trigger for this long-lasting severe weather episode was pronounced low-to-mid level warm air advection and resultant upglide over the top of the surging surface cold front, which effectively bisected Nebraska from southwest-to-northeast at sunset. After dark, an 850 millibar low level jet ramped up to around 40 knots, resulting in a rapid uptick in convection. Around midnight, most unstable CAPE values averaged around 1000 J/kg in the presence of very strong 0-6 kilometer vertical wind shear of 60-70 knots. On the synoptic scale, this event occurred downstream from an expansive, highly-amplified mid level trough centered near the Four Corners, and in the presence of low-amplitude shortwave troughs ejecting northeast from the parent system into the Central Plains on strengthening southwesterly flow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This gust was measured from a home weather station.

Between 730-815 pm CDT on this Saturday evening, a severe multicell storm tracked northeast through western portions of Dawson County, pelting areas in and near Gothenburg with large hail ranging from quarter to baseball size. In Gothenburg itself, hail up to golf ball size was reported, with the baseball size report originating seven miles northeast of town before the storm slipped out of the local area into Custer County. This hail storm was by far the most notable of a handful of multicell storms that affected western portions of South Central Nebraska mainly between 730-1030 pm CDT. Prior to entering the local area, the initial development of strong to severe convection took place during the mid-late afternoon hours near the Nebraska-Kansas-Colorado border before tracking east-northeast. After darkness fell, the South Central Nebraska storms steadily faded away, as most activity ended up confined to Dawson, Gosper, Furnas, Harlan and Sherman counties. ||On the large scale aloft, this event occurred under fairly modest quasi-zonal flow, as South Central Nebraska remained well-removed from stronger flow associated with broad longwave troughs centered near both the northwest and northeast corners of the United States. However, the presence of subtle, low-amplitude shortwave troughs passing through the flow helped ignite strong to severe convection in an environment featuring steep low to mid level lapse rates, around 2500 J/kg mixed-layer CAPE, and around 35 knots of 0-6 kilometer deep layer wind shear. At the surface, late afternoon and evening dewpoints in the Dawson County area averaged in the low-mid 60s, as steady south-southeast breezes prevailed to the east of a High Plains lee trough and south of a synoptic warm front aligned from western South Dakota into far eastern Nebraska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail ranging in size from quarters to golf balls was reported in Gothenburg.

Thunderstorms developed in south central Nebraska during the afternoon of June 14th as an upper level trough over the northern Rockies lifted across the northern plains causing a warm front to move over eastern Nebraska.  The storms intensified as they moved across mainly northeast Nebraska and west central Iowa.  Besides large hail and damaging winds a few of the storms produced heavy rain which caused localized flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On the south side of the city of Fremont there were a couple of large entire trees down, while in the city of Fremont there some more isolated 3 to 5 inch limbs down.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust to around 60 mph was reported. This resulted in scattered tree damage across town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This was the the final severe weather event in a series of occurrences that were associated with a seasonably deep upper level low pressure over the northern Plains. Instability increased during the afternoon along and ahead of a stationary front across eastern Nebraska from low pressure over the Dakotas. Isolated to scattered thunderstorms developed in the late afternoon and tracked across eastern Nebraska and into western Iowa during the evening hours. Although isolated reports of damaging winds were reported, most of the events were large hail, some of which was significant.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to 1.5 inches in diameter fell a few miles north of Waverly.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed along a cold front extending southwestward from central SD down across north central and western NE into northern CO.
Thunderstorms developed along a cold front extending southwestward from central SD down across north central and western NE into northern CO.
Thunderstorms developed along a cold front extending southwestward from central SD down across north central and western NE into northern CO.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Some crop damage occurred from the hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another burst of late winter visited mainly northern portions of South Central Nebraska on Monday the 22nd, as parts of Valley, Greeley and Nance counties received 1-4 inches of wet snow from a rather quick-moving precipitation shield that passed through the area primarily between 11 AM and 2 PM CDT. Although snow did not last all that long in any given location, it accumulated quickly as some of the highest totals per NWS cooperative observers and NeRAIN observers featured 4.5 nine miles northwest of Ord, 4 at Arcadia and eight miles north-northeast of Scotia, and 3.5 in Ord. Accompanying this falling snow was sustained north winds around 20 MPH and gusting over 30 MPH behind a sharp cold front. While accumulating snow was confined to these far northern counties, temperatures remained just warm enough across the majority of the 24-county area to keep precipitation in the form of a steady, chilly rain. Temperature-wise, this passing front ushered in a very cold few days by late April standards, as Ord airport only registered highs of 39 and 37 degrees, respectively, on the 22nd and 23rd. ||In the mid and upper levels, forcing for this event was provided in part by lift associated with a low-amplitude lead shortwave trough ejecting out ahead of a larger-scale, positively tilted trough centered along an axis from south central Canada to Idaho. Central Nebraska was also positioned under a pronounced area of upper level divergence between the exit region of a 110 knot upper jet streak oriented from northern Colorado into western Kansas, and the entrance region of another jet streak centered from northern Minnesota into Ontario. At the surface, mid-day analysis depicted the leading edge of the aforementioned strong cold front aligned from extreme southwest Kansas into southeast Nebraska, with temperatures across the local frontal zone ranging from 29 degrees at Ord, to 38 in Hastings, to 53 in Hebron.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A total of 3.0 inches of snow fell in Spalding. This report was from an NERAIN observer.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: According to the U.S. Drought Monitor, April marked the tenth consecutive month of severe (or worse) drought across South Central Nebraska. This continued one of the most notable droughts since the 1950s, although much of the area saw some encouraging trends during the month. Based on Drought Monitor weekly issuances, for the first time in several months there was a widespread - albeit slight - improvement in drought classification. More specifically, most locations generally west of a line from Franklin-Aurora-Silver Creek improved from D4 Exceptional Drought (the worst-possible category) to D3 Extreme Drought, while most places east of this line improved from Extreme to D2 Severe Drought. In fact, only southwestern Furnas County remained in Exceptional drought at month's end. Based on reports from around 50 NWS cooperative observers, and also incorporating radar-derived estimation, roughly 70 percent of the area tallied above normal April precipitation (based on normals generally between 2-3 inches). Even for the roughly 30% of the area that measured below normal precipitation, most of these places checked in only slightly below normal and tallied at least 1.50 for the month. In other words, no areas were left bone-dry through the entire month, as had been the case several times during the past year. Thanks largely to multiple rounds of thunderstorms that trained over the same corridor on the night of the 8th, the highest monthly precipitation totals of 3-5 inches (and locally higher) concentrated within a southwest-to-northeast axis running roughly 15 miles either side of a line from Holdrege-Ravenna-Fullerton. Within this zone, some of the highest-known April totals included 6.01 seven miles north-northeast of Central City, 5.07 two miles west of Genoa, 4.96 at Fullerton and 4.75 at St. Paul. At Fullerton, it was the 11th-wettest April on record dating back to 1901. On the comparably drier side of things, those areas that received less than 2.25 of precipitation during the month were largely concentrated within parts of Adams, Webster, Franklin, Nuckolls and Furnas counties. A few of the lowest monthly totals were reported at Red Cloud and two miles northeast of Upland (1.57), Franklin (1.91) and Beaver City (1.98). As for April snowfall, a predominant below normal temperature regime set the stage for generally above normal amounts, with a few spots such as Ord and Belgrade accumulating as much as 7 inches. ||Despite the positive news for much of South Central Nebraska during April, there was still a long way to go to fully relieve the area of severe or worse drought given that longer-term impacts to subsoil moisture and groundwater deficits remained. The trend was on the right track, however, as most of the 24-county area observed between 75 and 115 percent of normal precipitation during the first four months of 2013. According to the USDA Nebraska Crop Progress and Condition Report for the week ending May 5th, state-averaged topsoil moisture had decreased to 36% short to very short, a notable 44% improvement since late March. However, subsoil moisture supplies still rated 84% short to very short, only a 12% improvement over the same time span. The state-averaged wheat crop rated 49% poor to very poor, and hay and forage supplies rated 70% short to very short, 11% worse versus late March. Pasture and range conditions across Nebraska averaged 70% poor to very poor, however, stock water supplies were only 21% short to very short, a 15% improvement since late March.

In other words, no areas were left bone-dry through the entire month, as had been the case several times during the past year. Thanks largely to multiple rounds of thunderstorms that trained over the same corridor on the night of the 8th, the highest monthly precipitation totals of 3-5 inches (and locally higher) concentrated within a southwest-to-northeast axis running roughly 15 miles either side of a line from Holdrege-Ravenna-Fullerton. Within this zone, some of the highest-known April totals included 6.01 seven miles north-northeast of Central City, 5.07 two miles west of Genoa, 4.96 at Fullerton and 4.75 at St. Paul. At Fullerton, it was the 11th-wettest April on record dating back to 1901. On the comparably drier side of things, those areas that received less than 2.25 of precipitation during the month were largely concentrated within parts of Adams, Webster, Franklin, Nuckolls and Furnas counties. A few of the lowest monthly totals were reported at Red Cloud and two miles northeast of Upland (1.57), Franklin (1.91) and Beaver City (1.98). As for April snowfall, a predominant below normal temperature regime set the stage for generally above normal amounts, with a few spots such as Ord and Belgrade accumulating as much as 7 inches. ||Despite the positive news for much of South Central Nebraska during April, there was still a long way to go to fully relieve the area of severe or worse drought given that longer-term impacts to subsoil moisture and groundwater deficits remained. The trend was on the right track, however, as most of the 24-county area observed between 75 and 115 percent of normal precipitation during the first four months of 2013. According to the USDA Nebraska Crop Progress and Condition Report for the week ending May 5th, state-averaged topsoil moisture had decreased to 36% short to very short, a notable 44% improvement since late March. However, subsoil moisture supplies still rated 84% short to very short, only a 12% improvement over the same time span. The state-averaged wheat crop rated 49% poor to very poor, and hay and forage supplies rated 70% short to very short, 11% worse versus late March. Pasture and range conditions across Nebraska averaged 70% poor to very poor, however, stock water supplies were only 21% short to very short, a 15% improvement since late March.

Another burst of late winter visited mainly northern portions of South Central Nebraska on Monday the 22nd, as parts of Valley, Greeley and Nance counties received 1-4 inches of wet snow from a rather quick-moving precipitation shield that passed through the area primarily between 11 AM and 2 PM CDT. Although snow did not last all that long in any given location, it accumulated quickly as some of the highest totals per NWS cooperative observers and NeRAIN observers featured 4.5 nine miles northwest of Ord, 4 at Arcadia and eight miles north-northeast of Scotia, and 3.5 in Ord. Accompanying this falling snow was sustained north winds around 20 MPH and gusting over 30 MPH behind a sharp cold front. While accumulating snow was confined to these far northern counties, temperatures remained just warm enough across the majority of the 24-county area to keep precipitation in the form of a steady, chilly rain. Temperature-wise, this passing front ushered in a very cold few days by late April standards, as Ord airport only registered highs of 39 and 37 degrees, respectively, on the 22nd and 23rd. ||In the mid and upper levels, forcing for this event was provided in part by lift associated with a low-amplitude lead shortwave trough ejecting out ahead of a larger-scale, positively tilted trough centered along an axis from south central Canada to Idaho. Central Nebraska was also positioned under a pronounced area of upper level divergence between the exit region of a 110 knot upper jet streak oriented from northern Colorado into western Kansas, and the entrance region of another jet streak centered from northern Minnesota into Ontario. At the surface, mid-day analysis depicted the leading edge of the aforementioned strong cold front aligned from extreme southwest Kansas into southeast Nebraska, with temperatures across the local frontal zone ranging from 29 degrees at Ord, to 38 in Hastings, to 53 in Hebron.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A total of 3.0 inches fell 3 miles south southeast of Scotia. This report was from an NERAIN observer.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Long term dry soil conditions and low flows on the Missouri River contributed to lingering drought at the start of May. The severe to extreme drought steadily abated as precipitation increased during the first part of the month, with the sensitivity of the area to potential dry summer periods decreasing.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Long term dry soil conditions at the start of May improved steadily in early May with increased precipitation, and the effect of low Missouri River flows also decreased. The sensitivity of crops and water supplies to future dry weather steadily decreased.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several episodes of heavy-rain producing thunderstorms caused flooding along the Missouri River in southeast Nebraska, as well as the the Big Blue River, Turkey Creek, and the Big Nemaha River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Due to repeated heavy thunderstorm rains across eastern Nebraska and western Iowa the Missouri River went into flood. Only minor flooding was observed in Otoe County with the river cresting twice at Nebraska City, first on the morning of the 30th at 18 feet, and then fell briefly below flood stage cresting again on the 31st at 18.62 ft. This created areas of lowlands flooding along the river.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several episodes of heavy-rain producing thunderstorms caused flooding along the Missouri River in southeast Nebraska, as well as the the Big Blue River, Turkey Creek, and the Big Nemaha River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Missouri River at Rulo went above flood stage late in the morning of May 28th, crested around 23.5 feet late in the evening of the 30th and eventually fell below flood stage early in the morning of June 2nd.   Although the river mostly flooded agricultural lowlands, several cabins and possibly a business in the area either experienced minor flooding or were threatened by the flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several episodes of heavy-rain producing thunderstorms caused flooding along the Missouri River in southeast Nebraska, as well as the the Big Blue River, Turkey Creek, and the Big Nemaha River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall of 3 to 6 inches within the Big Blue River basin over several days pushed the river into flood. The Big Blue River crested on the morning of the 30th with a river stage of 22.68 feet at the city of Crete. This created significant lowland flooding along the river and led to the closure of several rural roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several episodes of heavy-rain producing thunderstorms caused flooding along the Missouri River in southeast Nebraska, as well as the the Big Blue River, Turkey Creek, and the Big Nemaha River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Repeated heavy rainfall over the Big Blue River basin lead to the river going into flood across parts of Gage County. This was most prevalent from Beatrice and to the north. The river crested at Beatrice on the morning of June 1st at 17.98 feet. This created lowland flooding, but few impacts to the city of Beatrice.

A few of the lowest monthly totals were reported at Red Cloud and two miles northeast of Upland (1.57), Franklin (1.91) and Beaver City (1.98). As for April snowfall, a predominant below normal temperature regime set the stage for generally above normal amounts, with a few spots such as Ord and Belgrade accumulating as much as 7 inches. ||Despite the positive news for much of South Central Nebraska during April, there was still a long way to go to fully relieve the area of severe or worse drought given that longer-term impacts to subsoil moisture and groundwater deficits remained. The trend was on the right track, however, as most of the 24-county area observed between 75 and 115 percent of normal precipitation during the first four months of 2013. According to the USDA Nebraska Crop Progress and Condition Report for the week ending May 5th, state-averaged topsoil moisture had decreased to 36% short to very short, a notable 44% improvement since late March. However, subsoil moisture supplies still rated 84% short to very short, only a 12% improvement over the same time span. The state-averaged wheat crop rated 49% poor to very poor, and hay and forage supplies rated 70% short to very short, 11% worse versus late March. Pasture and range conditions across Nebraska averaged 70% poor to very poor, however, stock water supplies were only 21% short to very short, a 15% improvement since late March.

According to the U.S. Drought Monitor, April marked the tenth consecutive month of severe (or worse) drought across South Central Nebraska. This continued one of the most notable droughts since the 1950s, although much of the area saw some encouraging trends during the month. Based on Drought Monitor weekly issuances, for the first time in several months there was a widespread - albeit slight - improvement in drought classification. More specifically, most locations generally west of a line from Franklin-Aurora-Silver Creek improved from D4 Exceptional Drought (the worst-possible category) to D3 Extreme Drought, while most places east of this line improved from Extreme to D2 Severe Drought. In fact, only southwestern Furnas County remained in Exceptional drought at month's end. Based on reports from around 50 NWS cooperative observers, and also incorporating radar-derived estimation, roughly 70 percent of the area tallied above normal April precipitation (based on normals generally between 2-3 inches). Even for the roughly 30% of the area that measured below normal precipitation, most of these places checked in only slightly below normal and tallied at least 1.50 for the month. In other words, no areas were left bone-dry through the entire month, as had been the case several times during the past year. Thanks largely to multiple rounds of thunderstorms that trained over the same corridor on the night of the 8th, the highest monthly precipitation totals of 3-5 inches (and locally higher) concentrated within a southwest-to-northeast axis running roughly 15 miles either side of a line from Holdrege-Ravenna-Fullerton. Within this zone, some of the highest-known April totals included 6.01 seven miles north-northeast of Central City, 5.07 two miles west of Genoa, 4.96 at Fullerton and 4.75 at St. Paul. Thunderstorms developed along a stationary boundary near the Kansas and Nebraska border on the afternoon of May 27th. Due to high amounts of instability and shear in the atmosphere these thunderstorms quickly became severe. The thunderstorms moved east over southern Nebraska, moving into southeast Nebraska by early evening. Although other isolated severe weather was reported, one particular storm moving through Jefferson and Gage Counties produced the majority of the severe weather. The storm complex exited southeast Nebraska prior to midnight producing severe weather around the Falls City Nebraska area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy thunderstorm rains of 2 to 3 inches fell across the city of Beatrice. This led to several intersections in the city to become flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the midst of one of the top-10 coldest months of April on record for much of South Central Nebraska, a late-season winter storm brought a widespread 1-4 inches of snow to most counties during a roughly 15-hour period between the late evening of Wednesday the 17th and the early-mid afternoon of Thursday the 18th. Areas west of Highway 281, which saw the greatest coverage of localized 3+ inch amounts, observed most accumulating snow during the overnight hours. Meanwhile, for most counties along and east of Highway 281, snow began during the night but peaked in intensity after sunrise before ending or diminishing to flurries during the afternoon. Adding to the mid-April misery were brisk north-northwest winds sustained 20-30 MPH and gusting 35-40 MPH, resulting in occasional visibility restrictions to one-half mile or less and wind chill temperatures between 10-20 degrees. The majority of the 24-county area measured at least 1-2 of snow from this event, except for far southeast counties such as Nuckolls and Thayer that averaged less than 1. According to NWS cooperative observers and NeRAIN observers, 3+ inch amounts primarily focused within the far western counties of Furnas, Dawson and Gosper, including 4 at Wilsonville, 3.5 eight miles south of Elwood and six miles south-southeast of Lexington, and 3 at Beaver City and three miles west of Sumner. However, even farther east, narrow bands of post-sunrise snow pushed Grand Island up to 4, which set a daily record for the 18th and resulted in the largest April snowfall in 10 years. Temperature-wise, daytime highs across South Central Nebraska on the 18th only averaged 33-37 degrees, which was around 30 degrees below normal for the date and established record-cold daily maximums at Grand Island and Hastings. ||On the synoptic scale aloft, this event was powered by a highly amplified and slightly positively tilted mid-upper trough slowly lumbering across the Central United States. As a 100+ knot upper level jet streak rounded the base of the trough and took aim on the Central Plains, a mid level low and resultant area of enhanced frontogenesis became increasingly organized at 700 millibars, resulting in widespread snowfall. At mid-morning on the 18th, surface maps revealed that strong north-northwest winds were being promoted by a tight pressure gradient between a still-deepening 1000 millibar low over northeast Missouri and a 1028 millibar high centered over the Wyoming area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A total of 3.5 inches of snow fell 8 miles south of Elwood.

At mid-morning on the 18th, surface maps revealed that strong north-northwest winds were being promoted by a tight pressure gradient between a still-deepening 1000 millibar low over northeast Missouri and a 1028 millibar high centered over the Wyoming area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A total of 3.2 inches of snow fell 7 miles east of Cozad. This report was from an NERAIN observer.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the midst of one of the top-10 coldest months of April on record for much of South Central Nebraska, a late-season winter storm brought a widespread 1-4 inches of snow to most counties during a roughly 15-hour period between the late evening of Wednesday the 17th and the early-mid afternoon of Thursday the 18th. Areas west of Highway 281, which saw the greatest coverage of localized 3+ inch amounts, observed most accumulating snow during the overnight hours. Meanwhile, for most counties along and east of Highway 281, snow began during the night but peaked in intensity after sunrise before ending or diminishing to flurries during the afternoon. Adding to the mid-April misery were brisk north-northwest winds sustained 20-30 MPH and gusting 35-40 MPH, resulting in occasional visibility restrictions to one-half mile or less and wind chill temperatures between 10-20 degrees. The majority of the 24-county area measured at least 1-2 of snow from this event, except for far southeast counties such as Nuckolls and Thayer that averaged less than 1. According to NWS cooperative observers and NeRAIN observers, 3+ inch amounts primarily focused within the far western counties of Furnas, Dawson and Gosper, including 4 at Wilsonville, 3.5 eight miles south of Elwood and six miles south-southeast of Lexington, and 3 at Beaver City and three miles west of Sumner. However, even farther east, narrow bands of post-sunrise snow pushed Grand Island up to 4, which set a daily record for the 18th and resulted in the largest April snowfall in 10 years. Temperature-wise, daytime highs across South Central Nebraska on the 18th only averaged 33-37 degrees, which was around 30 degrees below normal for the date and established record-cold daily maximums at Grand Island and Hastings. ||On the synoptic scale aloft, this event was powered by a highly amplified and slightly positively tilted mid-upper trough slowly lumbering across the Central United States. As a 100+ knot upper level jet streak rounded the base of the trough and took aim on the Central Plains, a mid level low and resultant area of enhanced frontogenesis became increasingly organized at 700 millibars, resulting in widespread snowfall. At mid-morning on the 18th, surface maps revealed that strong north-northwest winds were being promoted by a tight pressure gradient between a still-deepening 1000 millibar low over northeast Missouri and a 1028 millibar high centered over the Wyoming area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A total of 3.0 inches of snow fell 3 miles west of Sumner. This report was from an NERAIN observer.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: According to the U.S. Drought Monitor, April marked the tenth consecutive month of severe (or worse) drought across South Central Nebraska. This continued one of the most notable droughts since the 1950s, although much of the area saw some encouraging trends during the month. Based on Drought Monitor weekly issuances, for the first time in several months there was a widespread - albeit slight - improvement in drought classification. More specifically, most locations generally west of a line from Franklin-Aurora-Silver Creek improved from D4 Exceptional Drought (the worst-possible category) to D3 Extreme Drought, while most places east of this line improved from Extreme to D2 Severe Drought. In fact, only southwestern Furnas County remained in Exceptional drought at month's end. Based on reports from around 50 NWS cooperative observers, and also incorporating radar-derived estimation, roughly 70 percent of the area tallied above normal April precipitation (based on normals generally between 2-3 inches). Even for the roughly 30% of the area that measured below normal precipitation, most of these places checked in only slightly below normal and tallied at least 1.50 for the month. In other words, no areas were left bone-dry through the entire month, as had been the case several times during the past year. Thanks largely to multiple rounds of thunderstorms that trained over the same corridor on the night of the 8th, the highest monthly precipitation totals of 3-5 inches (and locally higher) concentrated within a southwest-to-northeast axis running roughly 15 miles either side of a line from Holdrege-Ravenna-Fullerton. Within this zone, some of the highest-known April totals included 6.01 seven miles north-northeast of Central City, 5.07 two miles west of Genoa, 4.96 at Fullerton and 4.75 at St. Paul. At Fullerton, it was the 11th-wettest April on record dating back to 1901. On the comparably drier side of things, those areas that received less than 2.25 of precipitation during the month were largely concentrated within parts of Adams, Webster, Franklin, Nuckolls and Furnas counties. A few of the lowest monthly totals were reported at Red Cloud and two miles northeast of Upland (1.57), Franklin (1.91) and Beaver City (1.98). As for April snowfall, a predominant below normal temperature regime set the stage for generally above normal amounts, with a few spots such as Ord and Belgrade accumulating as much as 7 inches. ||Despite the positive news for much of South Central Nebraska during April, there was still a long way to go to fully relieve the area of severe or worse drought given that longer-term impacts to subsoil moisture and groundwater deficits remained. The trend was on the right track, however, as most of the 24-county area observed between 75 and 115 percent of normal precipitation during the first four months of 2013. According to the USDA Nebraska Crop Progress and Condition Report for the week ending May 5th, state-averaged topsoil moisture had decreased to 36% short to very short, a notable 44% improvement since late March. However, subsoil moisture supplies still rated 84% short to very short, only a 12% improvement over the same time span. The state-averaged wheat crop rated 49% poor to very poor, and hay and forage supplies rated 70% short to very short, 11% worse versus late March. Pasture and range conditions across Nebraska averaged 70% poor to very poor, however, stock water supplies were only 21% short to very short, a 15% improvement since late March.
