Based on Drought Monitor weekly issuances, for the first time in several months there was a widespread - albeit slight - improvement in drought classification. More specifically, most locations generally west of a line from Franklin-Aurora-Silver Creek improved from D4 Exceptional Drought (the worst-possible category) to D3 Extreme Drought, while most places east of this line improved from Extreme to D2 Severe Drought. In fact, only southwestern Furnas County remained in Exceptional drought at month's end. Based on reports from around 50 NWS cooperative observers, and also incorporating radar-derived estimation, roughly 70 percent of the area tallied above normal April precipitation (based on normals generally between 2-3 inches). Even for the roughly 30% of the area that measured below normal precipitation, most of these places checked in only slightly below normal and tallied at least 1.50 for the month. In other words, no areas were left bone-dry through the entire month, as had been the case several times during the past year. Thanks largely to multiple rounds of thunderstorms that trained over the same corridor on the night of the 8th, the highest monthly precipitation totals of 3-5 inches (and locally higher) concentrated within a southwest-to-northeast axis running roughly 15 miles either side of a line from Holdrege-Ravenna-Fullerton. Within this zone, some of the highest-known April totals included 6.01 seven miles north-northeast of Central City, 5.07 two miles west of Genoa, 4.96 at Fullerton and 4.75 at St. Paul. At Fullerton, it was the 11th-wettest April on record dating back to 1901. On the comparably drier side of things, those areas that received less than 2.25 of precipitation during the month were largely concentrated within parts of Adams, Webster, Franklin, Nuckolls and Furnas counties. A few of the lowest monthly totals were reported at Red Cloud and two miles northeast of Upland (1.57), Franklin (1.91) and Beaver City (1.98). As for April snowfall, a predominant below normal temperature regime set the stage for generally above normal amounts, with a few spots such as Ord and Belgrade accumulating as much as 7 inches. ||Despite the positive news for much of South Central Nebraska during April, there was still a long way to go to fully relieve the area of severe or worse drought given that longer-term impacts to subsoil moisture and groundwater deficits remained. The trend was on the right track, however, as most of the 24-county area observed between 75 and 115 percent of normal precipitation during the first four months of 2013. According to the USDA Nebraska Crop Progress and Condition Report for the week ending May 5th, state-averaged topsoil moisture had decreased to 36% short to very short, a notable 44% improvement since late March. However, subsoil moisture supplies still rated 84% short to very short, only a 12% improvement over the same time span. The state-averaged wheat crop rated 49% poor to very poor, and hay and forage supplies rated 70% short to very short, 11% worse versus late March. Pasture and range conditions across Nebraska averaged 70% poor to very poor, however, stock water supplies were only 21% short to very short, a 15% improvement since late March.

For roughly seven hours on this Monday night and early Tuesday morning, the first widespread severe thunderstorm event of 2013 impacted mainly the northwest two-thirds of the 24-county area, largely focused northwest of a line from Alma-Hastings-Osceola along a very strong cold front advancing southeast through the region. Between 9 PM and 4 AM CDT, several rounds of multicell storms along with a few embedded supercell structures tracked north to northeast across the area, resulting in multiple reports of severe-criteria hail ranging from quarter to golf ball size and damaging winds of at least 60-70 MPH. Some of the most extensive damage occurred in Kearney around 1 AM CDT, due to measured wind gusts up to 76 MPH and hail up to golf ball size. In addition to considerable tree damage within the city, a mobile home was destroyed and several buildings on the University of Nebraska-Kearney campus sustained damage. This included the university library where a portion of the roof was ripped off, resulting in water damage to over 6,000 books. There were also several reports of severe winds and damage in and near Holdrege between 12-1 AM CDT, including a measured 74 MPH gust at the airport, a semi truck blown over on Highway 6/34 a few miles southwest of town and a large metal outbuilding destroyed a few miles northeast of town. Other severe weather reports during the night included quarter size hail at the Hastings NWS office and golf ball size hail in Phillips. In addition to hail and wind, widespread rainfall of 1-3 inches resulted from multiple storms training over the same general southwest-to-northeast corridor, with most 2+ inch amounts concentrated within 15 miles either side of a line extending from near Holdrege-Cairo-Fullerton. A few observers reported totals in excess of 3 inches, including 3.61 near Rockville and 3.41 near Scotia. Although flooding issues were held to a minimum, water was reported over a road near St. Libory. ||The first storms of the evening developed near and north of the Interstate 80 corridor between 9 PM and midnight CDT. Some of these initial storms exhibited low-level rotation on radar, and were likely near-surface based in an environment featuring temperatures in the low-60s and dewpoints in the upper-50s, although no tornadoes were reported. After midnight CDT, storms gradually tended to become more elongated and elevated as they were undercut by the surging cold front, although interactions with this front likely enhanced the damaging winds in the Holdrege and Kearney areas. Around 4 AM CDT, the last severe-warned storm exited the local area eastward out of Polk County, and thereafter only a few weak storms lingered through sunrise. As evidence of the strength of the cold front, 4 AM CDT temperatures across South Central Nebraska ranged from 33 degrees at Ord, to 44 at Hastings, to 66 at Hebron. On the mesoscale, the primary trigger for this long-lasting severe weather episode was pronounced low-to-mid level warm air advection and resultant upglide over the top of the surging surface cold front, which effectively bisected Nebraska from southwest-to-northeast at sunset. After dark, an 850 millibar low level jet ramped up to around 40 knots, resulting in a rapid uptick in convection. Around midnight, most unstable CAPE values averaged around 1000 J/kg in the presence of very strong 0-6 kilometer vertical wind shear of 60-70 knots. On the synoptic scale, this event occurred downstream from an expansive, highly-amplified mid level trough centered near the Four Corners, and in the presence of low-amplitude shortwave troughs ejecting northeast from the parent system into the Central Plains on strengthening southwesterly flow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A total of 3.61 inches of rain fell. This was a 24-hour rainfall amount as of 7 am CDT. This report was relayed through the Nebraska Rainfall Assessment and Information Network.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A supercell thunderstorm spawned a EF1 tornado 19 miles southwest of Chadron in Dawes County.  The tornado track was a mile long and lasted seven minutes before dissipating.  Ten minutes later, the same storm produced a brief EF0 tornado southwest of Red Cloud Campground.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The National Weather Service in Cheyenne rated the June 11 2013 Dawes County Nebraska tornado as EF1 on the Enhanced Fujita Scale with maximum 3 second wind gusts of 110 mph.  The tornado touched down 19 miles south-southwest of Chadron, just west of the T intersection of Hough Road. The tornado moved east, then slightly southeast, dissipating seven minutes later just north of Hough Road.  The damage path length and width were  estimated at one mile and 40 yards, respectively.  At least 16 structures were damaged, with at least eight outbuildings destroyed.  Numerous trees and telephone poles were snapped and two horse trailers were thrown several hundred feet.  Two distinct swirl marks were located along the damage path, estimated around 12 feet in diameter.  The supercell thunderstorm associated with the tornado produced very little rain or hail, but several cloud to ground lightning strikes near the tornado.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple supercells moved northward across far Southwest Nebraska during the late afternoon and evening producing large hail and severe wind gusts.  Hail up to 1.75 in diameter was reported near Parks.  During the late evening a wind gust of 58 MPH was measured at the McCook airport from one of the storms.

A lone, compact-but-intense supercell drifted slowly northeast across western portions of Valley County mainly between 8-10 pm CDT on this Sunday evening, dumping large hail up to two inches in diameter. This storm initially flared up just west of the local area in east-central Custer County around 730 pm CDT, and eventually exited Valley County into Garfield County in a weakening phase. Later in the night, primarily between 1130 pm-330 am CDT, the southern fringes of a linear mesoscale convective system (MCS) focused across north central and eastern Nebraska brushed through areas northeast of a line from Ord-York, but these storms remained sub-severe within the local area. Finally, even later into the night and early morning hours primarily between 430-700 am CDT, a southwest-northeast cluster of strong storms tracked through counties mainly along and north of Interstate 80, with penny size hail reported in Central City around sunrise. The cumulative result of all evening and overnight convection was a swath of 1-2+ inches of rain for many areas northeast of an Ord-York line, including 2.90 inches two miles west of Genoa. ||Leading into the initial evening supercell development, the mid and upper level pattern featured moderate quasi-zonal flow, with South Central Nebraska positioned downstream of a potent trough tracking through the Pacific Northwest and Northern Rockies. At the surface, the early evening map highlighted a well-defined quasi-stationary front aligned east-west through the heart of central and eastern Nebraska, providing a focus for low level convergence and storm development. Mesoscale parameters around the time of the Valley County supercell featured a favorable severe weather environment, with mixed-layer CAPE around 3000 J/kg and 0-6 kilometer deep layer wind shear around 35 knots. Low level shear near the surface front was also favorable for tornadic development, but only wall clouds and brief funnel clouds were reported in or near Valley County.

Several episodes of heavy-rain producing thunderstorms caused flooding along the Missouri River in southeast Nebraska, as well as the the Big Blue River, Turkey Creek, and the Big Nemaha River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In response to repeated days of heavy rainfall from thunderstorms across eastern Nebraska and western Iowa, the Missouri River went into flood. The Missouri River at Brownville crested on May 31st at a stage of 35.79 feet. This led to widespread lowland flooding of agricultural land.  This flooding persisted into early June.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of thunderstorms developed over south central Nebraska and spread east into an area of strong instability and weak inhibition over southeast Nebraska. As a low level jet increased, so did the areal coverage of thunderstorms. This cluster of storms produced a brief tornado spin-up, large hail, strong winds and heavy rain. Some flash flooding was also reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms trained to produced 3 to 5 inches of heavy rainfall where emergency management reported flooding with water over some roads in Broken Bow, a bridge was washed out on the Callaway Road and flooding over the road 4 miles west of Broken Bow.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail shattered double and triple pane windows, damaged siding, and left divots in the ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moderately unstable conditions developed over eastern Nebraska during the afternoon and evening of May 18th as temperatures warmed into the 80s and dew point temperatures reached the mid to upper 60s.  As an upper level trough approached from the Rockies, thunderstorms developed in western Nebraska and western Kansas.  These storms moved east across eastern Nebraska and western Iowa and produced isolated areas of wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts caused spotty tree damage in Columbus including a large hackberry tree which was downed in the town square which damaged a statue and ceremonial cannon.  Some power outages were also noted in town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several episodes of heavy-rain producing thunderstorms caused flooding along the Missouri River in southeast Nebraska, as well as the the Big Blue River, Turkey Creek, and the Big Nemaha River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Due to persistent heavy rainfall across the Turkey Creek basin, the river rose above flood state on the morning of May 28th. There were two crests associated with the heavy rain. The first coming on the morning of the 28th at 12.57 feet, and the second higher crest on May 30th at 16.58 feet. The second crest resulted in significant flooding of row crops in the flood plain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several episodes of heavy-rain producing thunderstorms caused flooding along the Missouri River in southeast Nebraska, as well as the the Big Blue River, Turkey Creek, and the Big Nemaha River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Persistent heavy rainfall across southeast Nebraska lead to a saturated ground and flooding along the Big Nemaha River near Falls City, NE. Lowland flooding was reported along the river from near Falls City to near Rulo. The river crested at 29.10 feet on the morning of the 30th of May, just over 4 feet above flood stage.

Thunderstorms developed along a stationary boundary near the Kansas and Nebraska border on the afternoon of May 27th. Due to high amounts of instability and shear in the atmosphere these thunderstorms quickly became severe. The thunderstorms moved east over southern Nebraska, moving into southeast Nebraska by early evening. Although other isolated severe weather was reported, one particular storm moving through Jefferson and Gage Counties produced the majority of the severe weather. The storm complex exited southeast Nebraska prior to midnight producing severe weather around the Falls City Nebraska area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A NWS storm survey indicated that a tornado developed approximately 2 miles south of Harbine. The tornado traveled in an east northeast direction impacting several farmsteads in Jefferson County. There was minor damage to 4 houses, and 5 out buildings were damaged beyond repair. Other damage included over-turned center pivots and tree damage. Approximately 2 miles east southeast of Harbine the tornado produced damage consistent with a weak EF2 tornado on the Enhanced Fujita Scale. Here a large recently well-built metal outbuildings were destroyed, minor damage was done to a home, and 2X4 boards were driven into the ground.

Around 4 AM CDT, the last severe-warned storm exited the local area eastward out of Polk County, and thereafter only a few weak storms lingered through sunrise. As evidence of the strength of the cold front, 4 AM CDT temperatures across South Central Nebraska ranged from 33 degrees at Ord, to 44 at Hastings, to 66 at Hebron. After dark, an 850 millibar low level jet ramped up to around 40 knots, resulting in a rapid uptick in convection. Around midnight, most unstable CAPE values averaged around 1000 J/kg in the presence of very strong 0-6 kilometer vertical wind shear of 60-70 knots. On the synoptic scale, this event occurred downstream from an expansive, highly-amplified mid level trough centered near the Four Corners, and in the presence of low-amplitude shortwave troughs ejecting northeast from the parent system into the Central Plains on strengthening southwesterly flow.

A cluster of thunderstorms developed over south central Nebraska and spread east into an area of strong instability and weak inhibition over southeast Nebraska. As a low level jet increased, so did the areal coverage of thunderstorms. This cluster of storms produced a brief tornado spin-up, large hail, strong winds and heavy rain. Some flash flooding was also reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This was a quick spin up tornado amongst a cluster of thunderstorms. No damage was reported with this brief tornado.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed along a stationary boundary near the Kansas and Nebraska border on the afternoon of May 27th. Due to high amounts of instability and shear in the atmosphere these thunderstorms quickly became severe. The thunderstorms moved east over southern Nebraska, moving into southeast Nebraska by early evening. Although other isolated severe weather was reported, one particular storm moving through Jefferson and Gage Counties produced the majority of the severe weather. The storm complex exited southeast Nebraska prior to midnight producing severe weather around the Falls City Nebraska area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy thunderstorm rains of 2 to 3 inches fell across the city of Cortland in a short time leading to flash flooding. Water covered highway 77 in Cortland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms moved/developed over far Southwest Nebraska during the afternoon.  Large hail up to 1.5 inches in diameter, wind gusts around 65 MPH, and  flash flooding were reported from the storms.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of thunderstorms developed over south central Nebraska and spread east into an area of strong instability and weak inhibition over southeast Nebraska. As a low level jet increased, so did the areal coverage of thunderstorms. This cluster of storms produced a brief tornado spin-up, large hail, strong winds and heavy rain. Some flash flooding was also reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding briefly covered sidewalks over parts of north Lincoln.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For roughly seven hours on this Monday night and early Tuesday morning, the first widespread severe thunderstorm event of 2013 impacted mainly the northwest two-thirds of the 24-county area, largely focused northwest of a line from Alma-Hastings-Osceola along a very strong cold front advancing southeast through the region. Between 9 PM and 4 AM CDT, several rounds of multicell storms along with a few embedded supercell structures tracked north to northeast across the area, resulting in multiple reports of severe-criteria hail ranging from quarter to golf ball size and damaging winds of at least 60-70 MPH. Some of the most extensive damage occurred in Kearney around 1 AM CDT, due to measured wind gusts up to 76 MPH and hail up to golf ball size. In addition to considerable tree damage within the city, a mobile home was destroyed and several buildings on the University of Nebraska-Kearney campus sustained damage. This included the university library where a portion of the roof was ripped off, resulting in water damage to over 6,000 books. There were also several reports of severe winds and damage in and near Holdrege between 12-1 AM CDT, including a measured 74 MPH gust at the airport, a semi truck blown over on Highway 6/34 a few miles southwest of town and a large metal outbuilding destroyed a few miles northeast of town. Other severe weather reports during the night included quarter size hail at the Hastings NWS office and golf ball size hail in Phillips. In addition to hail and wind, widespread rainfall of 1-3 inches resulted from multiple storms training over the same general southwest-to-northeast corridor, with most 2+ inch amounts concentrated within 15 miles either side of a line extending from near Holdrege-Cairo-Fullerton. A few observers reported totals in excess of 3 inches, including 3.61 near Rockville and 3.41 near Scotia. Although flooding issues were held to a minimum, water was reported over a road near St. Libory. ||The first storms of the evening developed near and north of the Interstate 80 corridor between 9 PM and midnight CDT. Some of these initial storms exhibited low-level rotation on radar, and were likely near-surface based in an environment featuring temperatures in the low-60s and dewpoints in the upper-50s, although no tornadoes were reported. After midnight CDT, storms gradually tended to become more elongated and elevated as they were undercut by the surging cold front, although interactions with this front likely enhanced the damaging winds in the Holdrege and Kearney areas. Around 4 AM CDT, the last severe-warned storm exited the local area eastward out of Polk County, and thereafter only a few weak storms lingered through sunrise. As evidence of the strength of the cold front, 4 AM CDT temperatures across South Central Nebraska ranged from 33 degrees at Ord, to 44 at Hastings, to 66 at Hebron. On the mesoscale, the primary trigger for this long-lasting severe weather episode was pronounced low-to-mid level warm air advection and resultant upglide over the top of the surging surface cold front, which effectively bisected Nebraska from southwest-to-northeast at sunset. After dark, an 850 millibar low level jet ramped up to around 40 knots, resulting in a rapid uptick in convection. Around midnight, most unstable CAPE values averaged around 1000 J/kg in the presence of very strong 0-6 kilometer vertical wind shear of 60-70 knots. On the synoptic scale, this event occurred downstream from an expansive, highly-amplified mid level trough centered near the Four Corners, and in the presence of low-amplitude shortwave troughs ejecting northeast from the parent system into the Central Plains on strengthening southwesterly flow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail covered the ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms over southeast Wyoming moved east and southeast into the southern Nebraska Panhandle during the late afternoon and early evening.  These storms produced large hail, strong winds, very heavy rain and a few tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm survey team from the National Weather Service in Cheyenne rated the June 22 2013 Banner County Nebraska tornado as a EF1 on the Enhanced Fujita Scale with maximum three second wind gusts of 100 mph.  The tornado, which developed from a high precipitation supercell thunderstorm, touched down about 17 miles west-northwest of Harrisburg Nebraska.  The tornado moved to the east-southeast for several miles before dissipating about 10 miles northwest of Harrisburg.  A total of eight structures were damaged with at least one outbuilding destroyed.  The outbuilding was supported by large wooden planks about six inches thick...most of which were snapped at the center.  The roof was removed from a three stall garage but all of the walls remained intact.  A two-story hone was damaged by flying debris, with a 2 X 4 inch plank driven through the second floor wall by the tornadic winds.  Sheet metal and wooden boards from damaged outbuildings were found 50 to 100 yards to the northwest in a shelter belt.  Multiple other structures suffered roof damage with sections of sheet metal peeled off.  Six power poles were snapped near the touchdown site with another damaged east of Stegall Road.  Several six to 12 inch diameter hardwood trees were also snapped.  Center pivots were either flipped or twisted in multiple directions along the tornado's path.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: More than three inches of rain in less than three hours caused flash flooding along Highway 71 between the Banner-Kimball County line and County Road 48.  Up to six inches of water flowed across this three-mile stretch of highway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy precipitation with rapid cooling produced a combination of freezing rain, sleet, and snow in Dixon County in extreme northeast Nebraska from the evening of April 9th, to the morning of April 11th. Moderate to heavy ice accumulations were reported, followed moderate to heavy snow and sleet accumulations. The ice accumulations, a quarter to a half inch, caused tree and power line damage. Fallen branches and limbs were numerous, roads were blocked, and some vehicles suffered damages from the falling limbs and branches. There were also power outages because of the power line and power pole damage. Strong winds with and following the storm, not strong enough to cause significant damage under dry conditions, contributed to the tree and power line damage. The snow and sleet accumulations made travel difficult, especially in areas where downed trees and power lines were already causing road blockages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An extended period of precipitation began with freezing rain and freezing drizzle producing light ice accumulations, then changing to sleet and then snow, with sleet and snow accumulations reaching 7.8 inches at Wakefield. The winter precipitation made travel very difficult, resulting in schools and businesses being forced to close.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 1545CST, a tornado briefly touched down a half mile west of Comstock with no damage produced. Average path width was 35 yards.

The storms intensified as they moved across mainly northeast Nebraska and west central Iowa.  Besides large hail and damaging winds a few of the storms produced heavy rain which caused localized flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced winds which caused tree damage, and also produced three quarter inch diameter hail, at Wakefield in extreme northeast Nebraska on the afternoon of May 16th.

Drought conditions across the area continued from April with little change.  Dundy, Hitchcock and Red Willow Counties remained in D3 (Extreme) to D4 (Exceptional) Drought.  May precipitation at cooperative observer sites ranged from 55 to 120 percent of normal.

Drought conditions across the area continued from April with little change.  Dundy, Hitchcock and Red Willow Counties remained in D3 (Extreme) to D4 (Exceptional) Drought.  May precipitation at cooperative observer sites ranged from 55 to 120 percent of normal.

The drought that began in June of 2012 lingered in May, although thunderstorms produced some significant rainfall to further ease the ongoing drought. All areas received a minimum of two inches of rainfall and some over eight inches. The areal coverage of the Exceptional Drought (D4) conditions in western Nebraska was reduced to focus over the south central sandhills, while a larger portion of northern and north central Nebraska were lowered into the category of Severe Drought.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The drought that began in June 2012 lingered into May. Convective rainfall produced  rainfall amounts from 3 to 8 inches with locally higher amounts in the far east central. The rain was beneficial to ease the extreme southwest into Extreme (D3) Drought conditions while the rest of the county remained in Exception (D4) Drought conditions by end of the month. The drought lingered into June.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As low pressure aloft deepened from western Nebraska into South Dakota, thunderstorms developed across central and eastern Nebraska during the early to mid afternoon hours.  Temperatures climbed into the 70s and 80s and dew point  temperatures in the 60s created moderately unstable conditions across the region.  Several lines of severe thunderstorms moved across the area producing some wind damage along with several reports of large hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This was a unique Tuesday afternoon and evening of weather across South Central Nebraska. Following a lull of several hours in the wake of overnight severe thunderstorms that produced widespread large hail and damaging winds, winter collided with spring to produce showers and elevated thunderstorms with a varied mix of sleet, hail and freezing rain. This was all made possible by strong frontogenesis and easterly warm air advection evident at 850 millibars flowing over the top of a shallow, cold low level airmass featuring mid-afternoon surface temperatures only between the low-20s and low-30s across most of the area, or 30+ degrees below normal for the date. In addition to widespread accumulating sleet in many locations, hail ranging from dime to quarter size was reported across various parts of the 24-county area mainly between 130-600 PM CDT. This included hail stones to quarter size at Fullerton, and nickel size at Grand Island airport, in Benedict and near Hastings. To cite just one example of the unusual nature of this event, as nickel size hail was falling with thunderstorms in the Hastings area at 530 PM CDT, the surface temperature was 25 degrees and north winds were sustained around 25 MPH, producing a wind chill temperature around 10 degrees. Despite this very cold surface regime, elevated thunderstorms thrived in an environment featuring most unstable CAPE of 500-1000 J/kg and 30-50 knots of effective deep layer vertical wind shear. ||Thunderstorms first broke out over counties west of Highway 281 between noon and 130 PM CDT, well to the west of a sharp synoptic cold front extending from extreme southeast Nebraska to south central Kansas. Through the rest of the afternoon and into the evening, multiple clusters and line segments of fast-moving elevated storms streamed north-northeast across areas mainly near and east of Highway 281, before finally exiting the local area east of the Highway 81 corridor around 9 PM CDT. On the synoptic scale aloft, this event occurred just downstream from a powerful, closed mid level low centered over Colorado, which anchored an expansive and highly-amplified longwave trough dominating the western half of the United States. The rapid increase in afternoon thunderstorm development was aided by the arrival of the exit region of a 100+ knot south-southwest jet streak evident at 300 millibars. As it turned out, the 9th marked the first of three consecutive notably cold April days across South Central Nebraska, as temperatures failed to exceed the mid-to-upper 30s through the 11th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail the size of pea to nickel was reported.

Through the rest of the afternoon and into the evening, multiple clusters and line segments of fast-moving elevated storms streamed north-northeast across areas mainly near and east of Highway 281, before finally exiting the local area east of the Highway 81 corridor around 9 PM CDT. On the synoptic scale aloft, this event occurred just downstream from a powerful, closed mid level low centered over Colorado, which anchored an expansive and highly-amplified longwave trough dominating the western half of the United States. The rapid increase in afternoon thunderstorm development was aided by the arrival of the exit region of a 100+ knot south-southwest jet streak evident at 300 millibars. As it turned out, the 9th marked the first of three consecutive notably cold April days across South Central Nebraska, as temperatures failed to exceed the mid-to-upper 30s through the 11th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny and dime size hail was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Sub-severe hail fell from one of numerous elevated thunderstorms that formed in a strong pulse of warm air advection and frontogenesis this Tuesday night. A shallow continental-polar air mass was in place over the northern two-thirds of the United States. With a deep upper-level trough, over the western U.S., moisture and warm air advection were occurring aloft and there was enough instability to initiate thunderstorms. One of the more significant reports was from the town of Fairmont, in Fillmore county, where hail accumulated one inch deep. While surface and mean-layer parcels were stable, the most-unstable parcels resulted in 500-1000 J/kg of CAPE.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mostly pea size hail fell with some penny size hail in the Geneva area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed in south central Nebraska during the afternoon of June 14th as an upper level trough over the northern Rockies lifted across the northern plains causing a warm front to move over eastern Nebraska.  The storms intensified as they moved across mainly northeast Nebraska and west central Iowa.  Besides large hail and damaging winds a few of the storms produced heavy rain which caused localized flash flooding.

Evening thunderstorms produced large hail and strong winds in portions of the northern Nebraska Panhandle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts were estimated at 60 mph in Chadron.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions across the area continued from April with little change.  Dundy, Hitchcock and Red Willow Counties remained in D3 (Extreme) to D4 (Exceptional) Drought.  May precipitation at cooperative observer sites ranged from 55 to 120 percent of normal.

Another burst of late winter visited mainly northern portions of South Central Nebraska on Monday the 22nd, as parts of Valley, Greeley and Nance counties received 1-4 inches of wet snow from a rather quick-moving precipitation shield that passed through the area primarily between 11 AM and 2 PM CDT. Although snow did not last all that long in any given location, it accumulated quickly as some of the highest totals per NWS cooperative observers and NeRAIN observers featured 4.5 nine miles northwest of Ord, 4 at Arcadia and eight miles north-northeast of Scotia, and 3.5 in Ord. Accompanying this falling snow was sustained north winds around 20 MPH and gusting over 30 MPH behind a sharp cold front. While accumulating snow was confined to these far northern counties, temperatures remained just warm enough across the majority of the 24-county area to keep precipitation in the form of a steady, chilly rain. Temperature-wise, this passing front ushered in a very cold few days by late April standards, as Ord airport only registered highs of 39 and 37 degrees, respectively, on the 22nd and 23rd. ||In the mid and upper levels, forcing for this event was provided in part by lift associated with a low-amplitude lead shortwave trough ejecting out ahead of a larger-scale, positively tilted trough centered along an axis from south central Canada to Idaho. Central Nebraska was also positioned under a pronounced area of upper level divergence between the exit region of a 110 knot upper jet streak oriented from northern Colorado into western Kansas, and the entrance region of another jet streak centered from northern Minnesota into Ontario. At the surface, mid-day analysis depicted the leading edge of the aforementioned strong cold front aligned from extreme southwest Kansas into southeast Nebraska, with temperatures across the local frontal zone ranging from 29 degrees at Ord, to 38 in Hastings, to 53 in Hebron.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A total of 4.0 inches of snow fell 8 miles north northeast of Scotia. This report was from an NERAIN observer.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong shortwave trough and associated upper level low began to influence the weather conditions, with a stationary front across far northern Nebraska and a dry line across far western Nebraska. Scattered severe thunderstorms focused along the aforementioned boundaries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent upper level low pressure system and associated strong shortwave, sparked scattered to numerous severe thunderstorms across western and north central Nebraska. The Storm Prediction Center had much of the area in a moderate risk for severe storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds destroyed a small metal garage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated severe thunderstorms developed over the northern Nebraska Panhandle during the early evening.  The storms produced hail up to the size of ping pong balls.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter to ping pong ball size hail was reported west of Hemingford.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This was a unique Tuesday afternoon and evening of weather across South Central Nebraska. Following a lull of several hours in the wake of overnight severe thunderstorms that produced widespread large hail and damaging winds, winter collided with spring to produce showers and elevated thunderstorms with a varied mix of sleet, hail and freezing rain. This was all made possible by strong frontogenesis and easterly warm air advection evident at 850 millibars flowing over the top of a shallow, cold low level airmass featuring mid-afternoon surface temperatures only between the low-20s and low-30s across most of the area, or 30+ degrees below normal for the date. In addition to widespread accumulating sleet in many locations, hail ranging from dime to quarter size was reported across various parts of the 24-county area mainly between 130-600 PM CDT. This included hail stones to quarter size at Fullerton, and nickel size at Grand Island airport, in Benedict and near Hastings. To cite just one example of the unusual nature of this event, as nickel size hail was falling with thunderstorms in the Hastings area at 530 PM CDT, the surface temperature was 25 degrees and north winds were sustained around 25 MPH, producing a wind chill temperature around 10 degrees. Despite this very cold surface regime, elevated thunderstorms thrived in an environment featuring most unstable CAPE of 500-1000 J/kg and 30-50 knots of effective deep layer vertical wind shear. ||Thunderstorms first broke out over counties west of Highway 281 between noon and 130 PM CDT, well to the west of a sharp synoptic cold front extending from extreme southeast Nebraska to south central Kansas. Through the rest of the afternoon and into the evening, multiple clusters and line segments of fast-moving elevated storms streamed north-northeast across areas mainly near and east of Highway 281, before finally exiting the local area east of the Highway 81 corridor around 9 PM CDT. On the synoptic scale aloft, this event occurred just downstream from a powerful, closed mid level low centered over Colorado, which anchored an expansive and highly-amplified longwave trough dominating the western half of the United States. The rapid increase in afternoon thunderstorm development was aided by the arrival of the exit region of a 100+ knot south-southwest jet streak evident at 300 millibars. As it turned out, the 9th marked the first of three consecutive notably cold April days across South Central Nebraska, as temperatures failed to exceed the mid-to-upper 30s through the 11th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dime to nickel size hail fell for five minutes. Report from Nebraska Rainfall Assessment and Information Network.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For roughly seven hours on this Monday night and early Tuesday morning, the first widespread severe thunderstorm event of 2013 impacted mainly the northwest two-thirds of the 24-county area, largely focused northwest of a line from Alma-Hastings-Osceola along a very strong cold front advancing southeast through the region. Between 9 PM and 4 AM CDT, several rounds of multicell storms along with a few embedded supercell structures tracked north to northeast across the area, resulting in multiple reports of severe-criteria hail ranging from quarter to golf ball size and damaging winds of at least 60-70 MPH. Some of the most extensive damage occurred in Kearney around 1 AM CDT, due to measured wind gusts up to 76 MPH and hail up to golf ball size. In addition to considerable tree damage within the city, a mobile home was destroyed and several buildings on the University of Nebraska-Kearney campus sustained damage. This included the university library where a portion of the roof was ripped off, resulting in water damage to over 6,000 books. There were also several reports of severe winds and damage in and near Holdrege between 12-1 AM CDT, including a measured 74 MPH gust at the airport, a semi truck blown over on Highway 6/34 a few miles southwest of town and a large metal outbuilding destroyed a few miles northeast of town. Other severe weather reports during the night included quarter size hail at the Hastings NWS office and golf ball size hail in Phillips. In addition to hail and wind, widespread rainfall of 1-3 inches resulted from multiple storms training over the same general southwest-to-northeast corridor, with most 2+ inch amounts concentrated within 15 miles either side of a line extending from near Holdrege-Cairo-Fullerton. A few observers reported totals in excess of 3 inches, including 3.61 near Rockville and 3.41 near Scotia. Although flooding issues were held to a minimum, water was reported over a road near St. Libory. ||The first storms of the evening developed near and north of the Interstate 80 corridor between 9 PM and midnight CDT. Some of these initial storms exhibited low-level rotation on radar, and were likely near-surface based in an environment featuring temperatures in the low-60s and dewpoints in the upper-50s, although no tornadoes were reported. After midnight CDT, storms gradually tended to become more elongated and elevated as they were undercut by the surging cold front, although interactions with this front likely enhanced the damaging winds in the Holdrege and Kearney areas. Around 4 AM CDT, the last severe-warned storm exited the local area eastward out of Polk County, and thereafter only a few weak storms lingered through sunrise. As evidence of the strength of the cold front, 4 AM CDT temperatures across South Central Nebraska ranged from 33 degrees at Ord, to 44 at Hastings, to 66 at Hebron. On the mesoscale, the primary trigger for this long-lasting severe weather episode was pronounced low-to-mid level warm air advection and resultant upglide over the top of the surging surface cold front, which effectively bisected Nebraska from southwest-to-northeast at sunset. After dark, an 850 millibar low level jet ramped up to around 40 knots, resulting in a rapid uptick in convection. Around midnight, most unstable CAPE values averaged around 1000 J/kg in the presence of very strong 0-6 kilometer vertical wind shear of 60-70 knots. On the synoptic scale, this event occurred downstream from an expansive, highly-amplified mid level trough centered near the Four Corners, and in the presence of low-amplitude shortwave troughs ejecting northeast from the parent system into the Central Plains on strengthening southwesterly flow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was covering St. Paul Road.

There were also several reports of severe winds and damage in and near Holdrege between 12-1 AM CDT, including a measured 74 MPH gust at the airport, a semi truck blown over on Highway 6/34 a few miles southwest of town and a large metal outbuilding destroyed a few miles northeast of town. Other severe weather reports during the night included quarter size hail at the Hastings NWS office and golf ball size hail in Phillips. In addition to hail and wind, widespread rainfall of 1-3 inches resulted from multiple storms training over the same general southwest-to-northeast corridor, with most 2+ inch amounts concentrated within 15 miles either side of a line extending from near Holdrege-Cairo-Fullerton. A few observers reported totals in excess of 3 inches, including 3.61 near Rockville and 3.41 near Scotia. Although flooding issues were held to a minimum, water was reported over a road near St. Libory. ||The first storms of the evening developed near and north of the Interstate 80 corridor between 9 PM and midnight CDT. Some of these initial storms exhibited low-level rotation on radar, and were likely near-surface based in an environment featuring temperatures in the low-60s and dewpoints in the upper-50s, although no tornadoes were reported. After midnight CDT, storms gradually tended to become more elongated and elevated as they were undercut by the surging cold front, although interactions with this front likely enhanced the damaging winds in the Holdrege and Kearney areas. Around 4 AM CDT, the last severe-warned storm exited the local area eastward out of Polk County, and thereafter only a few weak storms lingered through sunrise. As evidence of the strength of the cold front, 4 AM CDT temperatures across South Central Nebraska ranged from 33 degrees at Ord, to 44 at Hastings, to 66 at Hebron. On the mesoscale, the primary trigger for this long-lasting severe weather episode was pronounced low-to-mid level warm air advection and resultant upglide over the top of the surging surface cold front, which effectively bisected Nebraska from southwest-to-northeast at sunset. After dark, an 850 millibar low level jet ramped up to around 40 knots, resulting in a rapid uptick in convection. Around midnight, most unstable CAPE values averaged around 1000 J/kg in the presence of very strong 0-6 kilometer vertical wind shear of 60-70 knots. On the synoptic scale, this event occurred downstream from an expansive, highly-amplified mid level trough centered near the Four Corners, and in the presence of low-amplitude shortwave troughs ejecting northeast from the parent system into the Central Plains on strengthening southwesterly flow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Between 730-815 pm CDT on this Saturday evening, a severe multicell storm tracked northeast through western portions of Dawson County, pelting areas in and near Gothenburg with large hail ranging from quarter to baseball size. In Gothenburg itself, hail up to golf ball size was reported, with the baseball size report originating seven miles northeast of town before the storm slipped out of the local area into Custer County. This hail storm was by far the most notable of a handful of multicell storms that affected western portions of South Central Nebraska mainly between 730-1030 pm CDT. Prior to entering the local area, the initial development of strong to severe convection took place during the mid-late afternoon hours near the Nebraska-Kansas-Colorado border before tracking east-northeast. After darkness fell, the South Central Nebraska storms steadily faded away, as most activity ended up confined to Dawson, Gosper, Furnas, Harlan and Sherman counties. ||On the large scale aloft, this event occurred under fairly modest quasi-zonal flow, as South Central Nebraska remained well-removed from stronger flow associated with broad longwave troughs centered near both the northwest and northeast corners of the United States. However, the presence of subtle, low-amplitude shortwave troughs passing through the flow helped ignite strong to severe convection in an environment featuring steep low to mid level lapse rates, around 2500 J/kg mixed-layer CAPE, and around 35 knots of 0-6 kilometer deep layer wind shear. At the surface, late afternoon and evening dewpoints in the Dawson County area averaged in the low-mid 60s, as steady south-southeast breezes prevailed to the east of a High Plains lee trough and south of a synoptic warm front aligned from western South Dakota into far eastern Nebraska.

A lone, compact-but-intense supercell drifted slowly northeast across western portions of Valley County mainly between 8-10 pm CDT on this Sunday evening, dumping large hail up to two inches in diameter. This storm initially flared up just west of the local area in east-central Custer County around 730 pm CDT, and eventually exited Valley County into Garfield County in a weakening phase. Later in the night, primarily between 1130 pm-330 am CDT, the southern fringes of a linear mesoscale convective system (MCS) focused across north central and eastern Nebraska brushed through areas northeast of a line from Ord-York, but these storms remained sub-severe within the local area. Finally, even later into the night and early morning hours primarily between 430-700 am CDT, a southwest-northeast cluster of strong storms tracked through counties mainly along and north of Interstate 80, with penny size hail reported in Central City around sunrise. The cumulative result of all evening and overnight convection was a swath of 1-2+ inches of rain for many areas northeast of an Ord-York line, including 2.90 inches two miles west of Genoa. ||Leading into the initial evening supercell development, the mid and upper level pattern featured moderate quasi-zonal flow, with South Central Nebraska positioned downstream of a potent trough tracking through the Pacific Northwest and Northern Rockies. At the surface, the early evening map highlighted a well-defined quasi-stationary front aligned east-west through the heart of central and eastern Nebraska, providing a focus for low level convergence and storm development. Mesoscale parameters around the time of the Valley County supercell featured a favorable severe weather environment, with mixed-layer CAPE around 3000 J/kg and 0-6 kilometer deep layer wind shear around 35 knots. Low level shear near the surface front was also favorable for tornadic development, but only wall clouds and brief funnel clouds were reported in or near Valley County.

A strong shortwave trough and associated upper level low began to influence the weather conditions, with a stationary front across far northern Nebraska and a dry line across far western Nebraska. Scattered severe thunderstorms focused along the aforementioned boundaries.

Another burst of late winter visited mainly northern portions of South Central Nebraska on Monday the 22nd, as parts of Valley, Greeley and Nance counties received 1-4 inches of wet snow from a rather quick-moving precipitation shield that passed through the area primarily between 11 AM and 2 PM CDT. Although snow did not last all that long in any given location, it accumulated quickly as some of the highest totals per NWS cooperative observers and NeRAIN observers featured 4.5 nine miles northwest of Ord, 4 at Arcadia and eight miles north-northeast of Scotia, and 3.5 in Ord. Accompanying this falling snow was sustained north winds around 20 MPH and gusting over 30 MPH behind a sharp cold front. While accumulating snow was confined to these far northern counties, temperatures remained just warm enough across the majority of the 24-county area to keep precipitation in the form of a steady, chilly rain. Temperature-wise, this passing front ushered in a very cold few days by late April standards, as Ord airport only registered highs of 39 and 37 degrees, respectively, on the 22nd and 23rd. ||In the mid and upper levels, forcing for this event was provided in part by lift associated with a low-amplitude lead shortwave trough ejecting out ahead of a larger-scale, positively tilted trough centered along an axis from south central Canada to Idaho. Central Nebraska was also positioned under a pronounced area of upper level divergence between the exit region of a 110 knot upper jet streak oriented from northern Colorado into western Kansas, and the entrance region of another jet streak centered from northern Minnesota into Ontario. At the surface, mid-day analysis depicted the leading edge of the aforementioned strong cold front aligned from extreme southwest Kansas into southeast Nebraska, with temperatures across the local frontal zone ranging from 29 degrees at Ord, to 38 in Hastings, to 53 in Hebron.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A total of 4.5 inches of snow fell 9 miles northwest of Ord. This report was from an NERAIN observer.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent upper level low pressure system and associated strong shortwave, sparked scattered to numerous severe thunderstorms across western and north central Nebraska. The Storm Prediction Center had much of the area in a moderate risk for severe storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large tree limbs down and hardwood trees trunks were snapped. Power lines were down in the vicinity as well.

A powerful winter storm moved into the central Plains during the weekend of March 9-10th. The system initially brought warm weather and scattered showers and thunderstorms on the evening of the 8th into the morning of the 9th. As the main upper level storm system emerged on the Plains late on the 9th, a strong cold front moved from northwest to southeast across the area. Precipitation redeveloped behind this cold front associated with the upper level storm system and quickly changed from light rain to light snow during the early morning hours of the 10th. Isolated reports of sleet and light freezing rain were reported as the precipitation changed from light rain to snow, but significant ice accumulations were not reported. The snow, occasionally heavy, then continued through the day on the 10th and ended in the early morning hours on Monday March 11. Also associated with the system were strong northwest winds. Wind speeds of 30 to 40 mph were common during the day on Sunday March 10th leading to considerable blowing and drifting of the falling snow and reduced visibilities. This combination led to numerous traffic accidents and for authorities to close Interstate 80 for a time west of Lincoln in Lancaster and Seward Counties. Most schools were closed on Monday the 11th due to the poor travel conditions. Some of the higher snowfall totals ranged from 8.5 inches at the Naitonal Weather Service in Valley and in Seward, 9 inches at Eppley Airfield in Omaha and also in North Bend, 11 inches was reported in David City, and Fremont recorded 11.5 inches. Generally snowfall amounts of 6 to 11 inches were common with the event in the heaviest band.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Area automated observations indicated winds of 35 to 40 mph were common from late morning and into the afternoon hours on the 10th. The combination of the winds and heavy falling snow resulted in considerable blowing and drifting snow and very low visibilities for several hours. These conditions resulted in authorities closing both directions of Interstate 80 for several hours on the 10th from Lincoln west through Lancaster County. The poor road conditions also led to one fatal accident on a state highway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions improved little across eastern Nebraska during January.  Precipitation during the month generally ranged from 1/4 inch or less in northeast Nebraska to nearly an inch across the southeast.  These amounts were near or a little above normal across the southeast but a little below average elsewhere.  Thus, Exceptional Drought conditions continued in northeast Nebraska near and northwest of a line from Columbus to Pender with the Drought intensity remaining in the Extreme category southeast of there from around Lincoln and Beatrice northeast through Omaha and Tekamah.  Conditions were slightly better southeast of Lincoln and Omaha, but they were still in the Severe category.  The drought persisted into February.

Wind speeds of 30 to 40 mph were common during the day on Sunday March 10th leading to considerable blowing and drifting of the falling snow and reduced visibilities. This combination led to numerous traffic accidents and for authorities to close Interstate 80 for a time west of Lincoln in Lancaster and Seward Counties. Most schools were closed on Monday the 11th due to the poor travel conditions. Some of the higher snowfall totals ranged from 8.5 inches at the Naitonal Weather Service in Valley and in Seward, 9 inches at Eppley Airfield in Omaha and also in North Bend, 11 inches was reported in David City, and Fremont recorded 11.5 inches. Generally snowfall amounts of 6 to 11 inches were common with the event in the heaviest band.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread heavy snow was reported across the county from the event. Snowfall totals generally ranged from 7 to 10 inches. In Ceresco 9.1 inches fell, and 8.2 was reported south of Mead. Amateur radio reported 9 inches in Cedar Bluffs and 6 inches near Linwood.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful winter storm moved into the central Plains during the weekend of March 9-10th. The system initially brought warm weather and scattered showers and thunderstorms on the evening of the 8th into the morning of the 9th. As the main upper level storm system emerged on the Plains late on the 9th, a strong cold front moved from northwest to southeast across the area. Precipitation redeveloped behind this cold front associated with the upper level storm system and quickly changed from light rain to light snow during the early morning hours of the 10th. Isolated reports of sleet and light freezing rain were reported as the precipitation changed from light rain to snow, but significant ice accumulations were not reported. The snow, occasionally heavy, then continued through the day on the 10th and ended in the early morning hours on Monday March 11. Also associated with the system were strong northwest winds. Wind speeds of 30 to 40 mph were common during the day on Sunday March 10th leading to considerable blowing and drifting of the falling snow and reduced visibilities. This combination led to numerous traffic accidents and for authorities to close Interstate 80 for a time west of Lincoln in Lancaster and Seward Counties. Most schools were closed on Monday the 11th due to the poor travel conditions. Some of the higher snowfall totals ranged from 8.5 inches at the Naitonal Weather Service in Valley and in Seward, 9 inches at Eppley Airfield in Omaha and also in North Bend, 11 inches was reported in David City, and Fremont recorded 11.5 inches. Generally snowfall amounts of 6 to 11 inches were common with the event in the heaviest band.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Area automated observations indicated winds of 35 to 40 mph were common from late morning and into the afternoon hours on the 10th. The combination of the winds and heavy falling snow resulted in considerable blowing and drifting snow and very low visibilities for several hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Between the late afternoon of Tuesday the 29th and the pre-dawn hours of Wednesday the 30th, southeast portions of the area received a widespread 2 to 5 inches of snow, with very localized higher amounts up to 6 inches. The majority of snow fell during the evening hours, generally between 6 PM and midnight CST. Although snow ended in all areas by daybreak on the 30th, morning commuters were met with very slick roads. Strong winds were not a significant player during this event, but at least minor blowing snow issues developed during the overnight hours as sustained north winds increased to 20-30 MPH.||There was a rather sharp divide across South Central Nebraska between those areas that received 2+ inches of snow, and locations farther northwest that hardly saw any. In general, most locations northwest of a line from Beaver City-Grand Island-Osceola received less than 1, and in many cases barely even a trace. According to NWS cooperative observers and NeRAIN observers, a few of the highest totals included 6 near Tobias along the Fillmore-Saline County line, 5.3 near Superior and 5 at Hubbell. Along the far northern gradient of the snow shield, Hastings measured 2.4 while Grand Island reported merely 0.2. On the large scale aloft, forcing was provided by an expansive longwave trough passing through the central United States. On a more localized scale, South Central Nebraska snowfall focused underneath a band of mid level frontogenesis evident at 700 millibars, and within the entrance region of a 140+ knot upper jet streak centered from northeast Nebraska into Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals throughout the county generally ranged from 3 to 5 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: According to the U.S. Drought Monitor, January marked the seventh consecutive month of severe (or worse) drought across South Central Nebraska, as one of the worst droughts since the 1950s, which took hold during the summer of 2012, continued into the new year. According to Drought Monitor weekly issuances, there was essentially no change whatsoever in drought classification during the month. Category D4 exceptional drought (the worst possible category) remained firmly in place across all or part of 15 counties, primarily those north of Interstate 80 and also west of Highway 183. Meanwhile, category D3 extreme drought persisted across most of the other nine counties, while only a small sliver of category D2 severe drought held on in southern Thayer County. Based on reports from around 50 NWS cooperative observers, and also incorporating radar-derived estimation, over two-thirds of the 24-county area observed above below normal precipitation in January (per normals generally between 0.40-0.70). Continuing trends from recent months, the driest counties were in the northwest, especially northwest of a line from Elwood-Grand Island-Osceola. In contrast, above normal monthly precipitation dominated all or part of several southeastern counties such as Clay, Fillmore, Webster, Nuckolls and Thayer. Some of this above normal precipitation in the southeast was tied to a widespread 2-5 snowfall from the 29th-30th. Per NWS cooperative observers, a few of the highest January precipitation totals included 1.13 at Hebron, 1.05 near Superior and 1.04 at Bruning. On the much lower side of things were sites such as Cozad (0.01), Arcadia (0.11) and Osceola and Belgrade (0.12). Grand Island tied for its 20th-driest January on record out of 118 years with only 0.16.||Despite below normal January precipitation in most areas, many locations near and especially south of Interstate 80 tallied above normal precipitation for the combined months of December and January. Even so, this recent trend did little to ease serious drought conditions, largely due to the fact that winter precipitation events generally account for less than 10 percent of total annual precipitation. The January USDA Nebraska Weather and Crops report indicated little change since December, with around 50 percent of the state-averaged wheat crop still in poor to very poor condition, and only around 12% in good condition. Hay and forage supplies across the state averaged 40-50% short to very short, well below levels from one year ago in January 2012.

The drought that began in June 2012 and expanded to become an Exceptional (D4) Drought in October 2012 continues into February 2013. While the weather in February became more active, with two major snowfall events, only minor relief or recharge to the depleted soil moisture occurred. Long-term impacts to hydrological and ecological interests are expected to linger. In February, areas of significant snowfall did occur with monthly precipitations departures ranging from around a half inch above normal at North Platte and Valentine to near normal at Broken Bow and Imperial.  This helped to keep fire dangers and long term impacts from worsening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The drought that began in June 2012 continued through February 2013. The exceptional (D4) drought lingered despite near normal precipitation during the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions improved little across eastern Nebraska during January.  Precipitation during the month generally ranged from 1/4 inch or less in northeast Nebraska to nearly an inch across the southeast.  These amounts were near or a little above normal across the southeast but a little below average elsewhere.  Thus, Exceptional Drought conditions continued in northeast Nebraska near and northwest of a line from Columbus to Pender with the Drought intensity remaining in the Extreme category southeast of there from around Lincoln and Beatrice northeast through Omaha and Tekamah.  Conditions were slightly better southeast of Lincoln and Omaha, but they were still in the Severe category.  The drought persisted into February.

The heaviest of the snowfall fell in the southern half of the county with 11.5 inches reported in Fremont, 9 inches in North Bend, 7.5 in Nickerson, and 7 inches in Uehling. Snowfall totals quickly diminished in the northwest part of the county around Dodge and Snyder.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful winter storm moved into the central Plains during the weekend of March 9-10th. The system initially brought warm weather and scattered showers and thunderstorms on the evening of the 8th into the morning of the 9th. As the main upper level storm system emerged on the Plains late on the 9th, a strong cold front moved from northwest to southeast across the area. Precipitation redeveloped behind this cold front associated with the upper level storm system and quickly changed from light rain to light snow during the early morning hours of the 10th. Isolated reports of sleet and light freezing rain were reported as the precipitation changed from light rain to snow, but significant ice accumulations were not reported. The snow, occasionally heavy, then continued through the day on the 10th and ended in the early morning hours on Monday March 11. Also associated with the system were strong northwest winds. Wind speeds of 30 to 40 mph were common during the day on Sunday March 10th leading to considerable blowing and drifting of the falling snow and reduced visibilities. This combination led to numerous traffic accidents and for authorities to close Interstate 80 for a time west of Lincoln in Lancaster and Seward Counties. Most schools were closed on Monday the 11th due to the poor travel conditions. Some of the higher snowfall totals ranged from 8.5 inches at the Naitonal Weather Service in Valley and in Seward, 9 inches at Eppley Airfield in Omaha and also in North Bend, 11 inches was reported in David City, and Fremont recorded 11.5 inches. Generally snowfall amounts of 6 to 11 inches were common with the event in the heaviest band.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Area automated observations indicated winds of 35 to 40 mph were common from late morning and into the afternoon hours on the 10th. The combination of the winds and heavy falling snow resulted in considerable blowing and drifting snow and very low visibilities for several hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: According to the U.S. Drought Monitor, January marked the seventh consecutive month of severe (or worse) drought across South Central Nebraska, as one of the worst droughts since the 1950s, which took hold during the summer of 2012, continued into the new year. According to Drought Monitor weekly issuances, there was essentially no change whatsoever in drought classification during the month. Category D4 exceptional drought (the worst possible category) remained firmly in place across all or part of 15 counties, primarily those north of Interstate 80 and also west of Highway 183. Meanwhile, category D3 extreme drought persisted across most of the other nine counties, while only a small sliver of category D2 severe drought held on in southern Thayer County. Based on reports from around 50 NWS cooperative observers, and also incorporating radar-derived estimation, over two-thirds of the 24-county area observed above below normal precipitation in January (per normals generally between 0.40-0.70). Continuing trends from recent months, the driest counties were in the northwest, especially northwest of a line from Elwood-Grand Island-Osceola. In contrast, above normal monthly precipitation dominated all or part of several southeastern counties such as Clay, Fillmore, Webster, Nuckolls and Thayer. Some of this above normal precipitation in the southeast was tied to a widespread 2-5 snowfall from the 29th-30th. Per NWS cooperative observers, a few of the highest January precipitation totals included 1.13 at Hebron, 1.05 near Superior and 1.04 at Bruning. On the much lower side of things were sites such as Cozad (0.01), Arcadia (0.11) and Osceola and Belgrade (0.12). Grand Island tied for its 20th-driest January on record out of 118 years with only 0.16.||Despite below normal January precipitation in most areas, many locations near and especially south of Interstate 80 tallied above normal precipitation for the combined months of December and January. Even so, this recent trend did little to ease serious drought conditions, largely due to the fact that winter precipitation events generally account for less than 10 percent of total annual precipitation. The January USDA Nebraska Weather and Crops report indicated little change since December, with around 50 percent of the state-averaged wheat crop still in poor to very poor condition, and only around 12% in good condition. Hay and forage supplies across the state averaged 40-50% short to very short, well below levels from one year ago in January 2012.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions improved little across eastern Nebraska during January.  Precipitation during the month generally ranged from 1/4 inch or less in northeast Nebraska to nearly an inch across the southeast.  These amounts were near or a little above normal across the southeast but a little below average elsewhere.  Thus, Exceptional Drought conditions continued in northeast Nebraska near and northwest of a line from Columbus to Pender with the Drought intensity remaining in the Extreme category southeast of there from around Lincoln and Beatrice northeast through Omaha and Tekamah.  Conditions were slightly better southeast of Lincoln and Omaha, but they were still in the Severe category.  The drought persisted into February.

Wind speeds of 30 to 40 mph were common during the day on Sunday March 10th leading to considerable blowing and drifting of the falling snow and reduced visibilities. This combination led to numerous traffic accidents and for authorities to close Interstate 80 for a time west of Lincoln in Lancaster and Seward Counties. Most schools were closed on Monday the 11th due to the poor travel conditions. Some of the higher snowfall totals ranged from 8.5 inches at the Naitonal Weather Service in Valley and in Seward, 9 inches at Eppley Airfield in Omaha and also in North Bend, 11 inches was reported in David City, and Fremont recorded 11.5 inches. Generally snowfall amounts of 6 to 11 inches were common with the event in the heaviest band.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Area automated observations indicated winds of 35 to 40 mph were common from late morning and into the afternoon hours on the 10th. The combination of the winds and heavy falling snow resulted in considerable blowing and drifting snow and very low visibilities for several hours. These conditions resulted in authorities closing both directions of Interstate 80 for several hours on the 10th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Between the late afternoon of Tuesday the 29th and the pre-dawn hours of Wednesday the 30th, southeast portions of the area received a widespread 2 to 5 inches of snow, with very localized higher amounts up to 6 inches. The majority of snow fell during the evening hours, generally between 6 PM and midnight CST. Although snow ended in all areas by daybreak on the 30th, morning commuters were met with very slick roads. Strong winds were not a significant player during this event, but at least minor blowing snow issues developed during the overnight hours as sustained north winds increased to 20-30 MPH.||There was a rather sharp divide across South Central Nebraska between those areas that received 2+ inches of snow, and locations farther northwest that hardly saw any. In general, most locations northwest of a line from Beaver City-Grand Island-Osceola received less than 1, and in many cases barely even a trace. According to NWS cooperative observers and NeRAIN observers, a few of the highest totals included 6 near Tobias along the Fillmore-Saline County line, 5.3 near Superior and 5 at Hubbell. Along the far northern gradient of the snow shield, Hastings measured 2.4 while Grand Island reported merely 0.2. On the large scale aloft, forcing was provided by an expansive longwave trough passing through the central United States. On a more localized scale, South Central Nebraska snowfall focused underneath a band of mid level frontogenesis evident at 700 millibars, and within the entrance region of a 140+ knot upper jet streak centered from northeast Nebraska into Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals throughout the county generally ranged from 2 to 5 inches, with the highest totals falling across the southeast half of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions improved little across eastern Nebraska during January.  Precipitation during the month generally ranged from 1/4 inch or less in northeast Nebraska to nearly an inch across the southeast.  These amounts were near or a little above normal across the southeast but a little below average elsewhere.  Thus, Exceptional Drought conditions continued in northeast Nebraska near and northwest of a line from Columbus to Pender with the Drought intensity remaining in the Extreme category southeast of there from around Lincoln and Beatrice northeast through Omaha and Tekamah.  Conditions were slightly better southeast of Lincoln and Omaha, but they were still in the Severe category.  The drought persisted into February.

According to the U.S. Drought Monitor, January marked the seventh consecutive month of severe (or worse) drought across South Central Nebraska, as one of the worst droughts since the 1950s, which took hold during the summer of 2012, continued into the new year. According to Drought Monitor weekly issuances, there was essentially no change whatsoever in drought classification during the month. Category D4 exceptional drought (the worst possible category) remained firmly in place across all or part of 15 counties, primarily those north of Interstate 80 and also west of Highway 183. Meanwhile, category D3 extreme drought persisted across most of the other nine counties, while only a small sliver of category D2 severe drought held on in southern Thayer County. Based on reports from around 50 NWS cooperative observers, and also incorporating radar-derived estimation, over two-thirds of the 24-county area observed above below normal precipitation in January (per normals generally between 0.40-0.70). Continuing trends from recent months, the driest counties were in the northwest, especially northwest of a line from Elwood-Grand Island-Osceola. In contrast, above normal monthly precipitation dominated all or part of several southeastern counties such as Clay, Fillmore, Webster, Nuckolls and Thayer. Some of this above normal precipitation in the southeast was tied to a widespread 2-5 snowfall from the 29th-30th. Per NWS cooperative observers, a few of the highest January precipitation totals included 1.13 at Hebron, 1.05 near Superior and 1.04 at Bruning. On the much lower side of things were sites such as Cozad (0.01), Arcadia (0.11) and Osceola and Belgrade (0.12). Grand Island tied for its 20th-driest January on record out of 118 years with only 0.16.||Despite below normal January precipitation in most areas, many locations near and especially south of Interstate 80 tallied above normal precipitation for the combined months of December and January. Even so, this recent trend did little to ease serious drought conditions, largely due to the fact that winter precipitation events generally account for less than 10 percent of total annual precipitation. The January USDA Nebraska Weather and Crops report indicated little change since December, with around 50 percent of the state-averaged wheat crop still in poor to very poor condition, and only around 12% in good condition. Hay and forage supplies across the state averaged 40-50% short to very short, well below levels from one year ago in January 2012.

The drought that began in June 2012 and expanded to become an Exceptional (D4) Drought in October 2012 continues into February 2013. While the weather in February became more active, with two major snowfall events, only minor relief or recharge to the depleted soil moisture occurred. Long-term impacts to hydrological and ecological interests are expected to linger. In February, areas of significant snowfall did occur with monthly precipitations departures ranging from around a half inch above normal at North Platte and Valentine to near normal at Broken Bow and Imperial.  This helped to keep fire dangers and long term impacts from worsening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The drought that began in June 2012 continued through February 2013. The exceptional (D4) drought lingered despite near normal precipitation during the month.

A winter storm tracked from the southern Rockies into the the Central Plains. Strong northerly winds of 30 to 40 mph and gusty winds created blizzard conditions across much of western and north central Nebraska on February 9-10 2013.

Northerly winds of 30 to 40 mph created blizzard conditions with considerable blowing and drifting and low visibility of a quarter mile or less in open areas.  Snowfall amounts ranged from 5 to 7 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful winter storm moved into the central Plains during the weekend of March 9-10th. The system initially brought warm weather and scattered showers and thunderstorms on the evening of the 8th into the morning of the 9th. As the main upper level storm system emerged on the Plains late on the 9th, a strong cold front moved from northwest to southeast across the area. Precipitation redeveloped behind this cold front associated with the upper level storm system and quickly changed from light rain to light snow during the early morning hours of the 10th. Isolated reports of sleet and light freezing rain were reported as the precipitation changed from light rain to snow, but significant ice accumulations were not reported. The snow, occasionally heavy, then continued through the day on the 10th and ended in the early morning hours on Monday March 11. Also associated with the system were strong northwest winds. Wind speeds of 30 to 40 mph were common during the day on Sunday March 10th leading to considerable blowing and drifting of the falling snow and reduced visibilities. This combination led to numerous traffic accidents and for authorities to close Interstate 80 for a time west of Lincoln in Lancaster and Seward Counties. Most schools were closed on Monday the 11th due to the poor travel conditions. Some of the higher snowfall totals ranged from 8.5 inches at the Naitonal Weather Service in Valley and in Seward, 9 inches at Eppley Airfield in Omaha and also in North Bend, 11 inches was reported in David City, and Fremont recorded 11.5 inches. Generally snowfall amounts of 6 to 11 inches were common with the event in the heaviest band.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Area automated observations indicated winds of 40 to 45 mph were common from late morning and into the afternoon hours on the 10th. The combination of the winds and heavy falling snow resulted in considerable blowing and drifting snow and very low visibilities for several hours.
