EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifted north across southwest Nebraska and was the focus for thunderstorms to develop, intensify and produce large hail and damaging winds. Frontier County was hit the hardest by damaging winds estimated up to 80 mph as a thunderstorm bowed out. The high winds produced damage at 12 farmsteads. The most extensive damage occurred just north of County Road 741, where two large buildings and nine silos were destroyed. The wind damage included over 60 center pivot irrigation systems, livestock killed, large tree limbs down and approximately 40 power poles snapped. The McCook Public Power District reported 173 consumers lost power during the storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A bow echo produced straight-line winds that were estimated up to 80 mph for nearly 30 minutes. The wind damage encompassed over 50 square miles in central and northeast Frontier County, in an area from east of Stockville and west of West Canyon Road to the south and west of Eustis. The high winds produced damage at 12 farmsteads. The most extensive damage just north of County Road 741, where two large metal buildings and nine 10 to 15 thousand bushel silos were destroyed. The wind damage included roofs of homes and outbuildings, windows blown out, over 60 center pivot irrigation systems overturned, large tree limbs down and livestock killed. The strong winds took down power lines and snapped 40 power poles, where the McCook Public Power District reported 173 consumers lost power.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm tracked from the southern Rockies into the the Central Plains. Strong northerly winds of 30 to 40 mph and gusty winds created blizzard conditions across much of western and north central Nebraska on February 9-10 2013.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions began across Loup County during the early morning hours and persisted into the mid afternoon hours on February 10th. Northerly winds of 30 to 40 mph created blizzard conditions with considerable blowing and drifting and low visibility of a quarter mile or less at times in open areas.  Snowfall amounts ranged from 4 to 7 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful winter storm moved into the central Plains during the weekend of March 9-10th. The system initially brought warm weather and scattered showers and thunderstorms on the evening of the 8th into the morning of the 9th. As the main upper level storm system emerged on the Plains late on the 9th, a strong cold front moved from northwest to southeast across the area. Precipitation redeveloped behind this cold front associated with the upper level storm system and quickly changed from light rain to light snow during the early morning hours of the 10th. Isolated reports of sleet and light freezing rain were reported as the precipitation changed from light rain to snow, but significant ice accumulations were not reported. The snow, occasionally heavy, then continued through the day on the 10th and ended in the early morning hours on Monday March 11. Also associated with the system were strong northwest winds. Wind speeds of 30 to 40 mph were common during the day on Sunday March 10th leading to considerable blowing and drifting of the falling snow and reduced visibilities. This combination led to numerous traffic accidents and for authorities to close Interstate 80 for a time west of Lincoln in Lancaster and Seward Counties. Most schools were closed on Monday the 11th due to the poor travel conditions. Some of the higher snowfall totals ranged from 8.5 inches at the Naitonal Weather Service in Valley and in Seward, 9 inches at Eppley Airfield in Omaha and also in North Bend, 11 inches was reported in David City, and Fremont recorded 11.5 inches. Generally snowfall amounts of 6 to 11 inches were common with the event in the heaviest band.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Area automated observations indicated winds of 40 to 45 mph were common during the afternoon hours on the 10th. The combination of the winds and heavy falling snow resulted in considerable blowing and drifting snow and very low visibilities for several hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Between the late afternoon of Tuesday the 29th and the pre-dawn hours of Wednesday the 30th, southeast portions of the area received a widespread 2 to 5 inches of snow, with very localized higher amounts up to 6 inches. The majority of snow fell during the evening hours, generally between 6 PM and midnight CST. Although snow ended in all areas by daybreak on the 30th, morning commuters were met with very slick roads. Strong winds were not a significant player during this event, but at least minor blowing snow issues developed during the overnight hours as sustained north winds increased to 20-30 MPH.||There was a rather sharp divide across South Central Nebraska between those areas that received 2+ inches of snow, and locations farther northwest that hardly saw any. In general, most locations northwest of a line from Beaver City-Grand Island-Osceola received less than 1, and in many cases barely even a trace. According to NWS cooperative observers and NeRAIN observers, a few of the highest totals included 6 near Tobias along the Fillmore-Saline County line, 5.3 near Superior and 5 at Hubbell. Along the far northern gradient of the snow shield, Hastings measured 2.4 while Grand Island reported merely 0.2. On the large scale aloft, forcing was provided by an expansive longwave trough passing through the central United States. On a more localized scale, South Central Nebraska snowfall focused underneath a band of mid level frontogenesis evident at 700 millibars, and within the entrance region of a 140+ knot upper jet streak centered from northeast Nebraska into Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals throughout the county generally ranged from 3 to 5 inches, with an isolated 6 inch report southwest of Tobias near the Fillmore and Saline County line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper level low pressure system moved from the Rocky Mountains into the central plains spreading snow across a large portion of the nation's mid section. Light snow developed the morning of the 21st across eastern Nebraska and slowly spread east northeast throughout the day and into the early morning hours of the 22nd. A blanket of snow around 3 to 8 inches covered all of eastern Nebraska, with the heaviest amounts around Douglas, Saunders, Washington and Sarpy counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of around 6 inches fell over much of the county based on observations from surrounding sites and 5.5 inches reported in Brownville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The drought that began in June 2012 and expanded to become an Exceptional (D4) Drought in October 2012 continues into February 2013. While the weather in February became more active, with two major snowfall events, only minor relief or recharge to the depleted soil moisture occurred. Long-term impacts to hydrological and ecological interests are expected to linger. In February, areas of significant snowfall did occur with monthly precipitations departures ranging from around a half inch above normal at North Platte and Valentine to near normal at Broken Bow and Imperial.  This helped to keep fire dangers and long term impacts from worsening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The drought that began in June 2012 continued through February 2013. The exceptional (D4) drought lingered despite slightly above normal precipitation during the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions improved little across eastern Nebraska during January.  Precipitation during the month generally ranged from 1/4 inch or less in northeast Nebraska to nearly an inch across the southeast.  These amounts were near or a little above normal across the southeast but a little below average elsewhere.  Thus, Exceptional Drought conditions continued in northeast Nebraska near and northwest of a line from Columbus to Pender with the Drought intensity remaining in the Extreme category southeast of there from around Lincoln and Beatrice northeast through Omaha and Tekamah.  Conditions were slightly better southeast of Lincoln and Omaha, but they were still in the Severe category.  The drought persisted into February.

Although this Wednesday afternoon started out with a fairly low-key development of isolated strong to severe thunderstorms in northern portions of South Central Nebraska, this convective event ended up being one of the most significant heavy rain episodes of 2013 for much of the northeast two-thirds of the 24-county area, highlighted by a small zone of 8+ inch rainfall in southeastern Greeley County. In terms of actual severe weather, this event was small in scale, featuring only a handful of quarter to golf ball size hail reports in southern Greeley and northwestern Howard counties during the late afternoon and early evening, with the largest hail stones reported four miles south of Greeley at 535 pm CDT. However, as the large hail threat diminished by sunset, heavy rainfall took over as the main story for the next several hours lasting well into Thursday morning the 3rd, as the initially isolated development in northern counties morphed into a slow-moving, southward-drifting linear mesoscale convective system (MCS). By the time all convection finally exited South Central Nebraska shortly before sunrise on the 3rd, most places generally northeast of a line from Loup City-Gibbon-Blue Hill-Superior had received at least 2-3 of rain, but with a corridor of higher amounts generally 4-6 concentrated across much of Adams, northwestern Nuckolls and southwestern Clay counties. Within these counties, just a few of the highest reported rainfall totals included 6.35 a few miles south of Juniata and 4.75 a few miles south of Trumbull. At Hastings Municipal Airport, 3.49 of the storm total of 4.13 fell on the calendar day of Oct. 3rd, shattering the previous record for highest rainfall on any given day in October (2.95 on Oct. 16, 1968). Of even greater significance, however, was the incredible amount of rain that fell mainly between 4-9pm CDT within a small portion of southeastern Greeley County. Despite limited ground-truth from the epicenter of heavy rainfall in that area, an observer five miles northwest of Wolbach measured 8.46, and radar estimates suggested that even higher amounts might have fallen roughly 4-8 miles northeast of Wolbach. Although several counties in South Central Nebraska undoubtedly experienced short-term flooding issues from this event including lowland flooding and damage to rural roads, specific flooding reports were fairly limited. During these early stages, several very slow-moving to nearly stationary multicell storms dumped heavy rainfall and isolated large hail over many of the same areas. Then between 7-10 pm CDT, the initial storm clusters finally started sinking slowly southward toward the Interstate 80 corridor, while gradually expanding in coverage and elongating into a west-southwest to east-northeast linear system. From about 10 pm to 1 am CDT, the MCS propagated slowly southward across Interstate 80 and into counties along Highway 6 from near Holdrege to Geneva. During the pre-dawn hours of the 3rd, widespread convection continued drenching primarily those counties south of Interstate 80 and near/east of Highway 281, with backbuilding along the western edge of the complex resulting in the enhanced rainfall totals over Adams County and vicinity. However, a low-level focus for convective development existed in the form of a southwest-northeast oriented cold front that roughly bisected Nebraska during the late afternoon and evening hours. The initial mid-late afternoon storms flared up near this frontal boundary, aided by forcing from a subtle shortwave trough passing through the area. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warm and humid air returned to eastern Nebraska on October 2nd ahead of a cold front that was moving through eastern South Dakota to central Nebraska. As instability increased during the afternoon along and ahead of the cold front thunderstorms developed. Widespread severe weather was not observed, but isolated large hail occurred in northeast Nebraska. The thunderstorms formed into a line during the evening and slowly moved southeast producing heavy rain and isolated flash flooding. The line of thunderstorms also produced at least one report of damaging wind in southeast Nebraska overnight.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail started at dime size and grew to as large as half dollar size hail that covered the ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Historical rains fell across the front range of the Colorado Rocky Mountains September 9th-15th.  These rains, which totaled over the yearly average across this area which totaled up to 17 inches in places fell largely within the South Platte River basin.  As the water was routed downstream along the South Platte River in western Nebraska and then into the Platte River in central Nebraska record flooding was observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding occurred along the South Platte River from September 20th through September 26th as water from the historic rains in Colorado moved through the river system. Cropland and pastureland along the river was flooded. In North Platte, several homes had minor damage and 14 roads were closed as storm drains backed up in parts of the city. Southeast of North Platte, at least two roads were closed, with one business and an unknown number of outbuildings being flooded near and east of Nebraska Link 56G.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The same powerful storm system that produced severe weather across eastern Nebraska on October 3rd remain in the area on the 4th. A strong area of low pressure moved from eastern Colorado into southern South Dakota during the day. As this occurred, a dry line was sweeping across central and into eastern Nebraska. A warm front continued to lift north into northeast Nebraska and western Iowa by late afternoon. With unseasonably warm and moist air in the region the atmosphere became strongly unstable. This instability coupled with a strong wind field associated with the powerful storm system and created and environment favorable for significant severe weather. Supercells developed near the intersection of the dry line and warm front over northeast Nebraska during the afternoon and tracked northeast. The supercells were responsible for large hail, damaging winds, and significant tornadoes. Other isolated thunderstorms developed south along the dry line into western Iowa producing very large hail and damaging winds during the evening of the 4th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This EF3 tornado touched down approximately 2 miles southeast of Creighton. The tornado was initially weak with damage confined to softwood trees, irrigation systems, and siding and shingle damage. The tornado initially traveled north crossing highway 59 less than 1 mile to the east of the city of Creighton. The tornado made a direct impact to the Creighton Municipal Airport where 3 of the hangers received roof, garage and siding damage. There was also a storage building that lost a roof. The airport beacon was also lost during the tornado. Just to the south of the airport a building housing antique trackers was completely destroyed and was moved for 3 city blocks. The tornado then took a track to the northwest where a 10000 bushel grain bin was completely destroyed. There were also 5 other irrigation systems where destroyed as well as power poles snapped at their base. The tornado ended 1 mile north of Bazile Mills.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: According to the U.S. Drought Monitor, September 2013 marked the 16th consecutive month of category D2 Severe (or worse) Drought within South Central Nebraska, continuing one of the most notable droughts since the 1950s. However, there was positive news as the total number of counties affected by D2-or-worse drought continued to dwindle from 14 at the start of September to only 11 at month's end. Over the course of the month, the U.S. Drought Monitor reflected one-category improvement across much of the western half of the area, thanks in large part to a widespread 1.00-2.50 rainfall late in the month on the 27th. The resultant above normal monthly rainfall across several of these western counties was quite welcome, and targeted those places that needed it most based on long-term precipitation deficits. Meanwhile, generally the eastern half of the area saw little, if any change in drought classification during the month due to prevailing below normal rainfall. In fact, September marked the fourth-consecutive month of below normal rainfall across decent chunks of Hamilton, Polk, York and Fillmore counties. According to the U.S. Drought Monitor at month's end, the entire local 24-county area was void of not only category D4 Exceptional Drought (the worst-possible category) but also D3 Extreme Drought, for the first time since July 2012. Nonetheless, the lingering effects of longer-term dryness kept D2 Severe Drought firmly intact across southwestern counties, especially Gosper, Phelps, Furnas and Harlan. A separate, smaller zone of D2 drought also lingered much farther northeast, mainly within eastern Greeley and northwestern Nance counties. Meanwhile, category D1 Moderate Drought prevailed across the majority of South Central Nebraska at month's end, while a stripe of category D0 Abnormally Dry conditions (considered to be one category better than true drought) remained intact across most of Adams, Clay, Nuckolls and Thayer counties. ||Reports from around 50 NWS Cooperative Observers, and also radar-derived estimation, revealed that there was a pronounced west-versus-east split in September precipitation across the area (wetter in the west, drier in the east, and based on September normals that range from around 1.60��� in the far west to around 2.70��� in the far east). However, even within eastern counties there were localized exceptions of above normal rainfall, including areas near the intersection of Adams/Clay/Webster/Nuckolls counties, along with parts of southeastern Thayer County. As a whole, roughly 60 percent of South Central Nebraska had above normal monthly precipitation and 40 percent below normal. Per a combination of NWS cooperative observers and NeRAIN observers, a few of the highest September precipitation totals featured 4.87��� six miles north-northwest of Oxford, 4.81��� at Nelson, 4.34��� at Wilcox and 3.95 at Hubbell. On the opposite, drier side of the spectrum were sites such as Belgrade (1.04���), Polk (1.22���), Hastings airport (1.26���), and two miles west of Genoa (1.27���). In between these wettest and driest extremes, the majority of South Central Nebraska measured September rainfall totals somewhere between 1.50-3.25, including stations in or near Grand Island, Greeley, Gothenburg, Cambridge, Osceola, Aurora and Clay Center. ||Focusing on the longer-range picture of year-to-date precipitation (Jan. 1-Sep. 18), most of the 24-county area stood somewhere between 75-115% of normal, with the driest areas generally concentrated southwest of a line from Lexington-Red Cloud, including only 61% of normal eight miles south of Elwood. In contrast, the wettest areas so far in 2013 with near-to-slightly-above normal precipitation were largely concentrated in northern counties such as Greeley, Nance, Sherman and Howard (including 113% of normal at St. Paul), along with limited areas farther south including much of Nuckolls and western Clay counties. According to the USDA Nebraska Crop Progress and Condition Report for the week ending September 15th, state-averaged topsoil rated 60% short or very short and 40% percent adequate, a degradation from only 44% short or very short on August 18th. State-averaged subsoil moisture supplies remained in worse shape and rated 72% short to very short, up slightly from 64% four weeks prior. The state-averaged corn crop checked in 63% good to excellent and only 14% poor to very poor, although dryland corn only rated 36% good or excellent. Soybeans across the state rated 60% good or excellent, down from 72% in mid-August. Nebraska stock water supplies degraded slightly since mid-August to 28% short or very short. Pasture and range conditions also degraded very slightly, rating 38% poor or very poor, compared to 34% on August 18th.

In between these wettest and driest extremes, the majority of South Central Nebraska measured September rainfall totals somewhere between 1.50-3.25, including stations in or near Grand Island, Greeley, Gothenburg, Cambridge, Osceola, Aurora and Clay Center. ||Focusing on the longer-range picture of year-to-date precipitation (Jan. 1-Sep. 18), most of the 24-county area stood somewhere between 75-115% of normal, with the driest areas generally concentrated southwest of a line from Lexington-Red Cloud, including only 61% of normal eight miles south of Elwood. In contrast, the wettest areas so far in 2013 with near-to-slightly-above normal precipitation were largely concentrated in northern counties such as Greeley, Nance, Sherman and Howard (including 113% of normal at St. Paul), along with limited areas farther south including much of Nuckolls and western Clay counties. According to the USDA Nebraska Crop Progress and Condition Report for the week ending September 15th, state-averaged topsoil rated 60% short or very short and 40% percent adequate, a degradation from only 44% short or very short on August 18th. State-averaged subsoil moisture supplies remained in worse shape and rated 72% short to very short, up slightly from 64% four weeks prior. The state-averaged corn crop checked in 63% good to excellent and only 14% poor to very poor, although dryland corn only rated 36% good or excellent. Soybeans across the state rated 60% good or excellent, down from 72% in mid-August. Nebraska stock water supplies degraded slightly since mid-August to 28% short or very short. Pasture and range conditions also degraded very slightly, rating 38% poor or very poor, compared to 34% on August 18th.

Over the course of the month, the U.S. Drought Monitor reflected one-category improvement across much of the western half of the area, thanks in large part to a widespread 1.00-2.50 rainfall late in the month on the 27th. Meanwhile, generally the eastern half of the area saw little, if any change in drought classification during the month due to prevailing below normal rainfall. Isolated strong to severe thunderstorms flared up in eastern portions of South Central Nebraska on this Thursday evening, primarily affecting Nance, Clay and especially Fillmore counties. Although at least small hail affected various parts of these counties, including nickel size hail reported north of Genoa, all severe-criteria reports originated in Fillmore County from a supercell storm, including golf ball size stones in the Geneva area around 9 pm CDT. While hail was the main issue, strong thunderstorm winds also blew over a semi trailer just south of Geneva. In east central Fillmore County, enhanced low-level rotation was detected by radar for a short time around 930 pm CDT, but no tornadoes were reported with this storm as all tornadic activity on this night occurred east of the local area in southeast Nebraska. These storms also produced localized heavy rainfall up to around 2 inches, with the highest reported amount of 2.36 from the cooperative observer in Geneva. ||Thunderstorms initiated over Clay and Nuckolls counties between 7-8 pm CDT, along a rather sharp quasi-stationary surface front aligned from southeast Nebraska into north central/west central Kansas. During this early evening time frame, winds north of the front were from the northeast while breezes south of the boundary prevailed from the south-southeast in the presence of surface dewpoints well into the 60s F, providing ample low level convergence. Between 8-11 pm CDT, a small cluster of strong to severe storms became established within Clay and Fillmore counties, with the eastern-most storm in Fillmore County organizing into a high-precipitation supercell before exiting east into Saline County. This upswing in storm coverage/severity shortly after nightfall focused along the nose of a developing 40 knot low level jet evident at 850 millibars. As the initial round of storms along the Highway 6 corridor started to weaken and depart, a separate cluster of strong thunderstorms blossomed farther north, primarily affecting Nance, northeastern Merrick and northern Polk counties between 10 pm-12 am CDT. Non-severe thunderstorms festered across these far northeast counties into the early morning hours of the 4th, but by 3 am CDT all convection had departed South Central Nebraska for the night.||The environment was ripe for supercell development by early evening, with mesoscale parameters featuring around 1500 J/kg most-unstable CAPE in the presence of around 40 knots of 0-6 kilometer deep layer wind shear. On the synoptic scale aloft, this event occurred in the presence of subtle, low amplitude shortwave troughs embedded within strengthening southwesterly flow in the mid-upper levels, downstream from a powerful closed low centered over the Central Rockies. On the following afternoon and evening of Oct. 4th, this stout low pressure system entered the Central Plains, but South Central Nebraska was narrowly spared a significant weather event thanks to a strong surface cold front and dryline pushing east of the area by late afternoon. It was a close call, however, as several damaging tornadoes raked northeast Nebraska and northwest Iowa during the late afternoon and evening of the 4th.

During this early evening time frame, winds north of the front were from the northeast while breezes south of the boundary prevailed from the south-southeast in the presence of surface dewpoints well into the 60s F, providing ample low level convergence. Between 8-11 pm CDT, a small cluster of strong to severe storms became established within Clay and Fillmore counties, with the eastern-most storm in Fillmore County organizing into a high-precipitation supercell before exiting east into Saline County. This upswing in storm coverage/severity shortly after nightfall focused along the nose of a developing 40 knot low level jet evident at 850 millibars. As the initial round of storms along the Highway 6 corridor started to weaken and depart, a separate cluster of strong thunderstorms blossomed farther north, primarily affecting Nance, northeastern Merrick and northern Polk counties between 10 pm-12 am CDT. Non-severe thunderstorms festered across these far northeast counties into the early morning hours of the 4th, but by 3 am CDT all convection had departed South Central Nebraska for the night.||The environment was ripe for supercell development by early evening, with mesoscale parameters featuring around 1500 J/kg most-unstable CAPE in the presence of around 40 knots of 0-6 kilometer deep layer wind shear. On the synoptic scale aloft, this event occurred in the presence of subtle, low amplitude shortwave troughs embedded within strengthening southwesterly flow in the mid-upper levels, downstream from a powerful closed low centered over the Central Rockies. On the following afternoon and evening of Oct. 4th, this stout low pressure system entered the Central Plains, but South Central Nebraska was narrowly spared a significant weather event thanks to a strong surface cold front and dryline pushing east of the area by late afternoon. It was a close call, however, as several damaging tornadoes raked northeast Nebraska and northwest Iowa during the late afternoon and evening of the 4th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel size hail was falling at the time of the report.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: According to the U.S. Drought Monitor, September 2013 marked the 16th consecutive month of category D2 Severe (or worse) Drought within South Central Nebraska, continuing one of the most notable droughts since the 1950s. However, there was positive news as the total number of counties affected by D2-or-worse drought continued to dwindle from 14 at the start of September to only 11 at month's end. Over the course of the month, the U.S. Drought Monitor reflected one-category improvement across much of the western half of the area, thanks in large part to a widespread 1.00-2.50 rainfall late in the month on the 27th. The resultant above normal monthly rainfall across several of these western counties was quite welcome, and targeted those places that needed it most based on long-term precipitation deficits. Meanwhile, generally the eastern half of the area saw little, if any change in drought classification during the month due to prevailing below normal rainfall. In fact, September marked the fourth-consecutive month of below normal rainfall across decent chunks of Hamilton, Polk, York and Fillmore counties. According to the U.S. Drought Monitor at month's end, the entire local 24-county area was void of not only category D4 Exceptional Drought (the worst-possible category) but also D3 Extreme Drought, for the first time since July 2012. Nonetheless, the lingering effects of longer-term dryness kept D2 Severe Drought firmly intact across southwestern counties, especially Gosper, Phelps, Furnas and Harlan. A separate, smaller zone of D2 drought also lingered much farther northeast, mainly within eastern Greeley and northwestern Nance counties. Meanwhile, category D1 Moderate Drought prevailed across the majority of South Central Nebraska at month's end, while a stripe of category D0 Abnormally Dry conditions (considered to be one category better than true drought) remained intact across most of Adams, Clay, Nuckolls and Thayer counties. ||Reports from around 50 NWS Cooperative Observers, and also radar-derived estimation, revealed that there was a pronounced west-versus-east split in September precipitation across the area (wetter in the west, drier in the east, and based on September normals that range from around 1.60��� in the far west to around 2.70��� in the far east). However, even within eastern counties there were localized exceptions of above normal rainfall, including areas near the intersection of Adams/Clay/Webster/Nuckolls counties, along with parts of southeastern Thayer County. As a whole, roughly 60 percent of South Central Nebraska had above normal monthly precipitation and 40 percent below normal. Per a combination of NWS cooperative observers and NeRAIN observers, a few of the highest September precipitation totals featured 4.87��� six miles north-northwest of Oxford, 4.81��� at Nelson, 4.34��� at Wilcox and 3.95 at Hubbell. On the opposite, drier side of the spectrum were sites such as Belgrade (1.04���), Polk (1.22���), Hastings airport (1.26���), and two miles west of Genoa (1.27���). In between these wettest and driest extremes, the majority of South Central Nebraska measured September rainfall totals somewhere between 1.50-3.25, including stations in or near Grand Island, Greeley, Gothenburg, Cambridge, Osceola, Aurora and Clay Center. ||Focusing on the longer-range picture of year-to-date precipitation (Jan. 1-Sep. 18), most of the 24-county area stood somewhere between 75-115% of normal, with the driest areas generally concentrated southwest of a line from Lexington-Red Cloud, including only 61% of normal eight miles south of Elwood. In contrast, the wettest areas so far in 2013 with near-to-slightly-above normal precipitation were largely concentrated in northern counties such as Greeley, Nance, Sherman and Howard (including 113% of normal at St. Paul), along with limited areas farther south including much of Nuckolls and western Clay counties. According to the USDA Nebraska Crop Progress and Condition Report for the week ending September 15th, state-averaged topsoil rated 60% short or very short and 40% percent adequate, a degradation from only 44% short or very short on August 18th. State-averaged subsoil moisture supplies remained in worse shape and rated 72% short to very short, up slightly from 64% four weeks prior. The state-averaged corn crop checked in 63% good to excellent and only 14% poor to very poor, although dryland corn only rated 36% good or excellent. Soybeans across the state rated 60% good or excellent, down from 72% in mid-August. Nebraska stock water supplies degraded slightly since mid-August to 28% short or very short. Pasture and range conditions also degraded very slightly, rating 38% poor or very poor, compared to 34% on August 18th.

During the pre-dawn hours on this Wednesday morning, a fast moving line of elevated thunderstorms raced east-northeast at 50-60 MPH across several counties in South Central Nebraska mainly along and north of Interstate 80. While the majority of this activity was sub-severe, producing only small hail, the southeast periphery of the line intensified between 530-630 am CDT, dropping quarter to golf ball size hail at various points in Merrick and Polk counties before exiting the local area.  The largest reported golf ball size hail occurred in Shelby at 620 am CDT. A few miles northeast of town, an NWS cooperative observer reported half dollar size hail still on the ground two hours after it fell, thanks to cool surface temperatures in the upper 30s F. This ended up being the final severe thunderstorm event of 2013 within South Central Nebraska.||The first thunderstorms of the morning flared up over Dawson County shortly after 4 am CDT. During the ensuing few hours, storms quickly expanded into a northwest-southeast oriented line as they tracked east-northeast. On the synoptic scale aloft, this event occurred in the presence of subtle shortwave disturbances ejecting northeastward into the Central Plains out ahead of an expansive, large scale trough centered over the Intermountain West. On the smaller scale, storm initiation and intensification was tied to increasing warm air/moisture advection evident in the 850-700 millibar layer, accompanied by steepening mid level lapse rates. Mesoscale severe weather parameters around the time of the large hail featured around 1000 J/kg most-unstable CAPE in the presence of strong mid level flow around 50 knots at 500 millibars. This elevated convection occurred well north-northwest of a quasi-stationary surface front looped from far northeast Kansas into the Oklahoma Panhandle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel size hail was ongoing at the time of the report.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Arctic high pressure building across the plains dropped low temperatures below zero across much of the area during the morning of December 23rd.  When combined with north winds of 10 to 15 mph, wind chill values over the region dropped to around 20 below for several hours.

Arctic high pressure building across the plains dropped low temperatures below zero across much of the area during the morning of December 23rd.  When combined with north winds of 10 to 15 mph, wind chill values over the region dropped to around 20 below for several hours.

Thunderstorms developed across the Nebraska Panhandle the evening of September 9th behind a cold front which was located from northeast Colorado into north central Nebraska.  One thunderstorm produced damaging winds southwest of OshKosh (Garden County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds of 60 mph were estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The drought that began in June 2012 persisted through September 2013.  Conditions did improve through the month as western and north central Nebraska received normal to above normal precipitation.  Although drought conditions improved, Extreme drought (D3) conditions remained from the Western Sandhills into southwest Nebraska at the end of the month, while the rest of western and north central Nebraska continued to be in Moderate (D1) to Severe (D2) drought.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The drought that began in June 2012 continued through September 2013. Observed rainfall ranged from under 0.50 in the northeast part of the county to near 3.00 inches in the west and south parts of the county. The rainfall allowed for improvement from Extreme Drought (D3) to Severe Drought (D2) for the western parts of the county.  By the end of the month the entire county was in Severe Drought (D2) which persisted into October.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed along a stationary front from northwest Kansas into east central Nebraska.  These thunderstorms became strong over southwest Nebraska, impacting portions of Frontier County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds tipped over some farm equipment.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving line of nocturnal thunderstorms moved into the western fringes of South Central Nebraska between 4 and 5 am CDT on this Thursday morning. In advance of these storms, new scattered showers and thunderstorms developed from St. Paul to Holdrege to Alma. The showers and storms filled in between 5 and 6 am CDT creating a continuous line of non-severe thunderstorms and taking over as the primary line. At 5 am CDT, one storm over northeastern Phelps County intensified. As it moved northeast across the city of Kearney, it produced hail up to the size of nickels at 5:26 am CDT.||These storms formed as a cold front advanced through the region. Deep, subtropical high pressure was over the southeastern states and Gulf Coast region. A shortwave trough was advancing through the Northern Rockies. High temperatures the previous day were in the lower 90s, with dewpoints in the mid 60s. South winds kept temperatures in the middle 70s until the thunderstorms arrived. Wednesday evening soundings showed the CAPE values were between 1000-2000 J/kg. Deep layer shear was estimated between 20 and 25 kts.

The same powerful storm system that produced severe weather across eastern Nebraska on October 3rd remain in the area on the 4th. A strong area of low pressure moved from eastern Colorado into southern South Dakota during the day. As this occurred, a dry line was sweeping across central and into eastern Nebraska. A warm front continued to lift north into northeast Nebraska and western Iowa by late afternoon. With unseasonably warm and moist air in the region the atmosphere became strongly unstable. This instability coupled with a strong wind field associated with the powerful storm system and created and environment favorable for significant severe weather. Supercells developed near the intersection of the dry line and warm front over northeast Nebraska during the afternoon and tracked northeast. The supercells were responsible for large hail, damaging winds, and significant tornadoes. Other isolated thunderstorms developed south along the dry line into western Iowa producing very large hail and damaging winds during the evening of the 4th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This violent tornado started 8 miles southwest of Wayne, Nebraska and flattened corn crops and tore off large limbs of some trees. Gaining strength as it headed northeast, the tornado struct a farmstead about 4.5 miles southwest of Wayne. Here barns and sheds were severely damaged or destroyed. The tornado became very wide at this point, nearly 1.25 miles wide. About 2 miles south of Wayne the tornado severely damaged a farmstead and then cased strong EF-3 damage to two homes along highway 15. The tornado then missed the downtown and residential district of Wayne and severely damaged a softball complex on the southeast side of town. ||The most significant damage occurred in the industrial part area on the east side of Wayne. Numerous metal building structures, some of them quiet large, sustained serious damage or were mangled into rubble. It was here the tornado was rated EF-3 with a couple of small pockets of low-end EF-4 damage. The tornado then crossed highway 35 and impacted the Wayne Municipal Airport. Two hangers were flattened. Some private aircraft were tumbled and ripped up. The automated weather observing station at the airport was also torn up. Continuing northeast, the tornado struck another farmstead and caused EF-2 damage to a home.  Damage continued northeast at it tracked into Dixon County. The damage in the Wayne area was estimated at $50 million.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed across western and north central Nebraska as a cold front moved across the state.  Damaging winds were produced by a few thunderstorms as they moved through the area.
Thunderstorms developed across western and north central Nebraska as a cold front moved across the state.  Damaging winds were produced by a few thunderstorms as they moved through the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were blown down near Swan Lake. The largest tree was about 10 inches in diameter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed along a stationary front from northwest Kansas into east central Nebraska.  These thunderstorms became strong over southwest Nebraska, impacting portions of Frontier County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds tipped over irrigation piping.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Although this Wednesday afternoon started out with a fairly low-key development of isolated strong to severe thunderstorms in northern portions of South Central Nebraska, this convective event ended up being one of the most significant heavy rain episodes of 2013 for much of the northeast two-thirds of the 24-county area, highlighted by a small zone of 8+ inch rainfall in southeastern Greeley County. In terms of actual severe weather, this event was small in scale, featuring only a handful of quarter to golf ball size hail reports in southern Greeley and northwestern Howard counties during the late afternoon and early evening, with the largest hail stones reported four miles south of Greeley at 535 pm CDT. However, as the large hail threat diminished by sunset, heavy rainfall took over as the main story for the next several hours lasting well into Thursday morning the 3rd, as the initially isolated development in northern counties morphed into a slow-moving, southward-drifting linear mesoscale convective system (MCS). By the time all convection finally exited South Central Nebraska shortly before sunrise on the 3rd, most places generally northeast of a line from Loup City-Gibbon-Blue Hill-Superior had received at least 2-3 of rain, but with a corridor of higher amounts generally 4-6 concentrated across much of Adams, northwestern Nuckolls and southwestern Clay counties. Within these counties, just a few of the highest reported rainfall totals included 6.35 a few miles south of Juniata and 4.75 a few miles south of Trumbull. At Hastings Municipal Airport, 3.49 of the storm total of 4.13 fell on the calendar day of Oct. 3rd, shattering the previous record for highest rainfall on any given day in October (2.95 on Oct. 16, 1968). Of even greater significance, however, was the incredible amount of rain that fell mainly between 4-9pm CDT within a small portion of southeastern Greeley County. Despite limited ground-truth from the epicenter of heavy rainfall in that area, an observer five miles northwest of Wolbach measured 8.46, and radar estimates suggested that even higher amounts might have fallen roughly 4-8 miles northeast of Wolbach. Although several counties in South Central Nebraska undoubtedly experienced short-term flooding issues from this event including lowland flooding and damage to rural roads, specific flooding reports were fairly limited. However, a few known reports included water running across several county roads in southeastern Greeley County, and also water up to the hood of a vehicle on Highway 92 east of Ashton near the Sherman-Howard County line. Fortunately, a stretch of predominantly dry weather during the preceding few weeks likely mitigated more significant flooding on larger creeks and rivers. As for thunderstorm winds during this event, there were no known-instances of severe-criteria gusts, although a RAWS sensor in western Clay County clocked a near-severe gust to 54 MPH. ||Taking a closer look at event timing, the initial development of localized strong to severe storms got underway within primarily Sherman, Howard, Valley and especially southern Greeley counties between 3-7 pm CDT. During these early stages, several very slow-moving to nearly stationary multicell storms dumped heavy rainfall and isolated large hail over many of the same areas. Then between 7-10 pm CDT, the initial storm clusters finally started sinking slowly southward toward the Interstate 80 corridor, while gradually expanding in coverage and elongating into a west-southwest to east-northeast linear system. From about 10 pm to 1 am CDT, the MCS propagated slowly southward across Interstate 80 and into counties along Highway 6 from near Holdrege to Geneva. During the pre-dawn hours of the 3rd, widespread convection continued drenching primarily those counties south of Interstate 80 and near/east of Highway 281, with backbuilding along the western edge of the complex resulting in the enhanced rainfall totals over Adams County and vicinity. Finally, between 6-7 am CDT, the back edge of weakening convection departed the eastern fringes of the local area, ending nearly 16 consecutive hours of thunderstorm activity within South Central Nebraska. On the synoptic scale aloft this was a fairly weakly-forced event, as South Central Nebraska resided under relatively weak west-southwest mid-upper level flow, downstream from a larger scale trough organizing over the Pacific Northwest and Northern Rockies. However, a low-level focus for convective development existed in the form of a southwest-northeast oriented cold front that roughly bisected Nebraska during the late afternoon and evening hours. The initial mid-late afternoon storms flared up near this frontal boundary, aided by forcing from a subtle shortwave trough passing through the area. During these early stages, mesoscale severe weather parameters within the local area featured fairly modest mixed-layer CAPE values of 1000-1500 J/kg and generally no more than 30 knots of 0-6 kilometer vertical wind shear. However, the combination of the weak shear environment featuring mid-upper level flow nearly parallel to the surface front, along with seasonably-high low level moisture reflected by surface dewpoints in the low-mid 60s F, fostered the slow storm movement and resultant heavy rainfall. As the night wore on, the southward expansion of the MCS was aided by the development of a south-southwesterly low level jet that ramped up to around 40 knots at 850 millibars, enhancing moisture transport into the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Creeks were running full across southeastern Greeley County. Water was running across many county roads. One county road was impassable and had to be closed due to high water.

By the time all convection finally exited South Central Nebraska shortly before sunrise on the 3rd, most places generally northeast of a line from Loup City-Gibbon-Blue Hill-Superior had received at least 2-3 of rain, but with a corridor of higher amounts generally 4-6 concentrated across much of Adams, northwestern Nuckolls and southwestern Clay counties. Within these counties, just a few of the highest reported rainfall totals included 6.35 a few miles south of Juniata and 4.75 a few miles south of Trumbull. At Hastings Municipal Airport, 3.49 of the storm total of 4.13 fell on the calendar day of Oct. 3rd, shattering the previous record for highest rainfall on any given day in October (2.95 on Oct. 16, 1968). Of even greater significance, however, was the incredible amount of rain that fell mainly between 4-9pm CDT within a small portion of southeastern Greeley County. Despite limited ground-truth from the epicenter of heavy rainfall in that area, an observer five miles northwest of Wolbach measured 8.46, and radar estimates suggested that even higher amounts might have fallen roughly 4-8 miles northeast of Wolbach. Although several counties in South Central Nebraska undoubtedly experienced short-term flooding issues from this event including lowland flooding and damage to rural roads, specific flooding reports were fairly limited. However, a few known reports included water running across several county roads in southeastern Greeley County, and also water up to the hood of a vehicle on Highway 92 east of Ashton near the Sherman-Howard County line. Fortunately, a stretch of predominantly dry weather during the preceding few weeks likely mitigated more significant flooding on larger creeks and rivers. As for thunderstorm winds during this event, there were no known-instances of severe-criteria gusts, although a RAWS sensor in western Clay County clocked a near-severe gust to 54 MPH. ||Taking a closer look at event timing, the initial development of localized strong to severe storms got underway within primarily Sherman, Howard, Valley and especially southern Greeley counties between 3-7 pm CDT. During these early stages, several very slow-moving to nearly stationary multicell storms dumped heavy rainfall and isolated large hail over many of the same areas. Then between 7-10 pm CDT, the initial storm clusters finally started sinking slowly southward toward the Interstate 80 corridor, while gradually expanding in coverage and elongating into a west-southwest to east-northeast linear system. From about 10 pm to 1 am CDT, the MCS propagated slowly southward across Interstate 80 and into counties along Highway 6 from near Holdrege to Geneva. During the pre-dawn hours of the 3rd, widespread convection continued drenching primarily those counties south of Interstate 80 and near/east of Highway 281, with backbuilding along the western edge of the complex resulting in the enhanced rainfall totals over Adams County and vicinity. Finally, between 6-7 am CDT, the back edge of weakening convection departed the eastern fringes of the local area, ending nearly 16 consecutive hours of thunderstorm activity within South Central Nebraska. On the synoptic scale aloft this was a fairly weakly-forced event, as South Central Nebraska resided under relatively weak west-southwest mid-upper level flow, downstream from a larger scale trough organizing over the Pacific Northwest and Northern Rockies. However, a low-level focus for convective development existed in the form of a southwest-northeast oriented cold front that roughly bisected Nebraska during the late afternoon and evening hours. The initial mid-late afternoon storms flared up near this frontal boundary, aided by forcing from a subtle shortwave trough passing through the area. During these early stages, mesoscale severe weather parameters within the local area featured fairly modest mixed-layer CAPE values of 1000-1500 J/kg and generally no more than 30 knots of 0-6 kilometer vertical wind shear. However, the combination of the weak shear environment featuring mid-upper level flow nearly parallel to the surface front, along with seasonably-high low level moisture reflected by surface dewpoints in the low-mid 60s F, fostered the slow storm movement and resultant heavy rainfall. As the night wore on, the southward expansion of the MCS was aided by the development of a south-southwesterly low level jet that ramped up to around 40 knots at 850 millibars, enhancing moisture transport into the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A rain total of 4.32 inches fell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system moved into the central Plains on Saturday morning December 21st. This storm system brought widespread significant ice accumulation to parts of the southern Plains to the upper Mississippi River Valley. To the north of the icing, a swatch of heavy snow extended from central Kansas northeast to eastern Iowa, include parts of southeast Nebraska. Snow spread into the area late on Saturday afternoon, becoming heavy during the evening, and tapered to flurries by Sunday morning as the storm system moved off to the east.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow was observed across Pawnee County. Snowfall was generally 6 to 7 inches across the county with a maximum of 7 inches reported in Pawnee City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A disturbance moved east to initiate thunderstorms that intensified to produce hail up to baseball size. As storms became elevated further northeast to produce strong winds and localized wind damage. The hail broke out windows of homes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed over southwestern Nebraska late on this Friday afternoon. After 5 pm CDT, these storms moved into Dawson, Gosper and Furnas counties in a short line oriented from northeast to southwest. Individual stronger storms were identifiable within the line. These storms produced several reports of winds between 40 and 55 mph. Winds were even strong enough to knock down a large tree near Oxford and large branches just north over Overton and in Beaver City. An irrigation pivot was also blown onto Highway 6 just north of Oxford. This line of storms weakened as they advanced further into South Central Nebraska. However, the remaining wide band of rain produced a swath of rainfall in excess of 2 inches from Ord to Loup City, Kearney and Holdrege.||The upper-level flow was highly amplified, with a ridge over the eastern United States, and a trough over the west. These storms were initiated by a slow-moving cold front associated with the trough. While archived instability information was unavailable, late afternoon high temperatures were in the upper 80s to near 90, with dewpoints in the middle 50s. These storms formed in a strong wind environment with 40 kts around 4500 ft above ground level and 50 kts at 6000 ft. Deep layer shear was near 70 kts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts were estimated near 60 mph.  Tree branches up to 5 inches in diameter were blown down and the roof of a small metal building sustained damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the pre-dawn hours on this Wednesday morning, a fast moving line of elevated thunderstorms raced east-northeast at 50-60 MPH across several counties in South Central Nebraska mainly along and north of Interstate 80. While the majority of this activity was sub-severe, producing only small hail, the southeast periphery of the line intensified between 530-630 am CDT, dropping quarter to golf ball size hail at various points in Merrick and Polk counties before exiting the local area.  The largest reported golf ball size hail occurred in Shelby at 620 am CDT. A few miles northeast of town, an NWS cooperative observer reported half dollar size hail still on the ground two hours after it fell, thanks to cool surface temperatures in the upper 30s F. This ended up being the final severe thunderstorm event of 2013 within South Central Nebraska.||The first thunderstorms of the morning flared up over Dawson County shortly after 4 am CDT. During the ensuing few hours, storms quickly expanded into a northwest-southeast oriented line as they tracked east-northeast. On the synoptic scale aloft, this event occurred in the presence of subtle shortwave disturbances ejecting northeastward into the Central Plains out ahead of an expansive, large scale trough centered over the Intermountain West. On the smaller scale, storm initiation and intensification was tied to increasing warm air/moisture advection evident in the 850-700 millibar layer, accompanied by steepening mid level lapse rates. Mesoscale severe weather parameters around the time of the large hail featured around 1000 J/kg most-unstable CAPE in the presence of strong mid level flow around 50 knots at 500 millibars. This elevated convection occurred well north-northwest of a quasi-stationary surface front looped from far northeast Kansas into the Oklahoma Panhandle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This report of nickel size hail was received via Facebook.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A short line segment of four thunderstorms moved into portions of South Central Nebraska late on this Saturday evening. At 10 pm, the line extended from Lexington to McCook. Two of the storms, over Gosper county, triggered a new storm which took over and become the dominant cell. While all other cells weakened substantially, this storm intensified over Phelps county. Strong winds, associated with this storm, broke off a significant portion of a large tree, in the city of Holdrege, around 11:30 pm CST. Peak winds at the airport were measured at 54 mph. The storm then weakened.||These storms developed as a weak cold front was sliding southeast through Nebraska. Just ahead of these storms, temperatures were in the upper 70s with dewpoints in the lower 70s. Mid-level lapse rates were steep (7.5 degrees/km in the 700-500 mb layer), resulting in MLCAPE between 2000 and 2500 J/kg. Deep layer shear was around 30 kts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts estimated near 60 mph resulted in tree damage in Holdrege.  A multi-branch portion of one tree split from the main trunk and was estimated at 15 inches in diameter. This portion of the tree landed on the corner of a home, damaging the roof.

Snow spread into the area late on Saturday afternoon, becoming heavy during the evening, and tapered to flurries by Sunday morning as the storm system moved off to the east.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow was observed across Richardson County. Snowfall was generally 6 to 7 inches across the county. Specific snowfall totals included 6 inches 3 west northwest of Humboldt, 6 inches 2 southwest of Shubert, and 6.7 inches 2 southwest of Rulo.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A disturbance moved east to initiate thunderstorms that intensified to produce hail up to baseball size. As storms became elevated further northeast to produce strong winds and localized wind damage. The hail broke out windows of homes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds estimated at 60 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree fell onto building.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Although this Wednesday afternoon started out with a fairly low-key development of isolated strong to severe thunderstorms in northern portions of South Central Nebraska, this convective event ended up being one of the most significant heavy rain episodes of 2013 for much of the northeast two-thirds of the 24-county area, highlighted by a small zone of 8+ inch rainfall in southeastern Greeley County. In terms of actual severe weather, this event was small in scale, featuring only a handful of quarter to golf ball size hail reports in southern Greeley and northwestern Howard counties during the late afternoon and early evening, with the largest hail stones reported four miles south of Greeley at 535 pm CDT. However, as the large hail threat diminished by sunset, heavy rainfall took over as the main story for the next several hours lasting well into Thursday morning the 3rd, as the initially isolated development in northern counties morphed into a slow-moving, southward-drifting linear mesoscale convective system (MCS). By the time all convection finally exited South Central Nebraska shortly before sunrise on the 3rd, most places generally northeast of a line from Loup City-Gibbon-Blue Hill-Superior had received at least 2-3 of rain, but with a corridor of higher amounts generally 4-6 concentrated across much of Adams, northwestern Nuckolls and southwestern Clay counties. Within these counties, just a few of the highest reported rainfall totals included 6.35 a few miles south of Juniata and 4.75 a few miles south of Trumbull. At Hastings Municipal Airport, 3.49 of the storm total of 4.13 fell on the calendar day of Oct. 3rd, shattering the previous record for highest rainfall on any given day in October (2.95 on Oct. 16, 1968). Of even greater significance, however, was the incredible amount of rain that fell mainly between 4-9pm CDT within a small portion of southeastern Greeley County. Despite limited ground-truth from the epicenter of heavy rainfall in that area, an observer five miles northwest of Wolbach measured 8.46, and radar estimates suggested that even higher amounts might have fallen roughly 4-8 miles northeast of Wolbach. Although several counties in South Central Nebraska undoubtedly experienced short-term flooding issues from this event including lowland flooding and damage to rural roads, specific flooding reports were fairly limited. However, a few known reports included water running across several county roads in southeastern Greeley County, and also water up to the hood of a vehicle on Highway 92 east of Ashton near the Sherman-Howard County line. Fortunately, a stretch of predominantly dry weather during the preceding few weeks likely mitigated more significant flooding on larger creeks and rivers. As for thunderstorm winds during this event, there were no known-instances of severe-criteria gusts, although a RAWS sensor in western Clay County clocked a near-severe gust to 54 MPH. ||Taking a closer look at event timing, the initial development of localized strong to severe storms got underway within primarily Sherman, Howard, Valley and especially southern Greeley counties between 3-7 pm CDT. During these early stages, several very slow-moving to nearly stationary multicell storms dumped heavy rainfall and isolated large hail over many of the same areas. Then between 7-10 pm CDT, the initial storm clusters finally started sinking slowly southward toward the Interstate 80 corridor, while gradually expanding in coverage and elongating into a west-southwest to east-northeast linear system. From about 10 pm to 1 am CDT, the MCS propagated slowly southward across Interstate 80 and into counties along Highway 6 from near Holdrege to Geneva. During the pre-dawn hours of the 3rd, widespread convection continued drenching primarily those counties south of Interstate 80 and near/east of Highway 281, with backbuilding along the western edge of the complex resulting in the enhanced rainfall totals over Adams County and vicinity. Finally, between 6-7 am CDT, the back edge of weakening convection departed the eastern fringes of the local area, ending nearly 16 consecutive hours of thunderstorm activity within South Central Nebraska. On the synoptic scale aloft this was a fairly weakly-forced event, as South Central Nebraska resided under relatively weak west-southwest mid-upper level flow, downstream from a larger scale trough organizing over the Pacific Northwest and Northern Rockies. However, a low-level focus for convective development existed in the form of a southwest-northeast oriented cold front that roughly bisected Nebraska during the late afternoon and evening hours. The initial mid-late afternoon storms flared up near this frontal boundary, aided by forcing from a subtle shortwave trough passing through the area. During these early stages, mesoscale severe weather parameters within the local area featured fairly modest mixed-layer CAPE values of 1000-1500 J/kg and generally no more than 30 knots of 0-6 kilometer vertical wind shear. However, the combination of the weak shear environment featuring mid-upper level flow nearly parallel to the surface front, along with seasonably-high low level moisture reflected by surface dewpoints in the low-mid 60s F, fostered the slow storm movement and resultant heavy rainfall. As the night wore on, the southward expansion of the MCS was aided by the development of a south-southwesterly low level jet that ramped up to around 40 knots at 850 millibars, enhancing moisture transport into the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A rainfall total of 5.92 inches occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system moved into the central Plains on Saturday morning December 21st. This storm system brought widespread significant ice accumulation to parts of the southern Plains to the upper Mississippi River Valley. To the north of the icing, a swatch of heavy snow extended from central Kansas northeast to eastern Iowa, include parts of southeast Nebraska. Snow spread into the area late on Saturday afternoon, becoming heavy during the evening, and tapered to flurries by Sunday morning as the storm system moved off to the east.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow was observed across Nemaha County. Snowfall was generally 5 to 7 inches across the county with a maximum of 6.3 inches reported 5 miles east southeast of Auburn.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Historical rains fell across the front range of the Colorado Rocky Mountains September 9th-15th.  These rains, which totaled over the yearly average across this area which totaled up to 17 inches in places fell largely within the South Platte River basin.  As the water was routed downstream along the South Platte River in western Nebraska and then into the Platte River in central Nebraska record flooding was observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding occurred along the South Platte River from September 18th through September 26th as water from the historic rains in Colorado moved through the river system. Cropland and pastureland along the river was flooded. Near Big Springs, two businesses sustained minor flood damage, while two truck stops and a motel were closed and evacuated for 48 hours. Road closures included U.S. Highway 138 and Nebraska Link 25B between Interstate 80 and the city of Big Springs. With roads leading into Big Springs being closed, the South Platte Public School was closed on Thursday, September 19th.  Two men were rescued from the river near Big Springs as they were canoeing and capsized. One of the individuals sustained minor injuries.

However, the combination of the weak shear environment featuring mid-upper level flow nearly parallel to the surface front, along with seasonably-high low level moisture reflected by surface dewpoints in the low-mid 60s F, fostered the slow storm movement and resultant heavy rainfall. As the night wore on, the southward expansion of the MCS was aided by the development of a south-southwesterly low level jet that ramped up to around 40 knots at 850 millibars, enhancing moisture transport into the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Although this Wednesday afternoon started out with a fairly low-key development of isolated strong to severe thunderstorms in northern portions of South Central Nebraska, this convective event ended up being one of the most significant heavy rain episodes of 2013 for much of the northeast two-thirds of the 24-county area, highlighted by a small zone of 8+ inch rainfall in southeastern Greeley County. In terms of actual severe weather, this event was small in scale, featuring only a handful of quarter to golf ball size hail reports in southern Greeley and northwestern Howard counties during the late afternoon and early evening, with the largest hail stones reported four miles south of Greeley at 535 pm CDT. However, as the large hail threat diminished by sunset, heavy rainfall took over as the main story for the next several hours lasting well into Thursday morning the 3rd, as the initially isolated development in northern counties morphed into a slow-moving, southward-drifting linear mesoscale convective system (MCS). By the time all convection finally exited South Central Nebraska shortly before sunrise on the 3rd, most places generally northeast of a line from Loup City-Gibbon-Blue Hill-Superior had received at least 2-3 of rain, but with a corridor of higher amounts generally 4-6 concentrated across much of Adams, northwestern Nuckolls and southwestern Clay counties. Within these counties, just a few of the highest reported rainfall totals included 6.35 a few miles south of Juniata and 4.75 a few miles south of Trumbull. At Hastings Municipal Airport, 3.49 of the storm total of 4.13 fell on the calendar day of Oct. 3rd, shattering the previous record for highest rainfall on any given day in October (2.95 on Oct. 16, 1968). Of even greater significance, however, was the incredible amount of rain that fell mainly between 4-9pm CDT within a small portion of southeastern Greeley County. Despite limited ground-truth from the epicenter of heavy rainfall in that area, an observer five miles northwest of Wolbach measured 8.46, and radar estimates suggested that even higher amounts might have fallen roughly 4-8 miles northeast of Wolbach. Although several counties in South Central Nebraska undoubtedly experienced short-term flooding issues from this event including lowland flooding and damage to rural roads, specific flooding reports were fairly limited. However, a few known reports included water running across several county roads in southeastern Greeley County, and also water up to the hood of a vehicle on Highway 92 east of Ashton near the Sherman-Howard County line. Fortunately, a stretch of predominantly dry weather during the preceding few weeks likely mitigated more significant flooding on larger creeks and rivers. As for thunderstorm winds during this event, there were no known-instances of severe-criteria gusts, although a RAWS sensor in western Clay County clocked a near-severe gust to 54 MPH. ||Taking a closer look at event timing, the initial development of localized strong to severe storms got underway within primarily Sherman, Howard, Valley and especially southern Greeley counties between 3-7 pm CDT. During these early stages, several very slow-moving to nearly stationary multicell storms dumped heavy rainfall and isolated large hail over many of the same areas. Then between 7-10 pm CDT, the initial storm clusters finally started sinking slowly southward toward the Interstate 80 corridor, while gradually expanding in coverage and elongating into a west-southwest to east-northeast linear system. From about 10 pm to 1 am CDT, the MCS propagated slowly southward across Interstate 80 and into counties along Highway 6 from near Holdrege to Geneva. During the pre-dawn hours of the 3rd, widespread convection continued drenching primarily those counties south of Interstate 80 and near/east of Highway 281, with backbuilding along the western edge of the complex resulting in the enhanced rainfall totals over Adams County and vicinity. Finally, between 6-7 am CDT, the back edge of weakening convection departed the eastern fringes of the local area, ending nearly 16 consecutive hours of thunderstorm activity within South Central Nebraska. On the synoptic scale aloft this was a fairly weakly-forced event, as South Central Nebraska resided under relatively weak west-southwest mid-upper level flow, downstream from a larger scale trough organizing over the Pacific Northwest and Northern Rockies. However, a low-level focus for convective development existed in the form of a southwest-northeast oriented cold front that roughly bisected Nebraska during the late afternoon and evening hours. The initial mid-late afternoon storms flared up near this frontal boundary, aided by forcing from a subtle shortwave trough passing through the area. However, the combination of the weak shear environment featuring mid-upper level flow nearly parallel to the surface front, along with seasonably-high low level moisture reflected by surface dewpoints in the low-mid 60s F, fostered the slow storm movement and resultant heavy rainfall. As the night wore on, the southward expansion of the MCS was aided by the development of a south-southwesterly low level jet that ramped up to around 40 knots at 850 millibars, enhancing moisture transport into the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A rainfall total of 4.75 inches occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Historical rains fell across the front range of the Colorado Rocky Mountains September 9th-15th.  These rains, which totaled over the yearly average across this area which totaled up to 17 inches in places fell largely within the South Platte River basin.  As the water was routed downstream along the South Platte River in western Nebraska and then into the Platte River in central Nebraska record flooding was observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding occurred along the South Platte River from September 18th through September 27th as water from the historic rains in Colorado moved through the river system.  Cropland and pastureland along the river was flooded. The Nebraska Link 51A Link and Nebraska Link 51B connecting Interstate 80 with the towns of Brule and Roscoe were closed. At least three businesses sustained minor flooding in and near the cities of Brule and Ogallala. Septic problems were noted at several residences around Brule causing some basement damage. Two homes southeast of Roscoe near Nebraska Link 51B suffered severe damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous early season winter storm brought moderate to heavy snowfall and strong northerly winds to much of the western Nebraska Panhandle. Wind gusts to 50 mph created blizzard conditions across the northern Panhandle where visibilities were reduced to near zero. Many roads and highways were closed by snow drifts up to four feet.  Snow accumulations ranged from two to four inches south to 8 to 14 inches north.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were several reports of six to seven inches of snow countywide.  Winds gusted to 40 mph which lowered visibilities to a half mile or less in blowing and drifting snow.  Some county and secondary roads were closed by one-foot snow drifts.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six to eight inches of snow was observed countywide.  Wind gusts of 35 to 45 mph created considerable blowing and drifting snow, with visibilities reduced to less than a half mile.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fresh snow pack over eastern Nebraska combined with Arctic high pressure that built into western Nebraska during the predawn hours of December 9th and brought very cold wind chill values to eastern Nebraska and western Iowa.  Low temperatures fell to around zero to 10 below over the region, and when combined with west to northwest winds of 10 to 15 mph, wind chill values fell to around 20 below or a little colder for several hours.

The drought that began in June 2012 persisted through October 2013. Conditions did improve through the month across north central Nebraska where above normal precipitation was observed. Although drought conditions improved, severe (D2) to extreme drought (D3) conditions remained from the Western Sandhills into southwest Nebraska at the end of the month, while the remainder remained was observed at Abnormally Dry (D0) to Moderate (D1) Drought conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The drought that began in June 2012 eased across Keya Paha County through October 2013. Widespread precipitation occurred with higher amounts in the west where 4 to 8 inches of rain fell. The beneficial rainfall reduced the impacts of the Severe Drought (D2) conditions in the west with no drought impacts throughout by the end of the month.

A vigorous early season winter storm brought moderate to heavy snowfall and strong northerly winds to much of the western Nebraska Panhandle. Wind gusts to 50 mph created blizzard conditions across the northern Panhandle where visibilities were reduced to near zero. Many roads and highways were closed by snow drifts up to four feet.  Snow accumulations ranged from two to four inches south to 8 to 14 inches north.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow and wind gusts of 40 to 50 mph created blizzard conditions with visibilities reduced to near zero.  Several county and secondary roads were closed by snow drifts of two to four feet.  Total snow accumulations of 8 to 12 inches were observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The drought that began in June 2012 continued across western Nebraska through November. The greatest impacts were across the southwest where Extreme Drought (D3) conditions were observed. Generally less than normal rainfall occurred during the month for little change in the drought. The drought continued into December.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The drought that began in June 2012 continued into November 2013. Less than normal precipitation fell with a half inch observed that resulted in the Severe Drought (D2)  conditions lingering in eastern Deuel County. The drought continued into December.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance moved across the plains on top of Arctic air that was in place over the region.  This resulted in an 18-24 hour period of light to occasionally moderate snow that fell across much of eastern Nebraska.  Due to the cold temperatures in place, readings in the single digits and teens, the ratio of snow to liquid was quite high, around 20 to 1 in many instances.  However, although winds were fairly light which limited the blowing and drifting, the extreme cold limited the effectiveness of any chemical/salt treatments and snow plowing still left many roads slick.  The early season nature of the event, and the slickness of the roads, caused many traffic accidents.  In the Lincoln area, several semi-trucks jackknifed on Interstate 80 near town closing the interstate down for a while.  Snow amounts were mostly 3 to 6 inches from Lincoln to Seward then south through Gage and Jefferson counties and also southeast across Pawnee and Richardson counties.  Higher totals included around 8 inches in Utica, and 6-7 inches near Friend, Fairbury, Adams and Shubert.  Around 5 inches fell in the Lincoln area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 6 to 8 inches fell over much of Seward county, including a little over 8 inches in Utica.  One person was killed on I80 during the evening of the 8th when their vehicle hit a patch of ice causing it to swerve into another vehicle and then slid off the road where it rolled.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the second week of September, an upper-level low pressure system formed and became cut off from the mean flow over the Intermountain West. This low slowly migrated from near Las Vegas north to Idaho. Subtropical high pressure remained stationary over the Southern Plains. This configuration channeled a stream of anomalously high moisture into the Rockies. With surface high pressure essentially stationary over the Northern Plains, several days of easterly upslope flow into the Colorado Rockies resulted in tremendous rainfall and catastrophic flash flooding in and around Boulder, Colorado. The highest amounts occurred on September 11th, 12th and 13th. Widespread multi-day rainfall totals were 7 to as high as 17 inches. In the city of Boulder, all rainfall records were broken for the month of September, for any calendar month and for any year. Northeastern Colorado is the headwaters of the South Platte River. This tremendous volume of water began its journey down the South Platte River, reaching the confluence with the North Platte on Friday, September 20th. ||By Saturday the 21st, water reached western Dawson county. River levels rose rapidly and dramatically at Gothenburg and points downstream. Year-to-date precipitation over the North and South Platte River Basins was roughly five inches below normal. So, over much of South Central Nebraska, Platte River levels were  below normal. The water reached Cozad Saturday night and in the following 24 hours, the water level increased from one foot to five feet. The peak crest was around 7 ft Tuesday morning the 24th. The story was similar as the water continued to move downstream, reaching Overton and Kearney Monday night and Grand Island Tuesday. In some areas, prior to the arrival of the flood-generated water, the Platte River was down to a narrow trickle a few inches to a couple feet wide. The flood water filled the breadth of the riverbed and its multiple channels, with standing water occurring in some low-lying areas adjacent to the riverbanks.||At all locations along the river, once water levels reached the crest, they were very slow to recede. While the water levels did not remain at the crest long, they took around three days to drop one foot from the crest height. This was partially due to rain that fell over South Central Nebraska. However, this still indicated the significant volume of water generated from the flooding rains far upstream, and it was especially impressive given that it took little more than a single day for the river levels to rise several feet.
