This low slowly migrated from near Las Vegas north to Idaho. Subtropical high pressure remained stationary over the Southern Plains. This configuration channeled a stream of anomalously high moisture into the Rockies. With surface high pressure essentially stationary over the Northern Plains, several days of easterly upslope flow into the Colorado Rockies resulted in tremendous rainfall and catastrophic flash flooding in and around Boulder, Colorado. The highest amounts occurred on September 11th, 12th and 13th. Widespread multi-day rainfall totals were 7 to as high as 17 inches. In the city of Boulder, all rainfall records were broken for the month of September, for any calendar month and for any year. Northeastern Colorado is the headwaters of the South Platte River. This tremendous volume of water began its journey down the South Platte River, reaching the confluence with the North Platte on Friday, September 20th. ||By Tuesday the 24th, water reached western Merrick and Hamilton counties. River levels in that area rose dramatically from 3.5 feet to 6.5 feet. The leading edge of the water generated from the Colorado floods continued downstream along the Merrick/Polk County border. However, the crest continued to decrease with gradual progression to the northeast. Recent rainfall resulted in a decent amount of water flowing through the Platte. So despite the impressive rises over Merrick, Hamilton and Polk counties, the extreme rises experienced upstream, where water levels were much lower, were not seen. The multiple channels had also dispersed the water enough that flooding was not a threat in Merrick, Hamilton and Polk counties. ||At all locations along the River, once water levels reached the crest, they were very slow to recede. While the water levels did not remain at the crest long, they took around three days to drop one foot from the crest height. This was partially due to rain that fell over South Central Nebraska. However, this still indicated the significant volume of water generated from the flooding rains far upstream, and it was especially impressive given that it took little more than a single day for the river levels to rise several feet.

The drought that began in June 2012 persisted through October 2013. Conditions did improve through the month across north central Nebraska where above normal precipitation was observed. Although drought conditions improved, severe (D2) to extreme drought (D3) conditions remained from the Western Sandhills into southwest Nebraska at the end of the month, while the remainder remained was observed at Abnormally Dry (D0) to Moderate (D1) Drought conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The drought that began in June 2012 eased in October 2013. This area saw rainfall that ranged from 2 inches to localized 4 inches that reduced drought conditions below Severe Drought (D2) conditions by mid month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front was the focus for a few thunderstorms October 2nd, one which was severe.

An upper level disturbance moved across the plains on top of Arctic air that was in place over the region.  This resulted in an 18-24 hour period of light to occasionally moderate snow that fell across much of eastern Nebraska.  Due to the cold temperatures in place, readings in the single digits and teens, the ratio of snow to liquid was quite high, around 20 to 1 in many instances.  However, although winds were fairly light which limited the blowing and drifting, the extreme cold limited the effectiveness of any chemical/salt treatments and snow plowing still left many roads slick.  The early season nature of the event, and the slickness of the roads, caused many traffic accidents.  In the Lincoln area, several semi-trucks jackknifed on Interstate 80 near town closing the interstate down for a while.  Snow amounts were mostly 3 to 6 inches from Lincoln to Seward then south through Gage and Jefferson counties and also southeast across Pawnee and Richardson counties.  Higher totals included around 8 inches in Utica, and 6-7 inches near Friend, Fairbury, Adams and Shubert.  Around 5 inches fell in the Lincoln area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 4 inches fell over much of Pawnee county, including Pawnee City, over about an 18 to 24 hour period.  Slightly higher amounts were reported just west and east of the county, so locally higher amounts probably fell over Pawnee county as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Arctic high pressure the pushed into southeast South Dakota during the morning of December 29th brought readings into the low single digits to eastern Nebraska and west central Iowa.  When combined with northwest winds of 15 to 30 mph, wind chill values dropped to around 20 below to 25 below zero for several hours.

According to the U.S. Drought Monitor, October 2013 marked the 17th consecutive month during which drought of at least category D2 Severe intensity resided within some portion of South Central Nebraska, prolonging one of the most notable droughts since the 1950s. However, on a positive note October was also the second consecutive month of drought improvement for parts of the area, as the total number of counties entirely or partially assigned D2 Drought dwindled from 11 at the start of the month to only six at month's end...the fewest number since early-July 2012. All six of these worst-off counties were clustered in the far southwest portion of the 24-county area: Dawson, Gosper, Phelps, Furnas, Harlan and Franklin. Meanwhile, much of the northeast half of South Central Nebraska, generally northeast of a Kearney-Superior line, observed a one-category drought improvement over the course of the month, thanks in large part to a significant rain event on the evening of the 2nd into the early morning of the 3rd, which dumped a widespread 2-5 inches and locally higher amounts. For eastern counties such as Hamilton, Polk, York and Fillmore, this event helped end a streak of four-consecutive months of below normal rainfall. Most areas that received this early-month deluge improved from D1 Moderate Drought to D0 Abnormally Dry (considered to be one category better than true drought). Furthermore, a small corridor running from eastern Adams County through southwest Clay County into northern Nuckolls County became void of all drought classification (including D0) as a result of this soaking rain event. Along the interface between the northeast areas that saw decent October rains and the much drier southwestern counties that did not, a generally north-south corridor of D1 Moderate Drought remained intact at month's end within the majority of the following counties: Valley, Sherman, Buffalo, Kearney and Webster. ||Reports from around 50 NWS Cooperative Observers, and also radar-derived estimation, revealed that roughly two-thirds of South Central Nebraska observed above normal October precipitation, and at least one-third of the area piled up at least twice-normal monthly totals (based on long-term October normals generally between 1.80-2.10). Per a combination of NWS cooperative observers and NeRAIN observers, a few of the highest October precipitation totals featured a remarkable 10.34 five miles northwest of Wolbach (most of which fell on the 2nd), 6.62 four miles east of Scotia, 6.59 five miles northeast of Osceola, 6.57 six miles north of Nelson and 5.85 at Geneva. At Hastings airport, the monthly total of 4.75 resulted in the 3rd-wettest October on record dating back 119 years. Within the much-drier southwestern section of the area, some of the lowest monthly precipitation totals were 0.78 at Franklin, 0.79 at Cozad, 1.11 at Harlan County Lake and 1.22 at Minden. In between these wettest and driest extremes, the majority of the 30-county area reported widely-varying amounts somewhere between 1.50-4.00.  ||Taking a look at longer-term precipitation trends, year-to-date (Jan. 1-Oct. 23) totals across most of the 24-county area ranged from 80-110 percent of normal, meaning that most areas hadn't observed an extreme surplus or deficit in annual moisture, in contrast to the notable deficits endured one year ago in 2012. However, without question the driest conditions so far in 2013 continued to focus within the southwest one-third of South Central Nebraska, mainly southwest of a line from Lexington-Red Cloud, including only 75% of normal precipitation eight miles south of Elwood. In contrast, the greatest coverage of at least slightly above normal precipitation so far in 2013 primarily focused north of Interstate 80 within counties such as Greeley, Sherman, Nance and Howard (including 123% of normal two miles west of Genoa), along with limited areas farther south including parts of Adams, Clay and Nuckolls counties. According to the USDA Nebraska Crop Progress and Condition Report for the week ending October 20th, state-averaged topsoil moisture supplies had improved considerably since mid-September, now rating only 29% short or very short and 69% adequate (versus 60% and 40%, respectively, back on Sep. 15th). State-averaged subsoil moisture supplies had also improved, but still rated 55% short or very short, down from 72% five weeks prior. The state-averaged corn crop checked in 68% good or excellent, and only 14% poor or very poor, although dryland corn only rated 46% good or excellent. As a whole, Nebraska soybeans rated 69% good or excellent and only 10% poor or very poor, although sorghum checked in 40% poor or very poor and only 35% good or excellent. Statewide stock water supplies exhibited a small improvement, now only 20% poor or very poor, versus 28% in mid-September. Finally, pasture and range conditions changed very little over the preceding five weeks, rating 37% poor or very poor and only 27% good or excellent.

An upper level disturbance moved across the plains on top of Arctic air that was in place over the region.  This resulted in an 18-24 hour period of light to occasionally moderate snow that fell across much of eastern Nebraska.  Due to the cold temperatures in place, readings in the single digits and teens, the ratio of snow to liquid was quite high, around 20 to 1 in many instances.  However, although winds were fairly light which limited the blowing and drifting, the extreme cold limited the effectiveness of any chemical/salt treatments and snow plowing still left many roads slick.  The early season nature of the event, and the slickness of the roads, caused many traffic accidents.  In the Lincoln area, several semi-trucks jackknifed on Interstate 80 near town closing the interstate down for a while.  Snow amounts were mostly 3 to 6 inches from Lincoln to Seward then south through Gage and Jefferson counties and also southeast across Pawnee and Richardson counties.  Higher totals included around 8 inches in Utica, and 6-7 inches near Friend, Fairbury, Adams and Shubert.  Around 5 inches fell in the Lincoln area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 4 to 6 inches fell over much of Richardson county over about 16 to 18 hours.  Heavier amounts included around 6 inches at Shubert.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm brought heavy snow along with blowing and drifting snow to northwest Nebraska on October 4-5, 2013.  Snowfall amounts were very heavy ranging to around 25 inches across far northwest Sheridan County with snow drifts up to 6 feet.  Snowfall amounts of 6 to 8 inches were widespread across the remainder of Sheridan County with drifts up to 3 feet.  Northwest winds of 25 to 35 mph caused considerable blowing and drifting. Sheridan County was included in  Nebraska Declaration DR-4156-NE.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported snowfall around 25 inches located 18 miles north of Hay Springs. Due to northwest winds of 25 to 35 mph, snow drifts up to 6 feet were reported along with numerous broken tree limbs.

The drought that began in June 2012 persisted through October 2013. Conditions did improve through the month across north central Nebraska where above normal precipitation was observed. Although drought conditions improved, severe (D2) to extreme drought (D3) conditions remained from the Western Sandhills into southwest Nebraska at the end of the month, while the remainder remained was observed at Abnormally Dry (D0) to Moderate (D1) Drought conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The drought that began in June 2012 persisted through October 2013. One to three inches of precipitation fell across eastern Cherry County during the month of October.  The heaviest moisture was observed across northern portions of the county, where drought conditions improved.  Only the southern half of the county remained in severe drought. Conditions persisted into November.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fresh snow pack over eastern Nebraska combined with Arctic high pressure that built into western Nebraska during the predawn hours of December 9th and brought very cold wind chill values to eastern Nebraska and western Iowa.  Low temperatures fell to around zero to 10 below over the region, and when combined with west to northwest winds of 10 to 15 mph, wind chill values fell to around 20 below or a little colder for several hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The drought that began in June 2012 persisted through September 2013.  Conditions did improve through the month as western and north central Nebraska received normal to above normal precipitation.  Although drought conditions improved, Extreme drought (D3) conditions remained from the Western Sandhills into southwest Nebraska at the end of the month, while the rest of western and north central Nebraska continued to be in Moderate (D1) to Severe (D2) drought.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The drought that began in June 2012 continued across the far northwest part of Brown County through September 2013.  This area saw near normal rainfall of 1.50 to 3.00 inches, keeping the drought conditions stable at Severe Drought (D2).  The drought persisted into October.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The drought that began in June 2012 persisted across the western part of Blaine County through September 2013.  This area saw near normal rainfall of 1.00 to 2.00 inches, keeping the drought conditions stable at Severe Drought (D2).  The drought persisted into October.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The drought that began in June 2012 lingered through July 2013. Exceptional (D4) drought conditions began the month over the central sandhills then lessened as convective rains moved across the area. At the end of the month the drought impact ranged from Extreme in portions of western Nebraska down to abnormally dry in the far north and north central. Precipitation for the month showed a range from a half inch to over 10 inches, with higher amounts in Hooker and Cherry counties that were beneficial in reducing the drought impacts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The drought that began in June 2012 lingered into July. Little change was seen with Extreme (D3) Drought conditions in the central and east and Severe (D2) Drought conditions in the west. AHPS observed precipitation showed rainfall amounts ranging from a half inch to locally 6 inches in the southeast. The drought continued into August.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This Thursday evening featured the most significant severe weather event of mid-late summer 2013 within South Central Nebraska, occurring mainly between 6 pm and midnight CDT as a series of multicell storm clusters with a few embedded supercells sank southward through the area, eventually congealing into a forward-propagating mesoscale convective system (MCS). Although nearly the entire 24-county area observed thunderstorm activity, there were two primary corridors of severe storms. In the west, Dawson County bore the brunt of severe weather with reports of quarter to golf ball size hail and estimated wind gusts up to 80 MPH. Just west of Gothenburg, four empty rail cars were blown over, and in Lexington, two empty semi trailers were toppled. Farther east, a more widespread corridor of severe storms initially entered far northern counties such as Valley, Greeley and Nance, before propagating southeast across the eastern Hall, Hamilton and Clay County areas before weakening. North of Interstate 80 most storm reports were marginally severe, consisting of quarter size hail and wind gusts to around 60 MPH, including a measured 59 MPH gust at Central Nebraska Regional Airport in Grand Island. However, storm mergers resulted in a notable upswing in storm intensity over southern Hamilton and especially Clay County, which was overall hardest-hit from a combination of quarter to golf ball size hail and winds estimated up to 80 MPH. This combo resulted in a significant swath of crop destruction, with radar data and limited reports suggesting that the worst crop damage extended roughly 30 miles from southwestern Hamilton County near Giltner through the Harvard, Clay Center and Edgar areas. Other notable storm reports included an irrigation center pivot blown onto Highway 6 southeast of Inland, along with a farm storage shed destroyed in the same area. Farther south, Edgar suffered significant wind damage, especially to trees but also to some structures. A grain bin that blew onto railroad tracks in Edgar was actually struck by a train, shutting down the tracks for several hours (this was the second significant severe weather event to strike Edgar in recent months, following an EF2 tornado on May 27th). Although a few storms during the evening exhibited radar-indicated rotation, and a funnel cloud was reported northeast of Wolbach shortly before 7 pm CDT, there were no confirmed tornadoes within the area. In addition to hail and wind, various locations received heavy rainfall of 2-4 inches, although flooding issues were held to a minimum. A few of the highest-known measured storm totals featured 3.90 near Cozad and 3.80 near Edgar. ||Breaking down event timing, several hours prior to the evening main show, an initial series of strong to marginally severe multicell storms developed southward out of north central Nebraska and skirted far northeast portions of the local area mainly between 8 am-noon CDT. The only report from this early activity was nickel size hail at Spalding. Following a multiple-hour lull, clusters of severe storms again built southward into primarily the northeast quadrant of South Central Nebraska between 6-8 pm CDT, a continuation of activity that initially flared up along the South Dakota border earlier in the afternoon. As these eastern storms started to concentrate near the Interstate 80 corridor around 9 pm CDT, a separate cluster of severe storms including an embedded supercell initiated just northwest of the local area over Custer County and dropped slowly southward into Dawson County between 930-11 pm CDT. During this same 930-11 pm CDT time frame, the eastern storms also intensified as multiple cells including another embedded supercell merged into a southward-surging MCS, creating the swath of Clay County damage before steadily weakening along the Nuckolls-Thayer County line. Over time, both the eastern and western convection joined together along a common outflow, gradually losing intensity and drifting southward through southern portions of the area. All thunderstorm activity finally vacated the entire 24-county area around 5 am CDT on the 2nd. On the synoptic scale aloft, this was a fairly weakly-forced event, with an absence of notable shortwave troughs within a west-northwest flow regime sandwiched between an upper ridge centered over the southern High Plains and an expansive low centered near Hudson Bay. However, mid-upper flow was seasonably strong over Nebraska, resulting in a favorable severe weather environment with 0-6 kilometer vertical wind shear around 40 knots during the evening hours. In the low-levels, convergence along a developing east-west warm front was the primary focus for initial afternoon development across northern Nebraska. As these storms propagated/developed southward into the local area, they encountered an unstable air mass featuring most-unstable CAPE values around 2000 J/kg and plenty of low-level moisture, evidenced by surface dewpoints well into the mid-upper 60s F. The southward propagation of convection was also aided by a southerly, 850 millibar low level jet ramping up to around 40 knots.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A combination of hail and strong wind created a swath of crop damage, which occurred from 2-3 miles west of Giltner to 2 miles south of Giltner. Leaves were stripped from crops. The time was estimated from radar data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The drought that began in June 2012 persisted through September 2013.  Conditions did improve through the month as western and north central Nebraska received normal to above normal precipitation. EVENT_NARRATIVE: The drought that began in June 2012 continued across portions of Wheeler County through September 2013.  Rainfall through the month varied across the county, from around 1.50 inches in the eastern part of the county to around 6.00 inches in the southwest part of the county.  The rainfall allowed for improvement in drought conditions, with only the eastern half of the county still in Severe Drought (D2) at the end of September.  The remainder of the area improved from Severe Drought (D2) to Moderate Drought (D1).  The drought persisted into October.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This Thursday evening featured the most significant severe weather event of mid-late summer 2013 within South Central Nebraska, occurring mainly between 6 pm and midnight CDT as a series of multicell storm clusters with a few embedded supercells sank southward through the area, eventually congealing into a forward-propagating mesoscale convective system (MCS). Although nearly the entire 24-county area observed thunderstorm activity, there were two primary corridors of severe storms. In the west, Dawson County bore the brunt of severe weather with reports of quarter to golf ball size hail and estimated wind gusts up to 80 MPH. Just west of Gothenburg, four empty rail cars were blown over, and in Lexington, two empty semi trailers were toppled. Farther east, a more widespread corridor of severe storms initially entered far northern counties such as Valley, Greeley and Nance, before propagating southeast across the eastern Hall, Hamilton and Clay County areas before weakening. North of Interstate 80 most storm reports were marginally severe, consisting of quarter size hail and wind gusts to around 60 MPH, including a measured 59 MPH gust at Central Nebraska Regional Airport in Grand Island. However, storm mergers resulted in a notable upswing in storm intensity over southern Hamilton and especially Clay County, which was overall hardest-hit from a combination of quarter to golf ball size hail and winds estimated up to 80 MPH. This combo resulted in a significant swath of crop destruction, with radar data and limited reports suggesting that the worst crop damage extended roughly 30 miles from southwestern Hamilton County near Giltner through the Harvard, Clay Center and Edgar areas. Other notable storm reports included an irrigation center pivot blown onto Highway 6 southeast of Inland, along with a farm storage shed destroyed in the same area. Farther south, Edgar suffered significant wind damage, especially to trees but also to some structures. A grain bin that blew onto railroad tracks in Edgar was actually struck by a train, shutting down the tracks for several hours (this was the second significant severe weather event to strike Edgar in recent months, following an EF2 tornado on May 27th). Although a few storms during the evening exhibited radar-indicated rotation, and a funnel cloud was reported northeast of Wolbach shortly before 7 pm CDT, there were no confirmed tornadoes within the area. In addition to hail and wind, various locations received heavy rainfall of 2-4 inches, although flooding issues were held to a minimum. A few of the highest-known measured storm totals featured 3.90 near Cozad and 3.80 near Edgar. ||Breaking down event timing, several hours prior to the evening main show, an initial series of strong to marginally severe multicell storms developed southward out of north central Nebraska and skirted far northeast portions of the local area mainly between 8 am-noon CDT. The only report from this early activity was nickel size hail at Spalding. Following a multiple-hour lull, clusters of severe storms again built southward into primarily the northeast quadrant of South Central Nebraska between 6-8 pm CDT, a continuation of activity that initially flared up along the South Dakota border earlier in the afternoon. As these eastern storms started to concentrate near the Interstate 80 corridor around 9 pm CDT, a separate cluster of severe storms including an embedded supercell initiated just northwest of the local area over Custer County and dropped slowly southward into Dawson County between 930-11 pm CDT. During this same 930-11 pm CDT time frame, the eastern storms also intensified as multiple cells including another embedded supercell merged into a southward-surging MCS, creating the swath of Clay County damage before steadily weakening along the Nuckolls-Thayer County line. Over time, both the eastern and western convection joined together along a common outflow, gradually losing intensity and drifting southward through southern portions of the area. All thunderstorm activity finally vacated the entire 24-county area around 5 am CDT on the 2nd. On the synoptic scale aloft, this was a fairly weakly-forced event, with an absence of notable shortwave troughs within a west-northwest flow regime sandwiched between an upper ridge centered over the southern High Plains and an expansive low centered near Hudson Bay. However, mid-upper flow was seasonably strong over Nebraska, resulting in a favorable severe weather environment with 0-6 kilometer vertical wind shear around 40 knots during the evening hours. In the low-levels, convergence along a developing east-west warm front was the primary focus for initial afternoon development across northern Nebraska. As these storms propagated/developed southward into the local area, they encountered an unstable air mass featuring most-unstable CAPE values around 2000 J/kg and plenty of low-level moisture, evidenced by surface dewpoints well into the mid-upper 60s F. The southward propagation of convection was also aided by a southerly, 850 millibar low level jet ramping up to around 40 knots.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four empty freight cars were blown over from the railroad tracks. The time was estimated from radar data.

Drought conditions eased a bit across extreme southwest Nebraska during the month of September thanks to above normal rainfall.  At the beginning of September, Dundy, Hitchcock and Red Willow Counties were all rated as being in D3 (Extreme) Drought.  By the end of the month, Dundy and Hitchcock counties improved to D2 (Severe) Drought with Red Willow remaining in D3 (Extreme) Drought.  Precipitation during the month was generally 2-3 inches, with Stratton Nebraska reporting 3.68 of precipitation.

The drought that began in June 2012 lingered through July 2013. Exceptional (D4) drought conditions began the month over the central sandhills then lessened as convective rains moved across the area. At the end of the month the drought impact ranged from Extreme in portions of western Nebraska down to abnormally dry in the far north and north central. Precipitation for the month showed a range from a half inch to over 10 inches, with higher amounts in Hooker and Cherry counties that were beneficial in reducing the drought impacts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The drought that began in June 2012 lingered into July. Little change was seen with  Severe (D2) to Extreme (D3) Drought conditions. AHPS observed precipitation showed rainfall amounts ranging from a half inch to locally 4 inches. The drought continued into August.

Farther east, a more widespread corridor of severe storms initially entered far northern counties such as Valley, Greeley and Nance, before propagating southeast across the eastern Hall, Hamilton and Clay County areas before weakening. North of Interstate 80 most storm reports were marginally severe, consisting of quarter size hail and wind gusts to around 60 MPH, including a measured 59 MPH gust at Central Nebraska Regional Airport in Grand Island. However, storm mergers resulted in a notable upswing in storm intensity over southern Hamilton and especially Clay County, which was overall hardest-hit from a combination of quarter to golf ball size hail and winds estimated up to 80 MPH. This combo resulted in a significant swath of crop destruction, with radar data and limited reports suggesting that the worst crop damage extended roughly 30 miles from southwestern Hamilton County near Giltner through the Harvard, Clay Center and Edgar areas. Other notable storm reports included an irrigation center pivot blown onto Highway 6 southeast of Inland, along with a farm storage shed destroyed in the same area. Farther south, Edgar suffered significant wind damage, especially to trees but also to some structures. A grain bin that blew onto railroad tracks in Edgar was actually struck by a train, shutting down the tracks for several hours (this was the second significant severe weather event to strike Edgar in recent months, following an EF2 tornado on May 27th). Although a few storms during the evening exhibited radar-indicated rotation, and a funnel cloud was reported northeast of Wolbach shortly before 7 pm CDT, there were no confirmed tornadoes within the area. In addition to hail and wind, various locations received heavy rainfall of 2-4 inches, although flooding issues were held to a minimum. A few of the highest-known measured storm totals featured 3.90 near Cozad and 3.80 near Edgar. ||Breaking down event timing, several hours prior to the evening main show, an initial series of strong to marginally severe multicell storms developed southward out of north central Nebraska and skirted far northeast portions of the local area mainly between 8 am-noon CDT. The only report from this early activity was nickel size hail at Spalding. Following a multiple-hour lull, clusters of severe storms again built southward into primarily the northeast quadrant of South Central Nebraska between 6-8 pm CDT, a continuation of activity that initially flared up along the South Dakota border earlier in the afternoon. As these eastern storms started to concentrate near the Interstate 80 corridor around 9 pm CDT, a separate cluster of severe storms including an embedded supercell initiated just northwest of the local area over Custer County and dropped slowly southward into Dawson County between 930-11 pm CDT. During this same 930-11 pm CDT time frame, the eastern storms also intensified as multiple cells including another embedded supercell merged into a southward-surging MCS, creating the swath of Clay County damage before steadily weakening along the Nuckolls-Thayer County line. Over time, both the eastern and western convection joined together along a common outflow, gradually losing intensity and drifting southward through southern portions of the area. All thunderstorm activity finally vacated the entire 24-county area around 5 am CDT on the 2nd. On the synoptic scale aloft, this was a fairly weakly-forced event, with an absence of notable shortwave troughs within a west-northwest flow regime sandwiched between an upper ridge centered over the southern High Plains and an expansive low centered near Hudson Bay. However, mid-upper flow was seasonably strong over Nebraska, resulting in a favorable severe weather environment with 0-6 kilometer vertical wind shear around 40 knots during the evening hours. In the low-levels, convergence along a developing east-west warm front was the primary focus for initial afternoon development across northern Nebraska. As these storms propagated/developed southward into the local area, they encountered an unstable air mass featuring most-unstable CAPE values around 2000 J/kg and plenty of low-level moisture, evidenced by surface dewpoints well into the mid-upper 60s F. The southward propagation of convection was also aided by a southerly, 850 millibar low level jet ramping up to around 40 knots.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous large trees fell onto homes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The drought that began in June 2012 lingered through July 2013. Exceptional (D4) drought conditions began the month over the central sandhills then lessened as convective rains moved across the area. At the end of the month the drought impact ranged from Extreme in portions of western Nebraska down to abnormally dry in the far north and north central. Precipitation for the month showed a range from a half inch to over 10 inches, with higher amounts in Hooker and Cherry counties that were beneficial in reducing the drought impacts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The drought that began in June 2012 lingered into July. Little change was seen with  Severe (D2) to Extreme (D3) Drought conditions. AHPS observed precipitation showed rainfall amounts ranging from a half inch to locally 4 inches. The drought continued into August.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The drought that began in June 2012 persisted through September 2013.  Conditions did improve through the month as western and north central Nebraska received normal to above normal precipitation.  Although drought conditions improved, Extreme drought (D3) conditions remained from the Western Sandhills into southwest Nebraska at the end of the month, while the rest of western and north central Nebraska continued to be in Moderate (D1) to Severe (D2) drought.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The drought that began in June 2012 persisted through September 2013.  Near normal rainfall of 1.00 to 2.00 inches fell across the county, keeping the drought conditions stable at Extreme Drought (D3) across the southern half of the county and Severe Drought (D2) across the northern half of the county.  The drought persisted into October.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The drought that began in June 2012 continued through September 2013.  Rainfall amounts of 2.00 to 4.00 inches fell across much of the county, which led to improvement of drought conditions. The entire county improved from Extreme Drought (D3) to Severe Drought (D2) by the end of September.  The drought persisted into October.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The drought that began in June 2012 continued through September 2013.  Rainfall amounts varied from around 1.50 inches to over 4.00 inches, which led to improvement in drought conditions for much of the county.  By the end of the month, only the eastern half of Deuel County remained in Severe Drought (D2).  These areas saw the drought persist into October.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This Thursday evening featured the most significant severe weather event of mid-late summer 2013 within South Central Nebraska, occurring mainly between 6 pm and midnight CDT as a series of multicell storm clusters with a few embedded supercells sank southward through the area, eventually congealing into a forward-propagating mesoscale convective system (MCS). Although nearly the entire 24-county area observed thunderstorm activity, there were two primary corridors of severe storms. In the west, Dawson County bore the brunt of severe weather with reports of quarter to golf ball size hail and estimated wind gusts up to 80 MPH. Just west of Gothenburg, four empty rail cars were blown over, and in Lexington, two empty semi trailers were toppled. Farther east, a more widespread corridor of severe storms initially entered far northern counties such as Valley, Greeley and Nance, before propagating southeast across the eastern Hall, Hamilton and Clay County areas before weakening. North of Interstate 80 most storm reports were marginally severe, consisting of quarter size hail and wind gusts to around 60 MPH, including a measured 59 MPH gust at Central Nebraska Regional Airport in Grand Island. However, storm mergers resulted in a notable upswing in storm intensity over southern Hamilton and especially Clay County, which was overall hardest-hit from a combination of quarter to golf ball size hail and winds estimated up to 80 MPH. This combo resulted in a significant swath of crop destruction, with radar data and limited reports suggesting that the worst crop damage extended roughly 30 miles from southwestern Hamilton County near Giltner through the Harvard, Clay Center and Edgar areas. Other notable storm reports included an irrigation center pivot blown onto Highway 6 southeast of Inland, along with a farm storage shed destroyed in the same area. Farther south, Edgar suffered significant wind damage, especially to trees but also to some structures. A grain bin that blew onto railroad tracks in Edgar was actually struck by a train, shutting down the tracks for several hours (this was the second significant severe weather event to strike Edgar in recent months, following an EF2 tornado on May 27th). Although a few storms during the evening exhibited radar-indicated rotation, and a funnel cloud was reported northeast of Wolbach shortly before 7 pm CDT, there were no confirmed tornadoes within the area. In addition to hail and wind, various locations received heavy rainfall of 2-4 inches, although flooding issues were held to a minimum. A few of the highest-known measured storm totals featured 3.90 near Cozad and 3.80 near Edgar. ||Breaking down event timing, several hours prior to the evening main show, an initial series of strong to marginally severe multicell storms developed southward out of north central Nebraska and skirted far northeast portions of the local area mainly between 8 am-noon CDT. The only report from this early activity was nickel size hail at Spalding. Following a multiple-hour lull, clusters of severe storms again built southward into primarily the northeast quadrant of South Central Nebraska between 6-8 pm CDT, a continuation of activity that initially flared up along the South Dakota border earlier in the afternoon. As these eastern storms started to concentrate near the Interstate 80 corridor around 9 pm CDT, a separate cluster of severe storms including an embedded supercell initiated just northwest of the local area over Custer County and dropped slowly southward into Dawson County between 930-11 pm CDT. During this same 930-11 pm CDT time frame, the eastern storms also intensified as multiple cells including another embedded supercell merged into a southward-surging MCS, creating the swath of Clay County damage before steadily weakening along the Nuckolls-Thayer County line. Over time, both the eastern and western convection joined together along a common outflow, gradually losing intensity and drifting southward through southern portions of the area. All thunderstorm activity finally vacated the entire 24-county area around 5 am CDT on the 2nd. On the synoptic scale aloft, this was a fairly weakly-forced event, with an absence of notable shortwave troughs within a west-northwest flow regime sandwiched between an upper ridge centered over the southern High Plains and an expansive low centered near Hudson Bay. However, mid-upper flow was seasonably strong over Nebraska, resulting in a favorable severe weather environment with 0-6 kilometer vertical wind shear around 40 knots during the evening hours. In the low-levels, convergence along a developing east-west warm front was the primary focus for initial afternoon development across northern Nebraska. As these storms propagated/developed southward into the local area, they encountered an unstable air mass featuring most-unstable CAPE values around 2000 J/kg and plenty of low-level moisture, evidenced by surface dewpoints well into the mid-upper 60s F. The southward propagation of convection was also aided by a southerly, 850 millibar low level jet ramping up to around 40 knots.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The drought that began in June 2012 lingered through July 2013. Exceptional (D4) drought conditions began the month over the central sandhills then lessened as convective rains moved across the area. At the end of the month the drought impact ranged from Extreme in portions of western Nebraska down to abnormally dry in the far north and north central. Precipitation for the month showed a range from a half inch to over 10 inches, with higher amounts in Hooker and Cherry counties that were beneficial in reducing the drought impacts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The drought that began in June 2012 lingered into July. No change was seen from the Severe (D2) Drought conditions. AHPS observed precipitation showed rainfall amounts ranged from a quarter inch to 3 inches. The drought continued into August.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front and upper level trough brought periods of light to moderate snowfall to the western Nebraska Panhandle.  Total snow accumulations of 3 to 6 inches was observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four inches of snow was measured in Hemingford.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The drought that began in June 2012 persisted through September 2013.  Conditions did improve through the month as western and north central Nebraska received normal to above normal precipitation.  Although drought conditions improved, Extreme drought (D3) conditions remained from the Western Sandhills into southwest Nebraska at the end of the month, while the rest of western and north central Nebraska continued to be in Moderate (D1) to Severe (D2) drought.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The drought that began in June 2012 persisted through September 2013.  Near normal rainfall of 1.00 to 2.00 inches fell across the area, keeping the drought conditions stable through September at Severe Drought (D3).  The drought persisted into October.

As these storms propagated/developed southward into the local area, they encountered an unstable air mass featuring most-unstable CAPE values around 2000 J/kg and plenty of low-level moisture, evidenced by surface dewpoints well into the mid-upper 60s F. The southward propagation of convection was also aided by a southerly, 850 millibar low level jet ramping up to around 40 knots.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The drought that began in June 2012 persisted through September 2013.  Conditions did improve through the month as western and north central Nebraska received normal to above normal precipitation.  Although drought conditions improved, Extreme drought (D3) conditions remained from the Western Sandhills into southwest Nebraska at the end of the month, while the rest of western and north central Nebraska continued to be in Moderate (D1) to Severe (D2) drought.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The drought that began in June 2012 persisted through September 2013.  Near normal rainfall of 1.00 to 2.00 inches fell across the area, keeping the drought conditions stable through September at Severe Drought (D3).  The drought persisted into October.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The drought that began in June 2012 continued through September 2013.  Rainfall amounts were slightly above normal as 2.00 to 3.00 inches fell across much of the county.  This rain led to improvement of drought conditions.  The southern half of the county improved from Extreme Drought (D3) to Severe Drought (D2), while the northern half of the county remained in Severe Drought (D2) throughout the month.  The drought persisted into October.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This Thursday evening featured the most significant severe weather event of mid-late summer 2013 within South Central Nebraska, occurring mainly between 6 pm and midnight CDT as a series of multicell storm clusters with a few embedded supercells sank southward through the area, eventually congealing into a forward-propagating mesoscale convective system (MCS). Although nearly the entire 24-county area observed thunderstorm activity, there were two primary corridors of severe storms. In the west, Dawson County bore the brunt of severe weather with reports of quarter to golf ball size hail and estimated wind gusts up to 80 MPH. Just west of Gothenburg, four empty rail cars were blown over, and in Lexington, two empty semi trailers were toppled. Farther east, a more widespread corridor of severe storms initially entered far northern counties such as Valley, Greeley and Nance, before propagating southeast across the eastern Hall, Hamilton and Clay County areas before weakening. North of Interstate 80 most storm reports were marginally severe, consisting of quarter size hail and wind gusts to around 60 MPH, including a measured 59 MPH gust at Central Nebraska Regional Airport in Grand Island. However, storm mergers resulted in a notable upswing in storm intensity over southern Hamilton and especially Clay County, which was overall hardest-hit from a combination of quarter to golf ball size hail and winds estimated up to 80 MPH. This combo resulted in a significant swath of crop destruction, with radar data and limited reports suggesting that the worst crop damage extended roughly 30 miles from southwestern Hamilton County near Giltner through the Harvard, Clay Center and Edgar areas. Other notable storm reports included an irrigation center pivot blown onto Highway 6 southeast of Inland, along with a farm storage shed destroyed in the same area. Farther south, Edgar suffered significant wind damage, especially to trees but also to some structures. A grain bin that blew onto railroad tracks in Edgar was actually struck by a train, shutting down the tracks for several hours (this was the second significant severe weather event to strike Edgar in recent months, following an EF2 tornado on May 27th). Although a few storms during the evening exhibited radar-indicated rotation, and a funnel cloud was reported northeast of Wolbach shortly before 7 pm CDT, there were no confirmed tornadoes within the area. In addition to hail and wind, various locations received heavy rainfall of 2-4 inches, although flooding issues were held to a minimum. A few of the highest-known measured storm totals featured 3.90 near Cozad and 3.80 near Edgar. ||Breaking down event timing, several hours prior to the evening main show, an initial series of strong to marginally severe multicell storms developed southward out of north central Nebraska and skirted far northeast portions of the local area mainly between 8 am-noon CDT. The only report from this early activity was nickel size hail at Spalding. Following a multiple-hour lull, clusters of severe storms again built southward into primarily the northeast quadrant of South Central Nebraska between 6-8 pm CDT, a continuation of activity that initially flared up along the South Dakota border earlier in the afternoon. As these eastern storms started to concentrate near the Interstate 80 corridor around 9 pm CDT, a separate cluster of severe storms including an embedded supercell initiated just northwest of the local area over Custer County and dropped slowly southward into Dawson County between 930-11 pm CDT. During this same 930-11 pm CDT time frame, the eastern storms also intensified as multiple cells including another embedded supercell merged into a southward-surging MCS, creating the swath of Clay County damage before steadily weakening along the Nuckolls-Thayer County line. Over time, both the eastern and western convection joined together along a common outflow, gradually losing intensity and drifting southward through southern portions of the area. All thunderstorm activity finally vacated the entire 24-county area around 5 am CDT on the 2nd. On the synoptic scale aloft, this was a fairly weakly-forced event, with an absence of notable shortwave troughs within a west-northwest flow regime sandwiched between an upper ridge centered over the southern High Plains and an expansive low centered near Hudson Bay. However, mid-upper flow was seasonably strong over Nebraska, resulting in a favorable severe weather environment with 0-6 kilometer vertical wind shear around 40 knots during the evening hours. In the low-levels, convergence along a developing east-west warm front was the primary focus for initial afternoon development across northern Nebraska. As these storms propagated/developed southward into the local area, they encountered an unstable air mass featuring most-unstable CAPE values around 2000 J/kg and plenty of low-level moisture, evidenced by surface dewpoints well into the mid-upper 60s F. The southward propagation of convection was also aided by a southerly, 850 millibar low level jet ramping up to around 40 knots.

This combo resulted in a significant swath of crop destruction, with radar data and limited reports suggesting that the worst crop damage extended roughly 30 miles from southwestern Hamilton County near Giltner through the Harvard, Clay Center and Edgar areas. Other notable storm reports included an irrigation center pivot blown onto Highway 6 southeast of Inland, along with a farm storage shed destroyed in the same area. Farther south, Edgar suffered significant wind damage, especially to trees but also to some structures. A grain bin that blew onto railroad tracks in Edgar was actually struck by a train, shutting down the tracks for several hours (this was the second significant severe weather event to strike Edgar in recent months, following an EF2 tornado on May 27th). Although a few storms during the evening exhibited radar-indicated rotation, and a funnel cloud was reported northeast of Wolbach shortly before 7 pm CDT, there were no confirmed tornadoes within the area. In addition to hail and wind, various locations received heavy rainfall of 2-4 inches, although flooding issues were held to a minimum. A few of the highest-known measured storm totals featured 3.90 near Cozad and 3.80 near Edgar. ||Breaking down event timing, several hours prior to the evening main show, an initial series of strong to marginally severe multicell storms developed southward out of north central Nebraska and skirted far northeast portions of the local area mainly between 8 am-noon CDT. The only report from this early activity was nickel size hail at Spalding. Following a multiple-hour lull, clusters of severe storms again built southward into primarily the northeast quadrant of South Central Nebraska between 6-8 pm CDT, a continuation of activity that initially flared up along the South Dakota border earlier in the afternoon. As these eastern storms started to concentrate near the Interstate 80 corridor around 9 pm CDT, a separate cluster of severe storms including an embedded supercell initiated just northwest of the local area over Custer County and dropped slowly southward into Dawson County between 930-11 pm CDT. During this same 930-11 pm CDT time frame, the eastern storms also intensified as multiple cells including another embedded supercell merged into a southward-surging MCS, creating the swath of Clay County damage before steadily weakening along the Nuckolls-Thayer County line. Over time, both the eastern and western convection joined together along a common outflow, gradually losing intensity and drifting southward through southern portions of the area. All thunderstorm activity finally vacated the entire 24-county area around 5 am CDT on the 2nd. On the synoptic scale aloft, this was a fairly weakly-forced event, with an absence of notable shortwave troughs within a west-northwest flow regime sandwiched between an upper ridge centered over the southern High Plains and an expansive low centered near Hudson Bay. However, mid-upper flow was seasonably strong over Nebraska, resulting in a favorable severe weather environment with 0-6 kilometer vertical wind shear around 40 knots during the evening hours. In the low-levels, convergence along a developing east-west warm front was the primary focus for initial afternoon development across northern Nebraska. As these storms propagated/developed southward into the local area, they encountered an unstable air mass featuring most-unstable CAPE values around 2000 J/kg and plenty of low-level moisture, evidenced by surface dewpoints well into the mid-upper 60s F. The southward propagation of convection was also aided by a southerly, 850 millibar low level jet ramping up to around 40 knots.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 12 hour total of 3.71 inches fell through 7 am CDT. This report was from the Nebraska Rainfall Assessment and Information Network (NERAIN).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The drought that began in June 2012 persisted through September 2013.  Conditions did improve through the month as western and north central Nebraska received normal to above normal precipitation. EVENT_NARRATIVE: The drought that began in June 2012 continued through September 2013.  Rainfall amounts of 1.00 to 2.00 inches were observed, which was slightly below normal.  This kept the drought conditions fairly stable through the month, with Extreme Drought (D3) across the southwest portion of the county, while the remainder of the county stayed in Severe Drought (D2).  The drought persisted into October.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: According to the U.S. Drought Monitor, August 2013 marked the 15th consecutive month of D2 Severe (or worse) drought within South Central Nebraska, continuing one of the most notable droughts since the 1950s. However, there was some positive news for parts of the 24-county area, as weekly issuances of the U.S. Drought Monitor reflected one-category improvement across a few counties mainly south of Interstate 80 and along/east of Highway 281. Meanwhile, there was little, if any change in drought categories for locations either north of Interstate 80 or west of Highway 281. In most places, a strong majority of rainfall occurred during the cooler first half of the month, while little if any fell during the much-hotter later stages of the month, putting stress on maturing crops. For southwestern counties such as Furnas and Harlan, the dry just kept getting drier, as these areas endured their fourth-consecutive month of widespread below normal rainfall. However, a secondary dry area also became increasingly-evident during the month, as the majority of far eastern counties such as Polk, York and Fillmore experienced their third-consecutive month of below normal precipitation. ||Per the U.S. Drought Monitor at month's end, category D4 Exceptional Drought (the worst-possible category) was confined to only about the southwestern half of Furnas County. However, D3 Extreme Drought continued across all/much of Furnas, Gosper, Phelps, Harlan and Franklin counties, along with small portions of bordering counties. Category D2 Severe Drought persisted through several northern and western counties including Buffalo, Valley, Greeley, Sherman and Howard. Category D1 Moderate Drought prevailed in most counties located along/east of Highway 281 and also along/south of Highway 92. On the somewhat better-off side of things, category D0 Abnormally Dry conditions (considered to be one category better than true drought) developed across much of Adams, Clay, Nuckolls and Thayer counties, which for most of this area reflected a one-category improvement since late-July. Reports from around 50 NWS Cooperative Observers, and also radar-derived estimation, confirmed that it was a roughly an even-split across South Central Nebraska between above and below normal August rainfall (with normal defined as roughly 3.15). Those areas that received above normal rainfall (mainly 3.50-6.00) were concentrated along a north-south corridor extending from the Valley/Greeley/Sherman/Howard/Nance county area in the north, southward through counties such as Hall, Adams, western Clay, Webster and Nuckolls.  A bit farther west, most of Buffalo and northern Dawson counties also received above normal rainfall. Per a combination of NWS cooperative observers and NeRAIN observers, a few of the highest August precipitation totals included 6.94 three miles southeast of Ravenna, 6.14 one mile northwest of Giltner and 5.83 at Clay Center. On the opposite, drier side of the spectrum were sites including Wilsonville (0.84), Holdrege (1.67) and three miles north of York (1.66). In between these wettest and driest extremes, the majority of South Central Nebraska measured August rainfall totals somewhere between 1.75-4.00, including stations in or near Elwood, Shelby, Kearney, Grand Island, Hastings, Minden and Fullerton. ||Focusing on the longer-range picture of year-to-date precipitation (Jan. 1-Aug. 19), most of the 24-county area stood somewhere between 70-105% of normal. In general, the greatest concentration of at least 90% of normal focused north of Interstate 80, including 119% of normal at St. Paul and also two miles west of Genoa. In contrast, the greatest coverage of locations with 75% of normal-or-less focused southwest of a line from Elwood-Red Cloud, including just 68% of normal at Cambridge. According to the USDA Nebraska Crop Progress and Condition Report for the week ending August 18th, state-averaged topsoil moisture had improved considerably versus four weeks ago, rating 44% short or very short and 55% adequate, compared to 70% short or very short in late July. State-averaged subsoil moisture supplies remained in worse shape but had also improved somewhat, rating 64% short to very short, down from 75% four weeks prior. As for the maturing corn crop, irrigated corn across Nebraska rated 79% good or excellent, while dryland corn only rated 45% good or excellent. However, D3 Extreme Drought continued across all/much of Furnas, Gosper, Phelps, Harlan and Franklin counties, along with small portions of bordering counties. Category D2 Severe Drought persisted through several northern and western counties including Buffalo, Valley, Greeley, Sherman and Howard. Category D1 Moderate Drought prevailed in most counties located along/east of Highway 281 and also along/south of Highway 92. On the somewhat better-off side of things, category D0 Abnormally Dry conditions (considered to be one category better than true drought) developed across much of Adams, Clay, Nuckolls and Thayer counties, which for most of this area reflected a one-category improvement since late-July. The drought that began in June 2012 persisted through September 2013.  Conditions did improve through the month as western and north central Nebraska received normal to above normal precipitation.  Although drought conditions improved, Extreme drought (D3) conditions remained from the Western Sandhills into southwest Nebraska at the end of the month, while the rest of western and north central Nebraska continued to be in Moderate (D1) to Severe (D2) drought.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The drought that began in June 2012 continued through September 2013.  Near normal rainfall of 1.00 to 3.00 inches fell across the county, keeping the drought conditions stable through the month.  The extreme southeast corner of the county stayed at Extreme Drought (D3), while the remainder of the county stayed at Severe Drought (D2).  The drought persisted into October.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The drought that began in June 2012 persisted through September 2013.  Conditions did improve through the month as western and north central Nebraska received normal to above normal precipitation.  Although drought conditions improved, Extreme drought (D3) conditions remained from the Western Sandhills into southwest Nebraska at the end of the month, while the rest of western and north central Nebraska continued to be in Moderate (D1) to Severe (D2) drought.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The drought that began in June 2012 persisted through September 2013.  Rainfall across the county varied; with near normal amounts of 1.00 to 2.00 inches for the western and far southern parts of the county, while amounts of 2.00 to 6.00 inches fell across the eastern half.  Where the higher rainfall occurred, drought conditions improved from Extreme Drought (D3) to Severe Drought (D2). Other areas saw drought conditions remain stable.  At the end of September, the northern half of the county and much of the southeast quarter of the county were in Severe Drought (D2), while the southwest quarter along with other portions of far southern Lincoln County remain in Extreme Drought (D3). The drought persisted into October.

In most places, a strong majority of rainfall occurred during the cooler first half of the month, while little if any fell during the much-hotter later stages of the month, putting stress on maturing crops. For southwestern counties such as Furnas and Harlan, the dry just kept getting drier, as these areas endured their fourth-consecutive month of widespread below normal rainfall. However, a secondary dry area also became increasingly-evident during the month, as the majority of far eastern counties such as Polk, York and Fillmore experienced their third-consecutive month of below normal precipitation. ||Per the U.S. Drought Monitor at month's end, category D4 Exceptional Drought (the worst-possible category) was confined to only about the southwestern half of Furnas County. However, D3 Extreme Drought continued across all/much of Furnas, Gosper, Phelps, Harlan and Franklin counties, along with small portions of bordering counties. Category D2 Severe Drought persisted through several northern and western counties including Buffalo, Valley, Greeley, Sherman and Howard. Category D1 Moderate Drought prevailed in most counties located along/east of Highway 281 and also along/south of Highway 92. On the somewhat better-off side of things, category D0 Abnormally Dry conditions (considered to be one category better than true drought) developed across much of Adams, Clay, Nuckolls and Thayer counties, which for most of this area reflected a one-category improvement since late-July. Reports from around 50 NWS Cooperative Observers, and also radar-derived estimation, confirmed that it was a roughly an even-split across South Central Nebraska between above and below normal August rainfall (with normal defined as roughly 3.15). Those areas that received above normal rainfall (mainly 3.50-6.00) were concentrated along a north-south corridor extending from the Valley/Greeley/Sherman/Howard/Nance county area in the north, southward through counties such as Hall, Adams, western Clay, Webster and Nuckolls.  A bit farther west, most of Buffalo and northern Dawson counties also received above normal rainfall. Per a combination of NWS cooperative observers and NeRAIN observers, a few of the highest August precipitation totals included 6.94 three miles southeast of Ravenna, 6.14 one mile northwest of Giltner and 5.83 at Clay Center. On the opposite, drier side of the spectrum were sites including Wilsonville (0.84), Holdrege (1.67) and three miles north of York (1.66). In between these wettest and driest extremes, the majority of South Central Nebraska measured August rainfall totals somewhere between 1.75-4.00, including stations in or near Elwood, Shelby, Kearney, Grand Island, Hastings, Minden and Fullerton. ||Focusing on the longer-range picture of year-to-date precipitation (Jan. 1-Aug. 19), most of the 24-county area stood somewhere between 70-105% of normal. In general, the greatest concentration of at least 90% of normal focused north of Interstate 80, including 119% of normal at St. Paul and also two miles west of Genoa. In contrast, the greatest coverage of locations with 75% of normal-or-less focused southwest of a line from Elwood-Red Cloud, including just 68% of normal at Cambridge. According to the USDA Nebraska Crop Progress and Condition Report for the week ending August 18th, state-averaged topsoil moisture had improved considerably versus four weeks ago, rating 44% short or very short and 55% adequate, compared to 70% short or very short in late July. State-averaged subsoil moisture supplies remained in worse shape but had also improved somewhat, rating 64% short to very short, down from 75% four weeks prior. As for the maturing corn crop, irrigated corn across Nebraska rated 79% good or excellent, while dryland corn only rated 45% good or excellent. State-averaged soybeans rated 72% good or excellent, up from 63% in late July. Nebraska stock water supplies had improved slightly from 25% to 21% short or very short over the past four weeks, and were now 78% adequate. Pasture and range conditions also improved slightly since late July, now rating 34% poor/very poor and 30% good/excellent.

The drought that began in June 2012 persisted through September 2013.  Conditions did improve through the month as western and north central Nebraska received normal to above normal precipitation.  Although drought conditions improved, Extreme drought (D3) conditions remained from the Western Sandhills into southwest Nebraska at the end of the month, while the rest of western and north central Nebraska continued to be in Moderate (D1) to Severe (D2) drought.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The drought that began in June 2012 continued through September 2013.  Near to slightly below normal rainfall of 1.00 to 2.00 inches fell across the county, keeping the drought conditions stable, with  Extreme Drought (D3) across the southeast half of the county and Severe Drought (D2) across the northwest half of the county.  The drought persisted into October.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The drought that began in June 2012 persisted through September 2013.  Conditions did improve through the month as western and north central Nebraska received normal to above normal precipitation.  Although drought conditions improved, Extreme drought (D3) conditions remained from the Western Sandhills into southwest Nebraska at the end of the month, while the rest of western and north central Nebraska continued to be in Moderate (D1) to Severe (D2) drought.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The drought that began in June 2012 continued through September 2013.  Near to slightly above normal rainfall of 1.00 to 3.00 inches fell across the county, keeping the drought conditions stable, with  Extreme Drought (D3) across the eastern two-thirds of the county and Severe Drought (D2) across the western third of the county.  The drought persisted into October.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The drought that began in June 2012 persisted through September 2013.  Conditions did improve through the month as western and north central Nebraska received normal to above normal precipitation.  Although drought conditions improved, Extreme drought (D3) conditions remained from the Western Sandhills into southwest Nebraska at the end of the month, while the rest of western and north central Nebraska continued to be in Moderate (D1) to Severe (D2) drought.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The drought that began in June 2012 continued through September 2013.  Near to slightly above normal rainfall of 1.50 to 3.50 inches fell across the county, which improved drought conditions, especially across the southern half.  Drought conditions remained at Extreme Drought (D3) across the northern half of the county, while improving from Extreme Drought (D3) to Severe Drought (D2) across the southern half of the county.  The drought persisted into October.
